COMMENT: Working in an institution, I would say one of the primary issues is valuing the inmate population over the staff. COMMENT:
I have developed checklists and job aids on my own to assist in successful operations because our centralized services units refuse to go on the record with current instructions or procedures.
My team is continually refining our products and documentation to remain relevant and effective.
I try to be proactive in my client interactions, holding empathy for those unfamiliar with our services.
Under-performing staff are not effectively coached nor held accountable for under-performance.
Managers and executives say they hold lofty values but in practice regularly exclude input from below and/or get defensive about calls for change.
Survey results and reports are delivered to the executive level and then fall into a black hole, never to be heard from again.
IDEA_TEXT: Coach under-performing staff and hold them accountable for poor work.

COMMENT:
All of my department seems to behave from a place of fear and not from a place of continuous improvement. Each department head and director rejects any change due to its potential for someone calling attention to it. Thus, nothing is ever improved for the good of the California consumer.
The upcoming reorganization of the Business, Consumer Services and Housing agency is a waste of money and time when the funds could be used to seriously address homelessness. The fear of even one person mis-using their housing allotment on something socially unacceptable has led to many layers of bureaucracy and few resources left for direct help to those who need it.
IDEA_TEXT: Change department culture from fear-based to improvement-focused. Encourage leaders to support positive changes instead of rejecting them.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Growth mindset; Management culture and leadership approach; Trust and openness; Work culture

COMMENT: I feel the largest hurdle we have to address is excess bureaucracy.  In operating such a large government, it is easy to have too many fingers in the mix.  It is our responsibility to analyze and identify areas we can cut down on bureaucratic surplus.  For example, I find it absurd to be required to submit an 18-page document just to repair an ice maker.  We must cut down on the number of forms and unnecessary hoops needed to complete the smallest of tasks.
IDEA_TEXT: Reduce bureaucracy and paperwork. Cut down on the number of forms and unnecessary steps needed to complete small tasks like equipment repairs.

COMMENT:
The Problem
California’s current model for investigating and prosecuting insurance fraud is fragmented and inefficient. The Department of Insurance (DOI) houses the Fraud Division, a law enforcement unit operating within a regulatory agency. Simultaneously, $80.9 million in annual grant funding is distributed to county District Attorney’s (DA) Offices to prosecute fraud cases. This dual structure presents several challenges:
Conflicting Missions: Regulatory agencies prioritize industry oversight and collaboration, which can conflict with the enforcement and prosecutorial roles of law enforcement.
Operational Inefficiencies: DOI lacks the infrastructure, procurement agility, and legal support systems typical of dedicated law enforcement agencies.
Jurisdictional Gaps: Only counties receiving grants can pursue fraud cases, leaving many areas underserved and creating geographic inequities.
Short-Term Incentives: Grant-based funding encourages quick wins over long-term, complex investigations.
Ethical Concerns: Financial incentives tied to prosecutions raise questions about the impartiality of justice.
Recruitment and Retention Issues: DOI struggles to retain investigators, many of whom leave for better opportunities at the Department of Justice (DOJ).
The Proposed Solution
The proposal recommends transferring the DOI’s Fraud Division (163 peace officers and 75 support staff) and the $80.9 million in grant funding to the California Attorney General’s Office.
IDEA_TEXT: Transfer the Department of Insurance Fraud Division (163 peace officers and 75 support staff) and $80.9 million in grant funding to the California Attorney General's Office.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Consolidate government agencies

COMMENT:
As a newer state employee, one of the biggest problems I’ve noticed is the lack of proper training and clear guidance for staff, especially when it comes to understanding their roles, responsibilities, and how to grow within their careers. A lot of employees are expected to learn on the fly, but they don’t always know who to go to for help or even what other units or departments are responsible for. This creates confusion, delays, and sometimes discouragement, especially for new hires.

If I could address one thing, it would be to improve communication and access to information. I’d love to see a more organized, user-friendly internal directory or onboarding resource hub that clearly outlines what each division does, who to contact, and what training or career development opportunities are available for your classification. I think mentorship programs or cross-division shadowing could also be super helpful to give employees real insight into how other parts of state government work.

At the end of the day, I believe that better training and communication leads to more confident employees, better service to the public, and stronger teams overall.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a user-friendly internal directory and onboarding resource hub that shows what each division does, who to contact, and what training and career development opportunities are available.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Career growth; Employee training; Internal communication; Knowledge management; Onboarding new employees

COMMENT:
Leadership: The executive leadership team does not have the best interests of the department in mind.  Nothing kills a team faster than a leader who doesn’t trust them.  Leaders—your people aren’t there to make you look good. You’re there to make them successful. Without them, you don’t have impact. You have a title. Lead with trust. The results will take care of themselves.

There is misalignment, miscommunication, trust, and a continued need to break down the silos. Also, for women in leadership: women should be lifting up and celebrating the successes of others. There is a lot to be gained from learning from others.

Lastly, leaders, managers, supervisors: do not elevate yourself on the backs of others.
IDEA_TEXT: Build trust between leaders and staff. Leaders should support their teams instead of using them to look good. Break down silos and improve communication between departments.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Organizational structure and culture; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Internal communication; Management culture and leadership approach; Trust and openness

COMMENT: Supporting, embracing and strengthening telework for CA employees. Understanding that not all CA employees can telework, but for those who can, telework has proven to be very productive with workflow, reduces congestion on the roads (every CA driver can support this, doesn't have to be a state worker), more environmentally friendly, and promotes flexibility. The recent independent audit about CA State worker telework states that CA could save up to $225 million in building expenses with telework.
IDEA_TEXT: Support and strengthen telework for California state employees who can work remotely. This reduces traffic, helps the environment, gives workers flexibility, and could save the state up to $225 million in building costs.

COMMENT:
Volunteering is working without pay.  It allows the volunteer to participate in something they are interested in, find purpose and might even be passionate about.  It allows for the utilization of innate talents, skills or the discovery of innate talents, skills.  Everything obtained through this experience should be considered in job hiring and promotional situations.   The skills used on the job should be converted into college credits.  We should be working with our colleges and universities to look into that conversion and offering on-site classes to obtain degrees.  We can fill the empty space in government buildings with college and university classes instead of expanding physical space on campuses.  It would allow students to work closely with professionals in their chosen fields of study easily.  It would allow employees to learn current practices and skills in their positions. Or explore other areas of interest.
I think management positions should be renamed Leadership.  Leadership should include management skills but lean heavily on what it means to lead.   Training and development, apprenticeship programs should be required throughout every department. Our workforce should be highly knowledgeable of the department they work for and continuously learning skills.     There could be an organization created of representatives from each department learning about each other, building relationships and working together on specific projects.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a cross-department group with representatives from each department to learn about each other and work on shared projects.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Internal communication; Shared resources

COMMENT: I have created and suggested ways to stream some of the processes in my work.  However, I am much more interested in working on teams charged with creating big change in the way we do business.  "We" through teams can create more of an impact to meet the needs of Californians efficiently, timely, ensuring high quality and effectively.  I think we need to document the way we are doing business (processes, procedures etc) currently.  Then, implement continuous improvement process to remove and improve.  We might utilize technology more for those tasks that could be done by a software program instead of time and money wasted on manual tasks.  Time and money used in making our employees more knowledgeable about state government (the departments, their impact on the public and their community) as well as their immediate office/unit/division/department. Time and money can be used in acquiring additional skills, honing current skills ect. Creating a workplace culture that encourages employees to stay.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a workplace culture that encourages employees to stay.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee retention; Work culture

COMMENT: I always look at certain tasks with a continuous improvement mindset.  I have improved processes and suggested improvements because of that mindset.  However, I believe there needs to be big steps, innovative steps taken to do the business of government differently and better.  This takes teams of employees.  We then become "we" instead of the "I".  We are public servants and in my opinion should work together to fulfill the needs of the public which means we need to create a culture that enables each employee to be encouraged, to bring their talents, skills and experience to the table.  We need more pathways to promote so employees dont feel stuck or having to take a position not well suited for the pay increase..  I think we need to look at the successes of other state departments, maybe city and county departments within California, maybe the Federal government, of other states and even other countries, public entities (corporations, nonprofits etc).  What about other leadership styles, combining ones that will work for us.  If you are not in a management or exec position it is hard if not impossible to make or submit suggestions for changes that are considered and implemented.  So, that might mean creating a situation requiring input, actional input, decision involvement from staff level employees up.  That situation would mean replacing managers with leaders and a culture supporting involvement throughout the department.
IDEA_TEXT: Build a culture that encourages all employees to share their talents and skills.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Organizational structure and culture; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal feedback; Trust and openness; Work culture

COMMENT: I'm reading the comments and recognize some of the suggestions mentioned have been used at one time or another based upon the direction of the exec staff at the time.  I was sharing my vision of California State government equating it to a large corporation with consistent processes connecting each department within it with a friend who then corrected me in responding with each department is a company and they operate independent of each other.........which is what we have.  I think we can do better.  A member of the church I attend complained that they sell a publication to several state departments and they have to complete several forms from each department who is interested in purchasing their publication.  Lean Six Sigma is a good continuous improvement methodology tool as well as, total quality management and there are others that might be worth looking into. The State had an external directory and call center.  I like the idea of a Resource Hub for all employees.  We had an Innovation lab at one time.  Be great to have a Statewide lab and Statewide Resource Hub for all State employees.  Mentorship programs come and go. Cross Training that includes T/D (an opportunity to work in a position for training and development for a period of time) should be required of all departments.
IDEA_TEXT: Build a statewide Resource Hub and Innovation Lab for all state employees to share tools and ideas.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Communication and knowledge sharing; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Knowledge management; Shared resources; Software and tools

COMMENT: Why are we being stuffed into cubicles like sardines because they don't have the proper space to accommodate full time work in office? If this is the case, they should not expect us to be in the office full time. We should continue working from home permanently and not go into the office at all. Especially the employees who work more than 50 miles from their office. The state hired people with the intent to be hybrid work and now want to take it back. No work is getting done for those in the office. I get 10x the amount of work done at home than in person.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow permanent work from home for all staff, especially those living more than 50 miles from the office.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: I think a lack of uniformity across state agencies and even within the organization creates a challenge. It seems each organization interprets the rules/regulations differently and as a result, the public is not necessarily receiving accurate information, and the red tape that our analysts end up dealing with causes major delays in our internal processes. Especially within the hiring process.
IDEA_TEXT: Create uniform rules and procedures across all state agencies. Make sure all departments interpret regulations the same way to reduce delays and give the public accurate information.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Communication and knowledge sharing; Governance and oversight
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Align policy and implementation; Cross-agency collaboration; Process design and methodologies

COMMENT: It would be important to teach employees how to speak truth to power, as it were.  We are often in positions where we are not heard, not consulted, and not valued.  Yet, we are the front line, in the trenches and know what is going on with the work of serving Californians.  Teach us how to speak to closed ears, to gain attention, and be heard will go a long way in state service becoming more effective.  How can we navigate the politics and still keep our shirts (and jobs) would be very useful.   Seems I can hear leadership laughing.  However, if the purpose of service is truly important, then we need to be taken seriously.  Leadership gets what leadership allows.  It may be time to shift what leadership allows.  Ty.  Peace,
IDEA_TEXT: Train employees to speak up to leadership. Teach staff how to get attention, be heard, and navigate politics while keeping their jobs.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Management culture and leadership; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Internal feedback; Management culture and leadership approach

COMMENT: As a new employee, I have struggled with receiving proper training. I think there should be a monthly onboarding call/orientation for all new state employees. Since we are all under one employer, there should be a standard for what information is shared to new staff. Each Department's HR has different capacities and people shouldn't have to wait 3 weeks to complete onboarding with HR.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a monthly onboarding call for all new state employees. Set a standard for what information is shared to new staff across all departments.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Budget and resources; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal communication; Onboarding new employees; Shared resources

COMMENT:
From my observations and experience, the misuse of consultants versus upskilling staff and providing a pathway for advancement.  Some CEA leadership not being qualified for their role and no way to address via HR or other pathway since they may be appointed. Poor leadership allows for poor moral.  Have noted leadership in some agencies unwilling to work with leadership from other.  While not the 'norm', it does happen and creates barriers. Would like to know that GO leadership encourages leaders from within agencies to work together and track for accountability. 

I think that having a feedback loop that includes a 360 review tool for reviewing 'up' is really important for culture development as well as building trust within an agency. Even if some of the feedback sounds trite or personal, it is still feedback and encourages transparent conversations. 

Encourage lunch and learns that focus on emotional intelligence and communication versus just via onboarding training. While not required as part of annual trainings that I'm aware of, could be promoted as 'optional and encouraged' - it is empowering for self-reflection and relationship building.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a 360-degree feedback system that lets staff review their supervisors. Use this to build trust and improve agency culture.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal feedback; Trust and openness; Work culture

COMMENT: I think we need to (where appropriate, legal, and feasible) leverage AI and or machine learning to reduce the time spent on routine compliance and monitoring functions. For example, machine learning can be used to identify fraudulent or suspicious transactions for high volume systems (i.e., CalFresh). AI could be used to speed up user access reviews for critical systems by identifying users who haven't logged on for a certain period of time, moved to different position within the department, or are no longer on paroles. The biggest hurdle to this would be getting ever department access to ML/AI specialists who could appropriately implement these technologies as well as training existing compliance subject matter experts on how to use these AI tools. However, the potential cost savings arising from a reduction in manual compliance and monitoring tasks could be well worth the investment in AI technicians and new trainings for compliance staff.
IDEA_TEXT: Use AI and machine learning to reduce manual compliance and monitoring tasks. Provide AI specialists to departments and train compliance staff on AI tools. Focus on systems like CalFresh fraud detection and user access reviews.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Process improvement; Governance and oversight; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: AI and large language models; Compliance; Digitize processes; Employee training

COMMENT:
I have brought my private sector experience to the agency and helped develop organizational policies and procedures on how to efficiently track and update workflows outside of full project management and provide a pathway for all employees on the team to participate. It hasn't totally worked because leadership did not recognize the value to the team, and I will continue to try. 

Always encourage bi-lateral communication styles that allow for the free flow of information even when being told 'that's not how it's done' - this comment indicates a barrier to a growth mindset that limits change and does not provide the appropriate guardrails for a responsive government. How we work internally reflects on how we are perceived externally, and it matters! Building trust where possible should be a priority always. What works well is when an agency has a clear and guiding mission statement and sticks to it - not through self-promoting sounds bites and LinkedIn posts but also with internal communications with the teams to help breakdown real and perceived silos -
IDEA_TEXT: Create and follow a clear mission statement that breaks down silos through internal team communications.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Internal communication; Management culture and leadership approach

COMMENT:
Right now, ASL Interpreters in California state service are often managed by people who don’t understand the profession or the needs of the Deaf/Hard of Hearing community. This creates mismanagement, inconsistent access, and unnecessary strain on both interpreters and the people they serve. A practical, immediate step would be to require each state department that employs ASL Interpreters to have an ASL Interpreter Coordinator—a position held by a qualified interpreter. This model already exists in the State Schools, where interpreters are managed effectively by someone with lived professional expertise. Expanding that model across departments would ensure proper support, accountability, and better access for Deaf/Hard of Hearing employees and clients.
Longer term, California could take a bold step by creating a Department of Language to house and organize all interpreters statewide—including ASL, Spanish, and other languages. This would centralize resources, guarantee professional management by people who understand the work, and ensure that language access is treated as an essential public service. Importantly, this would also allow interpreters to be shared across departments, so that if a DMV office (for example) suddenly needed an ASL Interpreter, the Department of Language could quickly dispatch one. This approach strengthens equity, efficiency, and accessibility across the state.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a Department of Language to centrally manage all interpreters statewide including ASL, Spanish, and other languages, allowing interpreters to be shared across departments as needed.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Shared resources; Translations

COMMENT: A fresh look at procurement practices is needed, for cost-saving measures. For example, I recall our Office Technician being told that he was required to order folders to use in our program that cost around $7 just for one, where I could have bought the same folder at retail price for less than half that cost. When ordering hundreds or thousands of items like we were having to do, the wasted tax dollars add up quickly.
IDEA_TEXT: Review and improve procurement practices to reduce costs. Allow departments to buy common office supplies at lower retail prices instead of requiring expensive government contracts.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Procurement

COMMENT: One of our biggest problems within state service that affects (likely) every service and product we offer is - Leadership. The way our duty statements are written and who we prioritize within the hiring process often promotes individuals into leadership roles with very specific (often niche) knowledge, but frequently lacking leadership skills (like compassionate communication, relational intelligence, teambuilding, conflict resolution, creative intelligence, etc). I would suggest two things: Add new AGPA roles (e.g., AGPA 1, AGPA 2, etc) to allow for growth within analyst positions and reduce the need to transition into leadership for monetary purposes. The same is likely true for other position types. And I would also institute better leadership development programs. What we offer now is below minimum standard and/or often suggestive in nature - you "could" take X Course, but why do it when you're so busy. I would like to see distinct and explicit development programs, mentorship opportunities, and a clear progression path that tracks which leaders are not only learning these skills but applying them, and then make job opportunities for them to succeed and evolve within their career. I think with better leadership a lot of the bureaucracy, bottlenecking,  communication, and problem-solving issues we face would likely "get better" on their own.
IDEA_TEXT: Build better leadership training programs with required courses, mentorship, and clear career paths that track skill development and application.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Career growth; Employee training; Manager training

COMMENT:
The primary problem with working for the State of California is the way staff are treated. Most supervisors receive thorough training on how to issue disciplinary actions, but very little, if any, on how to actually lead, motivate, and support their teams. This results in a culture of enforcement over empowerment, where employees often feel undervalued and micromanaged rather than inspired to perform at their best.

Suggested solutions: 

Implement hiring and promotion practices that prioritize: People skills, Proven ability to develop others, and Desire to lead not just manage. Select supervisors based on leadership potential. The work can be learned on the job but emotional intelligence needs to be there before they become a supervisor and have power over peoples careers.

Build accountability for “how” supervisors lead. Tie supervisor performance evaluations and promotions to: Team morale and retention metrics (if there is a high turn-over then something is wrong that shouldn't be ignored), 360-degree feedback from staff (this is one I have implemented upon myself), and Demonstrated leadership behaviors — not just output numbers.

Shift the culture from punitive to supportive. Encourage a workplace philosophy that emphasizes: Employee engagement over enforcement
Problem-solving conversations instead of automatic discipline, Psychological safety, where staff feel comfortable speaking up.

Invest in real leadership development not just a book club.
IDEA_TEXT: Change how supervisors are hired and promoted. Focus on people skills, ability to develop others, and leadership potential instead of just technical skills.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee performance reviews; Employee retention; Management culture and leadership approach; Manager training

COMMENT:
The only way to fix homelessness is housing first. Prior to the State, I worked directly w/ homeless people. Many would even out if given a stable place to live. The "bootstraps" mentality doesn't work when there aren't any boots. Also, what's better, people languishing on the streets in front of our tourist attractions, or the streets being clean because people are housed? 

FAQ: Why can’t they work?
- Many do but don't make enough for rent
- Many are disabled & can't work fulltime/at all. SSI is not enough to cover housing. Many are veterans who lost housing. Section 8 list is years long
- Many are elderly. Some are formerly “normal” people who lost everything in 2008 before they were going to retire & never recovered
- Some have criminal records which hinders hiring
- Job market is bad right now

FAQ: Why can't they just go to shelters? 
- Shelters break families up bc many are separated by gender, so some families prefer to stay together in their cars or on the street
- Curfews. Need to be in by 8 pm and out by 6 am, which is hard for working people
- Easy place to get your stuff stolen
- Not always accommodating for disabled people

FAQ: Why are they on drugs/alcohol?
- Some are actually not, they have MH disorders that can look like usage
- Some use uppers to stay awake because of theft, assault, etc
- Some just like to use, but we shouldn't not help bc of this. Why do we judge use among poor ppl, but look the other way at silicon valley tech bros using coke & ketamine?
IDEA_TEXT: Implement housing first approach to address homelessness. Provide stable housing as the primary solution before addressing other issues.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: One of the most pressing challenges I see is the restriction on using AI tools in our daily work. This limitation not only slows down innovation but also prevents us from serving our communities with the efficiency, creativity, and responsiveness they deserve.  AI, when used ethically and intentionally, is not a threat—it’s a partner. It can help us streamline repetitive tasks, generate accessible content, analyze data for equity gaps, and even support inclusive language in public communications. These are not luxuries; they are necessities in a modern public service environment. To solve this, I would propose a pilot program that allows the use of AI tools within a secure, policy-guided framework. We could start with low-risk applications—like summarizing meeting notes, drafting outreach materials, or organizing project timelines—and build trust through transparency and training. My vision is not just to adopt technology, but to steward it wisely. With the right guardrails, AI can become a tool for justice, clarity, and care—reflecting the very heart of public service.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a pilot program to allow AI tools for government work. Start with low-risk tasks like meeting notes, outreach materials, and project timelines. Use secure systems with clear policies and staff training.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Technology and digital innovation; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: AI and large language models; Digitize processes; Employee training; Software and tools

COMMENT:
Problem:
Managers are overextended and lack the capacity to coach and guide their teams effectively.

Why this happens:

-High performers are often promoted to management based on individual success, not leadership ability.

-Many managers have never received ongoing coaching (distinct from one-time training), so they may be unaware of their gaps or the impact on their teams.

-People don’t know what they don’t know, so they’re unlikely to seek out coaching on their own.

Solution:
Provide ongoing coaching for managers, including peer-to-peer formats. This isn’t about formal training programs, but rather open, topic-driven dialogue. For example, live forums could focus on a key topic, with breakout groups (2–4 people) every 10–15 minutes to discuss real-world scenarios and share solutions. CalSTRS does something similar once per quarter for their managers, so that might be a template to start from in other agencies. It's not required, but I think that's a misstep.

Why it matters:
When managers receive consistent coaching, they’re better equipped to guide their teams, set clear expectations, and connect individual work to broader goals, ultimately improving team morale and efficiency over time.
IDEA_TEXT: Provide ongoing coaching for managers through peer-to-peer formats and live forums. Use topic-driven dialogue with breakout groups to discuss real scenarios and share solutions, similar to CalSTRS quarterly manager sessions.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal communication; Internal feedback; Manager training

COMMENT:
Plain language requirements should be defined objectively in state law to ensure clarity and consistency across state communications. Currently, many state websites and publications are filled with jargon that confuses the public.

To address this, I recommend a peer review program where agencies evaluate each other’s websites and publications for clarity and readability. Internal experts often struggle to recognize how unclear their writing may be to the average Californian. External peer feedback can help identify confusing language and promote more accessible communication.
IDEA_TEXT: Create clear writing rules in state law and set up a peer review program where agencies check each other's websites and documents for plain language that regular people can understand.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal feedback; Plain language; Policy development process

COMMENT:
The State's hiring process is confusing, cumbersome, and inefficient for applicants AND hiring managers. 

From the internal perspective, hiring managers are inconsistently trained, some not at all, on the process. When will we hear back from CalHR with an offer? Do I have to/can I write to people who didn't get the job? Am I required to contact references? Can I give a verbal offer before I get the go-ahead from CalHR? Once when I was hiring, HR screened the applications, I interviewed people, chose a candidate, and then HR told me that person wasn't qualified -- that shouldn't happen! The whole process takes a very long time, and we lose the most qualified applicants because they start new jobs before we even finish interviewing people, or get the $ offer from CalHR. The confusion and lack of communication around the process causes extreme inefficiency and time wasted.

From the perspective of an applicant, the process lacks transparency: When will I hear back?; How high do I have to score on an exam to be considered?; I got a 95% on the exam, was highly qualified for the job, but I didn't ever hear a word from the agency or CalCareers after I submitted my application. Applicants shouldn't need to go to Reddit for answers to these questions. 

Staff need consistent-across-the-board training and guidance materials. We need to give applicants better, transparent guidance and communication. We need to make the hiring process more efficient and less cumbersome all around.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve hiring process training and communication. Train all hiring managers consistently on the process, give clear timelines to applicants, and create transparent guidance materials for both staff and job seekers.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Hiring and recruitment; Internal communication

COMMENT:
Issue 1 - Lack of software and interface training for new and existing employees.
Solution 1 - Make software training and tool updates easier to find and advertised. This includes Microsoft products and new tools.  There are tools out there we don't know about which can be useful as changes come, or new tools are available.

Issue 2 - Too much internal Spam.
Solution 2 - Set standard subject lines for Department/Statewide emails to better filter them to email folders. E.g. use "Job Posting" at the beginning of the subject for any kind of new or backfill positions being advertised.

Issue 3 - Reasonable Accommodation (RA) process.
Solution 3 - Allow 1st or 2nd level supervisors/managers to determine RA approval, and not have it go to headquarters.  IF denied, then the employee should have the option to push it up the chain.  HQ is a bottleneck and some folks have to wait 6 months or more for approval.

Issue 4 - Inflexible Telework
Solution 4 - Allow more flexibility of telework schedule by having 1st or 2nd level supervisor/manager to determine viability of telework for the position and the schedule (required in office vs. remote time), instead of one size fits all coming from headquarters or statewide.

Issue 5 - Most forms not e-sign ready
Solution 5 - Convert electronic forms to be e-sign ready instead of requiring convoluted procedures to "flatten" them.
IDEA_TEXT: Convert electronic forms to allow digital signatures instead of requiring staff to flatten them through complicated procedures.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Software and tools

COMMENT: State workers who commute to and from the office using public transportation who used to receive monthly vouchers for that expense are more recently required to submit reimbursement claims to the State Controller's Office (to CalAters Global) for review for each individual month and each request currently has to be reviewed forensically and approved as a unique non-travel expense for each individual commuter. This forensic review process and account reconciliation would seem to be waste of State resources if the commuter is going to continue to use the same public transportation each month. The suggestion is to bring the voucher system back. If the cost of a monthly transportation pass increases (which is infrequent) then the voucher amount would increase accordingly. Surely that would be less costly than CalAters reviewing every individual commuter's request for reimbursement every month by the accounting team.
IDEA_TEXT: Bring back the monthly voucher system for public transportation instead of requiring individual reimbursement claims through CalAters Global each month.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Workplace operations; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee travel; Expense reimbursement; Public transportation

COMMENT:
I would like to address the issue of recidivism at CDCR.

I have introduced an anti-recidivism initiative to CDCR, called 'Trade Ready' where we train the incarcerated people to be Tradesmen through on the job training maintaining the facility at Plant Operations. They are not only taught a trade, but more importantly how to be tradesmen, how to work with other Tradesmen to get the job done, under stressful time sensitive conditions. They learn the skills to be successful workers out on the streets. This is achieved by modifying the way, we utilize and train the Incarcerated. We want to release Tradesmen, not criminals into society. And all this is done at no additional cost to the State.
IDEA_TEXT: Implement the Trade Ready anti-recidivism program at CDCR. Train incarcerated people as tradesmen through on-the-job facility maintenance work to teach job skills and work habits needed for success after release.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public policy initiatives; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT: I would like to see state workers treated as equals to every other job. We get paid significantly less, yes because of our benefits but its disproportionally less. Rather then using us to make up the deficit of the governor, how about they learn to budget. OR how about all California's pay for the issues, not just state workers. Our 3% raise was taken, and we were given 5 hours off a month. Someone please explain what we are going to do with 5 hours off a month when we cant afford to go anywhere since we didn't get our raise, and cost of living here has gone up way more than 3%. State workers are not the private checkbook for the governor.  Our unions our weak, and scared to fight!
IDEA_TEXT: Make state worker unions stronger so they can better fight for employee rights and fair pay.

COMMENT: I facilitated Communities of Practice (CoP) convenings throughout one of my grant's lifecycles -- transforming routine communications into a shared ritual of learning, alignment, and empowerment.  These convenings became trusted spaces where colleagues could gather across programs to exchange wisdom, build trust, and co-create solutions. We didn't just talk, we equipped. Each session served as a launchpad for new directives and federal guidance, offering timely technical assistance and practical guidance to support implementation. By blending relational connection with strategic clarity, these CoPs helped cultivate a culture of responsiveness, where innovation is rooted in relationship, and public service becomes a shared craft.
IDEA_TEXT: Create Communities of Practice meetings across programs. Use these meetings to share knowledge, build trust, and create solutions together. Include training and guidance to help staff use new rules and processes.

COMMENT: I envision state agencies that are rooted in integrity and liberated from the shifting winds of political whim—where decisions are guided not by expedience, but by enduring principles of equity, stewardship, and service. In this vision, public servants are empowered to act with clarity and courage, supported by structures that honor truth over trend. Technical guidance flows freely, communities of practice thrive, and the work of governance becomes a sacred trust—not a partisan tool. I believe that when we anchor our institutions in wisdom and accountability, we create space for justice to flourish and for every policy to reflect the dignity of those it serves.
IDEA_TEXT: Build state agencies based on integrity and clear principles. Remove political influence from decisions and focus on equity, stewardship, and service. Support public servants with structures that value truth and create communities of practice.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Governance and oversight; Management culture and leadership; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Management culture and leadership approach; Trust and openness; Work culture

COMMENT:
as a state employee and working for CDCR, I fell we don't let the inmate population grow as if they were outside in today's environment, for example not letting them make their own medical appointments within the institution so we can cut down on the appointments within the institution. CDCR Officers are also expected to become Office technician when Officers sole responsibility is security and to make sure the inmate is accounted for, not check in and out after their appointment with medical. I believe if we as a department ran the medical areas as they do outside of the prison setting, we would be moving in the right direction.

CDCR also does not provide inmates vocational classes (i.e. HVAC, plumbing, etc.), so they can go out into today's society and excel. expand to job ready programs that way inmates can get a job once getting out and then we can offer more job fairs for inmates as well.
IDEA_TEXT: Add vocational training programs like HVAC and plumbing so inmates can get jobs after release.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public policy initiatives

COMMENT:
Battery powered bicycles and scooters need to have restrictions on speed when only under battery power.  If they exceed the speed they need to be registered as motor vehicle requiring insurance and all the laws that apply to motor vehicles.  E-bikes/scooters are getting out of hand and endanger pedestrians and other non-motorized modes of transport.

Less environmental hoops to jump through.  Projects which need to get done for traveling public due to increased accidents and deaths shouldn't get hamstrung by the coastal commission, waterboards, etc. making the project take a decade or more to get to construction - especially if the footprint isn't appreciably changing.

Stop blaming transportation agencies for increased pedestrian accidents, especially when the increase is the mostly fault of the pedestrians:  they cross wherever they please (not at cross walks) at all hours of the day, and especially at night wearing dark clothes.  If the vehicle pattern hasn't changed for decades, but pedestrian collision accidents have increased, we need to study the pedestrian effect.
IDEA_TEXT: Reduce environmental review delays for transportation safety projects. Limit coastal commission and waterboard reviews that slow down accident prevention projects, especially when the project footprint stays the same.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Workplace operations; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Environmental sustainability; Process delays; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT:
I would address the primary problem of departmental technology silos hindering effective digital government service delivery. Currently, departments solve similar problems other departments are solving or have already solved in isolation, resulting in inefficient and redundant workstreams. The digital service delivery is fragmented and inefficient, also contributing to a confusing and frustrating experience for Californians. 

To solve this, I would propose the following:

1. Mandate Inter-departmental Collaboration: The state should create a framework that incentivizes and, where necessary, requires departments and agencies to collaborate on key initiatives. This could involve creating a statewide playbook and process that all digital service projects align and follow with a central oversight. 

2. Standardize Technology and Data: We need to break down the technical barriers that prevent departments from working together. A statewide mandate should be put in place to standardize technology platforms and data-sharing protocols. For instance, all digital services should use a common API frameworks and be inter-operable. This would allow different agencies to easily share information, integrate services, and build a more seamless, user-friendly experience for the public.

By implementing these changes, we can move from a system of isolated, inefficient solutions to a collaborative, and cohesive government that is truly focused on better serving the people of California.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a statewide framework that requires departments to work together on digital projects. Use common technology platforms and data-sharing rules so agencies can easily share information and build better services for Californians.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Digital services; Shared data; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT: Allowing individual departments and work teams to make their own decisions about telework would significantly boost recruitment, retention, morale, and productivity. Per the Aug 2025 report released by the California State Auditor, it would also save taxpayers hundreds of millions of dollars and reduce the state's carbon footprint. Enabling staff at all levels to work from home allows staff to avoid expensive commutes and devote more energy to their work and families. As a manager, my observations have been that in-office work has done nothing to boost productivity among my staff and only hurts morale. If the state wants to operate at its highest capacity, it needs to recruit the best staff from across the state.  The best way to do this is to empower individual work teams to make their own decisions about telework.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow individual departments and work teams to make their own decisions about telework to boost recruitment, retention, morale, and productivity while saving taxpayer money and reducing carbon footprint.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Employee retention; Environmental sustainability; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: Provide maximum teleworking to all state employees that are able to perform teleworking as a part of their job function as a codified benefit. This will result in the ability for the state to consolidate its building footprint into a few major buildings throughout the state which can be used for hotelling. Take the remaining buildings no longer in use and convert them into section 8 or subsidized housing for homelessness which will reduce the need to spend billions on homeless shelters/programs. Take the savings from those programs and rebuild the rainy-day funds. This will also reduce the impact of vehicle emissions with at least 100k state workers being taken off the daily commute throughout the state. Everyone wins. I understand this would require a lot of logistics, but if the state wants to play hard ball with state workers, then they could trade the 3% GSI (general salary increase) for permanent teleworking enshrined in major MOUs between the unions and the state of California. Please execute this ASAP. Thank you.
IDEA_TEXT: Make teleworking a permanent benefit for all state workers who can do their jobs remotely. Use the office space savings to consolidate buildings and convert unused ones into housing for homeless people. Trade the 3% general salary increase for permanent teleworking rights in union contracts.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Employee pay and benefits; Employee retention; Physical infrastructure; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: The best way to increase efficiency, morale, and positive impact is to start with the facts (in this case, data). If data shows that one work method is more favorable/optimal than another, then that information could become an actionable item. If data is not immediately available, then look to your labor force for feedback. If your labor force is non-committal, then maybe it is time for a trust building exercise...these ideas are regularly explored in books regarding leadership. Read "The Five Dysfunctions of a Team" or Carly Fiorina's "Tough Choices". In this moment, millennials and younger generations have a lot to teach. They were born into the world of data. They know exactly how to improve their jobs. And having a platform to do so will not only increase productivity but save the state millions upon millions on labor force hours, lawsuits, and resource expenditures.
IDEA_TEXT: Create platforms for younger staff to share improvement ideas. This will boost productivity and save money on labor, lawsuits, and resources.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Organizational structure and culture; Management culture and leadership; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee retention; Internal feedback; Management culture and leadership approach; Work culture

COMMENT: A problem I'd like to raise is the importance of providing timely and meaningful feedback. Strong managers use the STD 638 to offer the truly earned feedback, where mediocre managers often rank everyone as "M" and provide little, if any,  constructive feedback there or in day-to-day interactions. For those who have been over-achievers all of our lives, to have a mediocre manager provide this is frustrating and off-putting. Encourage managers to work hard on these as this engagement will motivate staff, support professional growth and ensure that high performers feel valued. Less effective managers tend to provide only average or generic reviews, even for excellent employees, without offering guidance on how they can advance. This lack of feedback discourages staff and can hinder development. Additionally, promotions should be based on demonstrated leadership qualities rather than circumstance. A stronger vetting process is needed to ensure promotions go to those who consistently model dedication and performance. HR should also allow and encourage higher rankings for employees who truly give 110%. When staff exceed expectations but their reviews do not accurately reflect their effort, it creates deep frustration and drives talented employees to consider leaving for agencies where their contributions will be recognized. By strengthening both the feedback process and evaluation system, the organization can better retain top talent and truly promote a culture of excellence.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve manager feedback and evaluation systems. Train managers to use STD 638 forms effectively, provide meaningful feedback instead of generic ratings, and allow higher rankings for top performers to retain talent and promote excellence.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee performance reviews; Employee retention; Manager training

COMMENT: Programming workflow processes, I have found, is one of the most effective ways to improve efficiency in the public sector (as much as the private). Unfortunately, supervisors have been of the mindset that we "do not have the budget for implementing that sort of software". But this point becomes moot if the work is primarily done in-house and when the number of labor hours is reduced as a result. Streamlined workflow processes can provide the same quality of work, and a return on investment is almost always achieved.
IDEA_TEXT: Program workflow processes in-house to reduce labor hours and improve efficiency. Focus on streamlined processes that provide the same quality work with a strong return on investment.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Process design and methodologies; Process documentation

COMMENT: Recognize that as collective, state workers not only deliver the state services to our constituents, but we also are the people that live here and consume the services in California. When you take away from us, you are ultimately taking away from the state as a whole. Recognize us for our importance. Recognize us for our dedication. Recognize us for what we put into our communities and state.
IDEA_TEXT: Recognize state workers for their importance, dedication, and contributions to communities and the state.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Organizational structure and culture; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee retention; Trust and openness; Work culture

COMMENT:
With so many forms needed from any company to purchase products, or repair products we often go to the level of replace over repair simply due to the excess amount of forms required. If a repair of an item is $150, but a new one is $1400, many business will not spend the time required to fill out forms that don't really apply to the repair or problem. So the state ends up buying new and from a business that is charging a premium for simply drop shipping from major retailers only because they are willing to fill out the paperwork.  We should be spending money on quality equipment at a reasonable price. But all the red tape to purchase cost 2x as much for the same item that can be purchased with a credit card. Today's day and age with online shopping and shipping, three bids for items we can look up and know what they should cost is just wasting the retailer or employees time. 

Trim the paperwork red tape, and allow online purchases with known items removing the bid process. Hold the person responsible for buying outside of approved items with Credit card.
IDEA_TEXT: Reduce paperwork requirements for purchases and repairs. Allow online purchases with credit cards for known items and remove the bid process. Hold buyers accountable for purchases outside approved items.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Bureaucracy; Digitize processes; Procurement

COMMENT: When I first arrived, I focused on observing, listening and truly understanding what was happening in our department. Since then, I've been able to implement positive changes that have strengthened our operations. One of the first steps was addressing the organization. I created a RACI Matrix and lists that easily track all that my team has going on. We now have SOPs for each project and staff update them regularly, which ensures consistency and accountability. I also prioritized cleaning up of outdated files, archiving older versions so the most current documents are always easy to find--nothing is deleted, just properly stored. This has made our system streamlined and accessible. I've also modeled simplicity and clarity. I encourage staff to think not only about making their tasks easier for managers, but also about how our work impacts our internal and external partners. That means avoid unnecessary complexity--do not force people to open multiple attachments or links, we summarize key information and provide references as necessary. Our department now feels like a well-run ship because I consistently listen to concerns, take notes, pay close attention to details and operate with a growth mindset that is always focused on continuous quality improvement. Much of my early work felt like cleaning up messes, but through the foundation, it allowed for more strategic planning. I've helped to create a culture of efficiency and accessibility, and respect for everyone's time.
IDEA_TEXT: Create clear organization systems. Use RACI Matrix to track team work, make standard operating procedures for each project, and have staff update them regularly.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal communication; Process design and methodologies; Process documentation

COMMENT: The most challenging meetings and problems I have been in are those that are trying to solve the problems of the 5th or 6th standard deviation from the norm. By doing so, we create so many exceptions and (my favorite word) nuances that a simple policy to would clearly affect 98% of the state is now confusing for 99% of the state for the sake of the 1% who we tried to carve an exception for. We are a large and diverse state. One size does not fit all. However, nuanced policies are extremely difficult to put on paper, create black and white rules, and thus subject to abuse. We must accept that there will be some who will be left out by policies we write, but also appreciate that a well drafted policy will help far more than will be left out. A poorly drafted policy, while perhaps well intentioned, often creates more waste and confusion than solutions.
IDEA_TEXT: Focus on simple policies that help most people instead of creating complex rules with many exceptions. Accept that some people may be left out but write clear policies that help far more than they exclude.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Governance and oversight; Process improvement; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Plain language; Policy development process; Policymaking

COMMENT: Throughout my career at the State, looking at ways to do better was always a priority. IDEA_TEXT: Support staff who want to improve processes. Remove barriers that prevent efficiency improvements from being implemented.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Management culture and leadership

COMMENT: I find that the primary problem is that too many large corporations take way too much from the CA taxpayers.  Many were not eligible for PPP loans and there's not enough law enforcement so it encourages criminal and/or misrepresentation in obtaining free funds from the State of California.   Some corporations illegally or inappropriately received PPP loans based on the honor system and took PPP loan deductions in their returns.  If the FTB shared this misrepresentation found during routine audits with other agencies such as SBA to enforce those (i.e. large and publicly traded corporations) that inappropriately received funds, the State of CA would be able revive the income.  When CA funds are inappropriately used to fund profitable corporations during the panddemic such as PPP loans and online criminals continuously stealing money from the State of CA's IT systems, more law enforcement is needed to avoid the illegal loss of funds .  Rather than cutting state salaries, budget cuts, and other reductions that harm the State of CA and our taxpayers, we should pursue the large corporations and criminals that illegally received the funds in the first place.  Placing more security on our IT systems is crucial for protecting our taxpayer funds from continual hacking and misappropriation of funds.  Instead of hitting the CA taxpayers with more costs and illegal use of taxapyer funds, the politicians should invest more in our IT crime prevention to protect the State of CA & its people.
IDEA_TEXT: Increase law enforcement for financial crimes. Add more resources to pursue corporations and criminals that illegally received state funds.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Governance and oversight; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT:
CalEPA's Boards, Departments, and Offices (BDOs) routinely require regulatory data to be uploaded to public databases annually, or within days of certain events (e.g., a rain event). For example, the State Water Board operates the SMARTS database wherein industrial permittees upload data throughout the year to demonstrate compliance with the General Industrial Storm Water Permit. Other BDOs operate similar types of databases. 

The state should strongly consider a system to compare the applicable numeric values in the self-reports against the regulatory thresholds. Automatic notices could be issued to the permittee or a report could be generated and provided to the enforcement unit of each BDO. This would improve compliance and accountability, while also benefitting human health and the environment. The opportunity for facilities to "hide and wait" for enforcement would be diminished.
IDEA_TEXT: Create an automated system to compare self-reported data against regulatory limits in CalEPA databases like SMARTS. Send automatic notices to permit holders when limits are exceeded and generate reports for enforcement units.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Technology and digital innovation; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Public service delivery and responsiveness; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT: Recruitment and retention of state peace officers is harder than ever over the last 10 years.  Many police agencies have adjusted to the times and now allow officers to wear well groomed beards and display tattoos that are of good taste and not objectively offensive.  However, the California Highway Patrol has not allowed tattoos to be displayed nor male officers to have beards, all for the sake of tradition.  Our modern culture has grown to accept both as acceptable in the workplace, and in fact, makes officers more relatable.  Many communities may be able to relate to an officer who has tattoos, especially younger people.  As the melting pot of the United States, many foreign cultures co-exist in California and universally respect male officers with beards as being elders with serious life, and job experience, particularly when they see gray in the beard.  This makes them more willing to listen and being compliant with an officer.  It's already permitted for religious exemptions, i.e. for Sikh officers.  It should be permitted across the board for all officers.  This may seem like a non-issue to someone not in law enforcement, but other agencies have noticed higher levels of recruiting and retention by allowing tattoos and beards.  It is also a huge morale booster and allows officers to express themselves as individuals and humans, rather than as de-humanized, monotonous, faceless agents of the government.  I would like to see State Personnel Board force this issue with CHP
IDEA_TEXT: Allow California Highway Patrol officers to display tattoos and wear beards to improve recruitment and retention. Have the State Personnel Board require CHP to update appearance policies to match other police agencies.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Employee retention; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: One primary challenge I would address is ensuring that state resources are used as efficiently and effectively as possible, particularly in programs that directly impact communities. Too often, valuable funding is underutilized, delayed due to administrative hurdles, or misused. To address this, I would focus on streamlining processes, improving interagency communication, and increasing transparency for both staff and the public. By simplifying procedures and making information more accessible, we can ensure that funds are deployed on time, maximize their impact, and maintain public trust.
IDEA_TEXT: Streamline government processes and improve communication between agencies. Make information more accessible to staff and the public to ensure funding is used on time and effectively.

For example, I helped streamline the grant review process by creating a centralized tracking system, which reduced processing delays and improved communication between staff and grantees. COMMENT:
Two primary challenges I’ve noticed in my workplace are the lack of standardization and the amount of repetitive, manual work. Like others have mentioned, inadequate training resources, limited access to information, and redundant forms often slow down our ability to work efficiently. To address these issues, I’ve taken initiative by using Microsoft Office tools (SharePoint, Power Automate, Power Apps, etc.) that are already available to us at no additional cost to the state.

Here are some of the steps I’ve implemented:

1. SharePoint Site Development
-Centralized and standardized all forms required for my role.
-Used Power Automate to auto-fill redundant fields, reducing repetitive data entry.
-Migrated resources from local drives to the cloud in compliance with IT policies, improving accessibility and collaboration.

2. Interactive Training Resources
-Built a Wiki page to consolidate and standardize the training I’ve received from management.
-Created step-by-step work instructions with embedded GIFs (instead of static screenshots) so employees can visually follow the process in real time.

By streamlining processes and digitizing resources, I’ve helped improve efficiency, reduce errors, and make information easier to access—all while keeping costs neutral for the state.
IDEA_TEXT: Use Microsoft Office tools already available to the state to reduce manual work and improve efficiency. Create SharePoint sites to centralize forms, use Power Automate to auto-fill repeated data entry, move resources to the cloud, and build interactive training materials with step-by-step instructions and visual guides.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Technology and digital innovation; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Employee training; Software and tools; State cloud storage

COMMENT:
Having worked for the state for over ten years across two departments and three locations, I have consistently noticed a lack of standardization and collaboration that affects overall efficiency. Even within the same department, different offices often perform the same tasks in completely different ways. This creates confusion, inconsistent training, and inefficiencies. There is also a widespread lack of clear and accessible documentation for processes and procedures. Without it, onboarding is more difficult, and new employees spend unnecessary time trying to figure out how to complete routine tasks.

There is also very little collaboration or sharing of knowledge between departments. When I have reached out for guidance or documentation, I rarely receive a response, and when I do, it is often not useful. It can feel like departments are operating in silos, keeping information to themselves instead of working together. I think focusing on standardizing processes and building a more open, collaborative culture would significantly improve how effectively we function as a state.
IDEA_TEXT: Standardize processes across all offices within departments. Create clear documentation for all procedures and improve collaboration between departments to share knowledge and resources.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Communication and knowledge sharing; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Digitize processes; Process documentation; Shared resources

COMMENT: I know it's almost impossible to reign in at this point, but the favortism in my dept (CDCR food services) is out of control. I feel my dept doesn't do enough to show appreciation for the role that we play everyday in rehabilitating the incarcerated population. We tend to get left behind. We don't feel valued by our agency.

A problem I have noticed is the lack of camaraderie and unity within departments and boards. A solution I may suggest would be quarterly employee enrichment activities. I believe such activities have a tremendous impact on morale. I think that happier state workers can produce more positive interactions with the public.
IDEA_TEXT: Hold quarterly employee activities to build teamwork and improve morale.

IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Trust and openness; Work culture

COMMENT: Telework, when possible, is the biggest issue.  Telework works.  Consolidating office space (combine multiple departments/agencies into spaces) will save money, as per the state auditor's report. State Workers working remotely will also save money by reduced commutes and the associated climate change savings, which used to be important to our state government, and by putting money back in their own communities, versus being expected to prop up the communities around the office buildings their departments are located in.  It also increases employee happiness, and as a long-term state worker who has been in offices with both great morale and awful morale (both prior to the availability of telework, and not related to telework at all) happy employees make for more productive employees.  Telework works, the state has shown that.  Reversing course will only hurt the state going forward.
IDEA_TEXT: Expand telework options and consolidate office space. Allow remote work when possible and combine multiple departments into shared office buildings to save money and improve employee happiness.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Workplace operations; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Cross-agency collaboration; Office management; Physical infrastructure; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: Working in a human resources division, the largest complaint I have been given from the public is the lack of communication. I find that many NEW to State service candidates apply to multiple vacancies and do not receive any feedback or notice if a job was closed. I believe communication from hiring manager(s) needs to improve; whether a candidate interviewed or not, they should be receiving an auto-email explaining why they were not chosen. There are options for this in ECOS (from CalHR), so I am not sure why it is not being used (at least in my Dept). I feel this may alleviate some of the frustration from those wanting to join State service.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve communication with job candidates. Send auto-emails to all applicants explaining why they were not chosen, using existing ECOS system features from CalHR.

COMMENT: Southern District has a desk manual for operations Associates. We have several new Associates who are coming onboard in early September, so we have been updating the desk manual. Each Associate has been assigned to update a section of the manual. I think the Seniors specifically made sure that the section we work on this go-round is different than the section we worked on the previous go-round. The goal is for the desk manual to be a resource that makes it easier and quicker for new Associates to learn what their responsibilities are, and how to do them. And also provide a resource whenever an Associate needs to do something they don't do often.  Working on the section that I was assigned helped me understand that process better. Although no one looks forward to working on their assigned portion of the manual, the desk manual decreases snafus, and makes it easier for us to be both more effective and efficient.
IDEA_TEXT: Create and maintain desk manuals for operations staff. Assign each team member to update different sections regularly to help new staff learn their jobs faster and give current staff a reference guide for tasks they do less often.

COMMENT: We can make California government more effective by implementing long term telework. With digital systems, agencies can process requests, respond to the public and complete requests faster. In emergencies, public health crisis, or severe weather, employees can safely and efficiently report to their home offices without additional risk. Cost savings for California is clear when telework is implemented. Agencies save money on buildings, equipment, utilities, office supplies, etc. Telework will eliminate daily commutes, thus improving air quality in California, reducing risks of accidents, and giving employees time back to improve their mental health. California can attract top talent by offering this perk.
IDEA_TEXT: Implement long-term telework for state employees. Use digital systems to process requests faster, save money on buildings and supplies, reduce commuting and air pollution, and attract top talent.

IDEA_TEXT: Train staff in lean six sigma methods to reduce waste and improve efficiency.

IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Employee training; Process design and methodologies

COMMENT: Make telework flexible and based on operational needs as determined by first line managers and staff. A one size fits all telework model (RTO 2 or 4 days per week regardless of operational need) is not appropriate for most technical positions that can be done effectively from home, is wasteful of taxpayer dollars, and decreases employee morale, all of which impacts public service. Having a one size fits all telework model is also bad for retaining talent. We need technically proficient staff. If talented staff can telework full time with better work life balance and greater physical and mental health, and be treated with respect in managing their workload in the private sector, why would they opt to work for the state where we are treated like we can't be trusted to manage ourselves or allowed to telework based on operational need?  Who wants to work for an entity that frames the need to be in office as "because teachers and janitors have to report to a job site everyday so should all other state workers". This approach disrespects the life choices that folks have made regarding their career path. A teacher and a janitor are aware that their work involves reporting to an offsite office daily, whereas technical careers or those that do not require direct face to face with the public should be allowed to telework based on the operational needs of their position. Flexible telework fosters trust, is just as effective for collaboration, and more cost effective.
IDEA_TEXT: Make telework flexible based on job needs. Let managers and staff decide telework schedules instead of requiring all employees to work in the office the same number of days per week.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership approach; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT:
Economies that are structured with a primary goal of concentration of wealth have unintended negative results such as poverty, isolation and scarcity and ideologically based conflicts.

One of the ideas to diversify economic security is a fairer flat tax for all (removing exemptions). This flat taxation idea also helps secures housing and jobs for all thru subsidies offset by the flat tax and creates a liquid cash flow (capital) to help maintain the economy as we shift to more sustainable systems not dependent so much on non-renewable resources with are finite. By broadening the definitions of success we can address phenomenon like corruption, toxicity, hoarding and emphasize values like sharing and improve empathy and caring generally. As we share intelligence and apply it to these common goals people experience and learn from the experience of others so problems that threaten societies are minimized as the cooperation crosses typical socio-economic and other common isolating conditions that threaten an optimal healthy form of democracy and threaten environmental sustainability.  The results are predicted to offer a more sustainable way of life where greater forms of equilibrium and harmony may thrive and the diversity of our intelligence more positively be expressed and celebrated.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a flat tax system without exemptions to fund housing and job subsidies for all residents.

COMMENT: Moving off the network drive and working in SharePoint. Better tracking, better version control. Drives me crazy when people attach files for review via email. Put on SharePoint and send me the link to review. We can then all collaborate on in real time and there's no question of if this is the correct version.
IDEA_TEXT: Switch from network drives to SharePoint for file sharing and collaboration. Use SharePoint links instead of email attachments for document review to improve version control and enable real-time teamwork.

COMMENT: Stop spending so much money on outside consultants, work with actual employees. Set up SME's (Subject Matter Experts) and make them actually accessible to employees.
