COMMENT: I have seen a lot of wasted taxpayer money due to the lack of modern technology and practices, as well as the state's refusal to create a space to save millions of dollars by allowing their employees to have a right to work-life balance. Many city and county agencies are far more advanced, provide healthy work-life balances, and provide proper training to management. The little bit of training provided is toxic and our managers are taught poor leadership behaviors. We as adults, are treated like children and are not allowed to have a work-life balance because management uses unfounded information to justify saying no to its employees. We have been told by our management that we are all replaceable and are not valued as employees and this is very disheartening. For our commissioner to feel that way about his staff is demoralizing. This message from the commissioner and management is not going to motivate us or drive performance. As a state employee, I previously valued my work, my team, and the contributions I make every day. Mutual respect would create a healthy work culture, but unfortunately, this attitude from management is creating the opposite effect. No state agency is exempt from the law and exempt from transparency.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow flexible work arrangements and improve work-life balance.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Organizational structure and culture; Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Remote work and return to office; Work culture

COMMENT:
The biggest issue during my 5 years with the state as someone in IT is seeing computer illiteracy amongst employees. I often hear people say "I'm not a computer person" whenever they come across something they don't understand or have an issue with. I don't think this is an acceptable mentality when over 90% of their job is on the computer! I'm not asking them to troubleshoot everything but I would like to see some effort in at least Googling how to fix minor issues like adjusting a view in Outlook or their display settings.

Here's a few other common things I've encountered.

-Not knowing how to shut down a computer or the difference between shutting down, restarting, sleep, and closing the lid.
-What file explorer is and how to navigate network folders.
-How to use the search (Windows key) to find stuff.
-How to connect to the WIFI and VPN.

That is why I think we need some sort of basic computer literacy exam before people can apply for a job. Maybe add it as a component in CalCareers.

Another big problem I see is paper waste. We spend so much money on printers/faxes, and supplies when most things could just be a PDF. In my dept, I think we spent over a million on a filing system that can be moved electronically.
IDEA_TEXT: Reduce paper waste and printing costs. Switch from paper filing systems to electronic PDF systems.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Technology and digital innovation; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Environmental sustainability; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT: Some of the biggest changes that I have seen during my tenure with the state are the improvements to computer infrastructure such increased bandwidth, newer access points and servers, and more. These improvements allow us to work efficiently in the office and even telework. Previously, there were times in my office where one person was able to bring down the network with updates because the bandwidth was that low.

COMMENT: We need more protection against fraud. There are too many people who abuse our generous social programs such as EBT and SSDI by falsifying their applications. I've seen people say they live elsewhere and pay rent so that their benefits are more than they should be when in reality they live with a relative for free.
IDEA_TEXT: Strengthen fraud protection for social programs like EBT and SSDI. Improve application verification to prevent false claims about living situations and income.

IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Process design and methodologies; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT:
We in State service focus on implementing programs that distribute funds to solve a problem.  Distributing those funds are often under a regulatory time constraint.  The internal culture leaves little room to perform the Project Controls used in the private sector to hold grantees accountable.  Project Controls track the money and schedule progress to hold stakeholders accountable.  The current culture is to assume everyone is honest and playing by the rules, so we can hand out the funds and solve problems "efficiently" by skipping accountability.  But leaving standard Project Controls out of the process isn't an efficiency; skipping a step in basic project management is a problem.  It is an invitation to some to commit fraud.   

This isn't a "poor performance on the part of some people" issue.  It is a cultural habit in our institutionalized processes, maybe because we don't have to show profit and loss.

Incorporating the principles of Project Controls in our contract agreements with grantees - mostly invoice format requirements - is a part of the solution.
IDEA_TEXT: Add Project Controls to grant agreements. Require specific invoice formats to track money and schedule progress and hold grantees accountable.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Governance and oversight; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Contracts and vendors; Procurement

COMMENT:
Permanent/long term tasks being done in small temporary structures. 
For example: The Vacaville prison itself where inmates are is made of concrete, but the HR & clerical tasks buildings nearby are a couple of wooden portables. A prison is around so long it really merits a permanent office building for its administrative aspects.
IDEA_TEXT: Replace temporary wooden portable buildings with permanent concrete office buildings for long-term administrative tasks at prisons.

IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Office management; Physical infrastructure

COMMENT: I am fresh to the State but have decades of experience in the private sector. I have always been a self-leaner and the way I learn is by reading as much (requirement, design, technical, source code, etc) as possible. The challenge I found as a new state employee is that there is nothing available and there is no clear path as to where and how to make requests, even longer tenure colleagues need non-trivial effort to help me out.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a clear system for new employees to access documents and request help. Make requirements, designs, technical materials, and source code easy to find with simple steps to get what they need.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Onboarding new employees; Process documentation; Software and tools

COMMENT:
I have seen a number of large IT projects fail or limp along for years costing the taxpayers a lot of money. Rather than go out and have contractors propose solutions without fully understanding the ever-evolving business needs, the state should develop a "proof of concept" in house prior to going to RFP. This means developing enough of the tool to see system limitations and get an idea on its overall functionality. This would help develop requirements of the tool as we see shortcomings of what is developed but could also be used as a way to help contractors understand what is being sought out. I would suggest involving program (the internal state teams that would use the tool) early on to ensure the business rules are represented. At the discretion of the State, then we could move to a formal RFP and demo the proof of concept or we could continue development in house.

I will avoid referencing specific projects, but I can confirm that this approach would have ruled out proposals that relied heavily on tools that did not meet the need while saving a considerable amount of money.
IDEA_TEXT: Develop proof of concept systems in-house before hiring contractors. Build enough of the tool to test system limits and functionality, then use this to create better requirements and help contractors understand what is needed.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Communication and knowledge sharing; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Internal feedback; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT: Our Fraud Prevention Unit developed an Onboarding Training program for new hires.  It is an intensive 7 to 9 month process of 6 Modules providing orientation, definition, skills, guidance, and procedures to give each new hire a clear idea of expectations.  Our onboarding includes a significant amount of simulations - interviews and research presentation, mostly - to provide rehearsal and experience before engaging in an actual investigation.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a comprehensive onboarding training program for new hires. Include 6 modules over 7-9 months covering job expectations, skills, and procedures. Add practice sessions like mock interviews and research presentations before real work.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Onboarding new employees

COMMENT: Strengthen leadership to increase efficiency, reduce turnover and reduce costs of hiring and training new employees by reconsidering classification levels; the only way to advance should not be through promoting into a management role. That leads to having the wrong people in management roles which can create toxic workplaces. (Think Michael Scott of The Office.) Instead, the state should establish advance levels of classifications with earning potential on par with managers, which will result in the retention of seasoned employees, decrease hiring and training costs of having to hire new employees when these seasoned employees leave the workforce for higher pay, and would be more efficient because the state would not lose those seasoned employees with advance knowledge and skill and historical knowledge and continue to be a valuable resource to newer employees.
IDEA_TEXT: Create advanced job levels that pay as much as managers so skilled workers can advance without becoming managers. This keeps good workers from leaving for better pay and prevents wrong people from becoming managers.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Career growth; Employee pay and benefits; Employee retention

COMMENT: I would say that a lack of functional knowledge is one of the largest issues I see in government work. I would like to see more comprehensive training regarding writing and research skills. I would like to see more comprehensive training regarding professional behavior and correspondence. Most of our trainings are self paced, online trainings, where if there is a quiz at all, it is easily defeated with a google search. I would like to see trainings structured more like college courses where there are not just multiple choice knowledge checks but reviewed responses to open ended questions at several stages in the learning process, and work completed for review, feedback, and revision. I'm guilty of sometimes phoning in a training myself but I also take time to continue my education and I take pride in my work. Not everyone does and then I have to explain to them why or how what they've submitted is incorrect or incomplete. That is not always appreciated and I shouldn't have to train folks that are my superior or at my same level. Also, I would like to see us not use AI very much as it will enable these folks to continue not knowing what they are doing. Plus, the horrific impact to the environment in a state where we should be extra mindful of our environmental impact.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve government training programs. Replace self-paced online courses with college-style training that includes open-ended questions, work review, and feedback instead of just multiple choice tests.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Manager training

COMMENT:
One challenge in California government is that well-intentioned processes often pile up until they become bottlenecks. Ezra Klein’s Abundance talks about “bottleneck deserts,” and that idea really resonates with me. We spend so much time on checks, reporting, and auditing that sometimes the work of actually serving people drifts further and further away.

One solution I’d like to see is the idea of “bottleneck detectives.” These would be small cross-functional teams—made up of staff who know the programs inside and out—tasked with finding the points where work slows down unnecessarily. Twice a year, they would sit down with their teams, map out processes, and identify steps that don’t add value. If a new check or reporting requirement is added, an old one has to be removed. The detectives would track and report back on what’s been eliminated, making sure accountability is preserved without building endless new layers.

This kind of practice could help restore balance: keep oversight strong, but clear the path so the real work—serving Californians—stays front and center.
IDEA_TEXT: Create bottleneck detective teams. Form small cross-functional teams of experienced staff to find and remove unnecessary process steps twice a year. When adding new checks or reporting requirements, remove old ones to keep the same total number.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Bureaucracy; Cross-agency collaboration; Process delays; Process design and methodologies

COMMENT:
Since the RTO order went out, the morale and the dedication of the employees have hit rock bottom. 

My idea is to NOT have a mandate but rather make it based on the needs of the job. If a job can be performed fully remotely, then let those employees work remotely. 

Those that are allowed to work remotely are more engaged, happier, more dedicated as their life / work is better balanced and this is also good for the environment as there are less cars on the roads. 

Also maximum remote work helps hire from a larger pool of candidates which allows us to have more educated and qualified candidates and can hire the top talent, which is a major win and an asset for our state. Especially when it comes to professionals
IDEA_TEXT: Make remote work based on job needs instead of requiring all staff to return to office. Allow employees to work from home if their job can be done remotely.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: Reduce bureaucracy and forms whose only goal is to save miniscule amounts of state funds. For example, requiring employees to fill out TECs (with receipts) in order to receive the transit subsidy is very time consuming. It takes the staff 30 minutes to 1 hour to prepare the TEC for each month, and then there are 3 approvers who spend their time reviewing the TEC and associated receipts. The cost of these 4 people's time is significant and likely much greater than the amount of money saved (not paid to staff) by requiring TECs and receipts.
IDEA_TEXT: Remove or simplify the TEC form requirement for transit subsidies. The cost of staff time to complete and review these forms is likely more than the money saved.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Bureaucracy; Digitize processes; Employee travel; Process delays

COMMENT:
Favoritism and Nepotism in Hiring

Unqualified candidates getting hired or promoted due to personal connections, not merit.  Workplace morale and trust suffer when people perceive that opportunities aren't fairly earned. Erosion of public trust (especially in public or regulatory roles) when the public suspects favoritism in staffing.

We should advocate for more transparent recruitment and selection processes. Push for independent hiring panels, blind application reviews, or standardized interviews. Encourage whistleblower protections for those who report unfair practices.

Circumvention of Minimum Qualifications (MQs)

Some candidates may be circumventing qualifications by enrolling in quick, sometimes low-rigor or unaccredited online graduate programs, just to “check the box.”  This undermines the intent of MQs, which are supposed to ensure a candidate has a certain level of baseline expertise.  It can devalue the profession and create inequities against those who pursued more rigorous paths.

We should push for HR and classification teams to clarify standards: not just “enrollment” but substantial progress or completion from accredited institutions. Consider evaluating equivalencies in a more meaningful way — e.g., experience + coursework, or requiring specific coursework from recognized programs.  Advocate for quality assurance checks on accepted degrees — ensuring they come from regionally accredited institutions, not diploma mills or for-profit degree factories.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve minimum qualification standards. Require substantial progress or completion from accredited schools, not just enrollment. Check that degrees come from real accredited institutions, not diploma mills.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Job classification; Qualified staff

Unqualified candidates getting hired or promoted due to personal connections, not merit.  Workplace morale and trust suffer when people perceive that opportunities aren't fairly earned. Erosion of public trust (especially in public or regulatory roles) when the public suspects favoritism in staffing.

We should advocate for more transparent recruitment and selection processes. Push for independent hiring panels, blind application reviews, or standardized interviews. Encourage whistleblower protections for those who report unfair practices.

Circumvention of Minimum Qualifications (MQs)

Some candidates may be circumventing qualifications by enrolling in quick, sometimes low-rigor or unaccredited online graduate programs, just to “check the box.”  This undermines the intent of MQs, which are supposed to ensure a candidate has a certain level of baseline expertise.  It can devalue the profession and create inequities against those who pursued more rigorous paths.

We should push for HR and classification teams to clarify standards: not just “enrollment” but substantial progress or completion from accredited institutions. Consider evaluating equivalencies in a more meaningful way — e.g., experience + coursework, or requiring specific coursework from recognized programs.  Advocate for quality assurance checks on accepted degrees — ensuring they come from regionally accredited institutions, not diploma mills or for-profit degree factories.
IDEA_TEXT: Make hiring more fair and transparent. Use independent hiring panels, blind application reviews, and standard interviews. Protect whistleblowers who report unfair practices.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Governance and oversight
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Hiring and recruitment

I would like to see more money routed towards affordable housing, affordable childcare, and lowering the sky high cost of living in this state and less money routed to unnecessary government real estate leasing. COMMENT:
Almost everyone I work with agree that while teleworking, we all performed better and had a more dedicated, happier and healthier staff. 

Now with RTO morale is in the toilet, job satisfaction and dedication is almost non existent. 

Those that are required to be in the office do so because they HAVE to and are not willing to work ANY time that is beyond their quitting time, whereas while teleworking, most of us are encouraged to work longer hours till the job is done because we are thankful that we get to work from home. 

Better working conditions = happier, more dedicated workforce.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow more telework options. Keep remote work policies that made staff happier and more productive.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Remote work and return to office

COMMENT:
What’s worked well in my role is being clear about what really needs upper-level approval and what doesn’t. I’ve worked on teams where no one wanted to take responsibility for signing off on a public-facing document, so it kept getting pushed higher and higher to people who didn’t have the time to review it. That creates unnecessary bottlenecks.

On my current team, we’ve set clearer expectations: which types of documents need executive-level review, which can be approved by managers, and which can move forward at staff level. Everyone understands the process and the expectations, which has made things much more efficient.

We’ve also improved our editing and reviewing etiquette. Instead of slowing things down with multiple rounds of small changes, reviewers will often give feedback like, “Next time you do something like this, try it this way,” rather than holding up the current draft for minor points. That balance helps us get important materials out the door quickly, while still improving quality over time.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve document review process. Give feedback for future improvements instead of holding up current drafts for minor changes.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal feedback; Process design and methodologies

COMMENT:
Make more transactions/tasks accessible via online forms & scanning documents to a state website. 
This will cut down on parking & space requirements to handle crowds of public customers and can free up staffing to either work from home or use remote workers far away from the actual worksite and/or work 24 hours varying shifts reviewing documents online instead of just 8-5 in physical office. For example, aside from the actual driving test the DMV could accept scanned documentation for all other transactions. The federal government for passports allows private businesses like UPS to take photo for applicants, could just take step further & allow approved businesses to scan photo as well instead of crowds standing in line at DMV.
IDEA_TEXT: Make more government services available online with document scanning. This would reduce office crowding, allow remote work, enable 24-hour service, and let private businesses help with tasks like DMV photo scanning.

It seems that certain programs are not thoroughly thought out before implementing, and rather than being proactive in thinking, Department's then have to be reactive and search for cost cutting measures to make up for shortfalls. This is a disservice to state employees and citizens alike. COMMENT:
Government workers are often drawn to state service because of the stability, flexibility, and the mission of public service. That’s why the return-to-office mandate worries me—it risks pushing people out and discouraging new talent from joining. I’ve already seen good colleagues leave or begin planning their exit, and we can’t afford to lose strong employees when recruitment is already so competitive.

At the same time, I do recognize that some employees don’t always engage or collaborate well when working remotely. That lack of participation can create frustration and fuel the push for mandates. But instead of a blanket requirement, I think we should treat remote collaboration and remote work etiquette as part of performance expectations. If someone is showing up, communicating well, and delivering results, they should keep the flexibility. If not, then their performance review could require them to be in the office more often.

This approach keeps flexibility as a competitive perk, while also addressing real concerns about accountability and teamwork. It sets a higher bar for engagement without punishing those who are already working effectively in remote or hybrid environments.
IDEA_TEXT: Make remote work part of job performance reviews. Let staff who communicate well and deliver results keep flexibility. Require poor performers to work in the office more often instead of blanket return-to-office rules.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Workplace operations; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee performance reviews; Management culture and leadership approach; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT:
As a California resident, I want to see more focus on building homes at all income levels. Right now, the requirements builders have to navigate between state and local agencies are so complex that they delay projects and add costs. We’re in the middle of a housing crisis, and every layer of delay keeps us further from meeting the need.

What’s really needed are more resources to streamline the process—whether that’s funding for environmental work, faster permitting, or incentives that help projects move forward without getting stuck in endless back-and-forth. The state should make it easier, not harder, to build the housing Californians need.
IDEA_TEXT: Streamline housing approval process. Provide more funding for environmental work, faster permits, and incentives to help projects move forward without delays between state and local agencies.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Communication and knowledge sharing; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Cross-agency collaboration; Environmental sustainability; Permits, licensing, and fees

COMMENT:
Virtual training gave a great resolve for COVID, I feel like the classrooms need to come back now.  People learn differently, but as a manager, I noticed that more of my new hires come out of their virtual training confused and unclear about the topics that were covered.  Some really don't appreciate the electronic platform because it's impersonal and doesn't allow them to feel engaged in the lesson.  I also find that the electronic platforms do not offer the opportunity for networking, which is so valuable as you move on in your career.  I also find a lot of these trainings are being cut too short.  My employees don't feel like they are being given the opportunity to really retain the information, which only sets them up for failure.  I am a "hands-on" learner myself, and so when my new hires tell me that they would have liked to physically handle some of the example processes, instead of virtually working through them, I can completely empathize with that.  New hires are going out into their offices and causing even more exhaustion and congestion amongst those that already know the job and are trying to slow down enough to help the new hires catch up.  I find that there is much more "on-the-job" training needed, but we are already so stressed for workers that this causes a major inconvenience for the office to have to pick up the slack where the training is lacking.  
Conclusion: Bring back classrooms for "beginner" classes and elect dedicated mentors for new hires.
IDEA_TEXT: Bring back in-person classroom training for beginner classes and assign dedicated mentors for new hires.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Mentorship programs

COMMENT: The department of General Services wastes more money than it saves.  We lease buildings, that could have been built and owned 100 times over for the amount per month spent on rent.  Companies know the state is low bid, so all offers are over exaggerated and come in 1 dollar under max on a simple repair or replacement.  That’s just the beginning of DGS.
IDEA_TEXT: Buy buildings instead of leasing them to save money long-term.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Physical infrastructure

COMMENT: One of the state agencies I have worked for invested a lot of time and energy into ensuring leadership was well-trained not just in management but in how to be a good leader. Knowing my boss would always have my back really improved morale and made myself and my coworkers willing to explore unique solutions. My team ended up creating a reference library of filmed tutorials for onboarding new employees to make up for the training gap during the pandemic, and we never would have put in that effort without our boss' support. If more agencies invested in servant leadership like that I think it could be wonderful!
IDEA_TEXT: Invest in leadership training that focuses on servant leadership and supporting staff. Train managers to back their teams so staff feel supported to try new solutions.

COMMENT:
The SavingsPlus managers at CalHR recently improved the Target Date Funds. They swapped out a proprietary group of TDFs that were larded up with expensive, underperforming actively managed mutual funds and replaced them with a TDF series comprised entirely with index funds.  
If the CalPERS board would just do the same -- replace its expensive, poor performing actively managed mutual funds and private equity with transparent and low-cost index funds -- the CalPERS portfolio would also improve its performance, benefiting employees and the public.
IDEA_TEXT: Replace expensive, poor performing actively managed mutual funds and private equity with low-cost index funds in the CalPERS portfolio.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding

COMMENT:
Public pension boards -- CalPERS, CalSTRS and the 21 CERLS -- need simple ethic reform: no free meals or gifts of any amount for board members or for staff. (The current limit can be legally skirted when multiple companies collectively split the group meal tab for staff and pension board trustees, for example.)
If a ban on gifts was enacted, the free meals and gifts would mostly cease, and we would see fewer staff recommendations (and fewer board approvals) to send billions into costly investments that are proven to underperform low-cost index funds. Result: public pensions would improve their long-term investment returns by more than 1% per year, which would eventually translate into a reduced need to unnecessarily contribute extra billions into California public pension plans.
IDEA_TEXT: Ban all gifts and free meals for CalPERS, CalSTRS, and CERLS board members and staff to improve pension investment returns and reduce unnecessary contributions.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Governance and oversight; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Employee pay and benefits

COMMENT:
Many Caltrans processes still rely on PDF forms that require downloading, filling out, routing for signatures, and re-entering data into spreadsheets. This creates delays, duplicate work, and access issues — especially when PDFs are hard to use on mobile devices or when handwritten forms are difficult to read.

Instead of routing documents for signatures, we can use interactive online forms to collect structured data and automate approvals. Online forms are faster to set up, reusable, and mobile-friendly. They also ensure accuracy by requiring fields, validating entries, and feeding submissions directly into tabular formats like Excel or SharePoint Lists. This eliminates manual transcription, reduces errors, and cuts down on repetitive administrative work.

The benefits are clear:

- Saves time by reducing steps (no printing, scanning, or configuring signatures)

- Improves efficiency with real-time submission tracking and notifications

- Avoids waste by reducing paper use and redundant tasks

- Supports accessibility by providing legible, mobile-friendly entry options for both office and field staff

This small change could save staff and the general public hours each week and provide cleaner, more usable data for decision-making — aligning with Executive Order N-30-25’s call for operational efficiencies across state government.
IDEA_TEXT: Replace PDF forms with online forms. Use interactive web forms that collect data directly, automate approvals, work on mobile devices, and feed information straight into Excel or SharePoint Lists to eliminate manual data entry and reduce errors.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Process improvement; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Public service delivery and responsiveness; Software and tools

COMMENT:
There are a few, there are multiple examples of administrative layers that have created massive expenses which are intended to provide savings but do not. 

Office of State Publishing. The mandate to pay more for a lower quality product is counter productive. The savings does not equate to the personnel and infrastructure expense. We can produce superior products for less in private industry. 

Prison Industries, the requirement to purchase furniture from them at often prices exceed 300% more than the same higher quality item for elsewhere. We are forced to pay $700 for a $60 office chair. If it’s more than 10% over a competitor price for like item, we should have an automatic exception from the requirement to purchase from them. 

Small Businesses. State mandated purchasing for high price items also causes appalling markups to costs. Though it may support small business it costs the state an exorbitant amount of money. Because of procurement limitations this essentially forces the state to pay 15-80% more for a product if it’s above $9,999. Our procurement limitations/rules create a massive expense to the state. 

DGS auto inspectors. Again this one is staff and infrastructure overruns. In all of my years they have never assisted in saving any money, never disproved of any service. It is my opinion that they do not provide commensurate savings to come close to their cost to operate.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow agencies to buy from private companies when state-mandated vendors charge more than 10% above market price for Office of State Publishing, Prison Industries furniture, and small business procurement.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Procurement

COMMENT:
Our Governor has made important strides, but he is missing the mark on one key issue. If California truly wants to reduce its carbon footprint, the solution isn’t to force everyone into electric cars, it’s to reduce the need for cars and commuting altogether. REMOTE WORK is the future. It cuts emissions, lowers costs, and allows employees to be more productive while leaving face-to-face gatherings for when they matter most, social and collaborative purposes.
The Governor’s RTO order ignores both technology and the cost-saving benefits that remote work provides. It especially harms employees with disabilities who cannot safely or reasonably commute, even though they have already proven they can perform their jobs effectively from home. California should lead by example and show that sustainability starts with the state workforce, by embracing remote work, not resisting it.
IDEA_TEXT: Embrace remote work for state employees. Allow staff to work from home to cut emissions, lower costs, and improve productivity while supporting employees with disabilities who have proven they can work effectively remotely.

COMMENT: I think homelessness is a huge issue. There are many different organizations that work to provide support but no one actually making significant change. We need an overarching solution. People don't become homeless overnight, and they won't get out of it overnight. We need to have a 2-pronged approach. 1st - before someone becomes homeless, we need to have a program that would allow them to access grant funding to catch them up and keep them from becoming homeless. It's far less expensive to catch someone up than to pay for all of the services they will need once they are homeless. 2nd - once someone becomes homeless, we need the ability to get them into mid - term (1-2 year) housing with wrap around services to ensure they can have their physical/mental health needs addressed, get or increase their employment, find childcare if necessary, and save money. During this time, they should also have access to financial literacy education, job training, and if needed, drug treatment programs. Waiting until someone has exhausted all options and is sleeping on the street before "helping them" by providing them 30 days in a motel, is only perpetuating the problem and keeping people in poverty.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a two-part homelessness prevention program. First, provide grant funding to help people avoid becoming homeless by catching up on bills. Second, offer 1-2 year housing with support services including healthcare, job training, childcare help, financial education, and drug treatment to help people get back on their feet.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public policy initiatives; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT:
A community-based program utilizing goats or sheep to manage vegetation on CDCR grounds could offer significant benefits, including reduced operating costs, environmental sustainability, & community engagement. This approach could also pave the way for a vocational rehabilitation program for inmates, teaching ranching skills. A viable skill to make money and raise meat for your family. Some of the benefits:
1. Reduced Operating Costs:
Using animals to manage vegetation eliminates need for fuel, equip maintenance, & groundskeeper's salary.
2. Environmental Sustainability:
Goats & sheep provide a natural, eco-friendly alternative to mowing, reducing reliance on fossil fuels & machinery.
3. Community Engagement:
Partnering with 4-H or FFA youth groups provides a valuable community outreach opportunity & educational experience.
4. Inmate Rehabilitation:
A successful pilot program could lead to development of a ranching rehabilitation program, offering incarcerated individuals marketable skills & a potential path to employment upon release.
5. Fire Hazard Reduction:
Regular grazing by livestock can help reduce build-up of dry vegetation, a fire hazard, especially in California's climate. 
6. Security:
A thorough security assessment is crucial to ensure that program does not compromise safety & security of facility. 
7. Long-term Sustainability:
Develop a plan for ongoing maintenance, animal care, & program evaluation.
IDEA_TEXT: Conduct a thorough security assessment to ensure the livestock vegetation management program does not compromise facility safety and security.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Governance and oversight
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Compliance

COMMENT:
I think the State of California’s agencies should stop overcharging each other for work/services that are supposed to benefit our citizens. Our agency leases buildings from the Department of General Services (DGS). Anytime anything needs to be updated or fixed the quotes for a solution are usually 3x-5x the cost and 2x-3x the timeline for completion of work versus hiring a private company to do it. 

  Example: A few years ago a loading dock door’s heavy duty motor reached its end of life, and our landlord (DGS) quoted us upwards of $25K to replace it with the same exact model. I researched the exact motor, and the part alone was $2.5K.

We’ve got to figure out a better system as a state.
IDEA_TEXT: Stop state agencies from overcharging each other for services. Create a better system for inter-agency work that reduces costs and speeds up completion times.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Cross-agency collaboration; Procurement

COMMENT: Bureaucracy. In many spaces we’ve created workflows that are impossible to navigate and often create unnecessary hoops for people to jump through. Departments that are responsible for policies that are implemented statewide(DGS, CalHR) could benefit from months long job shadowing to see the policies they’ve created in action. They would be able to bring best practices back and hopefully use that information to process improve.
IDEA_TEXT: Streamline workflows to remove unnecessary steps and barriers. Have state policy departments like DGS and CalHR do months-long job shadowing to see how their policies work in practice and bring back improvements.

COMMENT: By far the largest issue I see in the state is the problem of turnover and employee retention. Our knowledge bases can take years to develop. Processes like contracting have extremely specific protocols and therefore very specific training. Whenever we lose an employee, that gap can take over a year to fill and the next person might take over a months to over a year to get up to speed. This issue also dovetails into the issue of severe under staffing. Some units are operating at well under 50% of the necessary staff, or even more. When appropriately staffed units need to interface with those who have poor staffing, that causes bottlenecks which lead to months of delay. There just are not enough people to do the jobs necessary to keep the fundamental processes of our bureaucracy moving smoothly. If we can improve retention and staffing, it will go a long way to accelerating timelines and improving efficiency.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve employee retention and increase staffing levels. Fill open positions faster and provide better training to reduce turnover and eliminate bottlenecks caused by understaffing.

COMMENT: There are several departments under the Health and Human Services Agency that serve the same individual.  It would be helpful and cut redundancy if all the departments could share one data system.  The idea is to have one database with one record for each individual served.  This would allow the departments to better coordinate care and services.  This could potentially cut the intake process and waiting times as data may already have been collected.   It would also help cut down the number of times an individual has to share their information.  I understand that California has the Open Data Portal, but not all departments participate and that is to report deidentified data.  A solution to solve this problem could be to require all departments to participate in a shared data base, sharing information and working together to have the most up to date information.  Departments could also potentially share the cost of creating such a platform.  This could potentially eliminate some of the separate database changes taking place at other departments and modernize older systems.  This could also help HHS comply with some of the new CMS Final Rules by allowing cross reporting and by making sure each individual is only counted one time.  I would love to hear other solutions.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a shared database system for all Health and Human Services Agency departments. Require all departments to participate and share costs. Use one record per person to coordinate care, reduce intake time, and eliminate duplicate data collection.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Communication and knowledge sharing; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Digitize processes; Shared data; Software and tools

Caltrans is doing a great job picking up litter from or highways and freeways, we are filling up thousands of plastic bags every month statewide and all the plastic bags end up in our local landfills.

COMMENT: There are a lot of duplication within the State. Too many people are serving in the same capacity to do the same job. I also feel that it takes too long to onboard someone into State service. There are many people who want to serve the State but cannot wait 6 months to a year in order to get a job. I feel that once people are onboarded, there is a lack of training because there is no one who can train that person on the job they are expected to do. There needs to be a transition period when one person is leaving and another is coming in to do that job for the new employee to be properly trained in all aspects of their job duties. There also needs to be consistency throughout the State in the expectations of a job.
IDEA_TEXT: Create overlap periods when staff leave so new employees can be properly trained.

IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Onboarding new employees

COMMENT: As a new state employee, one thing I have noticed is the lack of trust between leadership and analyst level staff.  There are so many steps to complete the most minute tasks, and those tasks seem to be complicated because leadership makes it complicated.  The strict hierarchical approach - at lease from my experience - has just created fear and insecurity about performing daily work.  There isn't an open forum to freely share ideas or talk about new ways of doing things.  We are relegated to a desk manual that is incomplete and doesn't actually allow anyone to think creatively.  I have been assured that every department is not like this, but I have also been told that I work in one of the best departments - so does that mean it's worse out there??  I would LOVE to see more flexibility, mentoring from leaders, allowing for mistakes, and basic access to leaders so we can ask how they got to where they are in the organization.  We look to our leaders for support and guidance.  When they only effort they make is to remind us about our timecards, that's not actually leadership.
IDEA_TEXT: Create more flexible leadership approach. Allow staff to make mistakes, provide mentoring, give access to leaders for guidance, and create open forums for sharing ideas instead of relying only on incomplete desk manuals.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Organizational structure and culture; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal feedback; Management culture and leadership approach; Mentorship programs; Trust and openness; Work culture

COMMENT: As someone who has been with the state for a few years, it appears that there is a culture where people feel its acceptable to neglect their obligations and duties without any real accountability. Most times the work is shifted to individuals who are actually doing the work and there is no real disciplinary action for those who don't. This attitude not only undermines productivity but also fosters a frustrating environment for individuals who are committed to their work. This disconnect can result in lack of motivation among employees who strive to uphold high standards. I believe we can do better in promoting accountability and recognizing the contributions of those who genuinely contribute to the team's success in order to have a more positive work environment. Employees should, in my opinion, have more ways to voice their opinions without fear of retalition, and they should be fairly offered opportunites for advancement based on their performance. Creating an environment that encourages open communication and rewards good work can make employees feel more motivated and involved.
IDEA_TEXT: Create accountability systems that discipline poor performers and reward good workers. Give staff safe ways to share feedback and fair chances for promotion based on performance.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee performance reviews; Employee retention; Internal feedback; Management culture and leadership approach

COMMENT: Our agency had a robust telework policy before the pandemic for professional staff.  We invested in more technology to make it even more efficient and expanded telework to support staff duirng covid.  We formerly spent significant taxpayer money traveling to conduct events in person, despite a regulation authorizing virtual events.  Since we've invested in the technology and nearly all events are virtual, we can reduce office space thus saving significant funds.  Converting the positions to telework permanently can allow for strategic planning.  For example, we can invest in training and  productivity tools to ensure taxpayers receive the best quality service while offsetting the unnecessary expense of partially unused offices and excessive travel.
IDEA_TEXT: Make telework permanent for all eligible staff. Reduce office space and travel costs. Invest savings in training and productivity tools to improve service quality.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Workplace operations; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Employee training; Public service delivery and responsiveness; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT:
It's not a simple problem to fix, but I think it cuts across many Departmental efforts which would save money and lives.  I think we need better Public Transportation.

The desired outcome: less traffic and congestion, fewer auto accidents, and reduced emissions.

I know for example it would save quite a bit money and Staff hours in DHCS, a seemingly unrelated Department, if we can reduce the number of auto accidents occurring in CA.  It's also known that the majority of DHCS funding goes to paying for medical services, so any opportunity to reduce serious injury will also provide state savings.

Beyond that, better public transit can reduce costs of living for Californians, promote better public health outcomes, aid CalEPA's mission, and hopefully in the long term also make Caltrans' job easier, with hopefully less travel infrastructure needing maintenance.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve public transportation across California to reduce traffic, accidents, and emissions while lowering costs for residents and the state.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Environmental sustainability; Public policy initiatives; Public transportation

COMMENT: PROBLEM: I work at the North Coast Regional Water Quality Control Board in the Cleanups Unit.  We transitioned to a paperless file system  back in 2006 when the State began requiring reports/data to be submitted and uploaded to the Geotracker database. There are still 100s of existing folders, reports, etc. (one estimate was that our unit's file room contains 1/3 of a mile of paper). Environmental pollution cases are unique: we're required to keep records of all sites, closed or not, in case land use changes in the future (also, it's the law). We routinely have PRA/FOIA requests to pull files for consultant review, and more than 75% of the time they'll ask us to scan and email them.  Our region is also vast- some of these consultants and landowners live 6 hours away from out office, and some have physical handicaps that limit their mobility, so we try to oblige. But actually taking apart a file, scanning it, categorizing it, and uploading it is extremely labor intensive. A small file will take a few hours, but even a medium sized one requires one or two days (the scanning is rapid, uploading and naming is laborious).  SOLUTIONS: 1) hire dedicated admin staff to do this work, 2) subcontract it out to a bonded document scanning service. BENEFITS: 1) Reduced labor costs for professional and admin staff 2) improved public access for these records 3) potential to upload to AI for detailed summation and analysis. Thank you,
IDEA_TEXT: Hire dedicated admin staff or contract with a document scanning service to digitize paper files. This would reduce labor costs for professional staff and improve public access to records.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Process improvement; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Digitize processes; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT: OK, I don't want to throw shade on other departments, but why does it take the Department of Toxic Substance Control 6 months to 2 YEARS to review and respond to reports that other environmental agencies respond to in a month? They have generally larger and more complicated sites, but many of their cases would have been closed years ago if assigned to other agencies. Sometimes it feels analogous to assigning a Homicide Detective to investigate a traffic ticket. Perhaps reducing the number of reviews and "draft Final Final" report reviews is in order? They do great work, but quite a bit of it seems like overkill.
IDEA_TEXT: Reduce the number of reviews and draft report reviews at the Department of Toxic Substance Control to speed up response times from 6 months-2 years to closer to 1 month like other environmental agencies.

IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee performance reviews; Process delays; Process design and methodologies

COMMENT: As a state employee, that is focused on cyber security related issues, the primary problem I would like to address is the state not properly addressing Data Sovereignty. As it stands a majority of the data collected, generated and used by departments is physically hosted and stored with third-party companies. Companies who have issues of their own with keeping themselves out of their customers data. Although we have contracts governing how they handle this data contracts alone are not enough to protect citizens from the fallout should these companies' data centers become compromised. The sole responsibility for governing and protecting California citizens data rests with the state and its civil service. My solution to this problem would be for the California Department of Technology restart it's program to create a state cloud. The technology to do so has come a long way since it's last attempt and there is opportunity to recruit recently laid off tech workers into the civil service to support such a project as well as opportunities for more smaller local technology companies to engage in contract work to support the open-source software that will underpin it all. This would ensure that California data is protected and guarded by Californian's as well as California leading the way in Data and Technology governance.
IDEA_TEXT: Restart the California Department of Technology state cloud program. Use recently laid off tech workers and local technology companies to build an open-source system that keeps California data in California instead of with third-party companies.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Technology and digital innovation; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Cross-agency collaboration; State cloud storage; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT: Another problem I would like to address is the lack of knowledge sharing between technology teams. There is a bill AB869 in the Senate right now that looks to make each state agency implement a Zero Trust Architecture by 2030. I think this is a worthwhile endeavor and as part of this mandate funding should be made to have technology teams be trained in Infrastructure as Code and DevSecOps. Once technologists within the state are trained in these tools technology teams will not only have a more reliable and secure environment but also the ability to share how they built those systems via Git. Technology teams in different departments can fork/download the code that was used to build other department's systems and use it themselves, make/commit improvements, iterate to create other systems, etc. This can foster a more collaborative environment for IT professionals within the state service.
IDEA_TEXT: Fund training for state technology teams in Infrastructure as Code and DevSecOps as part of the Zero Trust Architecture mandate (AB869). Create a shared code system using Git so technology teams can share, copy, and improve each other's work across different departments.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Communication and knowledge sharing; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Employee training; Shared resources; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT: Something that I have found useful, especially in working in a remote/hybrid environment is the creation of an internal communication guide. Something that tells people, especially when they first start, how communication channels work internally. Something that explains when to use an email, IM, support ticket, etc. With all of these new ways to communicate and especially in a remote/hybrid environment having a clear and explicit document on which form of communication you should use, how you should format your questions/requests and who/where you should submit that to can help immensely with stopping crosstalk, misunderstandings and delays in requests.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a clear communication guide for remote and hybrid work. Explain when to use email, instant messaging, or support tickets. Include how to format requests and who to contact for different needs.

COMMENT: I would really like to encourage the state to look into facilitating a more remote or hybrid work force. I live in Los Angeles and would have never been able to join the state service if my position wasn't remote. I enjoy what I do, and I believe I bring a unique perspective to my team who primarily live in Sacramento. But something I notice whenever I visit my team in Sacramento is ignorance to issues that other offices in my department have. Issues related to California's extremely diverse geography, climate, and communities. California is a huge state and has a wide range of day-to-day problems and issues depending on where you live that require state resources to solve. Having a more agile and geographically distributed workforce would help with that.
IDEA_TEXT: Expand remote and hybrid work options statewide. Create a geographically distributed workforce to better understand and serve California's diverse communities and regional issues.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Public-facing services and resident experience; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Public service delivery and responsiveness; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: The amount of taxpayer money spent on state office space is staggering, especially considering most work can now be completed remotely. Even without considering working conditions or what's best for the state employee (although I think telework supports those, too), utilizing telework dramatically reduces overhead costs with no negative impact to work product. Many departments reported improved efficiency during the pandemic with 100% (or near 100%) telework. Telework also improves recruiting, as departments aren't limited to candidates local to Sacramento and can effectively and efficiently hire from across the state. With finances growing tighter and tighter every year, telework is an easy way to address budget shortfalls in a way that unions and employees will also support (as opposed to layoffs and furloughs). I could talk about management inefficiencies, obtuse procedures, and other issues all day long, but this inefficient use of taxpayer dollars is the problem which has the most obvious and straightforward solution that will have the most significant immediate impact.
IDEA_TEXT: Expand telework programs to reduce office space costs and improve recruiting statewide.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Workplace operations; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Employee retention; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: As a California resident, I have to chime in again on the benefits of telework. It's been disheartening to see the traffic (and the rate of accidents) growing again on 50 and 99 since RTO kicked up. When I work from home, I'm spending money on my local mom-and-pop restaurants and businesses, keeping those dollars in the community where I actually live. We could convert all those office buildings downtown to affordable housing to encourage people to live downtown again—which would encourage more restaurants and businesses to open up there as well. I know it's a big change from how things have always been done, and change is scary for some of these powerful people, but a distributed workforce is better for our future in so many ways.
IDEA_TEXT: Convert downtown office buildings to affordable housing to encourage downtown living and business growth.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Physical infrastructure; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: I have implemented a scanning process to make the board more efficient, going green which also saves money, and online process to help stream line. Make simple and to the point desk procedures in order for anyone to understand even for those that know nothing of a process or program.
IDEA_TEXT: Implement digital processes to improve efficiency. Use scanning to go paperless, create online workflows, and write simple desk procedures that anyone can understand.

COMMENT: As staff and a California resident having to deal with state agencies for things, we really need staff to get the job done, to many take their time, but then we also have some places that are severely short staffed. Everyone should be working hard and working together.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve staff productivity and teamwork. Ensure all staff work hard and work together, while addressing staffing shortages where needed.

COMMENT:
As a seasoned CA employee, I see waste/fraud in the use of licensed BU 9 classifications. I would recommend an audit of all BU 9 duty statements to determine if a license is actually required to do the job. 

I say this because I have seen the state increase the use of "licensed" BU 9 staff when a license is not necessary to complete the work. DGS' Division of the State Architect, the Building Standards Commission, and the Office of Sustainability, as well as CalTrans previously hired non-licensed employees to complete the work that does not require a license. 

However, in the past decade these Departments/Divisions/Commissions have stopped using classifications that do require a license. The problem is that the work required by these entities can be completed by a combination of licensed and unlicensed staff, but by hiring only licensed staff, the state is wasting hundreds-of millions of dollars. 

A licensed architect/engineer gets paid (after benefits) $250k/year or more. An unlicensed person doing the work that doesn't require a license gets paid $150k/year. Multiply that $100k/year savings by the thousands of staff at DGS and CalTrans that are paid for their license but don't perform license-level work.

By hiring unlicensed staff to do the unlicensed work, there is also an opportunity for the state to recruit and maintain staff. 

But STOP paying licensed BU 9 staff to do work that non-licensed staff can do. It will save the state at least 1/2 a billion dollars.
IDEA_TEXT: Audit all BU 9 job descriptions to check if a license is actually needed. Stop hiring licensed staff for work that unlicensed staff can do. Use a mix of licensed and unlicensed workers based on actual job requirements to save hundreds of millions of dollars.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Job classification; Qualified staff

COMMENT: Procurement process. Sometimes needed items for efficient work can take 5-8 weeks if you are lucky to get even if you are out or plan ahead. What about the items that you cannot plan ahead for? There is no way to get things quickly, even paper, pens, cleaning supplies, and other needs. This often results in people paying out of pocket for supplies. There should be a way to get needed supplies when they are needed. The procurement process is redundant, too many unnecessary steps to aquire easy things.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a faster way to get basic supplies like paper, pens, and cleaning supplies when needed. Remove unnecessary steps from the procurement process.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Bureaucracy; Process design and methodologies; Procurement

COMMENT:
Across two state agencies, poor communication has consistently hindered productivity and morale. In one agency, management prohibited cross-unit dialogue, slowing progress and isolating teams. In another, annual surveys repeatedly flagged communication as the top issue, yet leadership insisted on injecting themselves in every conversation or discouraging communication with other units and divisions. Instead of seeking answers from the sources, managerial direction has focused on the interpretation of resources that often prove incorrect. These decisions negatively impact morale, cause frustrations in a job that would otherwise feel ideal, and reminds of the axom that people don't quit jobs, they quit managers. 

Managers often lack subject matter expertise, causing delays and confusion. Many openly promote a “fake it till you make it” mindset, prioritizing their own job security over the work required to excel when serving the public. The solution is simple: empower staff to communicate directly and collaborate freely, hire a strong human resources team to handle all of our regular issues, and reduce the layers of management. We can build a more agile, informed, and customer-focused workforce.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow staff to talk directly with each other across units and work together freely.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Organizational structure and culture; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Internal communication; Trust and openness

COMMENT: During the pandemic, EDD launched a special projects team that swiftly resolved payment issues. Its success stemmed largely from cross-unit collaboration, which enabled staff to prioritize tasks and support one another effectively. In contrast, prior management discouraged communication beyond one’s direct supervisor—a dated approach that stifled morale and hindered professional growth.
IDEA_TEXT: Create cross-unit collaboration teams like EDD's special projects team. Allow staff to work together across departments and communicate beyond direct supervisors.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Internal communication; Internal feedback

COMMENT: We too often provide inaccurate information to customers, and management frequently instructs staff not to correct it and/or advise us it is not someone else's responsibility to make that correction. In practice, that someone else does nothing. This practice undermines trust and accountability, and it sets a bad precedent for the next generation going in to management. It’s essential that all employees, leadership in particular, embrace the core principle that our role is to serve. Strong job protections should never be mistaken as a license to neglect our responsibility to help others.
IDEA_TEXT: Require all staff to correct wrong information given to customers. Make leadership enforce this rule and hold people accountable for helping others.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Governance and oversight; Management culture and leadership; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Internal communication; Management culture and leadership approach

COMMENT: Is there a better way to search through comments? The comments of all three sections seem to be sorted by likes, and then time. The "Share your idea" section has categories for each department, but there doesn't seem to be a search setting that helps with that.
IDEA_TEXT: Add search features to the comment system. Let users search by department categories and other filters beyond just likes and time sorting.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digital services; Software and tools

IDEA_TEXT: Make government websites easier to use for the public. Create user-friendly websites or set up a call center to help people find the right department.

COMMENT: I am a teacher and the process of attendance takes a long time, involves myself, as well as education officers and housing officers. It seems to me that it would be so much more streamlined if we could somehow use the barcode on each IPs ID. As IPs came through a building or left a building, they would scan their ID and their whereabouts would be uploaded directly to SOMS. This would eliminate the need for so many phone calls and such. Seems like a no brainer to me. Of course, their IDs would probably need to be changed to a bracelet or anklet of some kind so as not to be lost or used by a different IP.
IDEA_TEXT: Use ID barcodes for automatic attendance tracking. Have inmates scan their IDs when entering or leaving buildings to upload location data directly to SOMS system. Change IDs to bracelets or anklets so they cannot be lost or shared.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT:
I've been a teacher with CDCR since 2016. I never in my lift have seen so many "hands" being involved in the easiest of processes. For instance, to get student prior transcripts sent to us, I have student fill out transcript request, hand that form over to our OT, the OT then emails/mails the form to the said school, the school sends transcripts back. If student is a graduate, I fill out another form to request their removal from education, DocuSign it to my supervisor, and the supervisor then requests the drop from the institution's assignments officer. 
Attendance works similarly. Within 15 minutes of class starting, I fill out a form showing absent students and email it to our education officer. Education officer spends his time calling each IPs housing officer, if they can be reached, to verify if IP is coming to school or not, then the education officer notes "A" time, "S" time, etc ... and returns to me to fill out SOMS attendance. 
I feel there is so much wasted energy and time that could be used elsewhere to make our programs run more smoothly and with less time to process things.
IDEA_TEXT: Streamline administrative processes in CDCR education programs. Remove unnecessary steps and middlemen from transcript requests, student removals, and attendance tracking to reduce wasted time and energy.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Bureaucracy; Digitize processes; Process design and methodologies

COMMENT:
The S90 or Rejection of Probation code needs to be modified so that it reflects two possibilities: an actual rejection and an employee exercising their mandatory right to return to their former agency.  During the time of probation the supervisor/manager is evaluating the employee, but the employee should be doing the same thing and determining whether the agency is a good fit or not for them or not.  The employee should be able to switch without any negative impact if the agency is not a good fit.

At present, the code S90 is seen as negative, but there needs to be a way to distinguish what actually happened. It should be noted that the order and decision are not always in the Official Personnel File (OPF) and are based on the agreement or settlement between the parties involved.
IDEA_TEXT: Modify the S90 rejection code to show two different reasons: actual rejection by the supervisor versus employee choosing to return to their former agency. This would remove the negative impact when employees exercise their right to return during probation.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Employee retention; Internal feedback

COMMENT: I have found the State's technology is severely lacking in many areas. Simple things like completing timecards, requesting leave and determining how many hours of leave you have accrued require the employee to access three different databases, and even these databases do not reflect real-time, useful information. I have worked as a County employee in CA, and even the County had real time leave data that could be accessed easily by the employee. In my current employment, I have to track my time, by five minute increments, in one database, I have to add this information to my Outlook calendar, and I have to note it on my timecard. I am hoping there is way all of these databases can be integrated so that we are not spending so much time across multiple platforms to track our time working for the State. I also feel that during the mapping process with the Organizational Improvement Office, OIO, best practices should be shared with all department employees as they are working through their mapping and not at the very end once they have mapped all of the processes for the entire division. This would allow for immediate changes and streamlining to occur, instead of waiting for two years or more for the mapping to be completed before the results are released to the department. Change and innovate as we go, share the knowledge and best practices as soon as it is identified so employees can save valuable time and State money.
IDEA_TEXT: Combine all time tracking systems into one database. Allow staff to track time, request leave, and check leave balances in one place instead of three separate systems.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Budget and resources; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Shared resources; Software and tools

COMMENT: For HQ departments that process paperwork for all the districts, there should be more training offered to the districts on how the process/procedures works. The policies keep changing too quickly, so the districts who had people trained a long time ago are working off old information and passing that down to newer employees. It could be online sessions or onsite training, but somehow the districts need a better way to be updated.
IDEA_TEXT: Provide better training to districts on headquarters processes and procedures. Offer online sessions or onsite training to keep districts updated when policies change.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Governance and oversight; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Align policy and implementation; Employee training; Internal communication

COMMENT: I manage a department email inbox and I get all sorts of questions from internal and external partners. I strive to provide the best customer service to everyone. If I don't know the answer to their question, I try to find the correct department who can answer it and forward their contact details to the asker.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve customer service by finding the right department to answer questions and connecting people directly with the correct contact details.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Internal communication; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT: Purchasing. We are required to purchase certain items from CAL PIA. Many items are much more cost effective to purchase from a local retailer even after the potential savings from purchasing from another state agency.  Also we are restricted on whats available.   Allowing a credit card purchase at a local retailer with supervisory oversite could potentially save a large amount of money, allow for better products and faster aquisitions.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow credit card purchases at local retailers with supervisor approval instead of requiring CAL PIA purchases to save money, get better products, and speed up buying.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Expense reimbursement; Procurement

COMMENT: I think any sort of housing benefits would attract more people to be state workers. It could be a lower down payment percentage or something else. The generations nowadays are unable to afford housing, especially in California, and were sold the American dream that if you worked hard, you could afford a house. They're all disillusioned at this point and don't feel the need to work hard and contribute to society when the system isn't working for them.
IDEA_TEXT: Offer housing benefits to attract more state workers. Provide lower down payment help or other housing assistance programs.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Employee retention; Hiring and recruitment

COMMENT: Eliminate purchasing requirements that cost the state more money.  Many vendors know the thresholds and come in on a low bit and then quickly up the cost to the max before crossing the threshold.  This increases the overall cost and ultimately costs much more than necessary.  Eliminate small business requirements to allow a competitive rate.
IDEA_TEXT: Remove purchasing rules that let vendors start with low bids then raise costs. Allow all vendors to compete fairly without small business requirements.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Procurement

COMMENT:
I think technology and our internal workflows need a major overhaul. I started in January 2025 and was utterly shocked about how far behind we were compared to the rest of the country with business operations. I think our offices could greatly benefit from project management software and/or real customization of our software to makes us more efficient. Our office can't take any other payment other than checks, which sounds crazy to most of the public who walk into the office.  I see a lot of filing and literal paperwork happening in our office and we waste so much time "looking for files," which I feel is a waste of taxpayer dollars when we should be able to find what we need and do our jobs. And while it is great that we have a lot of long-time tenured employees in our ranks, we also need some major training overhauls once these updates are implemented. 

I also think training when I started was haphazard at best. No one explained and has not since explained how to properly navigate our manuals, or how to navigate the legal language used. This also deeply surprised me as someone new coming into the state. My expectations was that their training would be top notch. Myself and it sounds like many before me had to teach themselves many topics. Our offices could benefit from streamlined training programs when folks start their positions. Not just about how to be a supervisor, the only training I got, but how to do the actual job and workflows, manuals, concepts, etc.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve training programs for new employees. Create clear training on job duties, workflows, manuals, and legal language instead of only supervisor training.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Onboarding new employees; Process documentation
