COMMENT: In my career as public employee I have worked for different state departments. I have noticed there is a huge difference in the type of software and hardware that is being provided. In some departments I have had to purchase my own ergonomic equipment due to budget issues. As part of title 8 make changes to the ergonomic program where departments are required to have a separate fund for office, and ergonomic equipment. Including software programs that can assist employees improve completing their essential duties. Increase awareness in workplace bulling and harassment. Make it a mandatory yearly training and provide more resources to employees to encourage reporting.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a separate fund for office and ergonomic equipment under Title 8. Require departments to provide proper equipment and software to help employees do their jobs.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Technology and digital innovation; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Office management; Technology and data modernization

IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Trust and openness; Work culture

COMMENT: This is not an issue for me, but State Workers should not have to pay for parking. State Workers should have a pay dedicated to paying these fees. It makes it very difficult to for them to get to their job's sites. There should be dedicated and safe parking for them.
IDEA_TEXT: Provide free parking for state workers or give them a parking allowance to cover parking costs. Create dedicated and safe parking spaces for state employees.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Employee travel

COMMENT:
Personally, as a manager, I think our departments need better access to reporting for internal productivity. Currently the tools that i have available to me, have a very limited view on what I can pull and it is not in an excel format. Because of this, I can't comb through the data in a meaningful way, remove columns I don't need. I have to manually look up information, which is extremely time consuming and even what I am able to pull, can't tell me the information I need. I have to manually track what I can. 

And speaking of excel, previously my team would print out lists provided from HQ to do projects. Again these lists are not in an excel format, which makes it difficult to weed out other district office information and focus on my district. So manually, I have to find out what applys to my office. 

In the areas that I can, I have since shifted some of our projects to a shared drive, excel format and we color code what stage of the project my team is working on. When it is ready for my review, I can clearly see which ones need my attention and I can keep tabs in real time with where they are at. 

Using more digital tools could makes us incredibly efficient and be able to focus more on items like customer service. When we have good tools, we can focus on what matters.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve reporting tools for managers. Provide access to data in Excel format that can be filtered and customized for each district office.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Shared data; Software and tools; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT:
Personally, as a manager, I think our departments need better access to reporting for internal productivity. Currently the tools that i have available to me, have a very limited view on what I can pull and it is not in an excel format. Because of this, I can't comb through the data in a meaningful way, remove columns I don't need. I have to manually look up information, which is extremely time consuming and even what I am able to pull, can't tell me the information I need. I have to manually track what I can. 

And speaking of excel, previously my team would print out lists provided from HQ to do projects. Again these lists are not in an excel format, which makes it difficult to weed out other district office information and focus on my district. So manually, I have to find out what applys to my office. 

In the areas that I can, I have since shifted some of our projects to a shared drive, excel format and we color code what stage of the project my team is working on. When it is ready for my review, I can clearly see which ones need my attention and I can keep tabs in real time with where they are at. 

Using more digital tools could makes us incredibly efficient and be able to focus more on items like customer service. When we have good tools, we can focus on what matters.
IDEA_TEXT: Use more digital tools and shared drives. Move projects to Excel format with color coding to track progress in real time.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Software and tools; State cloud storage

COMMENT:
I never understood why every agency buys the same product(s) at a different contract rate when DGS handles the Statewide contracts that do not have dollar limits for their use and can purchase at scale for the whole state? I realize there is always a cost concern. From the numerous Department I have worked at throughout my career, I  found many agencies were able to save money by not renewing their own Agency contracts and leveraging statewide contracts at scale . I think there is something to be said about the state as a whole consolidating services especially when most are moving to cloud/SaaS . It blows me away that each agency has their own contracts with the same vendors for the same service and one might pay 1 million a year and the other 200K? 

Microsoft?
Nessus?
Cisco? 
Apple? 
HP? 
DELL?
RSA?
Splunk?
Just a couple of examples….
IDEA_TEXT: Use statewide contracts instead of separate agency contracts. Have all agencies buy products through DGS statewide contracts to get better prices and avoid paying different rates for the same services from vendors like Microsoft, Cisco, Apple, HP, Dell, and Splunk.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Cross-agency collaboration; Procurement

COMMENT:
(High-Speed Rail) Although I don't have direct experience with the High-Speed Rail. Admittedly, maybe I don't have enough understanding of the situation. But if there is still budget to put down some tracks. Why not start the High-Speed Rail between LA & Las Vegas. Could California not split the profits 50/50 with Nevada? I am sure that the casinos would love to assist paying for it as well since you would be bringing revenue to their casinos with the easy access between LA & Vegas. It is also a lot of desert between the two cities. At least then you would start generating revenue to continue the project. Aside from that I would imagine there would be increased revenue for the city of LA as people from Nevada may want to visit Disney Land, Hollywood, etc. with easier access.

Seems like it would be a win-win for both states and possibly a revenue generator for the program to get things going.
IDEA_TEXT: Start High-Speed Rail between Los Angeles and Las Vegas instead of current route. Split profits 50/50 with Nevada and get casinos to help pay for construction to generate revenue for the project.

COMMENT: Orgnaizational Structure. There seems to be overlapping of leadership in layers, with multiple director level positions in addition to the main Director of an organization.  In some cases, program directors oversee division chiefs while also maintaining separate subordinate staff. This may lead to duplication of responsibilities, unclear lines of accountability, and inefficiencies.  That increases admin cots, slow/hold up decision making, and create confusion about roles and responsibilities as well as drawing resources away from direct program support.  Recommend consolidating director level positions. Redefine the scope of program directors and focus on strategic guidance rather than duplicating operational oversight. Basically, simplifying the leadership structure to operate more efficiently, reduce overhead, and strengthen the ability to focus on its mission.
IDEA_TEXT: Simplify leadership structure by combining director-level positions. Redefine program directors to focus on strategic guidance instead of day-to-day operations. This will reduce overhead costs, speed up decisions, and clarify roles and responsibilities.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Process improvement; Management culture and leadership; Governance and oversight

COMMENT: I try and keep it simple for providing customer service. Treat your e-mail like a phone call. If you are a customer and you make a call and the person doesn't answer or you are put on hold waiting, you become already agitated. Same goes with e-mail. So, if you get a call or an e-mail and you simply answer the call or acknowledge the e-mail and reply back as quick as you can. Even if it's as simple as saying, thank you for the message, I will research and get right back to you as soon as I get the answer. You will make the customer feel like they are your priority.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve customer service response times. Treat emails like phone calls by quickly acknowledging receipt and providing status updates even when research is needed.

COMMENT: As a state employee with the LCO I have seen a lot of people promote to higher positions when they were not doing their previous position well. While others are not being promoted despite working hard and caring about what we do. Because of this I have seen us lose a lot of great workers and will continue to, as I am working on leaving the LCO as well. I have been here over 10 years with only a promotion from OT to MST. If we retained and promoted quality workers and not terrible workers, we would be far better off. I personally feel that the entire wage training team has been a waste of time and money because they do not appear to be effective or productive.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve promotion practices. Promote quality workers who perform well instead of poor performers to retain good staff.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Employee performance reviews; Employee retention

COMMENT:
The most prevalent issue that I have witnessed since becoming employed by the state is how poorly employees with disabilities are treated during the reasonable accommodation process. From my experience as an employee and union steward, it seems that disabled employees are treated like a burden during the reasonable accommodation process, especially if they are requesting telework. In most cases when the employee's doctor is recommending telework as a reasonable accommodation, departments will do anything to delay or deny telework as a reasonable accommodation. Departments have continually been allowed to ignore and violate the ADA, EEOC, and FEHA policies and regulations and oppose doctor's recommendations for appropriate reasonable accommodations. 

As a whole, the state needs to hold departments accountable for following appropriate policies and regulations pertaining to reasonable accommodations. More timely processes and procedures should be implemented. It is unreasonable to allow each department's EEO unit/section 90 days to investigate themselves, while not providing an employee with a disability their recommended reasonable accommodations. A more consistent and streamlined approach needs to be developed to help support California's employees with disabilities to better provide them more equitable and inclusive work environment when working for the state.
IDEA_TEXT: Hold departments accountable for following disability accommodation rules. Create faster, more consistent processes that don't let departments delay or deny doctor-recommended accommodations like telework.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Governance and oversight; Workplace operations; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accessibility; Accountability and transparency; Employee pay and benefits; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: The unnecessary spending. For example, in my line of work, we send people down south for training when the following month we do the same training her in Northern CA. I am all for training but when we are spending $20,000 to send four people down south, I feel like it is unnecessary money, especially when at times these are for new hires. Also, in my department, we have sent the same people year after year to the same training when the employee has come back and said that they didn't learn much more than the year before. Another example is the amount of time wasted on doing the same procedure multiple times, just different ways.
IDEA_TEXT: Coordinate training schedules better. Schedule training in Northern California instead of sending staff south when the same training will be offered locally the next month.

COMMENT: Teleworking, or lack thereof. Look, I understand not all jobs can function properly through telework but for those that are more than capable to telework, it needs to be a full 5 days a week thing. If you want to go into the office, by all means, but it should be decided by the employee and the quality of work they provide. Full telework is the closest we can get to what I think is the perfect work/life balance - 8 hrs sleep, 8 hrs work, 8 hrs your time. Instead, my current schedule is train station at 615, in Sac at 705, at work at 735, back to my car at 530. That's over 11 hrs dedicated for my job. In what world is that balanced?
IDEA_TEXT: Allow full-time telework for jobs that can work remotely. Let employees choose to work from home or office based on their work quality.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Workplace operations; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee retention; Remote work and return to office; Work culture

COMMENT: The primary problem I have seen is low morale. This has been caused by at least 2 different things: 1) RTO policies and refusing to listen to state employees; 2) poor management and bad leadership that results in inefficient or unfair division of labor. Leadership, starting at the very top, needs to respect and listen to state workers. When policies, like RTO, are implemented without any real consideration of how it will upend the lives of civil servants--that is disrespectful and will always lead to low morale. I have also seen morale be impacted by poor leadership and bad supervisors (i.e. ones that do not listen to their employees and refuse to pivot when needed, or just flat out assign work unfairly). Once morale is low, people will begin doing the "bare minimum."  They will take very little pride in their work. They will not care about outcomes. They will be so stressed and they will lose their focus at work. This is what leads to inefficiency. When you have workers who are able to pay their bills comfortably (because they are receiving a fair wage), when they see management take on the hard tasks (because management is making more money and should be taking on the more challenging projects), when they are allowed to work in places of safety and comfort (such as their own homes where they are mostly shielded from inappropriate conduct such as workplace violence, racist or sexist comments, physical-micro-management, etc.), that is what will improve efficiency for all!!
IDEA_TEXT: Improve leadership respect and listening to state workers. Stop return-to-office policies that hurt employee lives, ensure fair work assignments, allow remote work for safety and comfort, and provide fair wages so staff can pay bills comfortably.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Management culture and leadership; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Management culture and leadership approach; Remote work and return to office; Trust and openness; Work culture

COMMENT:
If the goals for the Engaged California project include to find challenges that undermine our ability to work efficiently and effectively, and to discover ways to better serve the people in our state, it doesn't make sense to take virtual work (or RTO) off the table for discussion. Additionally, this is supposed to be a deliberative democracy forum, as such reasoned positions are valid and should not be limited. Overall, as a state employee that is in a hybrid position working both in person and virtually at times, it is important to validate the values that virtual work affords state employees, state agencies, people in our state, and the state budget. It is also important to see how virtual work solves for problems in inefficiency and ineffectiveness, and can help to solve for ways to better serve the people in our state. 

A primary problem right now is the position that virtual work shouldn't be part of the Engaged California discussion to find solutions to problems (because it is supposedly doesn't "stay on topic" - see Engaged California's "Sharing Guidelines" ).  It makes no sense to talk about workplace efficiency and effectiveness, and to talk about better ways to serve the people in our state without considering talent management, workplaces, work mediums/channels, and work spaces. A set proposed solutions include opening this forum to the topics of virtual work, talent and workplace management, and work conditions.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow discussion of virtual work, talent management, and workplace conditions in the Engaged California forum to improve government efficiency and effectiveness.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Organizational structure and culture; Workplace operations; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee retention; Internal communication; Remote work and return to office; Work culture

COMMENT: Better management evaluations and hiring.  I would make a way for staff to evaluate them, if too many negative evaluations, they are not put into place.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve manager hiring and evaluation. Create a system for staff to rate managers and prevent those with too many bad reviews from being hired or promoted.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Communication and knowledge sharing; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee performance reviews; Internal feedback; Leadership review and oversight; Manager training

COMMENT: Low morale is the biggest killer of efficiency.  Low morale is caused by at least two things: 1) not listening or respecting state workers; 2) bad management/unfair division of labor.  When policies are implemented that do not effectively consider the potential impacts on the lives of civil servants, such as RTO--state workers will lose all drive to do good work.  Why work hard for a system that does not care about you and won't listen to you?  Poor leadership has the same effect.  Managers can get stuck in their ways and refuse to rethink processes or challenge other policies/processes that are clearly inefficient--even when their employees are expressing the need for change.  When employees are able to live and work comfortably (by being paid a fair wage, have a good work-life balance, be able to work in safe environments, like their homes, free from distracting co-workers and racist/sexist/inappropriate conduct), and when they see their management step up and be good leaders (by listening, pivoting when needed), that is what will improve efficiency!
IDEA_TEXT: Improve employee morale by listening to staff concerns, providing fair wages and work-life balance, allowing safe work environments like remote work, and training managers to be better leaders who adapt processes when needed.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Organizational structure and culture; Management culture and leadership; Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Management culture and leadership approach; Manager training; Remote work and return to office; Work culture

COMMENT: I believe there should be a universal intranet site with agency-specific and subject-specific subsites.  The main site could include spaces for disseminating information (e.g. statewide policy), posting training and job opportunities, hosting webinars, sharing news on projects and subjects of statewide interest, and collaborating/idea sharing.  This site is a good start but access to a statewide community should be automatic, not opt-in. I also believe the state would benefit greatly from further standardization of web/software design and a collaborative community of engineers. (e.g. having templates for commonly needed software, like procurement, inventory, appointment scheduling, marketing/newsletter, learning management, certification tracking, mobile apps, etc.) Agencies could then customize templates to fit their unique needs. This could make software more accessible and affordable, encourage development of the state IT workforce, reduce reliance on private sector contracts, and create a more universal user experience, reducing the need for training when someone changes agencies. Lastly, I believe data required/used by multiple agencies should centralized to reduce errors/discrepancies and time spent on data entry. A first step would be auditing existing databases to identify what data is being collected by multiple agencies currently.
IDEA_TEXT: Centralize data used by multiple agencies to reduce errors and duplicate data entry. Start by auditing existing databases to find what data multiple agencies currently collect.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Shared data; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT: When supervisors listen to their employees, who are on the front lines and therefore in the best position to know, and the employees are telling management the hurdles they are facing, and when those supervisors take that information, absorb it, process it, and pivot accordingly--that improves efficiency.  Instead a lot of managers refuse to listen, refuse to change, and refuse to believe they could be wrong.  Management often times is too afraid of losing their job or retaliation if they disagree with a higher-up or a policy that has come from the top.  Instead, middle management should be fighting for what is best for their employees (and thus what is best for all Californians-since they are almost always the same), even if that means upsetting people above them.  And Exec and higher-level management should cultivate and encourage dialogues and safe places to express ideas, again, even if it includes disagreeing with current policies that are inefficient, e.g. RTO policies.
IDEA_TEXT: Create safe spaces for staff feedback. Have supervisors listen to front-line employees about problems and change policies based on their input. Encourage middle managers to advocate for their staff even when it means disagreeing with higher-ups.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Communication and knowledge sharing; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal feedback; Management culture and leadership approach; Trust and openness

COMMENT:
The primary problem that I see are executives overloading lower management and analysts with projects and unrealistic goals. 

I also feel like we as State Workers are not appreciated by our own government and we are used as the patsies when things go wrong. We end up being the ones to suffer when the government fails to meet their goals. Billions are being diverted for other programs yet State Workers get their pay threatened constantly by going over budget. 

We need to be appreciated by our executives and our own governmental leaders. These are the issues I hear amongst coworkers.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve how executives treat staff. Stop overloading lower management and analysts with too many projects and unrealistic goals. Show appreciation for state workers instead of blaming them when things go wrong.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Organizational structure and culture; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership approach; Trust and openness; Work culture

COMMENT: If there isn't more money to give to state workers, we should "expand the pie" and think about other benefits to give them instead, such as telework, alternate work schedules, additional PTO, etc.
IDEA_TEXT: Offer non-monetary benefits to state workers instead of pay raises. Provide telework options, flexible work schedules, and extra paid time off.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Employee retention; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT:
The contracting process is broken. Californians want government to have easy to use online tools, one-stop-shop permits, data access portals, and to use the latest technology to make people's lives better. Both IT and non-IT contracts are cumbersome and require navigating gate keepers who do not understand agency needs. If a project is deemed IT by contracting personnel, my agency will simply not move forward. If a contract gets flagged for the PAL process with CDT, the contract is dead, and we will never resubmit. Money lasts 3 years, and the PAL process can take that long, leaving no time for the money to be used on the intended project. Further, getting non-IT contracts through DGS is time-consuming and cumbersome with little added value, decreasing the time for the money to actually do work. 

My suggestion would be to loosen up contract oversight on the frontend, actually provide training to staff, and provide more resources for auditing and enforcement of contract rules and ethics on the backend. In this way, SME staff will be trained on contract creation and execution (which is currently not the case) and can quickly get money out in flexible ways, taking advantage of as much of the 3 year money lifetime as possible, while knowing that they must strictly adhere to contract rules or their money will get pulled back and contracts will get cancelled. If you want staff to move fast, and use technology, this has to change.
IDEA_TEXT: Reduce contract oversight at the start and increase it at the end. Train staff on contract rules and give more resources for auditing. This lets trained staff get money out quickly while ensuring they follow contract rules or face penalties.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Budget and resources; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Employee training; Internal feedback; Procurement

COMMENT: I've been involved in multiple efforts to improve internal business processes using the lean six sigma methodology. From hiring, to travel expenses, data processing, and a few others, some projects went well and some less well. Ultimately, I feel all these projects have helped me in my own process for identifying areas of improvement, determining methods of measuring these areas, and helping teams develop ideas for improvements that can work and will actually be implemented.
IDEA_TEXT: Use lean six sigma methods to improve business processes. Apply this approach to hiring, travel expenses, and data processing to help teams find and measure improvements that can actually be put in place.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Workplace operations; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Employee travel; Hiring and recruitment; Process design and methodologies

COMMENT: The State and particularly Sacramento has the opportunity to be a model for other states and levels of government, but to accomplish this we need to reduce the barriers keeping motivated and innovative people from applying and getting state jobs.  In particular, I have seen how the cumbersome process of verifying MQs for folks coming from the private sector causes us to lose out on great candidates and discourages others from trying at all.
IDEA_TEXT: Simplify the hiring process for private sector candidates. Reduce barriers in verifying minimum qualifications (MQs) to attract and retain talented applicants from outside government.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Hiring and recruitment; Qualified staff

COMMENT: There is a disconnect between those in authority (i.e. Sacramento) and those who have the responsibility to enact the policy "on the ground."  this has been my experience both as a public school teacher and as a psychologist with Department of State Hospital.  Sacramento is top heavy with staff (at least in DSH) and they have little exposure or spend limited time in the hospitals with the patients.  Therefore, they do not appreciate what the staff limitations are.  We are short-staffed on the floor and struggle with budget restrictions, while many in SAC work from home and create policy that are not practical in their implementation.
IDEA_TEXT: Require Sacramento leadership to spend regular time working directly in hospitals with patients and front-line staff to better understand real conditions before making policy decisions.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Communication and knowledge sharing; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal feedback; Management culture and leadership approach; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: Full time telework for all state workers. If any agency or department wishes to deny telework, it should be supported by verifiable data. If there is a legitimate need to report in office, the reasons should be specific to the state worker and their department, division, branch, unit, etc.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow full-time remote work for all state workers. Require agencies to provide data-backed reasons if they deny telework requests.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: Accounting/Trust: Gobs of inefficiencies with a chain of command that doesn't know the details when I bring up better ideas. HQ does NOT understand what we do in office at institutions and policies contradict each other. We waste thousands of sheets of paper per month on statements when they could go out on the tablets. We spin our wheels answering GA22s for balance inquiries for hours a day when again, make it available on the tablets. We print monthly court checks for mandatory payments from IP accounts, however each court seems to be entered several times with a slightly different title (ie: Fresno US District vs. Fresno USD) and so multiple checks print out to the same exact place. This is wasteful and time consuming. Correct the duplicate entries so that one check prints per court. Also, SOMS pay sheets mostly print ONE IP per page. This is so wasteful and makes for lots more signing and takes up more room in storage. When asked about at HQ, no one knows how it works so it cannot be fixed. Someone has to know.
IDEA_TEXT: Change SOMS pay sheets to print multiple inmates per page instead of one per page.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Process design and methodologies

COMMENT: A glaring example is the fact that the DSH does not have an electronic health record.  In this century, it is inexcusable that a system this size does not have a modern way to document and share medical information.  Yet, this process has been in the hands of SAC for over 10 years, with no progress.
IDEA_TEXT: Implement an electronic health record system for the Department of State Hospitals (DSH) to replace outdated paper documentation and improve medical information sharing.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Shared data; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT: We waste a lot of money on mental health with 1:1 observation status. Currently when an i/p cites that he/she is suicidal we send them to an alternative housing unit temporarily for up to 24 hours before placing them or transferring them to a MHCB or back to the yard. This requires multiple nursing staff to watch and round for this task. INSTEAD: pull them out, strip down their cell and place a CO on them for the observation. 1. a CO is a cheaper overtime than nursing. 2. this would cut down on the secondary gain from the observation and the more behavioral i/p will think more about what they are doing before eliciting a CIT. This way we can pay more attention to the truly SI/SA i/p.
IDEA_TEXT: Replace nursing staff with correctional officers for mental health observation duties. Use correctional officers instead of nurses to watch suicidal inmates because officers cost less in overtime and may reduce fake suicide claims.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Qualified staff

COMMENT: For improved state government efficiency, I’d suggest waiting until the AI hallucination rate decreases before implementing AI into state work. As an example, Microsoft Copilot has a hallucination rate of 27% and some specialized technical models have up to 60-80%. While policy requires double checking AI output, it is incredibly inefficient and embarrassing to have to retroactively correct published AI-generated hallucinations. I have used Copilot for non-work purposes, but these inaccuracies make me extremely hesitant to use it for my work, which involves data analysis and coding. Why should we as state workers trust AI output when there’s a proven record of hallucinations?
IDEA_TEXT: Wait to use AI tools in state work until error rates drop below current levels of 27-80 percent.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: AI and large language models; Risk aversion

COMMENT: Medicaid provides health insurance to low-income populations in the U.S. Because private health insurance premiums have become so expensive, many people on Medicaid would be uninsured if not covered by Medicaid, while others could pay for private insurance only by cutting back on other necessities that indirectly affect health. Extensive research has shown that cutting Medicaid coverage leads to worse health and increased risk of premature mortality. As a possible solution, the State of California could implement corresponding state-level tax increases to partially backfill federal safety net cuts. Since some of these cuts won’t take effect for a couple of years, there is the possibility that the control of the House and Senate could change in 2026, and then legislation could be passed to undue some of the federal cuts to Medicaid. Time will tell whether these cuts will have the devastating impact they are intended to have on people’s health.
IDEA_TEXT: Implement state-level tax increases to replace federal Medicaid funding cuts.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT:
I'm all about helping our team grow and unlocking everyone's potential. Right now, our department is seriously missing the mark when it comes to developing talent. My boss hasn't really been stepping up to guide and mentor people who could be future leaders. It feels like we're stuck in this old-school management mode that's more about checking boxes than actually developing people.
I'm pushing hard for a different approach – one that's all about building real leadership skills. We need to focus on helping people connect, understand emotions, and level up their professional game. Imagine managers who can actually spot what each team member needs to grow and thrive. This isn't just about training; it's about creating a workplace where people feel supported, challenged, and excited to bring their best selves to work.
By investing in our people like this, we're not just checking HR boxes – we're building a team that's flexible, motivated, and ready to crush whatever challenges come our way. It's time we stop managing and start truly leading.
IDEA_TEXT: Change management approach from task-focused to people-focused. Train managers to develop leadership skills, emotional intelligence, and ability to identify individual growth needs for each team member.

COMMENT: the RTO is just all about politics and not the quality of life for state employees.  State employees are productive workers remotely.  Remote work allows for flexibility, and quality of life.  No, we are not spending all day doing household chores but we are spending the time that we would otherwise be commuting doing our family duties.  Commuting is a waste of our time.  Packing breakfast and lunch every day is a waste of our time.  Picking clothes to wear to work is a waste of time.  When the work day ends, we want to be home with the family not on the road.  Commuting is congestion, pollution and stressful to our health.  The building developers can figure out how to fill their vacancies on their own.  The restaurant owners can figure out how to serve more customers in the private industry.  These cannot be reasons end remote work for state workers.  As a remote worker, I have time to wake up and exercise, drink my tea, coffee or smoothie in peace and not in the car driving and make a healthy breakfast to get my day started.  It allows personal time in the morning that cannot be enjoyed while commuting.  For the state employees who take advantage of teleworking, they need to be held accountable for their work by their supervisors.  Don't take away telework for everyone.  For those who meet with the public, they don't get to telework.  It's that simple.  My building has been vandalized multiple time s and we had to go home because of no water.   No reimbursement for missed
IDEA_TEXT: Continue remote work for state employees. Hold remote workers accountable through supervisor oversight instead of ending telework for everyone.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership approach; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT:
Honestly, giving my team more ownership has been a total game-changer! By really tuning into what each person brings to the table and creating space for creative problem-solving, I've watched my employees transform from just doing their jobs to being real innovation champions. Sure, not every experiment has been a home run, but we've learned so much along the way.
The coolest part? My team is now super confident and actually excited about suggesting improvements. They're not just waiting for instructions - they're proactively thinking about how we can do things better. It's like we've shifted from a traditional top-down approach to this dynamic, collaborative environment where everyone feels empowered to speak up and make a real difference.
Some of our pilot programs have been incredibly successful, and the energy in the office has completely changed. People are more engaged, more invested, and genuinely passionate about our collective goals. Who wouldn't want a team like that? By trusting them and giving them room to shine, we've created something pretty special.
IDEA_TEXT: Give teams more ownership and decision-making power. Create space for creative problem-solving and let staff suggest improvements instead of waiting for instructions.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Organizational structure and culture; Management culture and leadership; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal feedback; Management culture and leadership approach; Trust and openness; Work culture

COMMENT: Internally, the State of California and how it operates does not reflect that we are the 4th largest economy in the world. The fact we would ever have a budget deficit defies all logic and basic arithmetic. Since, in the United States, we are considered a "donor state" in reference to our relationship with the federal government, anytime California projects a deficit, we should just reduce our "donation" to the federal government to keep it balanced at the very least, and create a buffer of revenue as a means to be fiscally disciplined. Secondly, we need to take large corporations to task for boasting California as their home, but slighting the State when it comes to taxes and being disciplined environmental stewards. As an example, the oil and utility companies are running rough shod over the State and poisoning our lands and waters. Ultimately, this recouped revenue would go along way to make State worker pay, not only competitive, but superior to the private sector. Sweetheart deals between the State and big business need to be thoroughly scrutinized and contracts rescinded for bad actors.
IDEA_TEXT: End favorable contracts with big businesses and cancel deals with companies that violate agreements.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors

COMMENT: California is a leader in a lot of areas. I would like to see a significant challenge to the two-party system that dominates the entire country. If we are to lead by example, we should have no less than 20 political parties in order to address the concerns of as many people as possible. The system is rigged to the point where, even our vote no longer has the power it should. The two-party system is a major contributor to this very undemocratic reality. We have all this technology which, if applied intelligently, could be used to restore power to the voice of the individual. There is no mystery here, no unknown path, we just lack the will to be the best democracy the world has ever seen. Many other states would follow California's lead, and finally we could start to break apart the coup that is Oligarchy in our great state and country.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a multi-party political system with at least 20 political parties to better represent all Californians and reduce the power of the two-party system.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: Regulations are completely necessary only because for-profit businesses refuse to grow up and be responsible environmental stewards. We can not put profit above natural balance. We need harsher penalties for large multi-national corporations that do business in California and call California home, but pollute the land, air and waters around us. We need to rein in acceptable marketing practices, we need to discourage gaming the system, we need to preserve and maintain the natural balance of our State's great natural resources. Lastly, we need to ban artificial intelligence from existence. It will never live up to what is promised and it is cost prohibitive, both with the natural resources it wastes and the cost to humanity. California could lead the way for making artificial intelligence a fad, a silly plaything and have it fall quickly out of favor.

COMMENT: I am still fairly new, and I came from the private sector, specifically I most recently came from a tech start up environment. My main suggestion would be better benefits, specifically dental options (I think this is usually determined through the unions but figured I would throw it out there). The other thing I would suggest is performance-based raises on top of the structured ones. This gives the harder workers more incentive to stay and work hard and more money which usually improves moral.

COMMENT:
Data suggests that there is a lot of taxpayer dollars that could be saved by fully embracing as much telework as possible, while simultaneously consolidating the amount of physical space and building leases the state takes up. I would love to one day see the state consolidate multiple departments into something like the FTB campus, freeing up space by making telework the default work type for any position that does not require work to be done in office. Embracing telework in this way opens up state jobs to many more Californians all across the state rather than just people that live in a couple metro areas, it would reduce carbon emissions and traffic, as well as lower the cost of road maintenance in those areas, as well as be a huge benefit to the all employees. The FTB campus also has lightrail access, as well as a free parking lot, which would be a cost savings to employees who currently work downtown and have to pay for parking. the money saved from things like this could be used to help departments balance their budget and we would be able to better fund things that actually benefit residents of the state. 
I know this is an incredibly lofty idea, and would require a lot of planning, but I think it could provide a great deal of savings, and would make jobs with the state more appealing to most people. if we as a state are committed at being the best, we need to embrace forward thinking tactics, not stick to "what we always have done"
IDEA_TEXT: Maximize telework and consolidate state office space. Make telework the default for jobs that can be done remotely, consolidate multiple departments into shared campuses like the FTB campus with light rail access and free parking, and reduce building leases to save taxpayer money while expanding job access statewide and reducing emissions.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Communication and knowledge sharing; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Cross-agency collaboration; Environmental sustainability; Office management; Physical infrastructure; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT:
One of the primary problems I see in California government is that our technical infrastructure is outdated and creates unnecessary barriers for both employees and the public. For example, many departments are still relying on PDF forms for timecards and approvals, which slows down processes, increases errors, and limits accessibility.
To solve this, I would prioritize modernization of internal systems by moving toward cloud-based, user-friendly platforms that allow for real-time updates, digital signatures, and mobile access. This would save staff time, reduce administrative burden, and increase efficiency across departments.
Another major issue is how state departments communicate with the public. Too often, forms, notices, and websites are written in dense, bureaucratic language that can be confusing. I would recommend shifting toward plain language communication across all state agencies, making information clear, simple, and accessible to all Californians. Alongside this, we should invest in practical resources like website tutorials, FAQs, or short instructional videos (e.g., YouTube walk-throughs) that guide people step-by-step in accessing services or filling out forms.
Additionally, California has many departments that offer overlapping or complementary services—such as the Department of Rehabilitation (DOR) and the Department of Developmental Services (DDS), which often work with the same populations but in different ways. Stronger cross-department collaboration.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve coordination between departments that serve similar populations like Department of Rehabilitation and Department of Developmental Services.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration

The CA Climate Initiative came closer to changing this. CA Jobs First is getting at that problem, but may not make it to the finish line when the administration changes. There is no CA tracking mechanism or over arching council that holds that type of knowledge. There is No system in place to make sure that anyone trying to do workforce development knows what each department is doing in that area or if multiple departments are trying to attack affordable housing all departments have a mechanism to get information. All these programs exist but I have found so many state employee have no idea. Then employees approach their departments with a "novel" idea to address these types of examples above and a whole new program is started including money being put toward it, when it exists somewhere else.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a statewide tracking system and council to coordinate work across departments. This would help departments know what other departments are doing in areas like workforce development and affordable housing to prevent duplicate programs and wasted money.

COMMENT:
One big problem is the great brain drain. i.e. older employees that have been working the area for years are retiring and from what I have been seeing is that most of the time their supervisory positions are filled after they have retired.  And taking the wealth of knowledge with them. 
There is also a bit of a disconnect between the age groups of the older staff soon to retire and the younger staff coming it.

IDEA_TEXT: Create a knowledge transfer program where new hires shadow experienced employees before they retire, and provide training to help different age groups work together better.

COMMENT: One of the primary problems I see in California government is that out technical infrastructure is outdated and creates unnecessary barriers for both employees and the public. For example, many departments are still relying on PDF forms for timekeeping management, which slows down the processes, increases errors, and limits accessibility.  To solve this I would prioritize modernization of internal systems by moving toward cloud -based, user friendly platforms that allow for real-time updates, digital signatures, and mobile access and also provide adequate training especially for LEADERSHIP! Another major issue is how state departments communicate to the public.  Too often, forms, notices, websites, and presentations are written in dense bureaucratic language that can be confusing.  I would recommend shifting toward PLAIN LANGUAGE communication across all state agencies, making information clear, simple and accessible to all Californians.  Alongside this, we should invest in practical resources like website tutorials, FAQs, or short instructional YouTube videos, that guide people step by step filling out forms and accessing services or joining public online meetings.
IDEA_TEXT: Modernize technical systems. Move to cloud-based platforms with real-time updates, digital signatures, and mobile access. Provide training especially for leadership.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Process improvement; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Manager training; State cloud storage; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT:
In my role, I’ve taken initiative to improve how our department serves both staff and the public. For example, I’ve worked on modernizing outdated processes, streamlining communication, and promoting plain-language resources to make forms and services more accessible. I’ve also advocated for tools such as tutorials and online guides that help people navigate state services with confidence. These improvements not only save time for employees but also make it easier for Californians to access the resources they need.
Implementing these changes hasn’t always been easy. At times, I struggled to get buy-in from my direct supervisor and had to elevate ideas above her to leadership in order to move them forward. Navigating those layers of approval—and the hoops that come with them—can be frustrating, but persistence has paid off. I believe these efforts demonstrate both the need for stronger leadership support of innovation and the real impact small, thoughtful changes can have across state government.
IDEA_TEXT: Modernize outdated processes and improve communication. Create plain-language resources, tutorials, and online guides to help staff and the public access state services more easily.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Process improvement; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Internal communication; Plain language; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT:
One of the primary challenges I see as a Californian and a state employee is the culture of leadership that often relies on the mindset of ‘I endured it, so others must as well.’ This approach can create stagnation, where old practices remain simply because that is how things have always been done. While this may maintain stability, it can also limit innovation and discourage growth among both workers and leaders.

I believe that true leadership development means breaking this cycle and fostering a culture where leaders are empowered to challenge the process, question outdated methods, and seek improvements that benefit both employees and the public we serve. By emphasizing open communication and a willingness to innovate, we can build leaders who not only draw from their experience but also strive to leave systems better than they found them. For me, ‘challenging the process’ is not about disregarding tradition, but about ensuring we continually evolve to meet the needs of Californians with greater efficiency, safety, and accountability.
IDEA_TEXT: Change leadership culture to encourage innovation. Empower leaders to challenge old practices, question outdated methods, and seek improvements that benefit staff and the public through open communication and willingness to innovate.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Organizational structure and culture; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Growth mindset; Management culture and leadership approach; Trust and openness; Work culture

To achieve this, we would need to implement more robust teleworking policies.

COMMENT: I work in one of the many Legal departments. I receive in service of process. When someone sues the department and its employees, it is codified in law that each person/department gets a copy of the document.  Just last week, I had an individual name/sue 30 different people. So per law, each one needed a copy of the over 500 page document.  We need to change the law so that we can take electronic service. That way instead of piles and piles of paper (where I scan in only one copy) and distribution happens electronically. What a waste of paper and human effort. I have heard the law needs to be changed. Okay, let's change it! This effects every legal department in the state and also anyone else who serves papers on anyone in any law suit.
IDEA_TEXT: Change the law to allow electronic service of legal documents instead of requiring paper copies for each person named in a lawsuit.

COMMENT:
I believe if we invested in the development of a user-friendly CDTFA cellphone app for business owners, CDTFA could 1. Gain more transparency with the business owners and 2. Become more efficient the collect sales taxes for the state. 

For example, my collectors and I meet with the business owners with collection accounts at the field office and during our field calls at the business locations. Almost all the business owners we meet do not use a computer or own one and therefore, rely on their cellphones conduct day-to-day business activities like accepting a payment through Zelle for the sale of a t-shirt or food, business banking, creating a sales invoice to give to a customer, using Square to swipe a credit card payment, etc. Specifically, I would like to see that a user-friendly cellphone app is developed for CDTFA to make it easy for a business owner to register for a seller’s permit, make changes to the account (like an address changes or adding/closing business locations), pay sales taxes/state revenues, eFile tax returns for up to two locations, show past due taxes owed, setting up payment plans, and making a request for tax relief from penalty, interest, etc. I specifically would like this app to cross-register the business owner for other licenses and fee accounts under the authority of CDTFA.   The interface of this app should be easily uploaded to CDTFA's database in real time or within 24-hours. Add a chat function using AI to answer basic questions.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a user-friendly CDTFA mobile app for business owners. Include features to register for seller permits, update account info, pay taxes, file returns for up to two locations, view past due amounts, set up payment plans, request tax relief, and cross-register for other CDTFA licenses. Add real-time database sync and AI chat support for basic questions.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: AI and large language models; Digital services; Software and tools; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT:
At a broader statewide level, I believe that a universal statewide department cellphone app should be developed that cross-registers all state-required permits and licenses applicable for a business owner to operating in the state of California.  The purpose of creating a universal statewide department app is because each department has different information from the business owner. For example, one state department is notified timely that a business has closed or moved to a new location, yet another state department was never notified.  the unversal app solves this problem by streamlining the changes for all departments at once. 

Also, the purpose of developing a universal statewide department cellphone app is to ensure 
1. Efficiencies in collecting state revenues yet simplifying the process for a small business owner to register for state permits and licenses specifically related to their business. 
2. Creating a universal database for state departments to track data.  

Then, I would like to see that a QR code is then created for the business owner to use to easily identify themselves and could also be used for all the state permits and licenses held by the business owner.  

Account changes to any state permit and licenses like an address change could be handled through this app and that the changes are in real time or within 24-hours.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a universal statewide mobile app for all state business permits and licenses. Include cross-department registration, real-time updates within 24 hours, QR codes for business identification, and a shared database for all state departments to track business data and changes like address updates.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Communication and knowledge sharing; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Digital services; Permits, licensing, and fees; Shared data; Software and tools

COMMENT: Creating a safe work environment for both upper management and subordinates to share the ideas freely. Working collaboratively with all levels of staffing helped me to improve the customer service experience with the department. I always include my management, peers and subordinates in decision making processes. It helped me to craft an innovative and efficient procedure. It is all possible when there is trust between the management and subordinates as a whole. It is a two-way street, and it takes time to build this bridge between them. It is possible through transparency, working side by side with your team, and authenticity of caring about each other. In my current position, it took me at least 1 year plus to have the great relationship with my team and management. A lot of patience, changes, and hard work on how my team fits in goals, mission, and vision of the department.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a safe work environment where all staff levels can share ideas freely. Include management, peers, and subordinates in decision making. Build trust through transparency, working side by side with teams, and showing authentic care for each other.

COMMENT: I believe that state employees should have a higher degree of data literacy. Most (if not all) of my colleagues work with data in some way - whether it be simply reading/reviewing reports, to complex data analysis. Yet, so few of my colleagues possess modern skill sets to analyze/process data, and limited understandings of statistics (which is often the most key aspect of regulatory science!). I have witnessed piecemeal efforts to increase data literacy and train employees to use modern coding tools (e.g., R, Python, GitHub, etc.) across CalEPA BDO's, however such efforts often require significant internal willpower to overcome inertia (which sometimes is due to deliberate resistance from management, but often is simply a product of good ol' fashioned government inefficiency).
IDEA_TEXT: Improve data skills training for state workers. Teach modern tools like R, Python, and GitHub. Overcome management resistance and government slowness that blocks training efforts.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Management culture and leadership approach; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT: I think a great way to make California's government more efficient is to allow those that can to work from home as much as possible. This saves the state money, it often allows for a better quality of living and saved money for the worker and doesn't seem to impinge on productivity as has been shown since working remotely since the pandemic. Those in management would do well to understand a happy workforce is a productive one, and forcing people into working conditions where they have to spend more money to work will not help. You can entice better workers and keep those that are doing well by offering this kind of incentive that so many are doing away with. The primary job of the government should be to serve its people.  Working from home does this, it helps everyone in this State.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow state employees to work from home as much as possible to save money, improve quality of life, and maintain productivity.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Workplace operations; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Employee retention; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT:
Housing! Housing! Housing! As a civil servant, I do not earn enough money to even afford a 1 bedroom apartment where I live. It would be amazing if the state could build more housing for low-income people who work full time, like me! 

Allowing people to telecommute is good for the environment, reduces traffic, and increases a department's ability to fill positions. I don't telecommute myself, but I think telecommuting should remain an option. If more people could telecommute, that means they can live anywhere. Spreading out the demand for housing outside of the Silicon Valley, Sacramento, and Los Angeles may help decrease the overall prices in those already congested areas. 

The state already owns a lot of land. Build work centers (hoteling?) for state employees in different areas where housing is cheaper. Build employee housing at places like Napa State Hospital to attract good candidates and keep them. We have a high turnover and our employees commute from all over. Our campus is beautiful and we already have our own police and fire department. Build little cottages that employees can rent for a decent price!

Or, provide housing subsidies for state employees who do not earn enough to afford rent in high cost areas.
IDEA_TEXT: Build more affordable housing for state workers. Create state-owned housing on existing state land like work centers in cheaper areas and employee housing at facilities like Napa State Hospital.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Budget and resources; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Physical infrastructure; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT:
Talent retention is a problem as many private sector jobs have higher compensation.  Here are a list of solutions:
1. Market rate compensation
2. Flexible work hours
3. Get rid of the RTO mandate
4. Put work from home into law
5. State worker compensation should not be a political issue
IDEA_TEXT: Improve state worker pay and work flexibility. Set pay at market rates, allow flexible work hours, end return-to-office mandates, make work-from-home a legal right, and keep worker pay out of politics.

COMMENT: I believe we could do better when it comes to employee training, from the onboarding process all the way through the employee's career. Providing more classes that are free would help during these financially challenging times for the departments. I also think CalHR should have oversight on this matter to ensure employees are able to take advantage of the training that is available while also providing career guidance tools. Setting a minimum threshold for training hours per employee, per year, could help with this goal, along with creating a Career Support website full of tools and guidance. More education will result in better retention, a more educated workforce, and a reduction in sick hours because employees will feel supported and enriched. This will all lead to more production within the tasks that are required to run the state, which will save the state money over time. You see this in the private sector, from which many of us came from.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve employee training throughout careers. Provide more free classes, have CalHR oversee training access and career guidance, set minimum training hours per year, and create a Career Support website with tools and guidance.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Career growth; Employee training; Manager training; Shared resources

COMMENT: In a previous role, I helped streamline the legal review process for marketing content. To keep it effective, we updated it three times a year, ensuring the process stayed accurate and relevant.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a searchable database of common legal edits to help content creators avoid mistakes and speed up the review process. Update the database three times per year to keep it current.

COMMENT: Again, updating antiquated infrastructure, such as not having an electronic health record, not having key cards (we each carry a ring of 10+ keys), outdated telephones and pagers, would go a long way in streamlining a demanding day.  It is also very discouraging to meet virtually with SAC staff who get "safety pay" for WFH jobs, while we are on the floor with patients with serious mental illness and aggressive criminal histories.  To hear that they often get more pay to tell us what we need to improve, is demoralizing at its best and infuriating at its worst.
IDEA_TEXT: Fix unfair pay differences. Stop giving safety pay to remote workers while front-line staff work with dangerous patients for less money.

COMMENT: Californians in general need to be educated in the cost of failure to invest in social services at the beginning of a person's "at risk trajectory."  Were more resources directed toward schools (not just money, but infrastructure, education of quality teachers, incentives for retention, etc.)  then the less money and resources would be required at the endpoint, namely the prisons and state hospitals.
IDEA_TEXT: Educate Californians about the cost benefits of early investment in social services. Invest more resources in schools including infrastructure, teacher training, and retention incentives to reduce future spending on prisons and state hospitals.

COMMENT: I very much appreciate the need for all State Employees to have a shared structure so we all have a similar experience with our State Employment through CalHR. Every department however has their own functionality and needs. Creating a structure that allows for more flex and takes into account the needs of each state department.  This would be parameters outside of the Union contracts.  Creating a framework that has less structure but provides broader guidance instead would allow different departments to create more tailored pay scales and position classifications enabling a more competitive recruiting position. This could include types of bonus plans, performance incentives, singing bonuses, efficiency creation, etc.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a flexible framework for state departments. Allow each department to make their own pay scales and job types within broad guidelines. Include bonus plans, performance rewards, signing bonuses, and efficiency incentives outside of union contracts.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Governance and oversight
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Management culture and leadership approach; Org structure and hierarchy

COMMENT: I have been with the state for 9 years and 3 months. As a state employee I feel that the new hires are not getting the proper training to succeed in their job, that is why we have a lot of turn around and a lot of mistakes.  I believe that our state should train these new hires properly, with in classroom training and not just virtually on the computer. COMMENT: I designed and carried out a training program for my coworkers to learn how to use the version control tool GitHub for their coding projects. Living here in California is expensive, and telework helps reduce expenses and improves work/life balance by removing unnecessary commuting.
IDEA_TEXT: Continue flexible telework options to improve productivity and work-life balance while reducing costs for employees.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Workplace operations; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee retention; Remote work and return to office; Work culture

COMMENT:
I think that we have to fix the contracting process.  It simply takes too long to be efficient. A large part of the problem is the multiple hurdles that have been imposed on us from the Legislature, which passes these changes to the law, but never seems to revisit them to see if it's working. I had been working on a contract for months when I was told at the last minute that I had to use a different contracting process because of a legal change.  Madness.  

My recommendation would be for the Governor to call a special session of the Legislature to focus on how the State conducts business, specifically contracting. Maybe include some State employees who actually do the work to testify, rather than just the upper management.
IDEA_TEXT: Fix the contracting process by having the Governor call a special legislative session. Include state employees who do the actual work to testify, not just upper management. Review and simplify contracting laws and requirements.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Public-facing services and resident experience; Budget and resources; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Internal feedback; Policy development process; Procurement; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: As a state worker, we have access to guides (for ex, DRE uses elicensing and we have instruction guides to help callers through a non user friendly portal). I have suggested that these guides be shared to the public to help lower elevated calls on the call center. The elicensing guides are supposed to be added to the public website in the next update. I would suggest we add similar instruction guides in regards to assisting filing out apps or guides to online portals to help the public decrease the processing wait times and with the call centers.
IDEA_TEXT: Share instruction guides publicly. Add guides for filling out applications and using online portals to help residents and reduce call center volume and processing wait times.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digital services; Public participation; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT: DHCS pursuing non-competitive bids to award contracts worth tens of millions of dollars. Urgency in procuring a vendor is manufactured due to poor planning by leadership and the only solution proposed is to circumvent the competitive, transparent procurement process. Current vendors who are not meeting their obligations (and who were similarly retained through a non-competitive process) are not held accountable. Leaders are awarding contracts which pay state and federal funds based on personal relationships with vendors and not as the result of a comprehensive, objective assessment of costs and capabilities.
IDEA_TEXT: Use competitive bidding for all contracts. Hold current vendors accountable for meeting their obligations and use objective assessments instead of personal relationships when awarding contracts.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Governance and oversight
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Contracts and vendors; Procurement

COMMENT: Improving technology always improves efficiency. Introduce new software and train users on how to use it, so they can stop using the old process and learn the new, more efficient one.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve technology to boost efficiency. Add new software and train staff to use it instead of old processes.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Technology and digital innovation; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Employee training; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT:
Communication between supervisors and employees is a huge issue with every department I have worked in. There just does not seem to be ways to build as a team within when supervisor's don't take an active role in being personable and approachable to their employees. 
Also, "suits & ties" at headquarters need to stop making decisions and changing policies when they haven't taken the time to get to know the issues and successes of the employees and supervisors. Administration should be focused on true employee wellness, not just making it look good for the public's eye. Part of that means being personable and truly transparent with staff members. Find out what they are really dealing with in their departments/fields.
IDEA_TEXT: Focus on real employee wellness instead of public image. Be more personal and transparent with staff to find out what they actually deal with in their jobs.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal feedback; Trust and openness; Work culture

COMMENT: In my career with the state, I've always been surprised as to how different departments use different tools to do the same thing. Similar to the FisCAL effort, which shows how hard this can be, all departments should be using the same software and tools for things like personnel and HR information, web site content, and project management (SharePoint).

COMMENT:
One primary problem that often arises is the lack of streamlined processes and the underutilization of advanced methodologies like Six Sigma, especially in the context of AI advancements.
Here are a few ideas to address this problem:
-Recognition and Integration of Certifications: Advocate for the recognition of Six Sigma and other relevant certifications within your department. This could involve presenting case studies or data that demonstrate the tangible benefits of these methodologies in similar organizations.
-Training and Development Programs: Implement comprehensive training programs that focus on process improvement techniques, including Six Sigma. This could be part of a broader professional development initiative aimed at enhancing the skills of state employees.
-AI-Driven Process Optimization: Leverage AI to analyze current processes and identify inefficiencies. AI can provide insights into areas where time and resources are being wasted and suggest more efficient workflows.
-Pilot Projects: Start with small-scale pilot projects to demonstrate the effectiveness of new processes and methodologies. Successful pilots can build momentum and support for broader implementation.
-Cross-Department Collaboration: Encourage collaboration between different departments to share best practices and successful strategies. This can help create a culture of continuous improvement and innovation.
-Feedback Mechanisms: Establish robust feedback mechanisms to continuously gather inpu
IDEA_TEXT: Encourage departments to work together and share best practices. Set up ways to get ongoing feedback from staff.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Internal feedback

COMMENT: All of California's departments should be sharing training resources for efficiency, consistency, and to save money. Rather than each department purchasing their own training resources contracts, there is one contract that provides them for every department. The more licenses there are being purchased, the higher the discounts are.
IDEA_TEXT: Create one shared training contract for all California departments instead of each department buying their own training resources to save money and increase consistency.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Employee training; Shared resources

COMMENT: Unfortunately, I have noticed most of the dangerous driving habits I witness are coming from seniors (aside from distracted drivers). I believe regular driving tests would be beneficial for all ages, especially for our elders who are struggling.
IDEA_TEXT: Require regular driving tests for all ages, especially older drivers.
