COMMENT:
My team is fully crossed trained and have a back up (the back up has a back up too) for when they are on vacation or sick. When a member of the team has a planned outage they provide a list of pending items, what they are waiting on, and any contact information for the pending items to the whole team. The back up works the pending items as the necessary information is provided or updates are made. When the peer returns the back up provides a summary of what has been completed and what is outstanding. 
The back up usually gets extra support for their peers to ensure nothing is missed. This is a win-win for everyone because the person that is out gets to enjoy their time off, work doesn't stop, deadlines aren't missed, when the person returns they are able to return without the stress of playing catch up, and the peers that are working know when they take time off they get the same benefits. This works when you set the expectation from the beginning and you take the time to cross train your team and if you have documented processes. Duties are rotated on a quarterly or semi-annual basis.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a cross-training system with backup coverage. Train all team members to cover each other's work. Have backups for the backups. When someone takes time off, they give the backup a list of pending work and contacts. The backup handles the work and gives a summary when the person returns. Rotate duties every few months and document all processes.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Budget and resources; Communication and knowledge sharing; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Employee training; Process documentation; Shared resources

COMMENT: California is in a housing crisis. To make California's government more effective in responding to this crisis, I suggest that State properties be reevaluated to see if entire properties or portions of properties would be suitable for housing conversion. For example, the agency I work for has a large open parking lot adjacent to its office building, all of which is State property and near public transit. Could we maximize the use of this open parking lot by moving the parking space underground and building medium to high density housing units on top? Could there be a public-private partnership so that the State continues to own the land but leases it to a private developer so that the State ensures its needs are met? I know some transit agencies are converting transit parking lots into housing developments. Akin to some school districts, could we dedicate a portion of new housing to State employees at a discounted rate to attract and retain State employees?
IDEA_TEXT: Review state properties for housing conversion. Move parking underground and build housing on top. Create public-private partnerships where the state owns land but leases to developers. Reserve some units for state employees at discounted rates to help with hiring and keeping staff.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Communication and knowledge sharing; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Employee pay and benefits; Employee retention; Physical infrastructure; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: I would like to reiterate my idea of reevaluating State properties to see if all or a portion of them are suitable for housing redevelopment. Can parking lots on State property be converted so that parking goes underground and mid-to-high density housing goes on top?
IDEA_TEXT: Review state properties for housing development. Convert parking lots to underground parking with housing built on top.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Physical infrastructure; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT:
The primary problem I would address is homelessness. I have several ideas to fix this problem, and they can be implemented in stages. First and foremost, make it easy to build affordable housing. Recognize that homelessness is a housing problem. 

2nd, Prioritize the people who just slipped into homelessness and have potential and will to come out of it. Help them to come out permanently and join mainstream.  Right now, I serve as board member for HART of Folsom, and we are proud to have reduced Folsom's homelessness by 10% this way.
3rd, go deeper and help people that are homeless longer and probably will never come out.  Build shelters with services for them and make rules to humanely require them to take those services. Those rules might curtail some of their freedom, but they should know that it's cost to pay for not being able to come out of homelessness by themselves. 

Finally, to do all this work, encourage and fund the non-profits directly from the state so that the rules are uniform. One positive side effect is that more non-profit jobs are created this way. Each city and county having its own rules and managing and distributing funds will only create more red tape and confusion and dysfunction. Re-organize the current machinery and people work under one state agency with simple and uniform set of rules.
IDEA_TEXT: Build shelters with required services for long-term homeless people who may never recover on their own.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: The current status of government limits the methods that can be employed to improving California.  We have to be open to alternative methods and do not dismiss ideas because "we do not do things that way around here".  Most of the leadership in the departments and agencies are career bureaucrats.  They only know one way to address issues.  When a new approach is brought up, it is almost always shot down before it can even be explored.  This happens because people in leadership are afraid that if they verve from the normal process, they will lose their jobs.
IDEA_TEXT: Be open to new methods and ideas. Encourage leadership to explore different approaches instead of dismissing them because they are not the usual way of doing things.

COMMENT: As a state employee for more than 15 years, I would like our employer to highlight the work state workers do with the focus on changing the public perception of State Workers.  We work hard for the citizens of California and often dedicate unpaid time to ensure those we serve are receiving the care and attention required to prevent disruption in the services we provide.  In times of extreme hardship, we work long and exhausting hours without complaint.  State workers are of value to California.  A campaign to lift up the heroes that work for California, highlighting the  great workers that support our state is needed.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a public campaign to highlight the valuable work state employees do for California citizens and change negative public perception of state workers.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Organizational structure and culture; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal communication; Public participation; Public service delivery and responsiveness; Work culture

COMMENT:
If possible, I would implement a system that allows official business with the public to be conducted safely through email, text notifications, and secure online portals. This approach would provide instant communication, ensuring that more people especially those without stable mailing addresses can be reached quickly and reliably.

By transitioning to digital access, the public could view, complete, and submit forms electronically rather than relying on traditional mail. This would significantly reduce printing and postage costs, streamline processing, and enhance overall efficiency.

Number of the individuals my department serves are transient due to unstable housing, making electronic communication far more effective than mail. Allowing technology to replace paper-based processes would not only save government resources but also give the public greater control and faster turnaround times when submitting information. Printing and mailing forms require multiple steps and personnel, consuming unnecessary time and resources that could be better allocated elsewhere. For some people,  unnecessary delays and wait time for benefits can be quite catastrophic and we must at least explore a better process so that we can serve the public better. 
Perhaps a more obvious one is simply finding a way to increase staffing to reduce backlogs which is significantly impacting the public my agency serves. Staffing shortage is very concerning.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a digital system for public services. Allow people to conduct official business through email, text alerts, and secure online portals instead of mail. Let the public view, complete, and submit forms electronically to reduce costs and improve service speed.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Process improvement; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digital services; Digitize processes; Public service delivery and responsiveness

Allowing technology to replace paper-based processes would not only save government resources but also give the public greater control and faster turnaround times when submitting information. Printing and mailing forms require multiple steps and personnel, consuming unnecessary time and resources that could be better allocated elsewhere. For some people,  unnecessary delays and wait time for benefits can be quite catastrophic and we must at least explore a better process so that we can serve the public better. 
Perhaps a more obvious one is simply finding a way to increase staffing to reduce backlogs which is significantly impacting the public my agency serves. Staffing shortage is very concerning.
IDEA_TEXT: Increase staffing levels to reduce backlogs that impact public services.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public service delivery and responsiveness; Understaffing

COMMENT: From my perspective as a Californian, another area where efficiency and fairness could be improved is in the treatment of permanent intermittent (PI) electricians. Although many PIs work full-time for years, they do not receive the same benefits as their permanent counterparts, including boot allowances, regular pay dates, or timely raises. They are also last in line for overtime, unable to post and bid for jobs, and even when serving as lead workers, are not permitted to step in as temporary supervisors. Having spent 24 years as an electrician in the private sector, I can see how this inequity discourages skilled workers from staying with the state. Addressing these disparities would improve retention, morale, and ultimately the quality of service provided to the public.
IDEA_TEXT: Give permanent intermittent (PI) electricians the same benefits and opportunities as permanent staff. Include boot allowances, regular pay dates, timely raises, overtime priority, job bidding rights, and temporary supervisor roles.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Employee retention; Limited-term positions

COMMENT: The streambed alteration permitting program (EPIMS) is extremely difficult for the layperson to navigate and complete successfully. We receive requests for assistance daily, which distracts Environmental Scientist staff from other priorities. The helpdesk is understaffed, and the phone contact is no longer operational. Aside from scrapping the current program altogether for new in-house designed software (should be a top priority), a virtual in-program assistant would exponentially improve the end user experience, and scientific staff would receive far fewer assistance requests.
IDEA_TEXT: Add a virtual in-program assistant to the EPIMS system to help users complete permits and reduce staff assistance requests.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Public-facing services and resident experience; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Public service delivery and responsiveness; Software and tools

COMMENT: As both a citizen of CA and a public sector worker, I see an issue with each state and county departments/agencies/boards being siloed with their data. This means I have to submit my information to each department every time I need services or to update my information to continue services at every stop. I would like to see all CA state depts move to a centralized data warehouse. I want to add or update my info 1x and have that info accessible by all state and county departments/agencies/boards. This reduces the burden on me to manage, increases data integrity, and allows for beneficial enhancements such as leveraging AI to review my info automatically and inform me when I qualify for a services across the state, and auto-populating my global data when I apply for these services - potentially doing all the work for me. It would also improve statical reporting statewide as the data would be pulled from one global system. CA citizens should have a CA profile, not a DMV profile, FTB profile, EDD profile, etc.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a centralized data warehouse for all California state and county departments. Allow residents to update their information once and have it accessible across all agencies. Include features like AI to notify residents of services they qualify for and auto-populate applications.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Technology and digital innovation; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: AI and large language models; Cross-agency collaboration; Digital services; Public service delivery and responsiveness; Shared data

COMMENT: Most, if not all of California's prisons were built in an era before the current focus on robust medical, mental health, and rehabilitative services. Accordingly, few of these facilities have the physical space to accommodate these missions. One solution that has yet to be considered is repurposing incarcerated person housing units into spaces that could support the programming needs of the modern correctional mission. Given the continued reduction in the prison population, there's an opportunity to convert one housing unit on each prison yard into a space that meets these needs without massive financial outlay for new buildings. There would be an expense in performing the retrofit, but this is a far cheaper alternative to constructing new facilities.
IDEA_TEXT: Convert unused prison housing units into spaces for medical, mental health, and rehabilitation programs instead of building new facilities.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Physical infrastructure; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: Efficiency, cost-savings and environmental benefits. Based on the best available data (also supported by the recent State Auditor's findings) the state has the opportunity to leverage a hybrid work schedule of two days in office to create a significant cost savings by reducing unused office space, decrease transportation-related pollution, and providing significant health and financial benefits to state employees by helping increase work-life balance and reduce transportation-related cost such as fuel and parking. The data also supports a hybrid work schedule of two days per week as increasing employee efficiency by creating a work environment that is less stressful and one in which the employee is able to focus on the work without the distractions and stressors related to commuting and potential inter-office conflict.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a hybrid work schedule with two days in office per week to reduce office space costs, cut pollution from commuting, and improve work-life balance for employees.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Environmental sustainability; Physical infrastructure; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT:
State agency procurement is unnecessarily complex, involving too many stakeholders in processes that could be significantly streamlined through modern technology.
I propose establishing a state marketplace through Amazon's platform where departments can efficiently order goods and services. Within this centralized portal, vendors could submit competitive bids while ensuring compliance with all applicable state mandates and regulations.
This system would benefit vendors by allowing them to register once with Amazon and become eligible to sell to all state agencies, eliminating the current burden of navigating separate registration processes across the multitude of agencies that issue requests for bids on goods and services.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a state marketplace through Amazon's platform where departments can order goods and services, vendors can submit competitive bids, and vendors register once to sell to all state agencies.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Budget and resources; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Cross-agency collaboration; Procurement; Software and tools

COMMENT: As someone who has been with the state for many years, I find Upward Mobility to be a challenge. COMMENT:
As a DTSC PM for affordable housing projects it would be nice if we could work more efficiently internally and not cause an undue burden/cost on project proponents by potentially delaying projects due to our some times slow turn-around times and multiple iterations of document review necessary prior to approval.

I would propose that in general more scoping meetings should occur, and more up front requirements should be expressed by DTSC technical staff to proponents as early on in the document review process as possible.  It would also be great if supervisors and staff at higher levels could provide priority lists to staff with large amounts of work requests in order to prioritize for faster-paced projects with more aggressive timelines.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve DTSC project review process. Hold more scoping meetings and give clear requirements to project applicants early in the review process. Have supervisors create priority lists for staff to handle urgent projects with tight deadlines faster.

COMMENT: I worked for the state 10+ years before I understood the class spec, minimum qualifications, etc. process as far as applying.  I wondered, "do I qualify?", am I understanding this correctly, etc. so I can only imagine the difficulty for many from outside state service coming in and asking the same questions and getting frustrated and abandoning the process which is sad too because there are great workers out there!  It feels like a little bit of "gate-keeping" if you will.  I do appreciate now that they have an SOQ sort of informational document and my department has made it a priority to list that on every job bulletin that requires an SOQ.  I'm only a little part of the process but I make it a point to be very clear in my explanations and answer any questions I am able to for persons, and they really appreciate that because many don't understand the process. I hope that they share, share, share and I hope all of you will too!
IDEA_TEXT: Make the state job application process clearer and easier to understand. Provide better explanations of job requirements and qualifications. Train staff to clearly explain the hiring process and answer questions from job applicants.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Hiring and recruitment; Plain language

COMMENT:
I think that there are really 2 major problems: 
1 - we've over-regulated to the point that nothing can get accomplished and we're making it unappealing for businesses to operate in California. We can't even properly rebuild after the fires and we were unable to manage water to keep our people and homes safe. We're unable to properly manage our forests to prevent fires from being as destructive. We've even prevented police from enforcing current laws to keep people safe and we're seeing a surge in crime and violence.

2 - We need a stable power grid. We retired our nuclear plants even though they're the safest and cleanest source of stable power which our grid desperately needs - especially if we want to move towards all electric cars. Solar is great on existing infrastructure, but we've been clear cutting the forests to build those instead which destroys our beautiful countryside and it only generates power when the sun is shining. We should be developing fusion and building out a grid to support our efforts to reduce carbon emissions and cleaning up our air. We need trees and plants in the cities to help clean the air there and reduce the impact of brake dust and other particulates in the air. We can't claim to be wanting green energy while simultaneously clear cutting forests and countryside for something that results in brown outs and black outs because it's not stable enough to meet the core energy demands of the people.
IDEA_TEXT: Build a stable power grid with nuclear and fusion energy. Stop clear cutting forests for solar farms and add more trees in cities to clean the air.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Environmental sustainability; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: White supremacy is the primary problem that needs to be addressed. This  is an imperfect term that I use here as a catchall for systemic misogyny, racism, and ableism that promotes "rugged individual" myth. There needs to be a major culture shift in our approach to governance and resource management that would allow all people to participate in government. An inclusive government will more effectively serve all Californian's by promoting discussion and rational thinking over our current "follow the strongman" model. This survey feels like a box checking exercise that leadership can use a proof that they asked for input that will not be acted upon. State leadership would benefit from cultural humility training, however, it will take generations to bring about the cultural shift necessary to cause meaningful change. The State of California needs to own its racist actions, past and present. We need to acknowledge that we became the fourth largest economy through genocide, exploitation, and unsustainable resource extraction that continues today, and only benefits a few. Most state employees are ignored, especially scientists who point out that our resource extraction is unsustainable, and racial equity practitioners who point out how our racist policies are hurting oppressed communities. We need to stop being defensive and own our wrongdoing. We cannot solve a problem if we continually deny it's existence.
IDEA_TEXT: Acknowledge California's racist past and present actions. Own wrongdoing from genocide, exploitation, and unsustainable resource extraction instead of being defensive.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Governance and oversight; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: I would love to see various departments and programs integrated. Currently we are in silos with different departments doing the same task or money being spent twice for the same service.  For instance, we currently pay doctors to be on call for the incarcerated population(I/P), but since we do not have point of care testing, we send the I/P off-site for diagnostics. Looking at the data, I believe we spend an excess of $200 million that we could have avoided if we had point of care testing, telemedicine communication ability and a contract with each county hospital er for after-hours care.
IDEA_TEXT: Integrate departments and programs to avoid duplicate work and spending. Set up point of care testing, telemedicine communication, and contracts with county hospital emergency rooms for after-hours care for incarcerated people.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Public-facing services and resident experience; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Cross-agency collaboration; Public policy initiatives; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT:
As a Californian and state employee, I am most troubled by the ongoing homelessness problem in our state. In order to entice billionaires to stay in the state (and continue to fund our state government with their income taxes!) we need to do a MUCH better of making housing accessible to every Californian.

Recently, I used Sacramento County 311 to report a homeless individual who was encamped near my house and abusing alcohol to the point he might have died in the summer heat. I am happy to report that someone from the County cleaned up the encampment and removed the trash. However, I have no idea who was responsible for removing the encampment, or what became of the homeless person. To improve our state, I would like it to become common practice for citizens to report encampments and homeless residents AND be able to know what organization responded to the 311 report, and the where the homeless person was taken, and what services they were offered.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve tracking and transparency for homeless encampment responses. Make it standard practice for citizens to know which organization responded to 311 reports, where homeless individuals were taken, and what services they received.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Governance and oversight
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Public participation; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT: Used limited available Microsoft resources to develop processes that do not require or rely on email communications and allow for low level process automation. Study the SAM and SCM to set up administrative (procurement, personnel, etc.) processes that are compliant but efficient. For example, leveraging LPAs and open POs to streamline procurement and reduce the state paperwork required for every fair and reasonable purchase. This resulted in a significant reduction in the number of POs at the branch which freed up time for branch supervisors, employees, and the admin division of our department.
IDEA_TEXT: Use Microsoft tools to create processes that do not need email and allow basic automation. Study state rules (SAM and SCM) to set up compliant but efficient processes for buying supplies and managing staff. Use tools like LPAs and open purchase orders to make buying easier and reduce paperwork.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Process improvement; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Process design and methodologies; Procurement; Software and tools

COMMENT:
Communication between departments is lacking and there is often a silo effect. Meaning each department operates in an isolated fashion and lacks cross-departmental interaction. This often leads to inefficiencies, errors, duplicate efforts, a struggle to resolve issues, and failure to learn from each other.

It would be wise to implement more cross-functional teams to interact and collaborate on projects. Our leadership teams can model collaborative behavior and encourage cross-communication. Also ensuring that we are sharing information whenever possible, so that all teams are aware and included in decisions.
IDEA_TEXT: Create cross-functional teams to work together on projects. Have leadership model teamwork and encourage departments to communicate and share information with each other.

COMMENT: As a California resident and taxpayer, I'd like to see less wasteful practices (i.e., purchases, return to office mandates, new building leases, etc.). We are supposed to advocate for the health of the environment and the well-being of the people of California.
IDEA_TEXT: Reduce wasteful spending on purchases, office mandates, and building leases to better serve California residents and the environment.

COMMENT: I have seen significant progress when excluded communities (BIPOC, LGBTQIA, Immigrants, etc...) are actually listened to. Empowering these communities allows for actual progress instead of the usual responses that are aimed at preserving profits for a small, privileged segment of the population. Indian tribes throughout the state lead environmental conservation efforts that benefit everyone. A great example is the Klamath River in which Tribes are leading restoration efforts that will benefit the watershed. The Black Panthers and other groups conducted urban pollution monitoring to show that Black, Brown, and Indigenous communities are exposed to higher levels of carcinogens and are at greater risk of exposure to other stressors (extreme heat/weather, poor air quality). The work that these groups do highlight the need for environmental protections that will benefit all people. Unfortunately, they are often ignored or persecuted. We need to go beyond box checking and actually listen to, and empower communities that have the empathy and will to cause real change.
IDEA_TEXT: Listen to and empower excluded communities like BIPOC, LGBTQIA, and immigrant groups. Go beyond checking boxes and actually use their input to create real change instead of preserving profits for a small group.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Organizational structure and culture; Public-facing services and resident experience; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Inclusion and diversity; Internal feedback; Public participation; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: I would like to see higher education made available to students in rural communities.  Tuition is already expensive, the hours dedicated to study, and then the cost of living away from home makes it almost impossible for our rural youth to become professionals.  Then when they do move away for school they rarely return so their home community loses that talent.  Instead of paying professionals to work in underserved communities, we should have the CSU/UC system pair with local community colleges for tele-classes so rural children can live at home while attending university.
IDEA_TEXT: Partner CSU/UC system with local community colleges to offer remote classes. Allow rural students to attend university from home instead of moving away.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digital services; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: State employees who drive a state vehicle for their jobs, such as inspectors, investigators, etc, have an assigned WEX Fuel Card to purchase gas while on the road.  With the transition to ZEVs, why can't these WEX cards be used at EV charging stations?  Especially Tesla?  Departments must either have a separate credit card number to provide employees on their Tesla app, which then subjects the card to misuse and unauthorized non-state purchases, or employees must use their own personal credit cards and then submit a travel expenditure claim to request reimbursement.  If a WEX card could be used to charge an EV, it would be so much easier for state staff and less work for accounting staff to manage multiple cards, process travel claims, and management to review and approve so many different payment mechanisms.  If the WEX card could also be expanded to be used for small repairs and oil changes, staff wouldn't need to spend so much time finding a repair shop that will accept state payment policies, and can also get repairs done much faster without downtime.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow WEX Fuel Cards to be used at electric vehicle charging stations and for small vehicle repairs. This would eliminate the need for separate credit cards and reduce paperwork for staff and accounting.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Process improvement; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Employee travel; Expense reimbursement; Process delays

COMMENT: Day to day, I keep meeting on point and not have meeting if they aren't needed. I try to listen to my team and address issues immediately. Always include relevant staff in meetings and emails, even if they may be from other departments, because the silo effect helps no one. On a larger scale, I have been working as part of a workgroup to improve Health Equity within my organization. Assisted with development and training for the Peer Support Specialist Program for the incarcerated population. This program has already improved the lives of many, and I believe will help make prisons safer and lower recidivism rates in the long run.

COMMENT: DGS makes it unreasonably difficult for departments with owned vehicles to replace those that constantly break down because of excessive mileage or such an extensive repair history that it costs more to keep them on the road than to just replace them.  I understand the need for DGS to review and scrutinize additional fleet assets, but why are replacement vehicles so difficult to obtain?  It takes DGS 3-4 months just to approve the purchase (and DGS recently said departments will need to submit their Fleet Acquisition Plans 60 days sooner than in the past), and then once a vehicle is identified departments find out DGS has updated the state contract and the replacement vehicle is no longer even available.  From the time a department submits a Fleet Acquisition Plan (April 1), DGS approves by June 30, and then it often takes at least until December or January for the vehicle to arrive.  So if a vehicle is broken down, a department knows it's going to take at least 9 months to get that vehicle replaced and they need to rent a vehicle in the meantime.  DGS only allows short-term rentals for up to 30 days, anything longer tmust also be submitted to DGS for approval. The long-term rental process for DGS, even in these instances, results in tons of justification and back-and-forth between DGS and departments and in the meantime work cannot be completed or staff with a vehicle must do double the work to make up for the vehicle shortfall. Can DGS streamline the replacement process?
IDEA_TEXT: Streamline the DGS vehicle replacement process. Reduce approval time from 3-4 months, allow longer-term rentals without extra approval, and keep vehicle contracts current so approved replacements stay available.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Process delays; Procurement

COMMENT:
The core issue I want to address isn’t just about telework policies—it’s about a deeper, more entrenched problem within government culture: the tendency to default to “business as usual.” There’s a persistent inclination among leadership to repeat the same processes simply because they’re familiar and easy to enforce. This isn’t innovation—it’s inertia.

Take the push to return employees to the office four days a week. That’s a straightforward directive, but it sidesteps the harder, more meaningful challenge: making departments truly more efficient. It’s easier to mandate physical presence than to rethink workflows, modernize systems, or confront outdated bureaucratic habits.

The pandemic was a once-in-a-generation disruption that forced us to reevaluate how we work. It showed us that change is possible—and sometimes necessary. Yet instead of building on that momentum, many agencies are retreating to old norms. That’s not just a missed opportunity; it’s a failure to evolve.

The most urgent transformation we need isn’t logistical—it’s cultural. We must shift from a mindset of comfort and repetition to one of curiosity, accountability, and continuous improvement. Real progress begins when we stop asking, “How do we get back to normal?” and start asking, “How do we build something better?”
IDEA_TEXT: Change government culture from repeating old processes to continuous improvement. Shift leadership mindset from comfort and repetition to curiosity, accountability, and asking how to build something better instead of returning to normal.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Organizational structure and culture; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Growth mindset; Management culture and leadership approach; Trust and openness; Work culture

COMMENT:
One of the most powerful examples I witnessed during the pandemic was how it compelled our workplace to evolve. Faced with unprecedented challenges, we didn’t just adapt—we transformed.

We accelerated the digitization of our processes, shifting away from paper-heavy workflows and outdated systems. This wasn’t just about convenience; it was about survival. By streamlining operations and cutting through layers of bureaucracy, we empowered teams to do their jobs more effectively and with greater autonomy.

We also introduced flexibility for employees—something that had long been resisted. Remote work options, flexible schedules, and trust in individual accountability led to a noticeable boost in morale. People felt seen, respected, and more engaged.

Perhaps most importantly, we reimagined how we communicate within the department. Barriers broke down, silos dissolved, and collaboration became more fluid. These weren’t temporary fixes—they were foundational changes that made us stronger, more responsive, and more human. But unfortunately, all this under threat with mindset of we have go back to office and return of the old way of doing things mindset
IDEA_TEXT: Improve communication and teamwork. Break down barriers between departments and encourage collaboration.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Internal communication; Work culture

COMMENT:
Finally, I believe it’s time to fundamentally rethink how government employees work—and how government itself operates. The old blueprint no longer fits the realities we face today. We need to reduce redundant frameworks, streamline operations, and encourage departments to consolidate and collaborate across silos. Efficiency shouldn’t be a buzzword—it should be a mandate.
But this isn’t just about internal reform. It’s about reimagining the physical footprint of government. Many office buildings now sit underused or entirely vacant. These spaces represent an opportunity—not a liability. By converting surplus government buildings into affordable housing, we can breathe new life into downtown economies that have long depended on a steady flow of government workers.This kind of transformation would not only address housing shortages but also diversify and strengthen urban centers. It’s a chance to shift from a government-centric downtown to a community-centric one—where people live, work, and thrive in more dynamic, inclusive environments.
IDEA_TEXT: Convert unused government buildings into affordable housing. Transform downtown areas from government-focused to community-focused where people live, work, and thrive.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Physical infrastructure; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT:
Finally, I believe it’s time to fundamentally rethink how government employees work—and how government itself operates. The old blueprint no longer fits the realities we face today. We need to reduce redundant frameworks, streamline operations, and encourage departments to consolidate and collaborate across silos. Efficiency shouldn’t be a buzzword—it should be a mandate.
But this isn’t just about internal reform. It’s about reimagining the physical footprint of government. Many office buildings now sit underused or entirely vacant. These spaces represent an opportunity—not a liability. By converting surplus government buildings into affordable housing, we can breathe new life into downtown economies that have long depended on a steady flow of government workers.This kind of transformation would not only address housing shortages but also diversify and strengthen urban centers. It’s a chance to shift from a government-centric downtown to a community-centric one—where people live, work, and thrive in more dynamic, inclusive environments.
IDEA_TEXT: Reduce redundant frameworks and streamline operations. Encourage departments to work together across silos and make efficiency a mandate.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Bureaucracy; Cross-agency collaboration; Process design and methodologies

COMMENT: Have a system in place that allows rank and file staff to reviews their supervisor and managers. Having good leadership allows staff to be more engaged in their work. How do you know the management/supervisor are fit and competent in their roles? The beauraracy  prevents meritocracy in that state, and sadly HR enables this as they will always side with management.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a system for staff to review their supervisors and managers to improve leadership quality and staff engagement.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee performance reviews; Internal feedback; Management culture and leadership approach

COMMENT: One of the challenges that needs further analysis is the replacement of staff when they move on from State Service. In most cases, departments are unable to fill vacancies until after the position becomes vacant. This leads to an exodus of knowledge and expertise from the department that is not easily replaced as it often takes years for an individual to become fully proficient in their role, establish connections with others and gain career long learning. While this practice may lead to salary savings, it creates a knowledge deficit which then leads to efficiency loss until such time as the individual staff person learns the position and becomes proficieint and beyond. If departments had the ability to hire replacement staff while the existing staff person was still present, the knowledge transfer would be more streamlined, and the institutional knowledge can be much more easily passed on to the new staff member. An in-between step might involve the creation of desk manuals for new staff to access however these will not necessarily impart the same level knowledge as the in person tutelage of the out-going staff member.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow departments to hire replacement staff before current employees leave. This would help transfer knowledge and skills from outgoing to incoming workers.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Hiring and recruitment; Knowledge management

COMMENT: DGS acts as the building department for state-owned facilities. If a department has a repair or modification that needs to be done, it must go through DGS and they will do the work themselves, contract out, or very rarely delegate the project back to departments. DGS charges an absurd amount for their overhead and project management costs, and takes several years to complete projects.  For example, our department needed a new HVAC unit that took DGS 4 years to complete and a new roof that took 5.  A private contractor can do this work for pennies on the dollar and in days vs years, saving the state millions.  Why can't DGS delegate some of this work to departments?  They say the government code requires them to do everything, but how long do we need to keep referring to these code sections without considering amendments to streamline government?  My department has had to lay off seasonal staff because DGS' costs kept going up and we couldn't afford to pay for a repair that was taking 3 years and also continue our operations.  And if DGS has vacancies, the work just sits there and doesn't get done until they hire and train more staff.  This is an INCREDIBLE waste of state resources.  Also, take a look at Unit 9's salaries.  The amount it costs to pay DGS for this work is WAY above what the state could be paying a private contractor, even at a prevailing wage.  Please streamline this process and allow/require DGS to delegate more facility projects to the departments!
IDEA_TEXT: Allow departments to handle their own facility repairs and modifications instead of requiring everything go through DGS. Change government code to let DGS delegate more projects to departments, which would save millions and reduce project times from years to days.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Governance and oversight; Budget and resources; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Bureaucracy; Org structure and hierarchy; Physical infrastructure; Process delays

COMMENT: Stop hiring employees for temporary projects only to have to keep them employed after the work is complete. Either utilize more limited-term positions or hiring more contractors.
IDEA_TEXT: Use limited-term positions or contractors for temporary projects instead of hiring permanent employees.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Limited-term positions

COMMENT: We should incorporate Airtable to facilitate communication between Headquarters and districts, keep track of large projects across the State, and have real-time data snapshots of each District. The main Headquarters base could have another base for each department, which would be able to inform other bases or be locked, and only editors or those given permission could view/use the data stored in that base. This model could be used for each District. Another good use at the District (or Headquarters) level is have a separate base for approved contractors within their district, so Maintenance, Contracts, and Purchasing could all see the same list, keep it updated, and confidently arrange services. This would allow Superintendents to review projects, adjust timelines, or give approval where needed. It would improve accountability and reduce the amount of separate apps required.
IDEA_TEXT: Use Airtable to improve communication between headquarters and districts. Create shared databases to track projects, store real-time data, and manage approved contractor lists that all departments can access and update.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Internal communication; Shared data; Software and tools

COMMENT: Work from home has made things more efficient, plain and simple. State of California employees have become more productive with working from home which started with COVID and should continue from here on out. It is an easy way for the state to save money while staying productive, an absolute no brainer.
IDEA_TEXT: Continue work from home policies that started during COVID to save money and keep staff productive.

IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: There is definitely room for streamlining the DGS one-time acquisitions process.  Departments must have DGS do their procurements if it's over their delegation.  It takes DGS several months to complete this process, even if it's for an emergency; DGS staff are unfamiliar with department nuances as they relate to their operations when needing a one-time purchase; and departments spend a ridiculous amount of time going back and forth answering basic questions from the one-time acquisitions staff as they try and learn about the need for the purchase, the department's business needs, etc. Why can't DGS let the departments still complete the purchasing process if it's over their delegation and just have DGS approve/execute the PO?  It would be faster for departments if they could just do it themselves, and DGS would still have oversight since they would need to execute the PO.  Also, if the purchase request goes through the DGS non-competitive bid process, why does DGS need to start from scratch and re-review the entire purchase from the one-time acquisitions unit?  If the NCB unit approves the purchase, isn't that good enough?  If an NCB is approved, can that just count as a delegation for the department to do the purchase instead of starting over again for one-time acquisitions to do it?  This makes purchases take several months when departments can do them in just a month or two. Sometimes these purchases are critical for operations and cause work delays until DGS approves.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow departments to complete purchases over their delegation limit themselves, with DGS only approving and executing the purchase order instead of handling the entire process.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Bureaucracy; Procurement

COMMENT: I think a lot of times there is so much work to be done but so few staff to actually complete it, which leads to errors and issues. Hiring more staff to complete all the required work and making sure that staff is trained on how to do their job properly (and trained to look out for and share places where redundancies can be removed) would go far to fix this issue.

COMMENT: in our present fiscal crisis, a consolidation of governmental agencies should occur to transfer expenditures to front line positions directly impacting and/or benefiting Californians. Consolidating departments and headquarters with similar, associated, or adjacent roles and/or responsibilities will consolidate headquarters locations and all associated Headquarters personnel positions. It will streamline operations to ensure the maximum number of fiscal resources are spent where it makes the most impact on Californians or on State Employees.    Supportive administrative positions should be carefully examined to see if an entry level administrative telework position could do the same work.  Offering those employees to reside in areas of the state with lower costs of living and eliminating their commute time and commute related expenses.  This would make the positions attractive to more prospective qualified employees and should reduce vacancies and amount of time vacancies remain unfilled.  Many agencies have duplicate departments or divisions and the agencies may operate in similar or adjacent fields, consolidation of the executive and managerial leadership, elimination of duplicate faculties, transition of appropriate administrative roles to more entry level telework classifications, will improve effectiveness and efficiency of operations, improve the quantity and quality of services delivered to Californians, and reduce budgetary spending for the state overall.
IDEA_TEXT: Combine duplicate departments across agencies. Merge executive leadership and eliminate duplicate facilities to improve services and reduce state spending.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Organizational structure and culture; Communication and knowledge sharing; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Consolidate government agencies; Cross-agency collaboration; Physical infrastructure

COMMENT: I regularly review processes as I go through various job functions. I regularly ask other people in my unit why we do certain things. Sometimes there isn't a reason for why we do certain things, or the reason is really outdated and no longer relevant so I work with my supervisor, manager, and team mates to update and streamline our processes. I make this a regular habit and it does lead to faster, more accurately completed and consistent work.

COMMENT:
Primary problem is communicating complex ideas and regulations that we work on to the general public. We need to figure out better communication channels and messaging, as well as ways to better process and organize the voluminous information that our state agencies process.

Possible solutions: exploring AI tools and technology to help organize, summarize and message complex ideas and processes to the public.

COMMENT:
Primary problem: I would like to see more Leadership Training.  I understand there are mandated hours of training (and have taken them) which covers management resposibility, performance management, understanding union representation etc.  I think tangible leadership skills would also be helpful.  Leading a team (of any size) can be overwhelming and staged training that covers various skills and topics would help any leader.
Solution:  I would have a learning path that needs to be completed in one year - since that is typical probation period.   The learning path would have a series of trainings that cover soft skills as well as the content currently covered.  I also think having a buddy that you could stay in contact with and bounce ideas off each other would be helpful so it is an ongoing type of training.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a one-year leadership training program with soft skills courses and a buddy system for new managers to complete during their probation period.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee retention; Employee training; Manager training

COMMENT: The recruitment process is very cumbersome. The "exams" are quite lengthy and sometimes don't fit with the achieved purpose of the job. Applicants learn how to "cheat" the exam process just to get through. That's why there's not a lot of quality. SOQ process should be revised as these could be cumbersome-- 1 question max. Also onboarding is not really onboarding. An employee doesn't learn much about their job because their unit is so short staffed, its usually baptism by fire and they're so thankful just to have somebody show up, they're just thrown into their role without the proper training period. State service should be step above and employees should be compensated to attract quality so that employees want to stay and reduce turnover in order to serve the people of CA. I would love to see value reinstated in state service.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve new employee training. Create proper onboarding programs instead of throwing new hires into jobs without training.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Onboarding new employees

All agencies are so large, there's no way a new employee would know what everybody does. I have also created materials to be used when our employees are interacting with the public. COMMENT:
Tackle all the departmental inefficiencies.
Remove all blocks, the lowest level employee should be able to point out/request that a process, no matter how small, be addressed. 
Just as an example: My daily job requires working on another State departments application. I need to contentiously clear a screen or remove my hands from the keyboard to move forward. It may only add minutes to my day but multiple that by everyone working on the app and 100s of hours wasted. If I want to request that application be updated, it can take hours of completing document, then it goes through no less than three of my superiors.
IDEA_TEXT: Remove barriers to process improvement requests. Allow any employee to easily suggest changes to work processes without going through multiple supervisors.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Communication and knowledge sharing

This website would help streamline the process, allowing us to communicate with technicians in the field, have accurate reporting, real time updates, and automated processes to free up mental capacity and time.
IDEA_TEXT: Use Airtable to track housing repairs. This would help departments stay updated on current and urgent issues, communicate with field technicians, provide real-time updates, and automate processes to save time.

COMMENT: I would like to see the California legislature make revisions to the victim compensation board laws to make it more equitable and modern. Particularly when it comes to mental health. There are so many available reimbursement sources that very few victims actually why not getting paid out compared to the number that are denied. There are a number of places where the program itself could make changes to regulation or their guidelines and policies to result in fewer denials. But I think the legislature and other governing agencies need to step in and ensure that they do so.
IDEA_TEXT: Update victim compensation board laws to make them more fair and modern, especially for mental health cases. Change rules and policies to reduce the number of claims that get denied.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Governance and oversight; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Align policy and implementation; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT:
Current departmental structures exhibit overlapping responsibilities, resulting in duplicated efforts and suboptimal resource utilization. To address these inefficiencies, a strategic reallocation of roles and responsibilities across departments is essential. By streamlining operations and eliminating redundancies, organizations can significantly enhance productivity and operational effectiveness.

For instance, rather than maintaining individual warehouses at each worksite, we could establish centralized regional warehouses supported by dedicated distribution lines to service the worksites. This approach would reduce logistical complexity, lower operational costs, and improve inventory management across locations.
IDEA_TEXT: Reorganize departments to remove overlapping duties and duplicate work. Create regional warehouses with delivery systems instead of separate warehouses at each worksite.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Organizational structure and culture; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Consolidate government agencies; Cross-agency collaboration; Physical infrastructure

COMMENT: I would like to see less concentration of jobs up in Headquarters. Southern California's needs are very different than Northern California and we need boots on the ground down here, like our own trainers, investigators, EEOP units, Civil Rights departments, etc. I would like to see to see a Division for every agency concentrated in SoCal. I find it difficult in my agency when training or investigations have to be funneled up through HQ and it creates a funnel of backlog to complete a request. We deserve to have our needs met as well.
IDEA_TEXT: Spread out state jobs to Southern California. Create regional divisions with local trainers, investigators, EEOP units, and Civil Rights departments to serve Southern California needs directly instead of routing everything through Northern California headquarters.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Governance and oversight; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Employee training; Org structure and hierarchy; Physical infrastructure

COMMENT: Legislators write laws to benefit their constituents (which is great) but without thought to how state agencies will be affected by these laws. Some of these issues have been noted in previous comments such as additional steps to a process that adds no value to the public and in fact delays work that would be of benefit. A possible solution is to get experts that work in the field (not HQ) from different agencies as an advisory committee to the legislature.
IDEA_TEXT: Create an advisory committee of field experts from different agencies to help lawmakers understand how new laws will affect state agencies before passing them.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Governance and oversight; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Align policy and implementation; Cross-agency collaboration; Policy development process

COMMENT:
I have been with the State for just over a year, after working in the private sector for most of my life (with a couple of temp government jobs here and there). The biggest issue that I noticed from day one is the amount of bureaucracy that envelops almost every aspect of how the State operates. I pretty much knew that going in, but when I actually experienced it I was kind of shocked. Everything is just so difficult! I'm used to having ownership of my role and various tasks and getting things handled quickly - not having to go through 15 people for something or having to wait 8 months for a PO and then supervisor approval and to only order something from one specific place, for example. Also, I get a lot of emails and phone calls from the public and it can be incredibly difficult trying to determine who to forward to. I often go to our website and sit there scrolling through trying to find the info myself, with much difficulty. 

Oh and the way people make payments online - the entire system needs to be overhauled. All of the phone calls I receive are either about duplicate payments on their credit card, or they couldn't log in to their account to make their payment. I then have to take down all their info and email my contacts to ask them to help. Takes a lot of time!

As one of my co-workers said when I first started: "It's about accountability, not efficiency!"
IDEA_TEXT: Improve website navigation and contact information. Make it easier for staff to find who to forward public inquiries to.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digital services; Internal communication

COMMENT: I think the Cannabis program is great, but we should reduce the current tax load and allow the businesses to thrive a bit more, they still can't compete with illegal sales because it's expensive at the store. I'm sure the revenue generated is a tremendous help, but if the legal industry collapses due to taxes, there goes the enforcement revenue.
IDEA_TEXT: Reduce taxes on legal cannabis businesses to help them compete with illegal sales and prevent the legal industry from collapsing.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: A primary problem I would like to address is the severe lack of communication. There are a number of reasons why there is a lack of communication, such as superiority complexes within management teams, lack of guidance from management teams, fear of retaliation, lack of adequate training, and so on. Because of this lack of communication, it is often hard to collaborate with one another, thus leading to frustration and complicated work processes. Also, it is greatly discouraging and prompts employees to put in minimum effort rather than their best effort. Communication is key in many aspects of life, especially work. If communication were more present in the workplace, employees would feel less stressed and more rewarded for the work that they do for the state of California.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve workplace communication. Address management issues like superiority complexes, provide better guidance and training, and reduce fear of retaliation to help staff work together better and feel more motivated.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Communication and knowledge sharing; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal communication; Management culture and leadership approach; Trust and openness; Work culture

COMMENT:
Allowing DocuSign and paperless processing had speed up so many procedures.
No longer printing Direct Deposit
IDEA_TEXT: Use DocuSign and paperless processing to speed up procedures and eliminate printing for direct deposit.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Software and tools

COMMENT: As a manager, we have to remember to value our staff as well as our customers. Sometimes this can be lost in the statistics of the job, showing that measures haven't been met. I have tried to find a way to make it a positive outlook instead of consistently negative. Staff should feel motivated to provide the utmost customer service since our customers are the public. That can be hard for some that constantly get negative attitudes from the public and then negative evaluations on productivity. At Fiscal Year-end statistics, I have compared the prior year statistics to the recently completed year and show the improvement in percentage of reaching our goal instead of just the negative comment that we didn't reach our goal again for another year. I think this helps the staff stay engaged and motivated to attempt to meet the goal in the coming year.  There is always a reminder that we are a public service agency, and we are here to serve the public and educate them on the information they need.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve staff motivation by focusing on positive progress instead of just unmet goals. Compare current year stats to prior year and show improvement percentages rather than only highlighting failures.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee performance reviews; Management culture and leadership approach; Work culture

COMMENT:
I would like to standardize templates and basic duties across agencies and departments.  Having to relearn the job that you were already doing elsewhere because the culture is different is inefficient.  Provide a standard writing style guide for Contracts and Procurements across the state. 

Invest in a statewide procurement solution that all departments have access to. A way to track work, control who edits, and route for approvals. Maybe even tie it into the signature processes.  Something that would work for an analyst working in a small shop under 10 people as well as the large departments of thousands. 

Ensure consistent and appropriate levels of training. This promotes the transferability of skills and ensures a baseline for promotions.

Provide information on how analysts can successfully promote through the ranks. Workforce development stops with Executive Assistants, Secretaries and Office Technicians. Analysts and other employees would like assistance and a clear, step by step process for how to promote within the state.  Offering Interviewing training as a part of the career growth. 

Offer advanced analyst positions above AGPA or Research Analyst that does not require moving into management. Often people are subject matter experts, but that does not always transfer into leadership capabilities.
IDEA_TEXT: Create advanced analyst positions above AGPA or Research Analyst that do not require moving into management roles.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Career growth; Job classification

COMMENT:
Providing a detailed and department wide Contract Manager training. We need something like that statewide. It should be mandatory for anyone managing contracts so that they are aware of what they are expected to do. What the duties are for the contract managers versus the contract analysts.  How Contract managers can set up a budget, track invoices, and develop a scope of work. 

This is provided for Contract analysts. There are options through DGS, but the messaging isn't out there to provide the training needed for most Contract Managers to be successful and compliant.
IDEA_TEXT: Create mandatory statewide contract manager training. Include budget setup, invoice tracking, scope of work development, and role differences between contract managers and analysts.

COMMENT:
I believe that telework has improved efficiency and morale. It helps balance work and life. It provides opportunities for the disabled community to contribute to the State when they might not be able to otherwise.  It helps improve the environment. It has been shown over the past 5 years to be an effective option. 

I would also like to see term limits set into the California Constitution for politicians. I would also like to see the politicians make what the average person in their district makes with the same benefits. That way, if they don't like it, they are more motivated to make the necessary changes to improve it.
IDEA_TEXT: Add term limits for politicians to the California Constitution.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public policy initiatives

COMMENT:
The California Department of Corrections and Rehabilitation (CDCR) operates multiple firehouses across the state, but currently lacks a cohesive and standardized organizational framework. 

To enhance operational efficiency and emergency preparedness, the department would benefit from implementing a clear chain of command. This could include appointing a Sacramento-based Fire Chief to oversee statewide operations, supported by Regional Division Chiefs and Battalion Chiefs stationed at each firehouse. Battalion Chiefs would supervise Fire Captains and ensure consistent training, coordination, and readiness. 

Establishing this structure would significantly improve the department’s ability to respond effectively to emergencies, particularly during statewide crises
IDEA_TEXT: Create a clear chain of command for CDCR firehouses. Appoint a Sacramento-based Fire Chief to oversee statewide operations, with Regional Division Chiefs and Battalion Chiefs at each firehouse to supervise Fire Captains and ensure consistent training and coordination.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Governance and oversight; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Management culture and leadership approach; Org structure and hierarchy

COMMENT: A mentor program for first year or new employees who come into state service. Learning how the state functions, how long it takes for certain tasks or forms to be implemented can be daunting and confusing. Having a mentor or buddy to walk new employees on what to expect would be a great way to increase turn over and have employees engage with each other in meaningful ways.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a mentor program for new state employees. Pair new hires with experienced staff to help them learn how the state works and what to expect.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Mentorship programs; Onboarding new employees

COMMENT:
1.) Keep and Embrace the hybrid working environment. I don't need to elaborate on how bad traffic is in California. I'm lucky that I have an easy commute to work using Public Trans, but not everyone does. Gas and bridge tolls are insane! Having a long commute to and from work is not good for mental health, child/pet care, etc.
2.) Make starting and operating a business easier! Streamline the processes and cut back on all the lengthy fees, taxes and permits one must obtain. There is a reason so many businesses are exiting California - pay attention to it!
3.) I am a 4th Gen California native and it's been increasingly hard to sustain here - especially over the last 2-3 years. The amount of taxes I (and others) pay is not proportionate.
IDEA_TEXT: Keep hybrid work options to reduce commute costs and improve work-life balance.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: I would like to see my department utilize more up to date ways to share information. My department still has to produce binders with documentation for auditors who have access to our data and they have a SharePoint upload documentation. It would save hours of staff's time and save on supplies and environment. There are times we make 13 4" binders filled with documentation that they have already and can view before coming for the audit.
IDEA_TEXT: Replace paper binders with digital document sharing. Use SharePoint or other online systems to share audit documents instead of printing multiple binders.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Software and tools; State cloud storage

COMMENT: As a state employee, I see one primary problem in the way new employees orientation is conducted. currently, NEO often requires multiple stae employees to physically present material, which uses valuable time and resources.  This approach could be modernized and made more efficient.  My idea is to fully diitize the onboarding process.  Core orienation content could be presented throug standardized, prerecorded modules that employees can watch, ensuring consistency across the state.  to keep it interactive, new employees should be able to submit questions digitally during the session.  a designated proctor could collect the questions and forward them to the appropriate department for follow up by way of email.  This solution would save both time and money.
IDEA_TEXT: Modernize new employee orientation by creating digital training modules. Use pre-recorded videos for core content and allow new hires to submit questions digitally during sessions for follow-up by email.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Employee training; Onboarding new employees

COMMENT: The issues I see is those of every Californians are struggling with every day which are own/rent housing costs, grocery prices, gas, and taxes. Every year for the last 5 years people is moving due to these prices going higher every year. I have kids and family to take care of and when I have to go get another job when working at the state level there is a problem. There used to be a saying that if we got into the state we would be set to be able to live comfortably and provide for our loved ones and that's not true anymore. When I get a raise, something always goes up and takes it from me as if I never got it. To be able to buy anything whether house or car they are taxed to the roof and prices never go down. If they do something else goes up. California needs to be affordable to be able to thrive, more policing, and use of more natural resources that California has had for decades. You ask what ideas I have to solve these problems I address, well start thinking like the bottom person and American citizens of this state that are struggling to eat and live and put policies that create more jobs, businesses, and programs that help people. Less to those that don't deserve handouts or free assistance on the taxpayer's dime. Thank you
IDEA_TEXT: Increase policing to improve public safety.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public policy initiatives

You ask what ideas I have to solve these problems I address, well start thinking like the bottom person and American citizens of this state that are struggling to eat and live and put policies that create more jobs, businesses, and programs that help people. Less to those that don't deserve handouts or free assistance on the taxpayer's dime. Thank you
IDEA_TEXT: Use more of California's natural resources that have been available for decades.

COMMENT:
Problem: Each department that has public-facing programs has its own database and systems to identify and serve people. A person must provide repetitive information to each department they interact with (e.g., FTB, DMV, EDD). Each department spends time and money verifying the identity of each person they interact with. 

Solution: Create a centralized portal & database for the public to access all of the services provided by the state. A person gets one access point to see what's going on with all of their different interactions with the state. The departments get a source of verified people to whom they can provide their services.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a single online portal where residents can access all state services. Build a shared database so people only need to verify their identity once across all departments like FTB, DMV, and EDD.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Digital services; Shared data; Software and tools

COMMENT: Modernization of California's government is the best way to be more effective and efficient. There's so much waste and excessive bureaucracy that keep our constituents from the programs and benefits that they need and already pay for with their tax dollars. Telework, for example, frees up hundreds of millions of dollars to be spent on social programs instead, and yet for some reason we're clinging to the past of an office mindset from 40 years ago. CalFresh eligibility and paper applications and office visits, something I've seen firsthand, are incredibly difficult for people to get through. The application and reverification process that the Housing Choice Voucher program created online is incredibly effective and simple to use. It would be great to see all customer facing departments use a simple, online system that removes barriers and hurdles between the public and their benefits.
IDEA_TEXT: Create simple online systems for all customer-facing departments to remove barriers between the public and their benefits, similar to the Housing Choice Voucher program's online application system.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digital services; Public policy initiatives; Public service delivery and responsiveness

It is impossible to keep track of all the changes - a smart portal where an employee could submit their need, and receive a list of all the measures that need to be met would be helpful.

COMMENT: If a department is customer facing and requires information from them for approval or reverification, etc., and the customer is still having to fill out paper forms, then that department is about 20 years behind the times. The Housing Choice Voucher program relatively recently overhauled their yearly reverification process, and the online portal that utilizes digital forms and signatures has made the process exceedingly easy for the public to use. I have to applaud them for finally modernizing. Coming from the private sector to the public sector, I was able to bring many updated IT processes to the multiple departments that I've worked for, but nothing has been more effective and efficient than decentralization of our teams and workgroups through technology like Teams, SharePoint and other connectivity programs that allow us to more efficiently work outside of an office and keep productivity locked to a single location. This is how things have to change to keep pace with the modern world; CA government needs to embrace the change from a militaristic bureaucracy with "butts in seats" policies and a "because I told you so," leadership style, to a collaborative team environment that allows everyone to do their best work from where they work best.
IDEA_TEXT: Change from strict office-based policies to flexible team environments where staff can work from where they are most productive.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Organizational structure and culture; Workplace operations; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership approach; Remote work and return to office; Work culture
