COMMENT:
I would want to address the career promotional process. There are very talented and eligible people who are often overlooked because of managment bias toward a friend or particular ethnic demographic to fill a position.

The promotional process, though intended to be standardized, is still manipulated by hiring managers for what appears to be affirmative action reasons. I would like to see the political bias removed and the practice of hiring/promoting be solely based on merit of work performed and abilities demonstrated.
IDEA_TEXT: Remove bias from hiring and promotion decisions. Base all hiring and promotion choices only on job performance and skills shown, not on personal relationships or demographics.

COMMENT:
Public transit could be vastly improved by expanding our light rail and bus routes and funding additional maintenance for both. 

I would also love to see more single family homes built instead of even more apartment housing. We need affordable options for those who want to purchase a home that are not gigantic 5 bed 6 bath McMansions with no yard to speak of.  Bring back smaller homes that are not apartments or condos!
IDEA_TEXT: Build more small single-family homes that are affordable alternatives to large expensive houses and apartment complexes.

COMMENT: We absolutely need more frequent, safer public transportation. If it weren't for telework, it would take 2 hours and 34 minutes via 2 buses and a train to get to my office (which was closer when I joined the department but moved during the pandemic). That's absolutely ridiculous and impractical to expect anyone to endure a 5 hour commute every day. I'd also love to see more clean energy incentives. I recently looked into getting solar for my home and would be happy to feed extra energy into the grid to make it cheaper for everyone, but after everything was said and done, with current incentives, I would pay more for solar than I would for my current SMUD bill. We also need to nationalize PG&E. They are doing a lot of harm in their pricing to our constituents.
IDEA_TEXT: Expand clean energy incentives to make solar power more affordable for residents.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Environmental sustainability; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: After seven years with Caltrans, my problem is with private contractors. Since we are forced to award contracts to the lowest bidders on projects, we are constantly saddled with mediocre companies on multi-year projects. These companies often cause delays through dangerous practices, poor execution, and corner-cutting to save money. I propose the State implements a "blacklist" whereby demonstrably bad contractors are prohibited from bidding on some or all projects for a set time period, or until they can prove they are capable of greater responsibility.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a contractor blacklist system. Ban bad contractors from bidding on state projects for a set time or until they prove they can do better work.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Procurement

COMMENT: I would address knowledge transfer. People are so concerned with getting promoted quickly, that they don't take the time to learn the job. Since they can't get promoted in their field, they go into supervision. Then you have people who supervise/manage and review the work, who frankly can't do the work themselves.  Then they promote people who aren't qualified to do the work at that level (because they don't understand the work and what it means to get to the next level), and before you know it, you have everyone at the top of the earning ladder and no one who can adequatley do the work. Promotion is important, but they should be merit based, because you demonstrate a higher level of compentancy. And those at lower levels should accept knowlege transfer from people who have put in the time to learn and quit being focused on being at the highest level without having demonstrated that level of competency.
IDEA_TEXT: Make promotions based on merit and job skills. Require staff to show they can do higher-level work before getting promoted. Create better knowledge sharing between experienced workers and newer staff.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Career growth; Employee performance reviews; Employee training; Knowledge management

COMMENT:
Guidance and access to the guidance and processes. Also, the lack of knowledge sharing among subject matter experts (SME) or not knowing who the SME is. 

Example: We need to obtain signatures from our chain of command for approval of an item. Sometimes the need for wet/real signatures is required and sometimes electronic signatures are acceptable.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve access to guidance and processes. Create a system to identify and connect staff with subject matter experts. Clarify when wet signatures versus electronic signatures are required for approvals.

COMMENT:
To build a more effective and future-ready State workforce, we must address outdated systems that hinder collaboration, innovation, and talent development.

Leadership Culture
The traditional chain of command may suit high-risk environments, but in civil service, it often stifles collaboration and trust. Leadership based solely on tenure can lead to silos, disengagement, and missed opportunities for innovation. Incorporating 360-degree peer reviews and influence-based leadership models would promote accountability, humility, and a culture of shared success.

Classification System
Our current classification structure, designed for factory-style work, doesn’t reflect the dynamic, cross-functional nature of today’s roles. It limits mobility, professional growth, and alignment with modern competency-based hiring practices. A shift toward a flexible, skills-based framework would better support recruitment, retention, and continuous improvement.

Technology Barriers
Outdated systems discourage younger, tech-savvy professionals from joining the State. Investing in modern, user-friendly tools is essential to attracting talent and signaling that innovation is a priority.

These changes are critical to creating a more agile, inclusive, and responsive public sector.
IDEA_TEXT: Change the job classification system from rigid categories to a flexible skills-based framework that supports career growth and modern hiring.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Career growth; Job classification; Qualified staff

COMMENT: As a state employee we are always told things are out of budget. I work in law enforcement and other agencies charge for release forms (an impound for unlicensed driver) they charge up to $500. My agency does not charge for release forms, but we could. Even if we charged $50 for a storage and $100 for an impound we would make a ton of money.
IDEA_TEXT: Start charging fees for release forms. Charge $50 for storage and $100 for impounds to generate revenue for the agency.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Permits, licensing, and fees

COMMENT: I've noticed problems with engagement in one's work. I've seen employees "work the system" to take lots of time off or get out of certain projects/duties. Some employees seem very bored and unmotivated, and as a result put out mediocre work. One way to improve this would be focusing on colleague relations, i.e., allow or encourage people to socialize and create a sense of community in the office (within appropriate boundaries, of course). If we all knew each other better personally and/or professionally, people may feel more motivated and part of a team that puts out good work. As someone who is newer to state service, I know this is difficult and often discouraged for a reason; however, allowing an environment where a lack of communication and respect for coworkers to flourish is detrimental.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve workplace community and teamwork. Allow and encourage staff to socialize within proper limits to build better relationships and motivation.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal communication; Trust and openness; Work culture

COMMENT: My department hears out new ideas from anyone, employees and supervisors alike. I have made many suggestions to improve processes and provide better customer service, which have been noted and/or implemented by my department. Providing this atmosphere of openness to new ideas is great for getting better work done!
IDEA_TEXT: Create an open environment for new ideas. Listen to suggestions from all employees and supervisors, then implement good ideas to improve work processes and customer service.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Organizational structure and culture; Management culture and leadership; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal feedback; Management culture and leadership approach; Trust and openness; Work culture

COMMENT: Perception. Often times there is a negative public perception of State Employees and the work we do. The public sometimes feels like State Employees are not in touch with reality or the types of issues they are facing. I feel this could be improved by being more human in public interactions, being honest and transparent, even if it is not a positive topic, and by having more consistent training for all divisions within the enterprise, allowing for more educated and positive interactions between the public and State Employees, regardless of who they are interacting with. By having honest, educated, open interactions, even if both sides do not agree, they can understand, which can lead to acceptance. This would also apply internally between various levels of leadership and staff.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve public perception of state workers. Make interactions more human, honest, and transparent. Provide consistent training across all divisions so staff can have better educated conversations with the public and with each other.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Communication and knowledge sharing; Governance and oversight; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Employee training; Internal communication; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT: Some things I have done to improve processes and provide customer service is within my education and outreach efforts. While working with internal and external state partners, I have created an open communication process and partnership through my interactions with them by allowing for honest dialouge and by being transparent. It has created a stronger partnership with a common goal, and has allowed for concerns or requests to be completed timely and accurately, with everyone's input. I have also done this with my staff. It has helped build trust and a positive report, allowing for more buy in and commitment to our shared goals, as we are all on the same team with a common goal and everyone has input and ownership.
IDEA_TEXT: Create open communication and partnerships with internal and external partners. Allow honest dialogue, be transparent, and include everyone's input to build trust and shared ownership of goals.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Internal communication; Internal feedback; Trust and openness

COMMENT: The line from "issues in the community colleges" to "community college legislation" seems to be missing a critical piece from the field, particularly implementation timelines and an embedded accountability or tracking mechanism. COMMENT: The State has a penny-wise pound-foolish approach to promotions. "Promotions in place" are impossible to attain. The only way to be promoted is to find an open position req in a different department. I understand that we want to be good stewards of public funds, that we want to avoid even the appearance of nepotism or corruption, and that we don't want to promote without need. And there have been very public instances of nepotism and corruption in hiring and promotions. But the current system forces people who want a long-term career in public service to leave their departments to move up, taking all their "tribal knowledge" with them. It forces departments to cannibalize each another. The system does not consider the value of the months, or years, it takes to train the next person who eventually fills that loss. That value must be given more weight. And if a department is full of long-term people punching above their weight, there should be some sort of extenuating circumstances to make a PIP easier. It's worth it to promote people for their knowledge and ability once in a while, to encourage more people to consider long-term public sector careers.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow promotions within departments without requiring staff to move to different departments. Give more weight to the value of keeping experienced staff and their knowledge. Make it easier to promote people based on their skills and knowledge to encourage long-term public service careers.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Career growth; Employee pay and benefits; Employee retention; Job classification

COMMENT: one issue is the lack of communication to the general public about processes and steps individuals take to access services/programs.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve communication with the public about how to access government services and programs.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public participation; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT: Yes, Departments aren't sharing ideas on how to align and make the platforms for timesheets. Department of Technology, CalHHS and others have online platforms for timesheets and yet other Departments are stuck on still using filling out paper document.
IDEA_TEXT: Share timesheet platform ideas between departments. Have Department of Technology, CalHHS and other departments with online timesheet systems help departments still using paper forms switch to digital platforms.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Budget and resources; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Digitize processes; Shared resources

COMMENT: Poor communication is a huge problem.  Managers sometimes have zero training on how to effectively manage and communicate with their employees.  And the example and leadership really needs to start at the top.  Managers shouldn't be throwing curve balls and wrenches into things when they are not really, fully involved.  You should come in like a lamb and leave like a lion, not the other way around.  The latter causes confusion, chaos, frustration, and the former cultivates respect, efficiency, knowledge, and experience.
IDEA_TEXT: Provide management training. Train managers on how to communicate and lead staff effectively, starting with top leadership setting a good example.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership approach; Manager training

COMMENT: I believe that the processes that are currently in place with DGS are far too cumbersome and require too much time to work through. I believe we need to streamline processes and work towards getting things done rather than figure out what road blocks can go up to slow down the process.
IDEA_TEXT: Streamline DGS processes. Remove roadblocks and reduce time needed to complete tasks.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Bureaucracy; Digitize processes; Process design and methodologies

COMMENT: One of the primary problems as a state employee is being subjected to the whims of ever-changing political climates. It often feels like state worker's voices are ignored by those who hold our future in their hands. Example: RTO to appease landlords despite evidence that it is a waste of time, space, and money. Solution: Listen to state workers, believe the data, stand behind your employees. I believe that California is consistently at the forefront of progress for many causes. It is what makes me proud to live here. We should focus on the big picture ahead of us. What do we want our world to look like? I would like to see a world that is fighting against climate change. A world that challenges the existing 40-hour work week status quo. I would like to see a world that utilizes technology to better the lives of human beings instead of increasing profits. How can California lead the way to that world? TELEWORK.
IDEA_TEXT: Expand telework programs to fight climate change, challenge the 40-hour work week, and use technology to help people instead of just making profits.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Environmental sustainability; Remote work and return to office; Technology and data modernization

IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Communication and knowledge sharing; Technology and digital innovation

COMMENT: One primary problem at the California Highway Patrol is our use of physical mailing services like the USPS and FedEX. Our department's policies often require original signed documents to be snail-mailed or overnighted to our headquarters or division offices. Money spent on these mailing services could be avoided by switching to uploading and emailing scanned documents. The money saved could really benefit our department by using that money to increase the salaries of some of the lower paying clerical jobs such as the Office Assistant and Office Technician classifications. Our office has experienced volatile position turn-around because we don't make a livable wage working in these classifications. So, it's really frustrating to see vast amounts of money being spent on sending papers in the mail when that money could be used to better the lives of the people working in the offices throughout our department. Our policies on utilizing mailing services needs to be changed.
IDEA_TEXT: Switch from mailing physical documents to uploading and emailing scanned documents. Use the money saved to increase salaries for lower-paying clerical jobs like Office Assistant and Office Technician.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Process improvement; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Digitize processes; Employee pay and benefits

COMMENT: I work for a department with an outright BAN on the use of generative artificial intelligence (AI). I feel that this is a huge missed opportunity to improve public service and also efficiencies in day-to-day work. CalLearns has an amazing training, showing a variety of ways that AI can directly benefit public government organizations.  California State government should lean into AI, with more training, and introduction of AI tools in the workplace. The risks should be acknowledged and understood and mitigated rather than prohibiting AI altogether.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow and expand use of artificial intelligence (AI) tools in California state government. Provide more AI training through programs like CalLearns and introduce AI workplace tools while managing risks instead of banning AI completely.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: AI and large language models; Employee training; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT: The state needs to stop spending money on sending new hires to train at facilities that are far from their home office, especially for lower income classifications like the Office Assistant and Office Technician. Although these travel trips for trainings are exciting and refreshing, the return on investment is not enough to justify training trips. In most circumstances, lower paying classifications would benefit more from having a higher salary rather than going on a state sponsored training trip. Office Assistants and Office Technicians are more likely to quit or leave for higher paying jobs because these classifications do not make livable wages. California is expensive to live independently and there is more value in paying these classifications more, and less value in paying for travel and training expenses when employees in these classifications are highly likely to change jobs for something that pays more. The citizens of California would benefit more from having higher paying entry-level jobs in these classifications. Most people don't care about going on a state sponsored training trip when they are going to come back to a mediocre salary. They'll just leave for a higher paying job, thus resulting in funding and resources being wasted on that employee.
IDEA_TEXT: Stop sending new hires in lower-paying jobs like Office Assistant and Office Technician to training facilities far from their home office. Instead, increase salaries for these entry-level positions to help with retention and provide more value to California residents.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Employee retention; Employee training

COMMENT: The Administration DEFINITELY needs to take a look at all the reports that DGS requires of departments.  Every month it seems like there's another report that's needed, that requires tons of research and data collection from staff and facilities all over the state.  What are they used for?  They are very labor-intensive, and it seems like a lot of these reports just pull and compile information DGS already has in their fleet and facility databases.  Some of these reports, like the sustainability roadmap, take SO much time.  It probably costs each department at least a couple staff worth of people just to handle DGS' reporting requirements, and these are resources that could otherwise be providing services to taxpayers and fee payers. DGS should make a list of all the reports they require, do an analysis of what each one is used for and whether or not there's even a benefit, see where some can be consolidated or can be taken over by DGS since they already have the data, and then free those resources up so departments can better serve the public instead.
IDEA_TEXT: Review and streamline DGS reporting requirements. Make a list of all required reports, analyze their purpose and benefits, consolidate duplicate reports, and have DGS handle reports using data they already have instead of requiring departments to collect it.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Bureaucracy; Digitize processes; Process design and methodologies; Process documentation

COMMENT: The state is pushing departments to contract with and purchase from small businesses and disabled veterans, which is great.  However, there should be a different set of requirements they need to comply with in order to do business with the state to make it worthwhile for them. We try to contract with SB/DVBE vendors but they don't want to do business with the state since it requires them to have a $1 million insurance policy.  That's a super expensive cost for them to do business, so how are we helping them by requiring this and telling them we're giving them an advantage?  If we want to help them out, DGS really needs to look at how that can be done. It's unfortunate that government gets in the way of them being successful when at the same time government is bragging about how much they're helping them.
IDEA_TEXT: Create different insurance requirements for small businesses and disabled veteran business enterprises. Lower the $1 million insurance policy requirement that prevents these vendors from working with the state.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT:
As both a Californian and a state employee, I strongly support the continuation and expansion of telework. From my perspective, telework directly addresses the two goals of this discussion: increasing efficiency within state government and improving service to the public.

When state employees telework, we eliminate hours of commuting time each week. That reclaimed time translates into more energy and focus for our work, rather than starting the day already drained from traffic. Telework also reduces overhead costs for departments, with less need for office space, utilities, and parking reimbursements. Those savings could be redirected toward technology, staffing, or public programs that improve government services.

Telework allows employees to be more responsive to the public. With modern tools such as secure cloud systems, videoconferencing, and shared databases, most services can be delivered as effectively, if not more so, remotely. Constituents rarely care whether the employee helping them is in a cubicle or a home office. They care about getting timely, accurate, and respectful service. By avoiding commute-related stress and delays, employees are better equipped to provide exactly that.
IDEA_TEXT: Continue and expand telework programs. Use secure cloud systems, video calls, and shared databases to let staff work from home and serve the public better while saving money on office space and utilities.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Workplace operations; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public service delivery and responsiveness; Remote work and return to office; State cloud storage; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT:
One of the primary problems I would address is the state’s resistance to fully embracing telework, despite clear evidence that it benefits both employees and the public. For decades, Californians have struggled with long commutes, congested freeways, and poor air quality. As a state employee, I see firsthand how commuting drains productivity, raises costs for workers, and contributes to pollution that affects every Californian. Forcing employees back into offices undermines efficiency, increases taxpayer costs for office space and reimbursements, and contradicts the state’s own climate goals.

The solution is simple: expand and normalize telework as the default option whenever the duties of a position can be performed remotely. This requires modernizing policies, investing in secure technology, and measuring performance based on outcomes instead of physical presence. Employees should be trusted to deliver results, and in my experience, productivity and responsiveness actually increase when telework is supported. By keeping more workers at home, California can cut emissions, save money, and provide faster, more effective services to residents. Telework is not just a perk. It is a strategy that addresses climate change, budget efficiency, and the quality of public service all at once.
IDEA_TEXT: Expand telework as the default option for jobs that can be done remotely. Update policies, invest in secure technology, and measure performance based on results instead of office attendance.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Workplace operations; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership approach; Remote work and return to office; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT: Strongly recommend investment in broad LEAN6sigma training for continuous quality improvement. Staff who work with the complex bureaucratic processes every day should be empowered to find ways to make them more efficient. Lots of examples in many comments, including hiring, ordering supplies, repairing equipment.
IDEA_TEXT: Invest in LEAN Six Sigma training for all staff. Empower workers to improve daily processes like hiring, ordering supplies, and equipment repair.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Organizational structure and culture; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Employee training; Process design and methodologies; Work culture

COMMENT: Why does DGS not consider a UC/CSU to be a state agency when it comes to interagency contracts?  One department can contract with another and they can do so up to $2 million without DGS approval. Why isn't UC/CSU part of this delegation, instead of having the same delegation limits as outside contractors?  The state is going to contract with them no matter what, but not giving them the same level of delegation just means the process takes longer and the contract has to sit at DGS Office of Legal Services waiting for approval.  Seems like a waste of state resources that can better be used elsewhere.  Why are taxpayers paying for DGS to see if the state can do work for itself?
IDEA_TEXT: Give UC and CSU systems the same contract approval limits as other state agencies. Allow them to contract up to $2 million without DGS approval instead of treating them like outside contractors.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Procurement

COMMENT: Expansion of public transportation to state government satellite offices, such as those in Elk Grove, may reduce the traffic and congestion.
IDEA_TEXT: Expand public transportation to state government satellite offices like Elk Grove to reduce traffic and congestion.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public policy initiatives; Public transportation

COMMENT:
As a Californian and a state employee, I have seen how telework creates opportunities to improve efficiency and customer service. In my own work, I have streamlined processes that once depended on paper files, in-person signatures, or lengthy office meetings. By shifting these tasks online and coordinating virtually, I eliminated delays and reduced errors that came from moving documents between offices. This allowed staff to focus more on completing core work instead of chasing paperwork, which directly improved turnaround times for the public.

Another example is customer service. When working remotely, I am able to respond more quickly to inquiries because I am not losing hours each week to commuting. I can give residents my full attention without the stress and fatigue of traffic, which means they get clearer answers and faster resolutions. Many constituents rely on timely help, and telework enables me to deliver that without sacrificing quality.

Telework also fosters accountability. Using shared databases, secure online platforms, and video calls, collaboration with colleagues remains strong. In fact, it often becomes more intentional and focused. The ability to concentrate without the constant interruptions of an office environment has increased my productivity and accuracy.
IDEA_TEXT: Expand telework options for state employees. Move paper-based processes online, use virtual meetings instead of in-person meetings, and allow remote work to reduce commute time and improve customer service response times.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Process improvement; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Public service delivery and responsiveness; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: As a Californian, I believe one of the best ideas is to fully embrace telework as a long-term strategy. Fewer commuters on the road mean less traffic, cleaner air, and reduced greenhouse gas emissions, which directly supports California’s climate goals. Telework also saves taxpayer money by lowering the need for large office spaces, utilities, and parking reimbursements. From my experience as a state employee, productivity improves when staff are trusted to work from home, and residents receive faster, more focused service. Expanding telework is not just an employee benefit, it is a cost-effective, environmentally responsible way to improve how the state serves its people.
IDEA_TEXT: Fully embrace telework as a long-term strategy. This reduces traffic, improves air quality, cuts greenhouse gas emissions, saves taxpayer money on office space and utilities, and improves productivity and service to residents.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Public-facing services and resident experience; Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Environmental sustainability; Physical infrastructure; Public service delivery and responsiveness; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: IT projects typically follow one of two methodologies -- waterfall or agile. Waterfall is great for smaller projects when one can reasonably estimate the scope, time, and resources needed to complete a project. Agile is better when two of those items are a little fuzzy because the project is large, but it's the only way to get the project to succeed. I have seen projects fail because state control agencies attempt to run projects one way while the department they are overseeing wants to do it the other way. First, CDT needs to have clearly defined approaches for both methodologies. Second, as part of the pre-work (e.g., FSR part), there needs to be an open discussion between CDT and the organization that is undertaking the project about which methodology should be used for the project to level-set expectations. Finally, as part of the FSR process, CDT should insist on receiving an entity-attribute diagram for the data that is passing through the system, so that they know that the IT team understands what it is that they are trying to build. All three of these points will reduce the risk of IT project failure.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve IT project success by creating clear rules for waterfall and agile methods. Have CDT and departments agree on which method to use before starting projects. Require data diagrams during the planning process so IT teams understand what they are building.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Communication and knowledge sharing; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Process design and methodologies; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT: Taking paper, faxes, and Adobe PDF forms out of business processes and collecting data via secure web forms reduces the number of errors. That may not work for every business process, but, where it does, it eliminates unwanted queues and manual data re-entry. Complaints go way down when you do that, plus you often save  money.
IDEA_TEXT: Replace paper forms, faxes, and Adobe PDF forms with secure web forms to reduce errors, eliminate queues, cut manual data re-entry, reduce complaints, and save money.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Technology and digital innovation; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Public service delivery and responsiveness; Software and tools

COMMENT: CA Lottery is a highly inefficient tax on the statistically challenged. Make lottery winnings taxable and earmark those revenues for a higher child tax deduction, especially for younger kids.
IDEA_TEXT: Make lottery winnings taxable and use that money for higher child tax deductions, especially for younger children.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT:
Direct supervisors should have more leeway in managing the day to day of their teams. All workers work differently and have different needs that impact productivity. When decisions around the workday are made at the top as blanket mandates, it heavily affects team morale and ultimately impacts hiring and retention. These decisions tie the hands of the supervisors that know the needs of their team best.   When turnover is high and hiring is slow, it impacts the entire team and service to taxpayers is slowed down heavily. 

As the largest economy in the country and the State with the strongest worker protections, we should be leading the way in showing the rest of the country that people should always come first and that workers that are passionate, dedicated, and productive don’t always fit into boxes built at the top, nor should they have to. 

The solution I propose would be to allow supervisors and teams that have proven track records to have more leeway in decisions around the workday. This would improve morale, increase talent available for hiring, increase retention, and put the focus on results for taxpayers over arbitrary outdated ideas around work.
IDEA_TEXT: Give supervisors more control over daily team management. Allow proven supervisors and teams to make flexible work decisions instead of using top-down blanket rules.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Organizational structure and culture; Workplace operations; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership approach; Remote work and return to office; Trust and openness

COMMENT: Retention and recruitment is a major concern as departments are quite dependent on experienced staff and their institutional knowledge.  Constant turnover can be disruptive to program needs and if staff are just using entry level positions as just a way to get their foot in the door, supervisors can't make any long term plans to improve processes.  There needs to be more promotional opportunities and analyst positions to motivate staff to stay within their departments.
IDEA_TEXT: Create more promotion opportunities and analyst positions to keep experienced staff in their departments.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Career growth; Employee retention

COMMENT:
1. Hiring Process
It is my understanding that Directors and Deputy Directors may not go through HR & that reference checks are not always conducted. If that is the case, I believe reference checks should be required for all candidates. Additionally, I believe the current scoring process could be improved. In some cases, employees with many years of service in a department are not selected, which can be very discouraging. I suggest eliminating the scoring system and limiting interview panels to no more than two members to help ensure fairness and consistency.
2. Supervisor Evaluation
For employees who are not in supervisory roles, I recommend implementing a survey every six months to provide feedback on their supervisor. These evaluations could be reviewed by the CEA to help identify areas of improvement. I have observed that not all individuals in supervisory positions possess the people skills necessary for effective leadership, and this type of feedback could support their professional development.
3. Open Communication and Feedback
All employees, regardless of level, should feel comfortable sharing ideas or raising concerns about their department without fear of retaliation. To strengthen accountability, departments should provide feedback within five business days of receiving employee input. Too often, employees are told that leadership will “get back to them,” but no follow-up occurs. 
4. When hired at a new department proper onboarding should be provided.
IDEA_TEXT: Create supervisor feedback system. Survey non-supervisory staff every six months about their supervisor and have CEA review results to identify improvement areas.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee performance reviews; Internal feedback; Manager training

COMMENT: I have expressed ideas on keeping employees happy at work that not everything is black and white just because we work for the state.
IDEA_TEXT: Recognize that work situations are not always black and white to keep state employees happy.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Organizational structure and culture; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership approach; Trust and openness; Work culture

COMMENT:
1.	Interview Skills Training
I recommend implementing a course to help state employees strengthen their interview skills. This would provide valuable support for employees seeking to grow within the organization and ensure they are better prepared for competitive opportunities.
2.	Reporting Concerns About Leadership and HR
There should be a formal process, separate from the union, where employees can report concerns regarding the conduct of Directors, Deputy Directors, or HR staff. For example, if HR representatives are dismissive or rude, employees should have a safe and professional channel to raise these concerns. Employees have the right to follow up on matters that directly affect them, and there should be accountability when those rights are not respected.
3.	Transparency in Hiring Practices
I have observed instances where Directors or Deputy Directors appear to hire individuals they have previously worked with. While it is sometimes stated that leadership was not involved in the hiring process, those same individuals are still selected for high-level positions. To avoid the perception of favoritism, greater transparency in hiring practices should be required, including clearer documentation of decision-making and candidate selection.
IDEA_TEXT: Create interview skills training course to help state employees prepare for job opportunities and career growth.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Career growth; Employee training

To avoid the perception of favoritism, greater transparency in hiring practices should be required, including clearer documentation of decision-making and candidate selection.
IDEA_TEXT: Set up formal process outside the union for employees to report concerns about Directors, Deputy Directors, or HR staff conduct.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Governance and oversight; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Internal feedback

COMMENT: One issue is about growing the competency of our workforce. We tend to have a lot of staff, but few people doing the vast majority of the work. This is not equitable and leads to burnout and potential loss of talent. True staff development would help this, along with formal mentoring programs, and more opportunities (with less bureaucratic hoops) to have temporary training and development opportunities, or the opportunity to partner with non-State agencies to temporarily work in the field, etc. We need staff to understand the big picture, be engaged, be empowered, and be prepared for advancement.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve staff development and training. Create formal mentoring programs and provide temporary training opportunities with other agencies to help staff understand the big picture and prepare for advancement.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Career growth; Cross-agency collaboration; Employee training; Mentorship programs

COMMENT: Since COVID there has been a lot more automation of what used to be paper processes. Also, State websites and dashboards have improved, making it easier to find and use publicly available data and information.
IDEA_TEXT: Expand automation of paper processes and improve state websites and dashboards to make public data easier to find and use.

IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digital services; Digitize processes; Public service delivery and responsiveness

Also, State-sponsored or subsidized education programs with an agreement to stay at the State for a certain number of years, could improve the workforce.

COMMENT:
Telework can generate significant office cost savings for the State, provided state employees telework three or more days per week.
» Under the Governor’s April 2024 directive requiring state employees to work in the office two days per week, the state auditor estimated that 19 selected departments spent almost $117 million in fiscal year 2024–25 on nearly 3.2 million square feet of office space that was often unused in the seven large state-owned
office properties we reviewed.
» If state employees telework three or more days per week, DGS has estimated that the State could reduce office space in DGS-managed state-owned and leased buildings by approximately 30 percent, which could generate cost savings of as much as $225 million annually. 

Since the Executive Order that requires employees to return to the office 4 days a week, we have lost vital IT staff that were crucial to many important projects. To hire and train for their backfills, we have been set back years. Additionally, it will cost the agency more money to train and get them up to speed. 

I would like to see agencies be given back the autonomy to set telework policies that work for their departments.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow agencies to set their own telework policies that work for their departments instead of requiring all state employees to work in the office a set number of days per week.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: Software standards.  One example is ServiceNow. This could be used as an IT ticketing system for all state departments.  However, every department selects their own IT ticketing system and then they all pay differing licensing costs. One software where it makes sense, the state could realize cost savings and also continuity across departments, saving in training and add easier coordination.
IDEA_TEXT: Standardize software across state departments. Use one IT ticketing system like ServiceNow for all departments to save money on licensing costs and training.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Technology and digital innovation; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Shared resources; Software and tools; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT:
We have too many state agencies solving the same technical probelm for their individual agency. This is an inefficent use of resources and public funds. If we know that all state agencies need to maintain and submit certain information (i.e., time tracking, STD 200) why don't we create a statewide solution instead of each agency developing a solution?

We need to centeralize technology and systems that are used across the states. Create a central code repository that we can share across state agencies.
IDEA_TEXT: Create statewide technology systems for common needs like time tracking and STD 200 forms. Build a central code repository that all state agencies can share instead of each agency making their own solutions.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Budget and resources; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Shared resources; Software and tools; Technology and data modernization

IDEA_TEXT: Partner with other state agencies to share technical solutions. Work together to improve existing solutions and share benefits with all agencies involved.

IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Shared resources; Software and tools

COMMENT: State employees fall into 2 categories.. those that work, and those that don't, it's about 50/50. It should not be an expected standard that just because you are a state employee, you don't have to work, you can expect others to do the job, and no repercussions from management because you can't be fired; it is abused in the state & there should be accountability.
IDEA_TEXT: Create accountability measures for state employees. Hold non-performing staff responsible and ensure managers can take action against employees who do not work.

COMMENT:
There is a need to modernize data deidentification model and privacy compliance review to boost efficiency and unleash value of data. Current process to perform data suppression including secondary suppression is unsustainable and often involves excessive manual labor and subjective determination in excel spreadsheets. Over-suppression not only creates a bottleneck, delaying data releases for research, policy-making, and public transparency, it also renders public-use datasets less useful for researchers and community organizations. 

This manual, subjective model represents a significant opportunity cost for the taxpayers we serve. We need to move to more automated systems and more streamlined privacy standards and technologies. Another area of investment is to upskill analysts in robust, open-source tools in R or Python to codify privacy rules and to reduce manual hours in excel compliance review.
IDEA_TEXT: Modernize data privacy systems. Move from manual Excel reviews to automated systems with streamlined privacy standards and train analysts in R or Python tools to reduce manual work and speed up data releases.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Employee training; Shared data; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT: Lack of efficiency in our business practices. For instance, when I am purchasing supplies and services for my agency, due to the overwhelming number of rules I must abide by, the state is paying a significant amount more for the same product or service.  We are bound to only using “certified” small businesses that many times are off shore companies with physical address that comes back to a private parcel pick up location.  The majority of time these orders are fulfilled by the vendor through Amazon or Walmart.com.  A significant amount of time and money could be saved if agencies were allowed to utilize local vendors or store fronts.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow agencies to buy from local vendors and stores instead of only using certified small businesses that often fulfill orders through Amazon or Walmart.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Procurement

COMMENT: Allow “regional” managers to approve purchases and services.  The multi step processes we currently must go through at the field level to conduct business all end up having to route through to several channels before we get a green light from top management.  Give managers at field level operation the tools and authority to ensure business can continue uninterrupted.
IDEA_TEXT: Give regional managers power to approve purchases and services. Remove multi-step approval processes that require top management sign-off for field operations.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Management culture and leadership; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Bureaucracy; Management culture and leadership approach; Procurement

COMMENT: Allow staff and managers more flexibility to conduct business efficiently when it comes to procurement.  We pay far too much for equivalent supplies and services due to the policy set in place of who we can purchase from.
IDEA_TEXT: Give staff and managers more flexibility in purchasing. Allow them to buy supplies and services from more vendors to reduce costs.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Procurement

COMMENT: I have personally streamlined numerous processes within my organization to make it easier for State staff to manage a program as well as easier for the public to access resources/services.  Honestly, it does take a lot to reimagine a process, in both time and staff resources...but, I find that most of the time, my department at least, isn't willing to commit the resources to the effort unless there is some grant for funds (usually Federal grants) and/or public pressure (usually bad press) to do so.  Without one or both, it is all about maintaining the status quo.
IDEA_TEXT: Commit resources to streamline processes. Allocate time and staff to improve processes without waiting for federal grants or public pressure.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Digitize processes; Process delays; Process design and methodologies

COMMENT: Staffing has been a real issue for a lot of the departments. I know when I wanted to apply to be a state working nobody wanted to tell me how the process is done. Now that I been working 4 years in the department, I noticed that across the board hiring and retaining workers is a challenge. For the interview process if people don't show up, they are unable to go lower in the tier to hire candidates that didn't score high but are willing to work. Also people go out on injury for months at a time leaving areas understaffed. They are unable to put a temporary person or hire a new person while that person is on leave. This leaves the rest of the team overworked and unhappy to be doing several people's job. Also moving up we have meetings 1 a year about moving up but they make no effort to communicate with the people interested in moving up. I had communicated several times, and it seems they are always on vacation. I think upwards mobility is a myth. Also when issue's arrive with payroll leading to Sacramento someone is always on vacation. The next person in line usually does not want to be bothered and will not cover their shift unless absolutely necessary. I feel like there needs to be oversight and accountability. Alot of  California's need jobs but without proper instruction to score higher on test, or show how to apply. Or move the people up from lower positions they been working for years we cannot have a flow of employment.
IDEA_TEXT: Add backup coverage requirements for payroll and HR staff to prevent delays.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Process delays; Understaffing

COMMENT:
Support CA HS Dropouts
California currently has the lowest high school graduation rate in the nation—an alarming fact, especially for a state with the fourth largest economy in the world. We can and must do better for our youth and for the many adults who never had a real chance to earn a diploma.
In California, certain vulnerable student populations—such as those who have experienced homelessness, incarceration, or time in the foster care system—can graduate with a reduced requirement of 130 credits under AB 216. I propose that this same credit pathway be offered to adults, age 18 and older, who enroll in accredited adult school programs.
Many adults who dropped out of high school faced serious obstacles: poverty, family instability, violence in their communities, or disruptions caused by the COVID-19 pandemic. Without a diploma, these individuals are often stuck in low-wage jobs with little opportunity for advancement.
A HS diploma opens doors to job training programs, community college, military service, and promotions. 
•	The state gains from increased tax revenue as graduates earn higher wages.
•	Adult schools benefit from federal funding tied to improved student outcomes.
•	Employers gain access to a more skilled and motivated workforce.
•	Businesses benefit from the increased purchasing power of newly employed adults.
•	Communities see reduced crime rates and greater social stability.
Expand the 130-credit diploma option to adults. Its a low cost, high impact solution.
IDEA_TEXT: Expand the 130-credit diploma option from AB 216 to adults age 18 and older who enroll in accredited adult school programs.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: Every year we lose homes to wildfires and these losses are preventable. We need to mandate home hardening and defensible space including zone 0. There will be strong public outcry but often the hard thing and the right thing are the same. This will be costly and we need to provide funding for those who need it to accomplish this. This doesn't mean the $100k it may take to fully harden a home, but the low cost retrofits that are most impactful, on the order of $2-5k. Fuels reduction and prescribed fire are great and can have beneficial impacts to suppression efforts and ecosystem health, but if we need the money from somewhere it should be taken from there. Of course it's sad when the wildland burns, but if there is no loss of life or property most folks won't be too heartbroken. Work in the home ignition zone is what will have the biggest impact. We won't lose 0 homes, but the losses will be greatly reduced. This is a fixable problem, we just need the will to mandate it. There can be a timeline of multiple years to reach compliance if that will make it more palatable, but this needs to have teeth behind it.
IDEA_TEXT: Mandate home hardening and defensible space requirements including zone 0 to prevent wildfire losses. Provide funding assistance of $2-5k for low-cost retrofits that have the biggest impact. Create a multi-year timeline for compliance with enforcement mechanisms.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Workplace operations; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Environmental sustainability; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: I have pushed to use private contractors in addition to department crews to get fuels reduction work done. Due to the cost of pay/benefits/pension and the short length of the effective work day, it costs about $48k per acre for department crews to get fuels reduction work done. A private contractor can range from $3-8k per acre and will get it done much more quickly.
IDEA_TEXT: Use private contractors for fuels reduction work instead of department crews to reduce costs from $48k per acre to $3-8k per acre and complete work faster.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Contracts and vendors; Procurement

IDEA_TEXT: Hire an outside third party to review government operations and find ways to cut waste.

This includes environmental regulation, contracting manuals, procurement manuals, and hiring regulations. The money could have been better spent on employees or other programs that would benefit more people than 60 miles of track that hasn't been laid in what like 20 years... There's too much overall waste

COMMENT: The first thing I noticed when I became a state employee was how behind the times everything is. The modern world utilizes technology wherever possible. We should aim to do the same.  The state needs to rid itself of antiquated processes, procedures, technologies, equipment, etc. As the nation's leader in technology and innovation the State of California should focus on improving these things across all areas of business. We should be using electronic/virtual options wherever possible. Allow ECH/ACH/EFT payments to vendors. Use electronic communications. Strive to reduce wasteful spending on office and storage space by moving to a more modern virtual space. Revamp our websites to make them more user friendly and improve functionality. Provide more training to all state employees on the various programs and technologies that exist to improve communication and efficiency. Using modern technology would not only improve processing times but would also save the state and taxpayers money by reducing waste of paper, ink, office supplies, postage, time, space, and environmental impacts.
IDEA_TEXT: Modernize state technology and processes. Replace old systems with electronic options like ACH/EFT payments, digital communications, virtual workspaces, user-friendly websites, and provide staff training on new technologies to reduce costs and improve efficiency.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Technology and digital innovation; Process improvement; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Employee training; Public service delivery and responsiveness; Software and tools; Technology and data modernization

By emprasing technology and find applications that can be used department wide and in several ways, would make agencies manual processes go away and be eaiser to maintain.
IDEA_TEXT: Use technology to improve services and reduce manual work. Find software that can be used across departments and automate outdated systems.

COMMENT: While I understand that this is not where we are supposed to discuss work conditions, ignoring them is a disservice to all of California. Addressing these issues helps to ensure a happy and healthy workforce which is key to good customer service, performance, and efficiency. Workers need to have their needs met in order to perform at their best. The state should strive to provide the best work environment possible to each of it's employees. This should not be a one-size-fits-all approach as every human has different needs. Some people need more support than others. Some require more guidance. Others need less. The state should also do more to understand neurodivergence within the workforce as it plays a part in the needs of each individual. This is also true for each role, not classification. Each role has different requirements of technology, tools, communication, supplies, etc. Tailoring things to the individual and the role they fill will greatly improve efficiency, productivity, collaboration, communication, quality of work, etc.
IDEA_TEXT: Customize work environments and support to fit each employee's individual needs and job requirements. Provide different levels of guidance and support based on what each person needs, including understanding neurodivergent workers. Tailor technology, tools, communication, and supplies to match specific job roles rather than using one-size-fits-all approaches.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Organizational structure and culture; Workplace operations; Technology and digital innovation; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Inclusion and diversity; Office management; Software and tools

IDEA_TEXT: Streamline medical coding and billing processes. Provide clear instructions for proper coding and billing. Update processes to support the switch from paper to electronic health records.

COMMENT: We tend to lose the most skilled workers becasue the state offers very low wages. When the state tries to to push for a lower quality of life for state workers, such as RTO, we lose even more good workers. This seems like a very bad strategy because we end up losing our best people and often struggle to fill the positions. The state as a whole, including the citizens, all lose when this happens. If we want the best workers putting in their best efforts, we need to make them feel apprectiated and valued. The "pizza party" to make up for low wages is not a good strategy.
IDEA_TEXT: Increase state worker pay and benefits to compete with other employers and keep skilled staff.

COMMENT: As a tax auditor, I try to make the taxpayers that I audit feel like they are not being targeted to squeeze more tax dollars from them. I try to make them feel like audits are routine and when they do lead to adjustments, that it is only to make corrections according to the law.
IDEA_TEXT: Make tax audits feel routine and fair. Help taxpayers understand that audits are normal processes and adjustments follow tax law, not targeting for extra money.

IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public policy initiatives; Public service delivery and responsiveness

IDEA_TEXT: Modernize the California payroll system to support changing workforce needs and include new technology.

COMMENT: When a team works well together it can make things more effective or efficient. Management that treats their team like they are adults and doesn't micromanage creates trust within the enviornment.

IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Organizational structure and culture; Management culture and leadership

COMMENT: It is really expensive to live in California. Half of my take home pay goes to rent. The other half is fully taken by bills and food. Creating space for staff to work from home is really important to reduce gas, travel, etc...
IDEA_TEXT: Allow staff to work from home to reduce travel costs and gas expenses.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee travel; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: As a taxpayer and state employee the primary problem I would address is wasteful spending of tax dollars on unnecessary office space. Allowing remote work wherever possible will greatly reduce the amount of money wasted on building leases, maintenance, repairs, electricity, internet, gas, water, office supplies, soap, cleaners, paper towels, toilet paper, etc. Remote work allows for a more diverse workforce and provides opportunity to rural communities throughout the state, mothers, and people with disabilities. It improves productivity by reducing office distractions and stress caused from commuting. For neurodivergent folks working in the office environment can be very stressful and  downright overwhelming. The sounds, smells, lighting, temperature, masking, etc. impact the ability to focus leading to more mistakes, work being done slower, burnout, etc. Not to mention all the physical, mental, emotional, and personal issues caused by the stress of it all. It reduces carbon emissions, vehicle miles traveled, traffic congestion, and safety risks by having fewer people on the road, thus reducing funds spent on responding to crashes. It improves employee morale by allowing time that would be spent commuting to be spent with family, friends, etc. Shifting to the electronic/virtual world wherever possible will speed up processing times across the board. Emails are faster and save more money than snail mail & provide proof of delivery. Happier workers = better customer service.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow remote work wherever possible to reduce office costs and improve employee productivity. Shift to electronic and virtual processes to speed up work and save money.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Workplace operations; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Remote work and return to office; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT:
Technology is way out of date; it's like being stuck in the 1990s. The work is very manual, redundant, duplicate, and time-consuming for menial tasks.
1) Having an updated timesheet system requiring signature only with a few modifications from the employee and contain all information within that application would save so much time!
2) Having a system where information from cross-funcational teams can be placed, assigned, emailed and/or linked to documents and information, and track progress would be so much for efficient. Like Salesforce, ASANA, or other type of communication database.
3) Approval processes are multi-layer and take WAY too long often causing work to be delayed. Having a way to make approvals easier, quicker, and in one application (as mentioned in #2) might move approval processes along more quickly.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a shared system like Salesforce or ASANA where teams can assign tasks, email, link documents, and track progress.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Communication and knowledge sharing; Budget and resources; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Digitize processes; Shared resources; Software and tools

COMMENT: Creating policy and procedure overviews for a body of work, along with desk manuals for completing specific tasks, has helped new hires A LOT. This has cut down training time from 1+ years to 3-4 months before a new hire is ully up to speed and doing completed staff work.

COMMENT: How can we increase investment into meeting our forest health goals? We are not even meeting our current goal of 1 million acres/year (burning piles from several years ago doesn't count!), and can't expect to get to 2.5 million acres/year by 2045 if we don't do something different. This should include an all-of-government approach, but there are clearly walls preventing us from getting to real solutions (otherwise the private marketplace would have solved this!).
IDEA_TEXT: Use an all-of-government approach to increase investment in forest health goals and remove barriers preventing real solutions.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: Overhaul management training. Excellent analysts do not typically equate to excellent managers. The mandatory 80-hour supervisor training does not set up new managers well enough to be good leaders. They are often underprepared and have no idea what to do to help coach and train (especially new) employees. Many of them only promote for the extra compensation and don't care about their team excelling.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve the 80-hour supervisor training program. Make sure new managers learn how to coach and train employees effectively.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Manager training

COMMENT: Communication from the top down is not clear.  Often times the message construed from the Executives is not the same message/meaning which makes expectations unclear.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve communication from executives. Make sure messages are clear and consistent so staff understand what is expected.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal communication; Management culture and leadership approach

COMMENT: As a California resident, I would like to know my government is working as LEAN and efficiently as possible without wasting my tax dollars. By doing this, such as using updated technology to streamline processes, provide quicker turnaround times, reduce duplicate manual menial tasks, and provide better onboarding and training, California becomes more robust, efficient, and nimble. This also gives the State of California more focus on supporting recipients quickly - and can even place more staff in those areas of need - instead of managing a cumbersome bureaucracy, outdated systems, inefficient technology, poor communication, and delays in getting up to speed with new hires.
IDEA_TEXT: Update technology to make processes faster and reduce manual work. Improve staff training and onboarding to help new hires get up to speed quickly.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Process improvement; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Employee training; Onboarding new employees; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT: As a tax collecting agency, it is way too easy for people to lie, cheat and steal to avoid paying taxes. There is nothing more frustrating than being a law-abiding taxpayer, and watching people take advantage of the system and steal more in tax revenue in one year then I will likely earn in my lifetime.  People just start new corporations or other entities and ultimately have no personal responsibility after not paying any taxes for several years.  The system is way too easy to take advantage of and it very demoralizing to watch it be abused decade after decade and all they would have to do is make it easier to go after known cheaters and remove some of the protections corporations or other entities have and hold the owners responsible for cheating.
IDEA_TEXT: Make it easier to go after tax cheaters and hold business owners personally responsible for unpaid taxes by removing some corporate protections.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Governance and oversight
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT:
1. Communication with lower staff.
2. Streamline process to cut out unnecessary review. 
3. Empower lower staff to make decisions.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve communication with lower-level staff, streamline processes by removing unnecessary reviews, and give lower-level staff more decision-making power.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Management culture and leadership; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal communication; Management culture and leadership approach; Process design and methodologies

COMMENT: When it comes to Non-Industrial Disability Insurance (NDI), why can't excluded state employees file all claim paperwork online like SDI? There is so much room for error to rely on paper mail and CalHR to manage a disability claim for a individual staff.  SO much gets lost in the paper trail and having to chase down emails, attachments and snail mail with vital health information. The current workflow requires claim participants (Patient, Physician, etc.) to MAIL in claim documents, those documents are then processed by hand and an email exchange happens between CalHR and EDD. room for error includes rotating CalHR staff and EDD staff, lost documents in email, PII shared via email, having to resubmit paperwork due to error and extending the time to receive benefits, etc. The EDD claim system in digital for Californians and some State staff. Why not make it digital for ALL Californians and CA State staff? Save time, money, heartache for staff and make it digital!
IDEA_TEXT: Make Non-Industrial Disability Insurance (NDI) claims fully digital for all California state employees. Allow excluded state employees to file all claim paperwork online like other workers can do with State Disability Insurance (SDI).
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Software and tools

COMMENT: DGS Procurement rules that are same for different organizations.  It should be different for organizations that are buying papers/pens etc vs organizations that need to buy specialized equipment or instruments.   Procurement rules are not effective and efficient and  hinders other departments from doing their job in a timely manner.
IDEA_TEXT: Create different procurement rules for different types of organizations. Make simpler rules for basic supplies like paper and pens. Make specialized rules for technical equipment and instruments.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Process design and methodologies; Procurement

COMMENT: Problem: We have really smart employees that spend years doing their job, but their institutional knowledge often gets stuck or siloed, so when they leave, or the work increases beyond their capacity, it's hard to step in to do the work. I think some solutions could include better documentation protocols, with staff dedicated to upholding them (i.e., making sure our network and SharePoint folders get insane, which they always seem to), and staff time to document/communicate procedures and innovate ways to improve processes.
IDEA_TEXT: Create better documentation rules with staff to manage them. Give employees time to write down procedures and find ways to improve work processes.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Internal feedback; Process documentation

COMMENT: Empower leaders to take mild risks so we don't get stuck putting all our eggs in one basket. Smaller moves
IDEA_TEXT: Allow leaders to take small risks instead of putting all resources into one approach.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Growth mindset; Management culture and leadership approach; Risk aversion

COMMENT: One thing I have noticed after working for CDCR for over 25 years is that they put unqualified people in administrative jobs.  An example would be: instead of hiring someone with a business/accounting background to be in charge of "Business Services", CDCR hires someone that worked their way from up from an officer or secretary to run that department.  This happens in the medical department also.
IDEA_TEXT: Hire people with the right background for administrative jobs. Require business or accounting experience for Business Services roles instead of promoting from unrelated positions.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Hiring and recruitment; Job classification; Qualified staff

COMMENT:
Being clear on who is the project manager, which may sometimes be different than the org chart manager if the project is cross-functional.

Also fostering frequent, friendly connection with teams involved in a project that you don't have a lot of experience working with - a feeling of comradery and shared appreciation goes so far in making work feel satisfying and impactful!
IDEA_TEXT: Clearly assign project managers for cross-functional projects, even if different from organizational chart managers. Build friendly connections and teamwork between project teams to improve job satisfaction and impact.

COMMENT: When managers give autonomy to staff and allow communication and collaboration despite some impact on productivity, I've seen processes improve because staff can slow down and rethink what they're doing.
IDEA_TEXT: Give staff more freedom to work independently. Allow team communication and collaboration even if it slows down some work so staff can improve how they do their jobs.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership approach; Trust and openness; Work culture

COMMENT: There is no room for promotion unless we want to move to a different part of the State. I was told once you are in the State, you are set but then if you want to move departments or reach your pay cap at your current position. There is no motivation for higher ups to move you. Which as an employee, doesn't motivate me to work harder. I work in Admin and I feel that often Headquarters and the Districts seem to be working against each other. There are so many systems that don't speak to each other, rules change without warning or training. The solutions would include either extending pay caps or make promoting or moving around your own District easier. We need less systems that actually communicate and work. I feel like once I understand how to do something, it's changed in Headquarters, we at Districts don't find out until we are told we did it wrong.
IDEA_TEXT: Extend pay caps or make promoting and moving within districts easier to motivate staff.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Career growth; Employee pay and benefits; Employee retention

Folks like being able to reach us as an organization and feel heard. So actually taking the time to assist rather than acting as their opinions are an annoyance.
