COMMENT: Fewer barriers to receiving public benefits. Many more people qualify for benefits than those who receive them, often because the paperwork is just so onerous - too long, and required too frequently. A lot of information we ask for is available through other means, such as tax return data. If we design a system based on the goals of serving the least number of people as possible and catching every single moment of error or fraud, we end up hurting the people we are purported to serve, and conditions for Californians don't improve, further eroding trust in our mission.
IDEA_TEXT: Reduce barriers to getting public benefits. Make paperwork shorter and less frequent. Use tax return data and other available information instead of asking people to provide it again.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Public policy initiatives; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT: I know this won't be a popular opinion, but I'd like to see LLMs integrated into workflows with the objective of reducing labor costs over time. I wouldn't want to see it displace anybody already employed, I'd like to see a workforce reduction through attrition. I'd want departments to put teams together that consist of employees of all levels who go through every workflow function to determine what can be automated or streamlined with an LLM. I wouldn't want it rolled out until it was tested by folks not on the original team. I know for my job, about 40% of what I do could easily be automated.
IDEA_TEXT: Use AI tools to reduce labor costs over time. Create teams with staff at all levels to find tasks that can be automated. Reduce workforce through natural turnover, not layoffs. Test automation before rolling it out.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: AI and large language models; Digitize processes; Employee retention; Understaffing

COMMENT: The primary problem I would address is the delegation process. There are too many checks and balances which address the same liability and concerns via many different documents and protocols all addressing the same issues and concerns and not adding value to the process. In order to process design exceptions there are so many software, document types, sharing platforms, and not a cohesive process that streamlines and minimizes the documentation process and signatures required. There needs to be better tracking of documents, fewer software, and a more streamlined process followed by both the consultants and the reviewers to achieve any modicum of efficiency.  I would suggest singular core documents focusing on the projects and their exceptions with varied signature pages depending on the level of delegation. I would also suggest a singular software capable of handling the sharing and evolution of this document and the associated plans that would allow for real time sharing of revisions, corrections, and modification between consultants, delegated employees, and upper management with final approval authority.
IDEA_TEXT: Streamline the delegation process by creating one main document with different signature pages based on delegation level, and use one software system that allows real-time sharing and updates between consultants, staff, and management.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Process design and methodologies; Software and tools

COMMENT: I have been tasked as a delegate within the process and have created a spreadsheet tracker that could be applied to all the districts fielding the same types of exception requests which would add to the tracking and accountability of the individuals working on approving the exception documents but in order to improve the process as a whole bigger change is required from the top down approach. Starting with Headquarter taking what we are all saying and doing something about it rather than just accepting the status quo.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a spreadsheet tracker system for all districts handling exception requests to improve tracking and accountability.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Governance and oversight
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Digitize processes; Process documentation

COMMENT: I'd like to see an end to freeway expansion projects, and all that money get dumped into building robust public transit networks around metropolitan areas.  In conjunction with that, I'd like to see more incentives to build multifamily, mixed income housing around transportation hubs, including utilizing eminent domain to redevelop suburban neighborhoods into walkable communities linked together by public transportation. COMMENT: I joined this "discussion" today and have to click through 495 comments to engage with one that I resonate with. I have been "loading" more comments for 10 min and not even half way through the forum.. I gave up reading through since it became too repetitive with Telework and "NO to RTO" ideas. All of which I agree with given the many costs saving to tax payers, work life balance and increase in staff productivity when working from home!.. this platform tool is clunky and hard to navigate given the volume of commented ideas.

IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digital services; Software and tools

COMMENT:
Employee morale has significantly dropped over the last three years as the state tries to implement a one size fits all RTO solution, in the midst of multiple PLPs, rising benefits costs, older retirement ages, and low pay increases that do not keep up with the cost of living in California. State employees want to be more efficient and help California budget issues but doing it on state workers' backs is only going to drive out good, marketable employees. 

Consider implementing all department surveys to assess productivity, collaboration, efficiency, and department needs when making decisions about RTO. Transparency on actual costs of RTO- DGS and CalHR's responses in the budget subcommittee hearing that there was essentially no way to know the costs was disingenuous and disrespectful to state employees. Assessments for cutting costs to programs for people who do not work or unused/unmoderated social programs, before cutting pay of hard-working state employees.
IDEA_TEXT: Cut costs from unused social programs before reducing worker pay.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT:
I have been a state employee for 20+ years and have seen many Bills passed without the resources and input by Program(s) required to implement the Bill included which causes ineffectiveness in implementation process and results in an increase in cost of staff time and cost to the regulated communities.  I had the privilege of providing input to the language in an Assembly bill in 2019 to the legislative team, which created a more robust Bill that Program was aware of and knew ahead of time how they could implement it in a more effective manner and outlined any resources that would be needed to do so.  

To solve this problem I think that the authors of these Bills should do a exploritory process to get a better idea of the Program(s) their Bill would be effecting and have converstions with a Staff member(s) to have language the is clear and implementable, which makes it easier when the Bill is passed for Staff to implement.
IDEA_TEXT: Require bill authors to work with affected programs before writing bills. Have conversations with staff to make sure the language is clear and can be implemented easily.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Governance and oversight
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Align policy and implementation; Internal feedback; Policymaking

COMMENT: Streamlined processes, reduced paper for environmental purposes and better record storage and access.
IDEA_TEXT: Make processes simpler and reduce paper use for better record keeping and easier access.

COMMENT: Procurement and contracting have onerous rules and are time consuming.  Quality and fitness of purpose should be factored into the equation apart of other considerations.  Shifting the responsibility of vendor evaluation and selection back to the authorities within the requesting entities would help reduce delays and minimize the risk of mis or inadequate procurement.
IDEA_TEXT: Include quality and fitness of purpose in procurement decisions. Give requesting departments more control over vendor evaluation and selection to reduce delays and improve procurement outcomes.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Budget and resources; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Process delays; Procurement

COMMENT: I feel the procedures are not well defined and are changed too often by control agencies, which makes it difficult for admin staff. I also feel that admin staff are not as effective due to pressures from multiple layers of management within departments. I also feel there is a growing usage of high level "specialist" jobs that are used to retain seasoned staff rather than for the actual business usage. This excess of high-level specialist jobs take responsibilities away from the supervisors and management staff, while putting more pressure on the entry level staff to do more with less (leading to more errors and more labor hours). This has also caused discourse amongst R&F staff. I feel there is too many middle management positions, which is preventing staff from receiving consistent direction and guidance. Decision making is taking longer which cause more delays.  I feel the HR Manual from CalHR was a good start, but the manual need to continue to develop that resource (and CalHR needs to be given the support to do so).
IDEA_TEXT: Expand the HR Manual from CalHR. Give CalHR more support to keep developing this resource.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Communication and knowledge sharing; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Knowledge management; Shared resources

COMMENT:
Our government websites are not built to meet the needs of the people. They are built so a hierarchy of executives in government are satisfied that *their* needs are met. This usually results in sites that are unreadable, topics the public doesn't care about, and services that are hard to navigate.

Sites and services should be held to the state's Plain Language Equity Standard, meaning grade 8 reading level or below. They should be built with a human-centered design process. And they should be continually tested with real users and improved. 

The measure of success should be, "Are the people we serve happy with this?" We should frequently check user feedback, Google analytics, and participation rates to know if our delivery of the service is working or not. And if it's not, we should fix it.
IDEA_TEXT: Make government websites easier to use. Build sites using human-centered design, test with real users, and use Plain Language Equity Standard (8th grade reading level). Track user feedback and website data to measure success and make improvements.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Communication and knowledge sharing; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digital services; Human-centered design; Plain language; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT: One example of work I have done to make things more effective or efficient is setting up guidance documents and examples for future staff members to follow. There is a lot of turnover in State jobs and it took me about 2 years in my current position to put together the pieces that were left to me when I started this job after about a one year hiatus in the position. To save time for the next person, I've been putting together clear examples and guidance documents pointing future staff to work we've previously done, where examples can be found, and how to complete tasks that will be expected of them in this position.
IDEA_TEXT: Create guidance documents and examples for new staff. Set up clear instructions and work examples to help future employees learn their jobs faster when there is staff turnover.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Process improvement; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Knowledge management; Process documentation

COMMENT: It would be great to increase the outreach to new state hires, as well as create more incentives for residents to join State service. Often private companies pay higher rates and the cost of living in our state is so high. Sometimes it seems like we are pricing ourselves out of the job market.
IDEA_TEXT: Increase outreach to new state hires and create more incentives for residents to join state service to compete with higher private sector pay.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Employee retention; Hiring and recruitment

COMMENT:
I would like state and local government to ask us what services we need and shift funding to them accordingly. No one ever asks us!

For example, in my city we've been asking for easy public transportation to the airport for years. Cars to the airport clog the downtown streets. But instead, light rail gets built out to wealthy areas. I wonder if this would happen if we knew how many people wanted a different priority.
IDEA_TEXT: Ask residents what services they need and shift funding to match their priorities.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Participatory budgeting; Public participation; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: You should be able to navigate to the mission, vision, values, and executive team of each department within two clicks from the home page.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve website navigation. Make department mission, vision, values, and executive team information accessible within two clicks from the home page.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digital services; Process design and methodologies

COMMENT:
As a newer state employee with prior experience in the private sector, I have noticed a significant gap in leadership style and workplace culture. Too often, leadership at the state seems to operate from a place of fear rather than support. Leaders seem to focus on blame, micromanagement, and unclear expectations rather than encouragement, collaboration, and clear communication. It feels as though i'm working in my parent's and grandparent's generation. This has created an environment where employees feel disempowered, disconnected, and hesitant to share ideas or challenge the status quo. 

To build a more effective and modern workforce, I think California should invest heavily in leadership development and hold leaders accountable for fostering a supportive, transparent, and progressive culture. Leaders should be given the tools to lead the same virtually as they do in-person. Leaders need to be trained to authentically care about their team members, communicate virtually or in-person with kindness and respect, and engage with employees in a way that feels human and positive, rather than robotic and forced. With new generations entering the workforce, leadership needs to adapt to meet evolving workplace expectations. Shifting from a punitive to a supportive leadership model would empower employees to engage fully, collaborate openly, and drive meaningful innovation across state government.
IDEA_TEXT: Invest in leadership training and hold leaders accountable for creating a supportive workplace culture. Train leaders to communicate with kindness and respect, care about their teams, and shift from punitive to supportive management styles to meet modern workplace expectations.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Organizational structure and culture; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership approach; Manager training; Trust and openness; Work culture

COMMENT: I work in the public education arena. Not so much a problem, but a way of doing work - too much talk and not a whole lot of action.
IDEA_TEXT: Focus on action over discussion. Reduce time spent in meetings and increase time spent on getting work done.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Bureaucracy; Management culture and leadership approach; Process delays

COMMENT: Redundancy and outdated procedures.  It's 2025 and some of the state's procedures need to be revisited and revised to reflect the modern era.  I have worked for three different agencies in the last 8 years or so and I can say that all of them could be modernized in some way.  We need to pivot away from using fax machines, storing paperwork, accepting the lowest bid possible (you get what you pay for), things like that.  California needs to invest in it's self.
IDEA_TEXT: Update state procedures to modern standards. Stop using fax machines, reduce paperwork storage, and invest in better technology instead of always choosing the cheapest option.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Bureaucracy; Digitize processes; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT: I have worked closely with managers to modernize processes and workflow, including pivoting the office toward email vs. fax communication.  I have also reworked processes that were old, outdated, and redundant by reviewing the old process, identifying the issues in the process, rethinking the process, and then testing it out in actual practice to be sure that it adheres to what we're targeting.
IDEA_TEXT: Modernize office processes and workflows. Switch from fax to email communication and update old, outdated processes by reviewing, identifying issues, rethinking, and testing new approaches.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Process design and methodologies; Process documentation

COMMENT:
Departments work in silos, not together. There is no systemic way of sharing institutional knowledge, so innovation happens in uneven pockets and archaic processes persist.

There should be experiential learning opportunities across departments for people in job-alike roles (e.g., HR, budgets, accounting, contracting, IT, EEO, regulations, etc.) so that even new State employees can develop real networks and meaningful relationships outside of conference/workshop settings. We need to say "one team" less and act like one team more.
IDEA_TEXT: Create cross-department learning programs for staff in similar roles like HR, budgets, accounting, contracting, IT, EEO, and regulations to build networks and share knowledge across departments.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Organizational structure and culture; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Employee training; Networking

COMMENT: It's time to move forward...one of the things I have seen again and again in my previous office over the years was receiving bids to digitize files.  Every year my office would get multiple bids, however they would never actually accept the bid and move forward.  Here we are years later (in 2025) and there is still a huge file room (which costs the state money in space and supplies) and none of the files have been converted digitally.  This is a waste of employees time and state funds and resources.  And for what?  To appear as though we're doing more work?  This just takes away from the ongoing deliverables and projects that are much more crucial than the entertaining the fantasy of desiring to actually move into the digital age with most of the rest of the world.
IDEA_TEXT: Stop delaying file digitization projects. Accept bids and move forward with converting paper files to digital format to save space, supplies, and staff time.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Physical infrastructure; Procurement

COMMENT: Claimants inability to get in touch with Unemployment or Disability representatives over the phone. We are modernizing systems which allows us to better utilize technology which is great. I think continuing those efforts along with hiring more call center staff is essential for delivering timely service to californians.
IDEA_TEXT: Continue modernizing systems and hire more call center staff to help claimants reach Unemployment and Disability representatives by phone.

COMMENT: Recruitment - HR needs to work closely with hiring managers to set their process up for success from the start and reduce time-to-hire. Set a timeline, reserve time for application screening and interviews on calendars when the job is posted, not after it closes. Help hiring managers get to yes! Work with them on qualifications to understand the programmatic point of view instead of starting from no. When HR and program work together, it's better for everyone, including candidates!
IDEA_TEXT: Improve hiring process by having HR work closely with hiring managers from the start. Set timelines, reserve calendar time for screening and interviews when jobs are posted, and help managers focus on finding qualified candidates instead of rejecting them.

IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Hiring and recruitment; Internal communication; Qualified staff

COMMENT: I would keep duty statements current and honest and not gray the lines between SSAs, AGPAs, and lower-level support staff.
IDEA_TEXT: Keep job descriptions current and accurate. Clearly define the differences between Staff Services Analyst (SSA), Associate Governmental Program Analyst (AGPA), and support staff roles.

COMMENT: I'd like clearer communication from the state over employment/HR concerns. The recent agreement with SEIU left me confused over what actually was happening to my paycheck, and neither the state nor SEIU were helpful in determining that. I also received a promotion-related NOPA around the same time that was similarly opaque, and spent many hours back and forth with HR trying to figure out what the contradictory statements attached to it meant.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve communication about employment and HR issues. Make pay and promotion notices clearer and easier to understand.

COMMENT:
First, a few problems affecting individuals with disabilities:
1. Currently, the ADA and other state laws do not apply to rental housing (especially single-family homes) as well as new homes. Because of this an individual with a disability cannot get a home that has a ramp, walk-in shower, light-based fire alarm, or tactile controls. Many of these adaptations are available by default when accessing public facilities and commercial organizations open to the public. Yet, in the context of housing people with disabilities have to spend tens of thousands to make these conversions. There are exceptions for affordable housing, not all people are eligible for these opportunities. The State should make an effort to make sure accessibility is improved, so that homes are available that meet these requirements and the option to make these changes in new homes by lowering barriers.
2. Many states including Washington, Minnesota, Tennessee, and Massachusetts have Medicaid working disabled programs that operate without income or asset limits. This assists individuals with disabilities would like to work but cannot because insurance does not cover services like long-term care. To ensure that these programs remain financially viable the states have imposed premiums of at least 7% of a person's income. California should consider similar programs.

Second, the State should encourage remote work for employees. It improves retention, mental health and employee well-being.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a Medicaid working disabled program like other states. Allow people with disabilities to work without losing healthcare by charging income-based premiums.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: Management should foster an adult-adult relationship with staff and support and train staff on the concept of internal customer relationships, which is when one staff receives work product from another staff. The reason is that this environment ultimately better supports external customers. Management should remember that it is their job to ensure staff have the tools and information needed to get their jobs done. Sometimes staff are so bogged down keeping up that they are not current on tools and information. There should be a method for control and release of information to help ensure information is current and only the most current is available for use. Structure minimizes chaos and is sustainable.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a system to control and share information so only current versions are available.

COMMENT:
One of the biggest challenges I see is the complexity and inefficiency of internal processes. Employees often spend significant time navigating redundant approvals, unclear responsibilities, and siloed systems. This slows down work and delays how quickly Californians receive the services they need.
Ideas to Solve It
•	Streamline workflows by removing unnecessary steps and defining clear escalation paths.
•	Invest in modern, shared systems so departments can collaborate more effectively instead of duplicating efforts.
•	Empower employees with more training so decisions can be made at the right level, without excessive bottlenecks.
By simplifying processes and fostering collaboration, employees can spend more time focusing on service delivery and less time on administrative hurdles ultimately improving outcomes for residents.
IDEA_TEXT: Give employees more training so decisions can be made at the right level without excessive bottlenecks.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Process improvement; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Management culture and leadership approach; Process delays

IDEA_TEXT: Remove unnecessary steps from work processes. Challenge the "we've always done it this way" mindset to make work more efficient.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Bureaucracy; Growth mindset; Process design and methodologies

COMMENT: Many rural counties have school districts that are required by law to opt into our programs, but they do this individually and are overwhelmed with the administration and compliance requirements involved. It would be beneficial if the State offered the counties some financial incentive to sponsor the program for the many smaller districts, so that they would save $ on staffing at the small district, but also the State would need fewer staff for administration and compliance to run the program. This could be a one-time incentive or an incentive built into participation in the program. So, instead of 5-10 individual small districts, there would be one. Our programs are so complex, that some would rather not take part and collect the federal dollars than deal with the red tape.
IDEA_TEXT: Offer financial incentives to counties to sponsor programs for multiple small school districts instead of each district participating individually. This would reduce staffing costs for small districts and reduce state administrative burden.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Public policy initiatives; Shared resources

COMMENT: I focus on doing what is right for the business making sure the call center is always available with no service impact, so staff can deliver great customer service while staying within budget. By streamlining telecom inventory and validating hotlines, I help ensure reliable, cost-effective operations that support both employees and Californians.
IDEA_TEXT: Streamline telecom inventory and validate hotlines to ensure reliable, cost-effective call center operations that support staff and customers while staying within budget.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Budget and resources; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Digitize processes; Process design and methodologies; Public service delivery and responsiveness

An online portal system should be created for each state agency vs requiring state employees to fill out a multitude of state and department PDF forms which would reduce the review time and time spent routing items for approval, returned for editing, etc.
IDEA_TEXT: Create online portal systems for each state agency to replace PDF forms and streamline review and approval processes.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digital services; Digitize processes; Software and tools

COMMENT:
From my perspective as a Californian and state employee, I believe we can strengthen government services by:
Expanding digital tools so routine transactions (like licensing, claims, or payments) can be handled online without delays.
Building a culture of accountability and care inside state service when employees feel supported and trusted, they can serve the public more effectively.
Additional Idea – Psychological Safety
One area that could make a major difference is strengthening psychological safety in the workplace. From my own challenges, I have seen how difficult it is when employees don’t feel safe speaking up about concerns. On the other hand, when supervisors and leaders model openness and there is a strong support system in place, employees feel empowered to raise issues early, collaborate more effectively, and focus on delivering excellent service.
Investing in training and systems that reinforce psychological safety would not only protect employee well-being but also improve performance and service to Californians.
IDEA_TEXT: Build a culture of accountability and care by supporting and trusting employees so they can serve the public better.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Organizational structure and culture

COMMENT:
From my perspective as a Californian and state employee, I believe we can strengthen government services by:
Expanding digital tools so routine transactions (like licensing, claims, or payments) can be handled online without delays.
Building a culture of accountability and care inside state service when employees feel supported and trusted, they can serve the public more effectively.
Additional Idea – Psychological Safety
One area that could make a major difference is strengthening psychological safety in the workplace. From my own challenges, I have seen how difficult it is when employees don’t feel safe speaking up about concerns. On the other hand, when supervisors and leaders model openness and there is a strong support system in place, employees feel empowered to raise issues early, collaborate more effectively, and focus on delivering excellent service.
Investing in training and systems that reinforce psychological safety would not only protect employee well-being but also improve performance and service to Californians.
IDEA_TEXT: Expand digital tools so routine tasks like licensing, claims, and payments can be done online without delays.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Process improvement; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digital services; Digitize processes; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT: Traffic is an issue, teleworking is great for employee wellness.
IDEA_TEXT: Expand telework options to improve employee wellness and reduce traffic.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee retention; Public transportation; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: The absence of a flexible telework policy. Extensive empirical research links remote work to significant gains in productivity, higher job satisfaction, and reduced staff turnover. Furthermore, adopting a modern telework policy offers substantial fiscal benefits. It would enable the State to significantly reduce overhead costs by downsizing office space. The resulting savings could be reallocated to core mission priorities—such as our software and training needs—or invested in innovative downtown space utilization projects that better serve the public.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a flexible telework policy to boost productivity, job satisfaction, and reduce staff turnover while cutting office space costs to fund software, training, and downtown space projects.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Technology and digital innovation; Budget and resources; Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Employee retention; Employee training; Office management; Remote work and return to office; Software and tools

COMMENT: Telework makes a ton of sense for IT teams, especially for those that are not customer-facing. A majority, if not all, of the tasks we complete involve remote meetings or remoting into virtual infrastructure. By coming into the office to sit at a desk to remote into systems makes little sense.
IDEA_TEXT: Expand telework options for IT teams. Allow IT staff to work from home since most tasks involve remote meetings and connecting to virtual systems.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: Any grants that are released from the state should require fiscal reports but just as important, progress reports.  We should not be handing out state dollars without specifically knowing how and what those dollars are used for.  This is inconsistent.  Some grants have it in statute that an independent evaluation is done and others do not, it makes no sense.  Require state departments to develop specific ways they are measuring the cost/benefit analysis of distributing funds
IDEA_TEXT: Require all state grants to include fiscal reports and progress reports. Make state departments create specific ways to measure cost and benefit of giving out funds. Require independent evaluations for all grants, not just some.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Governance and oversight; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Public policy initiatives; State-spending audits

COMMENT:
Working remotely has huge benefits to accomplishing my important work. I've started my position post pandemic, and the office I've been assigned has 2 other coworkers, which makes the many meetings I have untenable. I cannot concentrate in my office nor can I provide my services with two other calls in the background. Often, I meet with tribals and it feels disrespectful not to be able to give them the privacy they need. 

Working in my office also drastically reduces my quality of life. My childcare is in the opposite direction from my office making pick up and drop off a burden. My quite home office allows me to concentrate on work and lowers the stress of my job because I can get my work done. I am healthier because I'm comfortable exercising at home and can make my own lunches. 

I joined the state during the pandemic and I have never had trouble building relationships and collaborating with my coworkers. I've successfully been mentored and mentored while working remotely. 

I believe that returning to the office will decrease my productivity and that of my fellow coworkers while increasing our stress.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow remote work to improve productivity and reduce stress for employees.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: Continuing California's groundbreaking work from home policy will increase government efficiency and help worker be more effective.
IDEA_TEXT: Continue California's work from home policy to increase government efficiency and help workers be more effective.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public service delivery and responsiveness; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: I have seen similar feedback shared. My time at my Department has highlighted just how hard it is to implement change. Workgroups, steering committees, SME participation, requests and requisitions, policies, procedures, IT procurement all making it nearly impossible for our programs to function in a modern sense. Why? It was always done ‘this’ way. Bureaucracy is good sometimes. Helps to stave off rash and quick decisions that could be costly. HOWEVER, this comes at a cost of being agile, providing better services and improvement.  Working in silos, being beholden to general fund and tax payer acceptance/willingness to fund our work is frustrating. Funded or no we are just trying to do our best to provide for you!
IDEA_TEXT: Reduce bureaucracy and red tape. Streamline workgroups, committees, requests, policies, and IT procurement to help programs work in a modern way while keeping needed oversight.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Technology and digital innovation; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Bureaucracy; Process design and methodologies; Procurement; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT:
Efficiency and sharing innovative ideas across departments - It has been proven in many cases that working in diverse team settings and across various projects helps individuals gain a broader perspective, encounter specific scenarios, and build the experience necessary for career advancement. At the same time, teams benefit from fresh managerial ideas and a willingness to take calculated risks that often come with different leadership styles.

Therefore, rotating managers and supervisors between departments can provide advantages on both sides. It fosters innovation, encourages the exchange of ideas, and allows successful practices from one department or agency to be implemented in another. This cross-pollination of knowledge and experience contributes to overall organizational growth and improvement.
IDEA_TEXT: Rotate managers and supervisors between departments to share ideas, encourage innovation, and spread successful practices across agencies.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Organizational structure and culture; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Management culture and leadership approach; Trust and openness; Work culture

COMMENT: I’m very fortunate to work for a bureau, program, division and department that values its employees. Treats employees like adults and supports their personal and professional growth. However, not everyone has this experience. Being open to feedback, take action on it, review for achievements should be a standard practice. Continuous quality improvement t.
IDEA_TEXT: Make feedback, action, and review a standard practice. Support employee growth and treat staff like adults across all departments.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Communication and knowledge sharing; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal feedback; Management culture and leadership approach; Trust and openness

COMMENT: There is a need for more transportation options to schools and workplaces. For working parents, especially those whose children attend schools outside their designated district, lack of transportation creates a significant challenge. Additionally, in many communities, there are limited or no affordable transportation options from home. As a result, valuable time is lost commuting—time that could otherwise be spent more productively at work.
IDEA_TEXT: Expand transportation options to schools and workplaces. Create more affordable public transit from homes to help working parents, especially those with children in schools outside their district.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public policy initiatives; Public transportation

COMMENT: Need a weighed promotion system. This means it has to include time in service, time in grade, performance reports, interview, accommodations, and promotional test results all graded up to 100 points total.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a weighted promotion system that includes time in service, time in grade, performance reports, interviews, accommodations, and test results scored up to 100 points total.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Career growth; Employee performance reviews; Employee retention

COMMENT: Real estate is a huge industry in this state and should be scrutinized on how it affects the state and can be best used to benefit the state.  Real estate seems to be dictating its wants and needs to the State more than it should.  Real estate as an industry finds ways to run up pricing to benefit itself at the expense of everyone else and our economy.  The more money that is locked up in real estate the less money is in circulation in the state.
IDEA_TEXT: Increase oversight of the real estate industry to better serve state interests and prevent price manipulation that hurts the economy.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Governance and oversight; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: If a department of areas within a department are going to restructure/reorganize their structure they would be looking at staff strengths to determine where they may be moved to and/or they should also be consider an employees seniority within the department.  Moving employees around to have duties that are not a passion or strength is toxic for the work environment.  Picking favorites and moving them where they want is also toxic.  HR should have processes in place to discuss concerns from employees when they receive new duties.
IDEA_TEXT: Consider staff strengths and seniority when restructuring departments. Create HR processes for employees to discuss concerns about new job duties.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Governance and oversight; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee retention; Internal feedback; Org structure and hierarchy

COMMENT:
The primary problem I like to address is homelessness in Sacramento, California especially those who have mental illness on the streets.  Build/create Treatment Centers to house mentally ill patients so they can receive medications along with balanced whole food, nutritious meals until they are stable to live on their own with government aid or with family posting no dangers to others. Besides counseling, medications, is important mentally ill people have nourishing and nutritious food on a daily basis to improve their mental health and physical health.

 "No health, no wealth" - Ralph Emerson

"Let thy food be thy medicine, let thy medicine be thy food." - Hippocrates
IDEA_TEXT: Build treatment centers for mentally ill homeless people in Sacramento. Provide medication, counseling, and nutritious meals until patients are stable enough to live independently with government aid or family support.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public policy initiatives; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT: Communicating to current and future owners of Mobile Home units on the proper and correct way to transfer ownership of these units. Multiple times I encounter situations where new owners bought MH units without proper documentation and without due diligence as to who the prior owners were, creating problems for them to register as the new owners.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve communication to mobile home buyers about proper ownership transfer procedures. Educate current and future owners on required documentation and due diligence steps.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Plain language; Public participation; Public service delivery and responsiveness

IDEA_TEXT: Include helpful printed materials with annual registration and tax renewals. Send flyers, FAQs, and related information with renewal notices.

IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Plain language; Public participation; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT:
Excessive bureaucracy.

Outdated Technology, laws, statutes, manuals, etc. Dollar amount thresholds in many cases are not updated to reflect inflation or cost of living increases. Outdated software programs that create more manual processes needed to accomplish tasks.

Allocation of funding towards deferred maintenance of equipment and buildings. Everything turns into an emergency once it's broken because no consideration is given for deferred maintenance. Government is always reactive vs proactive, and this costs time and prevents employees from getting their jobs done when equipment breaks down and the bureaucracy involved to get it repaired.

Lack of resources or resources that don't fulfill their obligations or doesn't respond to emails or communications (Departments that have oversight not responding to communications in a timely manner or at all in some cases).

Better HR processes to allow new staff to be trained before exiting staff leave. The state has zero regard for this, and each new employee is left to fend for themselves and try to figure out what the person they replaced was doing. This causes a ripple effect with everyone this person works with and creates more work for all involved.

Departments not meeting their own requirements and posted completion promises. For example, a department quotes a 10-business day review time, but reality is 30-45+ days is the normal.
IDEA_TEXT: Allocate funding for regular maintenance of equipment and buildings to prevent emergency repairs.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Physical infrastructure

COMMENT:
1. Eliminate parking fees on weekends and after 5pm on weekdays in Downtown Sacramento so more people can go to patronize restaurant business without the hassles and stresses of looking and paying for a parking spot.

2. Lower house purchasing costs for middle income earners to afford a house to live. 

3. Increase middle income earner's salary to match inflation.

4. Eliminate or lower HOA fees in California. 

5. Limit investors to buy 1 or 2 houses max for rental business and/or Airbnb business in California. Investors have been hiking up housing prices in California. 

6. Lower house/apartment rental costs. 

7. Increase dental coverage/have better dental coverage for low-income people that are on Medi-Cal. I volunteer at free dental clinics, demands for dental cleanings, extractions, fillings, etc. are high every time.

8. Erect Billboards (Dept of Transportation) along California Highways and major boulevards how to handle or avoid road rage.
IDEA_TEXT: Eliminate or lower HOA fees in California.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: I know we are looking for ways to be "more efficient" (spend less) but  that has lead to poor service for the people of California.  We always look to reducing staff costs when the state budget is tight. The way we staff our smaller departments is not resilient and the shocks are killing us. As a result, analyst work has been pushed up to supervisory/management levels, because the available staff to do analyst work is getting smaller and smaller. I'm hoping that the reclassification of the analyst series on will help, but it won't fix it. AI and technology solutions can provide some help, but they do not replace thinking people. You want to know why there is no decent onboarding, or written procedures, just learn what you can? Why so many grievances? Why it's so hard to learn state contracting or Fi$Cal? Why no updated policy or streamlined procedures?  Why duty statements are over 10 years old? Why no innovation? Because the mid-level leadership and front line analysts  that would be part of the team that develops those things, that can point out the real problems in the system, can only do day-to-day survival tasks, jumping from fire to fire. We have positions that are vacant for months b/c of budget, the work is spread out over fewer and fewer burnt-out staff, then we lose those positions because they were vacant. Succession planning is nice but staffing resilience and system capacity would be better.
IDEA_TEXT: Build staffing resilience in smaller departments. Maintain adequate analyst positions to prevent work from being pushed to managers and ensure departments can handle workload without constant crisis management.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Governance and oversight; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Org structure and hierarchy; Qualified staff; Understaffing

COMMENT: One of the biggest issues I have seen in my service time is the fact that we are such a reactionary institution rather than a proactive institution.  It has been one of my longest and deepest frustrations from my time in rank-and-file to my time in management.  I would like to see us spend more time engaging with our employees from the top down and having thoughtful discussions on how some of these problems can be solved rather than allowing just the executives to make them.  I was an executive and having sat in that chair, I am well aware that we do not have all the answers.  It is the boots on the ground individuals that have so much to add to how we can be ahead of the game in some of these budget issues, RTW, and other critical issues we are dealing with.
IDEA_TEXT: Change from reactive to proactive approach. Include all staff levels in problem-solving discussions instead of only executives making decisions.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Management culture and leadership; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal feedback; Management culture and leadership approach; Trust and openness

I think we should give them no options they either live where we want them to live, give them opportunity to fix themselves with work programs or go to jail. COMMENT: One of the biggest things we are lacking are processes.  I just recently came into a position where a program has been in existence for decades and had no processes yet is one that is a critical part of what we do.  One individual has been running it for over 15 years and had gotten complacent because they have had not oversight not because there was not the want but because there is no bandwidth. I came in, did a deep dive analysis and put together a way to revamp the entire program, build new procedures that would benefit not only HQ but also the field.  We are implementing it as we speak and with that the employee who has been doing the work has a fresh and new hope on what this program can be.  It just takes someone taking the time and effort to learn about what is working and what isn't and put things in place to ensure the success in the future because bottom line is, we are all moving on and going to different places and we want to make things better than what we found them.
IDEA_TEXT: Create clear processes and procedures for long-running programs. Do deep dive analysis to find what works and what doesn't, then build new procedures that help both headquarters and field staff.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Governance and oversight; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Align policy and implementation; Internal communication; Process design and methodologies; Process documentation

COMMENT: There should be one statewide system for employees to have an account that contains their information. When needing to fill out any form, they should be able to log into this account, search for and select the form, then it would auto-populate with their information. Once complete, the form should be sent to the agency requesting it. Agencies should also be able to assign forms to employees to complete. Staff should be able to log in to this account and view every form submitted. A system like this should be available to all citizens in California so they don't need to type their name, address, etc., on every form to access services.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a statewide system where employees and citizens can log in once to auto-fill all government forms with their saved information and track submissions.

IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Digital services; Shared data; Software and tools

COMMENT:
Many of the processes used at my department were not built with a focus on efficiently serving our customers. As such, there are often multiple wasteful and non-necessary steps both employees and customers are “required” to take before a customer transaction can be completed. We have failed to analyze and update many of these processes using new methodologies, ways of thinking, and tools that are already readily available to us. It has happened on several occasions that when an improvement is introduced it fails to receive the support that is needed for successful implementation because some members of management fear change and/or feel that they cannot devote employee hours to implementation and testing.
I believe systematic and proven process improvement techniques (like Lean Six Sigma, PDCA, Total Quality Management, etc.) performed by state-employed process-improvement specialists that have the support and resources that can be provided by department heads and agency-level management could act to improve inefficient processes across that state.
IDEA_TEXT: Use proven process improvement methods like Lean Six Sigma and Total Quality Management. Hire state-employed process improvement specialists with support and resources from department heads and agency management to fix inefficient processes across the state.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Digitize processes; Process design and methodologies; Qualified staff

COMMENT: Developed process and procedure documents that are routinely reviewed and kept up to date. A lot of time is wasted by staff searching or emailing to figure out how to do things that should be documented.
IDEA_TEXT: Create and maintain up-to-date process and procedure documents. Review and update them regularly so staff don't waste time searching for information or sending emails to figure out how to do their jobs.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Internal communication; Process documentation

I feel like we work in silos, and it would help us all tremendously if we could share more information with other agencies to find out ways that may work better. One solution could be to have forums where people with similar responsibilities/titles could meet and help one another.
IDEA_TEXT: Create forums where staff with similar jobs from different state agencies can meet and share better ways to work together.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Internal communication; Internal feedback

IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal feedback; Management culture and leadership approach; Trust and openness

COMMENT: This comment is highly specific to my division. So, I am not sure how relevant this particular issue will be to others. The first thing I would do is get rid of or upgrade the two outdated Call Report forms we utilize and condense it to only one upgraded Call Report form that can be downloaded from NMLS and uploaded to our MTFD database. Before the transition to NMLS ( Nationwide Multistate Licensing System & Registry), the licensee was only required to submit one Call Report form. This form was powered by Microsoft Excel with macros built in.  The form was very glitchy and required the licensee to make multiple submissions at times when the wrong button was selected or the form was saved improperly. Now, the licensee must submit two Call Reports, one in an Excel format and the other using the NMLS format. The NMLS Call Report no longer has the built in error checking or data validation of the original Call Report. So now examiners have to waste time having the licensee correct and resubmit Call Reports for minor housekeeping issues. For example, the Outstanding Transmission Liability should agree on both the Balance Sheet Page and Permissible Investment Page. In the past, the Call Report could not be saved or submitted until the corresponding amounts are in agreement. Further, the NMLS Call Report should be updated to reflect both year to date balances and quarter-to-date balances. The licensee should not have to submit a second Call Report with this information.
IDEA_TEXT: Replace two outdated Call Report forms with one upgraded form that can be downloaded from NMLS and uploaded to MTFD database. Include built-in error checking, data validation, and both year-to-date and quarter-to-date balances to eliminate the need for duplicate submissions and manual corrections.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Process design and methodologies; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT: The modus operandi at the state is "quick fixes" and treating the symptoms of problems rather than getting at the root cause. So, we end up with broken systems covered in Band-Aids rather than systems that work.  We need to really look closely at what is happening and make changes- slow down to speed up. First fixing our support systems and then our mission systems. Usually, you would want to spend all your time and effort on the mission, but you cannot make the mission process (why the department exists) better if the process to make the mission process better is siloed and broken. For example- if it takes 2 years to fix a typo on an external form, how do you expect to do any larger improvements for the customer experience or streamline the process for internal stakeholders? This is the reality of my current job with the State.
IDEA_TEXT: Focus on fixing root causes instead of quick fixes. First improve support systems, then work on mission systems to create lasting solutions.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership approach; Process design and methodologies

COMMENT:
Frivolous, unnecessary spending and paralysis by over-analysis. Too many levels of bureaucracy, and too many opinions to seek out before decisions can be made.  
Centralize purchasing authority within departments for furniture, IT, and office supplies. Every unit does not need to keep their own stockpile of supplies, nor control possession over certain state assets. 
Roll out a "How-To" process to teach people how to digitize files so we can eliminate expensive rent spending for paper file storage. Maximize the use of office space by configuring smarter. 
Yes, use Lean Six Sigma principles. We teach it. Let's actually use it.
IDEA_TEXT: Centralize purchasing authority within departments for furniture, IT, and office supplies to reduce waste and streamline operations.

COMMENT: As an examiner, I worked with my colleagues located in Southern California to develop a suggestion box. Another examiner and I would collect the suggestions of our colleagues anonymously (because I believe some feared retaliation) and present them to upper management. This was discontinued because (I believe) the Upper Managers perceived this as a threat. Examiners were collaborating division-wide and providing constructive criticism and feedback and the "Managers" were threatened. As I consequence, I noticed we haven't had a division meeting since. Or, at least there hasn't been a division meeting where I was present.
IDEA_TEXT: Create anonymous suggestion boxes. Let staff share ideas without fear of retaliation and present them to upper management.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Organizational structure and culture; Communication and knowledge sharing; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal feedback; Management culture and leadership approach; Trust and openness

COMMENT:
Problem: 80/20 rule with staffing. 20% do the majority of the work, 80% do bare minimum. 

Solution: Radical, trailblazing idea to overhaul state service and incentivize employees at all levels. Throw out what you about state service entirely. Clean slate. Ready? Here is the concept: Departments have employment bands. Imagine layered cake - at the bottom is entry-level band, then above that is the supervisor band, the analyst band, manager and, chief band. That is it for positions. Each band incorporates all jobs for those positions. Every 8-12 weeks, staff can rotate to another job - let's call them "missions". To move up a band, you have to complete X number of missions and have led X number of missions successfully. Every employee must complete at least 2 missions annually. This kills silos, creates an innovative and growth mindset culture, creates competition, provides constant learning and gamification, etc. Each mission you complete you get a deliverable in hand to show the work you did with a score for your input. There is a list and those with the most missions and missions led, with good scores, are at the top, a constant changing leaderboard. This leaderboard is used to decide who moves up a level when there's an opening. Pay scales with tenure, not position, so you could decide not to move up a band. Folks would need to be focused, quick learners, with high accountability, excellent collaboration... tune in to my next post to see how we find these employees!
IDEA_TEXT: Create a new employment system with job bands and rotating missions. Staff rotate between different jobs every 8-12 weeks within their level. To move up, employees must complete and lead a set number of missions. Use a scoring system and leaderboard to track performance and decide promotions. Pay based on time served, not job title.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Career growth; Employee pay and benefits; Employee performance reviews; Job classification

COMMENT:
1. Use datamining to find broken or inefficient processes that need to be improved.

COMMENT:
Problem: Hiring excellent employees is a struggle and failing folks on probation is not common enough. let's reduce the waste in our largest spending bucket statewide - people and work to maximize the ROI on that expense by ensuring quality staff is hired. 

Solution: No more SOQs or interviews. Instead, we have customer experience workshops where sprints are designed to challenge and assess potential candidates. They will be scored on their creativity, problem-solving skills, curiosity, collaboration, accountability, leadership, etc. Those scoring a minimum score or above are offered positions where they may be best suited within the department. Structured rubrics with behavioral anchors and blind scoring could be used to reduce bias. Basically, show me the value you will bring to our teams. Innovative sprint outcomes may be adopted at the department and accredited to the new candidate as part of their company onboarding and give them a solid start in their careers.
IDEA_TEXT: Replace traditional job interviews and written questions with hands-on customer experience workshops. Score candidates on creativity, problem-solving, teamwork, and leadership skills using structured rubrics and blind scoring to reduce bias.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Process improvement; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Hiring and recruitment; Internal feedback; Process design and methodologies

COMMENT:
California has many managers within the state government. However, there are few leaders amongst the managers from my viewpoint. And the staff of these managers have no voice. This causes both the management and teams members to stagnate and lose sight of serving the public; eventually many work environments become stressful and toxic.  

My idea is to one: give the voice to the team members as this forum does, but allow them to rate their managers or supervisors yearly. Two: measure the amount of turnover on a yearly basis under the tenure of a manager or supervisor. Managers, could be put on yearly training assignments where they can serve in different state organizations. Based on the metrics after 2 or 3 years use the metrics to determine if a manager is effective. If a manager doesn't prove to be effective after this tenure they should be released, re-assigned or demoted. If they are measured to be effective, can they can then be assigned to a permanent position but can be re-evaluated after another 3 years.

This is the basis of my idea. Currently, when a person occupies a managerial seat, there is very little to prevent them from becoming a bottle-neck to progress and there's nothing the team can do about this. I've seen millions of tax payers dollars go to waste because of this scenario. I have seen many managers who are micro-manage out of fear and ignorance and the staff is at their mercy which is a moral killer. 
There is no perfect system but we need new direction
IDEA_TEXT: Create a manager evaluation system. Allow staff to rate managers yearly, track turnover rates under each manager, and require managers to complete training assignments in different state organizations. Use these metrics over 2-3 years to determine if managers are effective. Remove, reassign, or demote ineffective managers after this trial period.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Management culture and leadership; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee performance reviews; Internal feedback; Leadership review and oversight; Manager training

COMMENT:
Problem: CA Citizens’ data is not centralized causing errors, duplicative entry, high costs

Solution: Centralize CA Citizens' data. All departments will pull and push to same data warehouse. This way, CA government can do better to ensure that citizens receive the services they apply for, reduce fraud, simplify data management, reduce public frustration, reduce data storage costs statewide. And, with this in place, AI could help inform citizens when they qualify for services and even automatically enroll them. This is a better way to serve Californians.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a central data warehouse for all California citizen information. All state departments would use the same system to store and access resident data. This would reduce errors, cut costs, prevent fraud, and help residents get services faster. The system could use AI to automatically tell people about services they qualify for and enroll them.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Communication and knowledge sharing; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: AI and large language models; Cross-agency collaboration; Public service delivery and responsiveness; Shared data; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT:
Californians are often confused by state laws and regulations and often have difficulty understanding what steps to take to accomplish certain transactions. As such, they resort to randomly calling any available state number they can find and/or submitting large amounts of unnecessary documentation to random state email addresses. This frustrates both the customer and the state employees on the receiving end of the calls and emails.
CA government agencies and departments must do a better job in creating easy to use websites, forms, and portals where information is presented in an understandable format.
IDEA_TEXT: Create easy-to-use websites, forms, and portals with clear information that residents can understand.

IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digital services; Plain language

COMMENT: I've found it so insane that we have to rely on another department to move up and/or down positions in another department all because of "seniority". One person who has shown and put themselves out there more than another and that person don't get anything because they don't have the seniority, but that other person will get a position they don't deserve.  It's not fair at all. Also, too I feel like if a person accepts a management position they should be required to takes classes and learn how to appropriately talk to and handle staff. Multiple complaints and nothing to be done because of "seniority" again. This program or "ways" need to be looked at again. Ultimately it should depend on management to determine who moves up or down.
IDEA_TEXT: Replace seniority-based promotion system with merit-based system where managers decide who gets promoted based on performance and qualifications.

COMMENT: When I first joined the state employee workforce, I was told how easy it is to advance through the ranks, but in my experience it was harder to advance unless I was willing to leave my branch/division/department. I think that promoting in place should be implemented more when employees have been in a particular place long enough. This requires management to be more open to and learned on the solutions available. But it also requires more of a team mentality and that is hard when people have to keep moving in order to advance. I think providing more genuine opportunities for colleagues to work and grow together will help.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow more promotions within the same department or division. Train managers on available promotion options and create genuine opportunities for staff to work and grow together as a team.

COMMENT: I am writing a process and procedure guide for everything my team does, because "ask so-and-so" is not a procedure. Also, "so-and-so" eventually retires! I have also streamlined some of the processes under my purview to eliminate redundancy, updated our forms to meet current needs, and developed an online file sharing site for our team, eliminating the need to store any paper files for our unit. COMMENT:
Because of the high cost of housing around cities where most jobs and government offices are located, there is substantial inequality of opportunity for many Californians who cannot afford to live in metro centers. By shifting to remote offerings, such as the ability to participate in hearings and government decisions by videoconference, we can ensure that people who live in more distant (and often more affordable) areas are given equal opportunities to have their voices heard and contribute to shared goals.

Remote offerings further align with California’s climate goals, avoiding tons of CO₂ that would otherwise be generated as Californians waste time and money driving to formerly in-person meetings. Videoconference offerings also help us include Californians with disabilities who may struggle to join us in person due to challenges with transit and accessibility. 

Since the COVID-19 pandemic, many state agencies have vastly enlarged the group of individuals who can participate through the change to remote and videoconference meetings and hearings. This conversion to a more accessible meeting style should be expanded so more people can benefit.
IDEA_TEXT: Expand remote and videoconference options for government hearings and meetings. Allow people to participate from home to increase access for those in distant areas, people with disabilities, and support climate goals.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Public-facing services and resident experience; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accessibility; Environmental sustainability; Public participation; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT:
Before the pandemic, many administrative hearings were required to be in person. Since videoconference hearings became available, the vast majority of parties have chosen to utilize them. This allows individuals to take an hour or two off work instead of an entire day and avoid having to find childcare because they can participate from home. People save significant time by eliminating the need to drive or fly to a hearing, in addition to the carbon emissions from those trips. This change saves average Californians and small businesses hundreds, if not thousands, of dollars.

It also saves the state money. California agencies that pay for hearings no longer have to reimburse participants for the time and mileage spent traveling to hearings. If there is a default, those costs are completely wasted. Agencies that shift to remote offerings can make a substantial impact by reducing travel costs and minimizing waste, particularly important in our current fiscal climate.
IDEA_TEXT: Expand videoconference hearings for administrative proceedings. Allow people to attend hearings remotely instead of requiring in-person attendance to save time and money for both participants and the state.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digital services; Public policy initiatives; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT: Many of the challenges California faces stem from the high cost of housing and rent. This has been exacerbated by landlords who are not responding to market signals and who keep rent prices high, even when units sit vacant. California desperately needs a statewide vacancy tax, along with stronger measures to break up real estate monopolies that are driving up costs and making it harder for residents to find affordable places to live and start businesses.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a statewide vacancy tax on empty rental units and break up real estate monopolies to lower housing costs.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT:
Return to office has been a massive waste in taxpayer dollars and employee time, even the two day a week mandate. We should not be attempting to prop up downtown businesses and real estate portfolios of people who donate large amounts to political campaigns on the backs of the taxpayers and state employees. 

As a state that prides itself in technological innovation and leading the way to fight climate change we should be mandating telework as much as possible. The rule should be if you don't need to physically do something in the office (open mail, hand sign/deliver documents) you should be working from home.
IDEA_TEXT: Mandate telework for all jobs that do not require physical office tasks like opening mail or hand-delivering documents.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: Problem: In many state departments, including my own, productivity is often hindered by rigid processes where documents must pass through multiple layers of review—sometimes six or more—before finalization. This not only delays critical deliverables but can render documents outdated or irrelevant by the time they are approved. Additionally, there is a noticeable gap in collaboration and communication between subject matter experts (SMEs) and executive leadership. This disconnect often leads to a reactive environment where teams are constantly putting out fires instead of proactively identifying and addressing potential issues. Solution: Introduce a tiered review system that empowers mid-level managers and SMEs to approve routine documents without requiring executive-level sign-off. This would reduce bottlenecks, increase accountability, and allow leadership to focus on strategic priorities. Implementing digital workflow tools with built-in version control and approval tracking can also enhance transparency and speed. Establish regular cross-functional strategy sessions between SMEs and executives to align on goals, anticipate challenges, and co-create solutions. This would shift the culture from reactive to proactive, encouraging innovation and long-term planning. Additionally, investing in leadership training that emphasizes trust in employee expertise and decision-making can help break down hierarchical barriers and build a more agile, responsive organization.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a tiered review system where mid-level managers can approve routine documents without executive sign-off. Use digital workflow tools with version control and approval tracking.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Management culture and leadership approach; Process design and methodologies

COMMENT: As a former private sector employee, I would say that state processes are much slower to change in order to keep up with existing technology. We have fewer instances of process documentation and very little consistent messaging around project processes and procedures. This is sometimes passed off as "nuance" between projects but the procedures should be largely the same for substantively similar tasks and no task is so nuanced that it can not be documented for the staff working on a specific project. Training is often one person showing another with no supervisor oversight or written documentation, so failures in a process persist through generations of staff. Some processes are intentionally obscured to mask waste or inefficiency, where they could and should be transparent and streamlined. Much of our technology was established in the 1990s and has never been updated or improved, despite being obsolete. Tech staff retention needs to be addressed and change management for technology needs to be better handled. Rank and file staff needs to be empowered to learn and understand technology. By reducing turnover in IT, training staff and providing written manuals for processes, and improving technological literacy we can bring our processes into the 2000s.
IDEA_TEXT: Create written process guides and training manuals. Document all procedures so staff can follow clear steps instead of relying on word-of-mouth training.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Process documentation
