COMMENT:
I wrote a manual for my project that was originally intended to just help me learn and remember the procedures, but it's become a team reference guide that we are constantly adding to and updating. It's been extremely helpful in getting new staff up to speed and has been shared with supervisors and project management to help them understand work that analysts do.
My project team meets monthly to talk about ways to make our project more effective and efficient. We sometimes will review documents together and make edits in real time so that we all agree with process updates.
IDEA_TEXT: Create team reference manuals that new staff can use to learn procedures quickly.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Onboarding new employees; Process documentation

COMMENT: I would like to address recruitment and retention issues from within the public sector. Lately, public sector workers have been losing important benefits that are used to attract high quality talent and leadership. Using tools like work from home flexibility and remote work will allow state units to attract top talent from sectors that are reverting back to RTO mandates.
IDEA_TEXT: Use work from home and remote work options to attract top talent from private companies that are requiring return to office.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Hiring and recruitment; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT:
As a California resident I'm very angry that my tax dollars are being spent on office leases that are unnecessary when the vast majority of work can be done from a remote location. I'm frustrated by traffic, I'm worried about air and noise pollution, and the offices we work in are disgusting. One of the toilets has been out of order for over a month now. 
I don't feel that collaboration needs to happen face-to-face every single day and the excuses we get from leadership are just an attempt to spin the truth: We're subsidizing billionaire real estate lords.
IDEA_TEXT: Reduce office space costs by allowing more remote work instead of paying for unnecessary office leases.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Office management; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT:
I have been working with a training team to revamp onboarding for new hires in our branch. The current training is 6-7 hours of watching and listening to PowerPoints every day for months. It's crazy. Luckily, my unit is filled with former teachers and educators who are working on great curriculum redesign to decrease the onboarding time AND make the training way more engaging.

In addition, my unit works on professional development programs that take folks from new hires... to advancement and promotion. It's important to upskill and capture the talents of folks who show promise from within the branch. Professional development is still undervalued within my branch.
IDEA_TEXT: Expand professional development programs to help new hires advance and get promoted. Focus on finding and developing talent from within the branch.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Career growth; Employee retention; Employee training

COMMENT:
I believe that California's state workforce is encumbered by extremely hierarchical structures that impede its ability to adjust to change. Bad ideas come down at the speed of light. Good ideas float up and die at a glacial pace.

I'll also echo an additional comment I see. Many leaders at the highest level act out of fear rather than a collaborative effort towards improvement. They seek out numbers that validate their priors and generate reports that make themselves look GREAT... even if the ultimate endpoints of service are stagnant or getting worse.
IDEA_TEXT: Change leadership approach. Train leaders to work together instead of acting out of fear and only looking for data that makes them look good.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Management culture and leadership; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal feedback; Management culture and leadership approach; Trust and openness

COMMENT:
More flexible public transportation. 
Real income tax is about 42%. That what I see discounted from each of my check.
And from whatever is leftover I pay many other taxes at general stores, gas stations and any other services. Taxation is outrages.
IDEA_TEXT: Make public transportation more flexible.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public transportation

COMMENT:
In my role I've observed decades worth of files and data being digitally stored that no one has any meaningful use for or knowledge of to make the data worth retaining. There appears to be no interest, energy, or time available to reorganize, archive, and/or eliminate this unnecessary data and it's an accepted nuisance that any file search results may provide both current and 25 year old documents. 

I see this as an inefficiency for two reasons: the monetary cost of securing and maintaining storage for these files that by default are considered valueless and the obscuring effect these unhelpful documents have when trying to find information.

I think files should be audited more regularly to ensure content is kept highly organized and easily discoverable, with unhelpful 'junk' documents archived or eliminated to reduce what is being retained year after year.
IDEA_TEXT: Audit files regularly to keep data organized and easy to find. Archive or delete old unhelpful documents to reduce storage costs and improve search results.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Process documentation; State cloud storage

COMMENT: We contract way too much to outside vendors who offer snake oil in terms of professional development and process improvement. We need to do better harvesting great ideas from within the public sector instead of relying on external vendors who do not know us. Here's  an idea for sure! Offer a Project Management pathway for professional development that is state-certified! We shouldn't need to go through private vendors to certify that we know how to do project management...
IDEA_TEXT: Create a state-certified Project Management pathway for professional development. Stop requiring staff to use private vendors for project management certification.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Career growth; Contracts and vendors; Employee training

COMMENT:
One of the biggest challenges the state faces is people feeling they will be chastised for speaking out and the lack of communication between high level management/executives and the people actually doing the work (not managers overseeing work being done, but the actual people at department and agency level doing the work) along with the vendor community seeking to contract with the state. Politicians pushing their objectives and the people making the rules and regulations have either never done the work or haven't done it in so long they do not understand the impact of the policies and procedures, or whether it will make sense to the end user applying the rule. Which also impacts having qualified training (or lack thereof). State training is normally developed and administered by people who are far removed from having done the work. Maybe there should be more in person, role-based training with SME's to assist. Maybe if each department had 1 non-management person (kind of like a SB/DVBE advocate) from each department/agency be a procurement representative to present department issues at a bimonthly forum (like SCAN).  
There should procurement meetings for the department/agency representatives where new laws that have been enacted can be reviewed to ensure understanding where they can have a voice to speak about how those are impacting their jobs, etc.
IDEA_TEXT: Add role-based training with subject matter experts. Have people who actually do the work help create and teach training programs.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Qualified staff

COMMENT: One of the most inefficient things my department sees is the push to transition to electric vehicles.  We have staff all throughout the state, and charging stations (at least stations that work) are not always available to our staff. Also, it takes staff several hours to charge electric vehicles. The Ford Lightning electric truck can take up to 14 hours to charge, so while it's charging we're either down a vehicle and that workload or we have to force other staff in a gas truck to double-up on their route and we need to pay them overtime. The First Purchasing Mandate says that we can request an exemption from an EV from DGS once we meet our 50% mandate, but DGS says they're being pushed to put more state EVs on the road because of the Governor's executive order so they deny the exemption requests.  What are departments supposed to do?  Pay their staff overtime to do the work a person in an EV can't?  Pay for a hotel and meals when field staff are stranded because they can't charge their vehicle?  Rent short-term rentals to try and offset the downtime with EVs?  None of this is efficient, and it costs double.  Also, has anyone done an analysis on the cost of an EV vs the cost of a gas vehicle?  Usually about 2-3 times as expensive for an EV when departments are also facing budget reductions. This seems like such a waste of taxpayer money, to make government more inefficient and more expensive so citizens pay more and get less.  Can DGS be more helpful on exemption requests?
IDEA_TEXT: Make DGS more helpful with electric vehicle exemption requests when charging infrastructure is not available or causes major delays.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Environmental sustainability; Public participation; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT: Wasteful government spending - such as return to office mandates. Also, spending resources creating a platform like this that is supposed to be for state workers but that is going to prohibit state workers/Californians from speaking on return to office mandates.
IDEA_TEXT: End return to office mandates to reduce wasteful government spending.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: It doesn't make sense that state departments who have the same IT needs all have to separately go through the IT process with DGS and CDT.  Electronic timesheets for example.  All departments need them, shouldn't there be some sort of standard system?  Several are using Tempo but must all go through the same business analysis, requirements identification, development, etc so that they capture the state's pay rules, tie into FI$Cal, etc.  If there can be a statewide effort on projects like this so they're only done once and not done individually by every department that wants to streamline a process?
IDEA_TEXT: Create standard statewide IT systems for common needs like electronic timesheets. Have one shared system instead of each department doing separate business analysis, requirements, and development work.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Communication and knowledge sharing; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Shared resources; Software and tools; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT: Sharing data amongst agencies is cumbersome and often requires emails and updates to multiple siloed databases. If we had interagency databases shared amongst the agencies that need access to that data we could eliminate duplicate databases and generate reports in real time automatically.
IDEA_TEXT: Create shared databases between agencies. Allow agencies that need the same data to access one database instead of keeping separate copies.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Technology and digital innovation; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Shared data; Shared resources

COMMENT: I have learned to become a SharePoint site manager to collaborate more effectively with outside stakeholders in order to more quickly share identified deficiencies and have them corrected, while still maintaining data safety and security. Due to our specific use case, our department's SharePoint administrator had to learn how to use a broader range of features in order to provide secure access to users outside of State service.
IDEA_TEXT: Train staff to become SharePoint site managers to work better with outside partners and share information more safely and quickly.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Employee training; Shared data; Software and tools

IDEA_TEXT: Focus more on technology and allow remote work for IT jobs to boost productivity. Explore tech upgrades for SCO and Medi-Cal systems.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Process improvement; Technology and digital innovation

COMMENT:
We are still using some tools and technologies in the State Government that are very old. There are numerous innovative technologies available on the market that can be integrated into the State Government's business, enhancing efficiency and effectiveness in delivering services and projects.

Explore possible innovative technologies on the market, conduct risk analysis, and take intelligent risks to implement them within the State Government. Start with some pilot projects, and if they prove useful, implement them across the State Government.
IDEA_TEXT: Explore and implement new technologies in state government. Start with pilot projects to test innovative tools, then expand successful ones statewide after conducting risk analysis.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Technology and digital innovation; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Process design and methodologies; Risk aversion; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT: training and communication. Mentorships and specific training classes available for each job role in order to advance up the chain and do the current job better.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve training and communication. Create mentorship programs and job-specific training classes to help staff advance and do their current jobs better.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Internal communication; Mentorship programs

COMMENT:
A major problem in California’s state workforce is the inefficiency caused by outdated hiring and workplace policies. Rigid minimum qualifications and slow/ouutdated HR protocols block many talented candidates with modern skills from entering public service, which limits innovation and slows progress.

An even larger issue is the state’s approach to workplace flexibility. Despite state audits and extensive research showing that remote work increases productivity, saves the state millions in office space costs, reduces emissions, and improves work-life balance, some leaders continue to enforce return-to-office mandates. This is hypocritical for a government that speaks of innovation, equity, and efficiency while ignoring evidence that remote work achieves those goals more effectively than traditional office models.

To address these challenges: First, modernize hiring by replacing outdated minimum requirements with skills-based assessments and streamlined digital applications. Second, adopt remote-first policies that align with research and fiscal responsibility, prioritizing cost savings, efficiency, and fairness for workers. Third, strengthen protections against retaliation and ensure leadership training emphasizes equity, accountability, and inclusion.

By modernizing hiring and committing to evidence-based remote-first practices, California can move past outdated systems and demonstrate it is serious about equity, innovation, and effective public service.
IDEA_TEXT: Adopt remote-first policies that prioritize cost savings, efficiency, and fairness for workers based on research evidence.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Public-facing services and resident experience; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Public policy initiatives; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT:
In order to implement process improvement, you have to be empowered to do so.  There are several areas the state could benefit and save money, (for example, state contracting from solicitation to management of the resulting agreement.), but they are often overshadowed by a higher agenda (whether political or executive).  
There are processes and procedures the state has in place to guide in the assistance of contracting overall. Reinforcing your people's value and empowering them to take ownership and accountability often allow for innovative ideas or improvements to be birthed while doing the work.  Engaging the people doing the work, asking thought provoking questions and providing a safe space will allow for overall improvements to be brought forth.
IDEA_TEXT: Empower staff to make process improvements. Give employees ownership and accountability to create innovative ideas, especially in state contracting from start to finish.

COMMENT:
Strategic investment planning for state buildings. Following the same standards we make private sector landlords follow. Currently state buildings are given an allotment that is the same year after year. The allotment is not calculated by taking any factors into consideration like inflation, rising interest rates, cost of living increases. 
The process has slowly driven state buildings into the ground. Old carpet, failing and obsolete building systems all adding up to energy waste and pollution.  
If you owned a home and rented it to the same tenant for 25 years and put 2% of the rent back into the property in that time, what would it look like? Not good is the answer. 
Currently DGS only puts back 2% into state buildings! That number needs to be 10% annually or more on older assets.  Convicted slum lords in California hover around 5% reinvestment and are put into Receivership Court by their banks. We can do better!
Currently DGS takes 98% of the rental income and spends it on unrelated programs. 
The buildings should get the funding they need before the landlord takes the revenue to spend on other things. The income from a building should first cover its expenses before a landlord takes profits. The practice of a property manager spending the rental income of an asset on other things is called Comingling of Funds. it is illegal and the cause of run-down slums. The plan DGS has been to run them down and then renovate at 200 times the cost of doing regular maintenance ovr time
IDEA_TEXT: Increase state building maintenance funding from 2% to 10% or more annually. Calculate building budgets based on inflation, interest rates, and cost of living instead of using the same amount each year.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Physical infrastructure

COMMENT:
As a Californian and state employee, one idea I would add is the need for stronger accountability in leadership. Many employees see innovative, research-backed proposals—like expanding remote work, modernizing hiring, or streamlining processes—dismissed not because they lack merit, but because leadership defaults to outdated practices or prioritizes optics over efficiency. This discourages staff from raising ideas and reinforces a culture where “do as you’re told” outweighs evidence-based improvement.

I also believe the state should re-examine how closely it aligns with corporate pressures. For example, enforcing return-to-office mandates to mirror private sector demands undermines both efficiency and equity, especially when research and state audits clearly show that remote work saves millions in costs, improves productivity, and reduces emissions. It is difficult for employees to trust in California’s commitment to innovation when decisions conflict with the very data the state commissions.

From my perspective as a Californian, a government that models fairness, inclusion, and modern workplace practices builds greater public trust. Investing in remote-first policies, skills-based hiring, and leadership accountability would not only improve the employee experience but also deliver better outcomes for the people of California.
IDEA_TEXT: Invest in modern workplace practices. Implement remote-first policies, skills-based hiring, and leadership accountability to build public trust and improve outcomes.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Public-facing services and resident experience; Workplace operations; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership approach; Public service delivery and responsiveness; Remote work and return to office; Trust and openness

COMMENT:
The Federal Patriot Act makes it very difficult for homeless Californians and Californians who have to live in cars, to open a bank account.  However, homeless Californians still need a bank account to apply for housing, obtain services, and apply for jobs and benefits.  I would do something to make sure every Californian can open some type of bank account.  

My Idea:
If that means providing an alternate way to verify the identity of homeless people, approved by the state, I would implement that.  I would not require a residential address to open a bank account if I knew someone had been homeless.  This may require federal approval.  Giving homeless people a way to identify themselves helps them apply for public services.  This is an important barrier to ending all homelessness.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a state-approved way for homeless people to prove their identity and open bank accounts without needing a home address.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public policy initiatives; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT:
I suggest the state adapt a “classification roadmap” that guides state employees on a general career path, differing from the Upward Mobility Program, this roadmap guides from classification to classification, i.e, as a Staff Services Analyst, what courses CalHR, higher education, training events, and skills are recommended to progress to Associate Government Program Analyst. 
Naturally, this would lead to how to progress from AGSA, to SSMI; from SSMI to SSMII; etc. the training, courses, education, recommended reading.
Another example, how to progress from Office Technician, to Business Services, etc.
The classification roadmaps could include the Departments that have the specific classifications and recommendations for “assignments” as part of your career path, i.e., a new to the state employee may want to start at a smaller department, then progress as an AGPA to a medium department. 
Granted this would be a massive undertaking and a classification duty statement can vary greatly based off the agency and its mission, however, I think it could be beneficial to provide that general path as some employees may lack the mentorship from their current department or supervisors or generally not know how to create a path to career within the state.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a classification roadmap system that guides state employees on career paths between job classifications. Include recommended courses, training, education, skills, and department assignments for each progression step from entry-level to advanced positions.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Career growth; Employee training; Job classification

COMMENT:
I work with my team to document everything digitally and cut the paper out of our workflow. We also use tracking for routed documents, so we don't have to call people to see status, and we get real-time feedback. We also use templates for as much as possible since there's so much "Paperwork" that goes with doing anything. We don't want to recreate the wheel. 
The team I supervise also works well independently and meets on their own to calibrate which I think gives them more autonomy and buy-in to do their work with fidelity, accountability, and consistency. 
We also rotate assignments when new projects emerge, and everyone is always willing to chip in. It's all very team oriented. 
We utilize teams to share projects where multiple people are working on it, since we tend to have connection issues with our network occasionally (both in the office and out of the office). It allows us to keep moving forward when having access issues.  For some reason, we tend to have a lot of internet or network failings when IN the office, so we also use flexibility to meet offsite to complete projects when necessary. It assists with morale, but also gets the work done efficiently.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow flexible work locations when office internet fails to maintain productivity and morale.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee retention; Office management; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT:
I work with my team to document everything digitally and cut the paper out of our workflow. We also use tracking for routed documents, so we don't have to call people to see status, and we get real-time feedback. We also use templates for as much as possible since there's so much "Paperwork" that goes with doing anything. We don't want to recreate the wheel. 
The team I supervise also works well independently and meets on their own to calibrate which I think gives them more autonomy and buy-in to do their work with fidelity, accountability, and consistency. 
We also rotate assignments when new projects emerge, and everyone is always willing to chip in. It's all very team oriented. 
We utilize teams to share projects where multiple people are working on it, since we tend to have connection issues with our network occasionally (both in the office and out of the office). It allows us to keep moving forward when having access issues.  For some reason, we tend to have a lot of internet or network failings when IN the office, so we also use flexibility to meet offsite to complete projects when necessary. It assists with morale, but also gets the work done efficiently.
IDEA_TEXT: Give teams more independence to meet and work together on their own to improve accountability.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Communication and knowledge sharing; Governance and oversight
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Cross-agency collaboration; Management culture and leadership approach

COMMENT: In my division of my department, we have a database to track projects that is so old it uses a platform that is no longer supported. When we tried in the past to replace this with a more functional, modern system, there were so many roadblocks that it was impossible to implement the new system. We are trying again.  My suggestion is to make modernizing or upgrading old computer systems easier. While I understand that it can be costly to do so, having old barely functioning systems is even more costly to the citizens of California.
IDEA_TEXT: Make it easier to upgrade old computer systems. Remove roadblocks that prevent departments from replacing outdated systems with modern ones.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Bureaucracy; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT:
The state faces many problems that telework could help alleviate. The state of CA can be a more attractive employer. We could lead in the space instead of trying follow the trends of the private sector. We have a completely different responsibility to the people we serve. Our "profits" are our efficiency and how well we manage our budgets and tax dollars.  We could lower cost, by reducing footprints. We can reduce environmental impacts by reducing commutes. Department should evaluate work and task that could be done via telework and in person. 

With a concerted effort, managers can be prepared, trained, and supported. Expectations can be clear and departments can maintain flexibility. 

In Person activities should encouraged, scheduled, and deliberate in with goals such as increasing collaboration/comradery. Trainings, in person-meetings, etc. should have the appropriate facilities available. 

Finally, I think there should be more data available to make better decisions. Surveys for staff and managers to find gaps. Departmental specific dashboards etc.
IDEA_TEXT: Collect data to improve decisions. Survey staff and managers to find gaps and create department dashboards.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Process improvement; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Internal feedback; Shared data; Software and tools

COMMENT: Postage and shipping costs. When we are able to provide resources electronically with confirmation receipt, we can eliminate mail, i.e., Family Medical Leave Act (FMLA), California Family Rights Act (CFRA) and Pregnancy Disability Leave (PDL), Workers' Compensation (WC). This will save the state budget in time used, paper and postage used, and shredding the resources after they are saved electronically.
IDEA_TEXT: Switch to electronic delivery for Family Medical Leave Act (FMLA), California Family Rights Act (CFRA), Pregnancy Disability Leave (PDL), and Workers' Compensation (WC) documents with confirmation receipts to save on postage, paper, and processing time.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Expense reimbursement; Process design and methodologies

COMMENT: There's a saying as a state worker, sink or swim. It would be nice to have an onboarding process, academy perhaps, that trains newbies on not just policies and their role, but all of the amazing benefits offered to state employees. A lot of the times, new state workers are not aware and don't take advantage. It would also be nice for in depth training from staff members assigned to train and not take away from their regular duties.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a comprehensive onboarding program or academy for new state workers. Train them on policies, job duties, and employee benefits. Assign dedicated training staff who focus only on training without other job duties.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Manager training; Onboarding new employees

COMMENT: There are very few state employes that work in my job classification, so I think there is a lack of understanding of what we can do under our scope of practice to provide safe, timely, effective, efficient, equitable, and patient centered care. In my role, I have developed new policies and protocols, but without proper understanding of what we can contribute, we will continue to be underutilized.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve understanding of job roles and scope of practice. Help leadership learn what different employee types can do to provide better patient care and use staff skills more effectively.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Job classification; Management culture and leadership approach; Qualified staff

I can show up to the place that provides for me financially with a grateful heart and expect it to be a growing environment. If it is not, I can use my resources to effect change.

COMMENT: I think every department/office/unit should have someone who answers the phone and can talk with constituents to either answer their question or direct them to the right person or another agency. We answer our phones, and people are always so grateful and somewhat surprised. Then we refer them to another office that maybe isn't so helpful and doesn't have a person answering the phone, and they are disappointed.  I know I personally get very frustrated going through phone trees when I have to call a retailer or other service provider. State agencies are supposed to serve our citizens and we should do that gladly and always. Phone numbers (and emails) should be up front and easy to find on all state websites.
IDEA_TEXT: Assign staff to answer phones in every department and office. Make phone numbers and emails easy to find on all state websites.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digital services; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT: Hiring the right people is greatly limited due to workplace headquarter designations. Ideas only come from major cities and not tribal lands and other diverse communities. Hiring should be opened up to the whole state and HQ designations should be available wherever you live.
IDEA_TEXT: Remove workplace headquarters requirements. Allow state hiring from anywhere in California including tribal lands and diverse communities.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Organizational structure and culture; Workplace operations; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Hiring and recruitment; Inclusion and diversity; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: Leaders in positions of power who can either support or discard an idea or suggestion for legislation should learn to LISTEN to those of us who make the suggestion. We are not just throwing out a wild idea for no reason. We are asking for support that is necessary for us to accomplish our mission. When you say "nope" and disregard the reasons and explanations provided, you are just killing an idea for the sake of killing it. Then, our jobs are more difficult because our idea can't be implemented.  We are the ones in the "trenches" doing the work and we KNOW what we need to be better or keep up the work. You're not even in the room, so you can't possibly know.  We are the SMEs! Please listen to us.
IDEA_TEXT: Train leaders to listen to and consider ideas from front-line staff who are subject matter experts in their work areas.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Management culture and leadership; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal feedback; Management culture and leadership approach; Trust and openness

COMMENT:
I believe the core issue lies in the lack of employee responsibility and accountability. Government bureaucracy is structured in a way that pushes decision-making upward through the chain of command, concentrating authority in the hands of a few. This results in an overabundance of policies and procedures that strip decision-making power from the subject matter experts—the individuals actually performing the hands-on work.

Furthermore, this system fosters a dense layer of middle management that often controls decisions without fully understanding their upstream or downstream impacts. This disconnect between decision-makers and practitioners undermines efficiency and responsiveness.

To truly improve government operations, this overarching management structure must be reformed. Such reform will likely require legislative action to decentralize authority, empower technical experts, and streamline bureaucratic processes.
IDEA_TEXT: Reform the management structure through legislative action. Decentralize authority to empower technical experts and reduce middle management layers that make decisions without understanding their impact.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Governance and oversight; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Align policy and implementation; Management culture and leadership approach; Org structure and hierarchy

COMMENT:
Since joining the State, I’ve encountered significant resistance when trying to introduce more efficient and effective practices. Coming from the private sector, I brought fresh ideas and a results-oriented mindset, but the reception from the Department and Branch has been far from enthusiastic.

Many individuals seem deeply resistant to change, clinging to legacy processes even when they no longer serve the mission. It often feels like trying to push a rope uphill—effort without traction.

For any meaningful reform to take hold, management must embrace and model a commitment to efficiency across all levels and tasks. Without unified leadership and a shared vision for improvement, efforts to modernize how the State of California operates will struggle to gain momentum.
IDEA_TEXT: Management must lead and model a commitment to efficiency at all levels. Create unified leadership with a shared vision for improvement to help modernization efforts succeed.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Management culture and leadership; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership approach; Process design and methodologies; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT:
CDCR Fire needs a complete reorganization. Currently, multiple fire stations operate independently, answering to Plant Operations or Wardens. Each station procures its own PPE, hoses, tools, and follows local policies—resulting in a fragmented system with no standardization or unified effectiveness.

While CDCR Fire Stations at correctional institutions primarily serve an educational mission, that role should not diminish their potential to be a valuable resource for the State of California during emergencies

With proper structure, CDCR Fire could become a valuable asset in California’s wildfire, flood and disaster response efforts. Instead, its lack of coordination makes it a liability.

A centralized command starting in Sacramento is essential. Appointing a Fire Chief and Deputy Chief, supported by three Division Chiefs (North, Central, South), would create statewide oversight. Each fire station would have a Battalion Chief supervising Fire Captains, ensuring consistency and accountability.

This structure would allow Division Chiefs to standardize regional operations and report directly to Sacramento. It would also enable smarter use of state funds, prioritizing purchases based on verified needs. Battalion Chiefs would conduct assessments, Division Chiefs would review them, and Sacramento leadership would allocate resources accordingly.

If modeled after CAL FIRE, CDCR Fire could evolve into a highly effective emergency response force—ready, reliable, and unified.
IDEA_TEXT: Reorganize CDCR Fire with centralized command structure. Create a Fire Chief and Deputy Chief in Sacramento, with three Division Chiefs for North, Central, and South regions. Each fire station gets a Battalion Chief to supervise Fire Captains. Standardize equipment purchasing, policies, and operations across all stations to improve emergency response effectiveness.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Budget and resources; Governance and oversight
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Org structure and hierarchy; Process design and methodologies; Shared resources

COMMENT: I believe there is a critical underutilization of the staff talent within the State, and before hiring additional personnel, the existing team dynamics should be analyzed to see how work can be better distributed amongst those already on board.
IDEA_TEXT: Analyze team dynamics and redistribute work better among current staff before hiring new people.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Organizational structure and culture; Governance and oversight
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Org structure and hierarchy; Understaffing; Work culture

COMMENT: I believe California's tax burden has become overwhelmingly excessive. From income tax and property tax to sales tax, fuel taxes (including those earmarked for Caltrans and high-speed rail), sin taxes, and luxury taxes—the list goes on. And that’s not even accounting for the numerous fees we’re hit with, like DMV registration and licensing fees. When you consider the full scope of these costs, it’s clear that a substantial portion of our hard-earned wages is being siphoned away. It’s time for a serious conversation about reducing the financial strain on everyday Californians.
IDEA_TEXT: Reduce California's tax burden. Lower income tax, property tax, sales tax, fuel taxes, sin taxes, luxury taxes, and government fees like DMV registration and licensing fees.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT:
We need to take a harder stance on crime. It is difficult to go into a store and see people walking out shoplifting with hundreds of dollars of merchandise and no one doing anything to stop it.   The State believes there is a lack of in-house shopping because there is a lack of people at work, however I personally do not want to shop where I do not feel safe. 

Also, increasing the minimum wage will not decrease inflation. Instead of increasing wages, how about we decrease the costs associated with a fair living.
IDEA_TEXT: Lower living costs instead of raising minimum wage to fight inflation.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: We are very poorly trained and lack operational consistency.  Every office is doing something different, every agency does the same process different ways, there needs to be a smoother process on how we do things in each agency.  There are too many hands in the pot, just to get something approved as simple as office supplies, it has a minimum of 5 people processing an order, we are spending more money in labor then what the supply request cost.  There should be statewide system for things like procurement, timesheets, basically any common function that is takes to run an agency.  It's too hard to come up with a solution to the problem because I don't know if anyone above rank and file want solutions.  There are to many EO, AEO, Deputy this and that.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a statewide system for common functions like procurement and timesheets.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Budget and resources; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Shared resources; Software and tools

COMMENT:
The procurement process is overly complicated, time consuming, and often not the most cost efficient.  For example, we are required to purchase chairs from CalPIA.  CalPIA charges $500+ for one desk chair and then another $30 per chair to deliver.  The same chair could be purchased from another retailer for a fraction of the price.  In addition, the ordering time is outrageous.  Purchases go through many levels of approval before getting to the vendor and many times the required vendor's turn around time is 60+ business days.  

Small businesses and disabled veteran-owned businesses get preference, and many times, these small businesses order from retailers like Amazon and then upcharge the State.  There is nothing cost efficient about the procurement process.
IDEA_TEXT: Simplify the procurement process to reduce costs and delivery times. Allow agencies to buy from cost-effective vendors instead of requiring expensive mandatory vendors like CalPIA.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Procurement

COMMENT: My program has procedures and desk manuals for every process. In all my years of service, I have never worked at another department that had desk/training manuals to help new hires. Manuals and procedures are updated regularly. Procedures are housed on our intranet, and desk manuals are in our shared drive. They also come in handy as reference materials when the SME is out of the office and their backup needs a refresher for completing a process.
IDEA_TEXT: Create detailed desk manuals and procedures for all work processes. Keep manuals updated and store them where all staff can access them easily.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Process documentation; Shared resources

COMMENT: There needs to be a better process for getting duplicate pay warrants issued. I often have employees waiting a month or more just to receive a replacement warrant. I like Cal Employee Connect but I think they could add functionality to it, giving the employee the ability to request their own duplicate warrant directly with the SCO.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve the process for getting replacement pay warrants. Add a feature to Cal Employee Connect so employees can request their own duplicate warrant directly from the State Controller's Office.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Technology and digital innovation; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Employee pay and benefits; Software and tools

COMMENT:
The State of California has a multitude of leased and owned office space that spans the entire state. Given the return-to-office mandate by the Governor (N-22-25), the state Department of General Services should allow State agencies to create a 'Hoteling' service/platform whereby office-based state employees from any agency can work 'in-office' at a State location convenient to them or for operational need.

The benefits could be numerous: 
Reduced commute times and easier schedules
GHG and emissions savings
Lower commute costs
Greater inter-agency collaboration
Greater state representation in the State's 58 counties
IDEA_TEXT: Create a hoteling service through the Department of General Services. Allow state workers from any agency to work at any state office location that is convenient for them.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Budget and resources; Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Office management; Remote work and return to office; Shared resources

COMMENT: I'd like to see all state departments prioritizing/resourcing the organizational development related work that we are tasked with as mission critical.  Far too often, effort such as strategic planning and business planning, State Leadership Accountability Act reporting, risk management, workforce and succession plans, engagement surveys and activities, change management process improvement, etc. fall off the plate as soon as something else is considered more important/critical.  It is often left to one area of the organization, delegated to staff as other duties as required (without proper training), and can even be sent to outside vendors.  If this work is required and important, then let's give it the priority and resources necessary to be successful and meaningful to the work we do in state service.
IDEA_TEXT: Make organizational development work a priority. Give proper resources and training for strategic planning, business planning, State Leadership Accountability Act reporting, risk management, workforce plans, engagement surveys, and process improvement instead of treating them as extra duties.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Organizational structure and culture; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Management culture and leadership approach; Process design and methodologies; Work culture

COMMENT: Streamlining and applying an all-digital timekeeping and payroll process, removing paper from the equation. Currently, hand transcription of attendance and payroll wastes many hours of state employee time across departments.
IDEA_TEXT: Switch to all-digital timekeeping and payroll. Remove paper forms and hand-written attendance records to save staff time across departments.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Technology and digital innovation; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Shared resources; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT: To make California’s government more effective and efficient, we need to prioritize human connection in the workplace. By creating safe spaces, motivating our employees, developing their skills, and consistently reminding them of the value they bring, we empower them to become the best version of themselves. When employees feel valued and cared for, they perform better, contribute more meaningfully, and help build a stronger, more effective government. I believe in dreaming big, staying positive, and starting small, because small actions often lead to the biggest impact.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve workplace culture to boost employee performance. Create safe spaces, motivate staff, develop their skills, and remind them of their value to help them do their best work.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Organizational structure and culture; Management culture and leadership; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee retention; Employee training; Management culture and leadership approach; Work culture

COMMENT:
What makes me proud about my role in public service is the opportunity to serve and make a meaningful difference in people’s lives. I take pride in helping individuals and teams discover their 'Why' both personally and organizationally. Therefore, they can connect with their purpose, love what they do, and ultimately make a greater impact. Supporting others in finding that clarity and alignment is incredibly fulfilling, and it’s what drives me every day. My personal Why is to make my grandma proud. 

To make California’s government more effective and efficient, we need to prioritize human connection in the workplace. By creating safe spaces, motivating our employees, developing their skills, and consistently reminding them of the value they bring, we empower them to become the best version of themselves. When employees feel valued and cared for, they perform better, contribute more meaningfully, and help build a stronger, more effective government. I believe in dreaming big, staying positive, and starting small, because small actions often lead to the biggest impact.

Yes, I’ve seen and contributed to more effective and efficient service within the State. One example from my own work involves helping departments align their workforce and succession planning efforts with their organizational goals. By guiding teams to identify their “Why” and connect it to their mission, we’ve improved employee engagement and clarity of purpose.
IDEA_TEXT: Help departments align workforce and succession planning with organizational goals. Guide teams to identify their purpose and connect it to their mission to improve employee engagement.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Governance and oversight
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Align policy and implementation; Employee retention; Employee training; Management culture and leadership approach

COMMENT:
As a California resident, I believe our government should reflect the diversity of our state. Representation matters, especially in leadership. We need more diverse women in leadership roles so they can help shape policies and decisions that reflect the needs and aspirations of all communities, including future generations.
In addition, we should embrace more innovative ways of working-leveraging technology, rethinking outdated processes, and encouraging creative problem-solving. Innovation, combined with inclusive leadership, can drive more effective, equitable, and forward-thinking public service.
IDEA_TEXT: Increase diverse women in leadership roles to better represent California's communities and shape policies for all residents.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Organizational structure and culture; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Inclusion and diversity; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: I would like to see less taxes on middle and lower classes.  I would like to see more benefits for California State employees, simple things like discounts on hotels, having funding to do things like monthly staff lunches, etc.  Things to boost employee morale.  I understand that we cannot get bonuses with taxpayer money but there should be a fund to celebrate and appreciate staff.  As of now, I pay out of my own pocket hundreds of dollars a year to buy lunches and do things to make my staff feel appreciated for their hard work.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a staff appreciation fund for state employees. Include simple benefits like hotel discounts and monthly staff lunches to boost morale.

IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Employee retention; Work culture

COMMENT: California desperately needs to fix the insurance crisis that’s preventing thousands from rebuilding their homes after the wildfires. The current Governor loves to talk about “inclusive decision-making” and “listening to survivors,” yet the number one barrier to recovery isn’t a lack of good ideas from state workers—it’s the fact that private insurers have fled high-risk areas, the FAIR Plan is overpriced and underpowered, and nothing substantial has been done to reform the state’s own outdated regulatory chokehold that created this mess.
IDEA_TEXT: Fix the insurance crisis by reforming state rules that caused private insurers to leave high-risk areas and improving the FAIR Plan to be more affordable and effective.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public policy initiatives; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT: The primary problem I see on a day-to-day basis is financial mismanagement and waste. Sometimes I wonder how much thought goes into whether my department in particular actually needs the things we contract for. I see a lot of money spent on things that I regard as questionable. I believe we really need to focus on using the talent contained within our own ranks. We contract out for services so often where I feel we can solve our own problems more effectively. Financial waste is a huge problem and I feel that we need to meet more to discuss options other than just throwing money at an issue. And we need to use the auditor hotline more frequently when we see waste such as this.
IDEA_TEXT: Use internal staff talent instead of outside contractors. Hold more meetings to discuss options before spending money on problems.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Process improvement; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Bureaucracy; Contracts and vendors; Internal feedback

COMMENT: Living in California has always been expensive, but it's just out of reach for many of us now. There is an extreme separation of upper and lower income families with the upper class families paying less in taxes than those who earn far less. Housing is not even close to being affordable for many of us. We also need more reliable public transportation. We also need to take a good look at how we care for our seniors. I have a family member who was recently denied for Cal Fresh even though he only has social security as a steay income and supports himself and 2 family members. They are barely making it yet he couldn't get any help. Perhaps it's time to reevaluate how much income is not keeping up with necessities in California.
IDEA_TEXT: Fix tax system so higher earners pay more than lower earners.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: I get assigned workload that managers know ahead of time requires a translator as I am not bilingual.  I can't figure out why the work can't go directly to the people who are bilingual and getting paid for that.  It takes double the time to complete the tasks when I have to ask coworkers to translate or call the translator.  I also don't think I am getting all the pertinent information when using translators.  The work that the translators are getting which is for English speakers could be assigned to me and others who aren't bilingual so as to not add work to anyone.
IDEA_TEXT: Assign translation work directly to bilingual staff who are paid for language skills. Give English-only work to non-bilingual staff to balance workloads.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Qualified staff; Translations

COMMENT: We have noticed how redundant and wasteful it seems to have each *program* in our office have their own printers/copiers (usually multiple per program). This is not an efficient use of resources or space. Why not just have a bank of printers & copiers per wing/suite/floor that all programs in that space can connect to? This would save $ on equipment, consumables (toner +/- paper), and maintenance while getting more out of each individual machine (less environmental impact). While I understand that programmatic budgets are distinct, why can't programs pay a proportional fee for use of the printing/copying equipment like we do for the office space, IT services, etc.? This would surely also save programs $ on wasted toner purchased for machines that become incompatible, outdated, and/or redundant. Streamlining in this way would also likely make machine maintenance and replacement easier.
IDEA_TEXT: Replace individual program printers with shared printer banks per wing or floor. Programs pay proportional fees for use like they do for office space and IT services.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Office management; Shared resources

COMMENT: I would like to see more attention and resources applied to the foundational infrastructure of our communities - such as roads, education, housing, food, and healthcare - over the "fluffy," feel-good projects that sap the State of resources while these other must-haves get more out of reach for many. I am all for new technology and protecting our environment! However, pet projects in these areas may not yield immediate benefits while the really tangible needs decline. We all see that - in the homelessness, hunger, bad roads, and declining literacy - around us. The high-speed rail line is a perfect example. To fulfill some desk-jockey fantasy, the design (even if well-intentioned) was very poorly thought out and neglects many realities on the ground. Sooo much $ is being thrown at it, when that $ could do far more good elsewhere. I *want* high speed rail, but not as it was erroneously conceived of and as the boondoggle it has become. Let's own up to the mistakes and correct them. Let's take care of more people's basic needs before embracing the next trend-setting pet project.
IDEA_TEXT: Prioritize funding for basic infrastructure like roads, education, housing, food, and healthcare over less essential projects.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT:
My ideas involve process improvement. Where are the bottlenecks in the processes and how can we resolve them. First, there should be increased access to information both internally and externally. Information is power and when we equip individuals with knowledge they may be able to solve problems. 

Next, reducing crime and fraud in the State systems. Implementing systems similar to those in the State Fund Special Investigation Unit (SIU) throughout the State in other agencies to spot fraud. Finally, looking for what areas we depend on the Federal Government and begin to reduce that dependency. Chart specifically where we receive the Federal Funding and begin to systematically self fund those areas. If we are not able to self fund look at reductions or creating alternative structures. As the 4th largest economy in the world we should be able to accomplish this task. 

Lastly, we need to create a State Insurance model for homeowners, auto and business insurance. We cannot be held hostage to national carriers that cherry pick and can chose to come and go from the California when it suits them. We can model rates and create programs that lead the way of the future in insurance modeling.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a state insurance model for homeowners, auto, and business insurance to reduce reliance on national carriers.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: As a long time, State employee and manager, I would like to see a more streamlined approach to purchasing and hiring.  The processes are constantly changing and many times when there are "Statewide contracts" (an example would be tires), there are not enough "official locations" to make the contract viable or easy to use. We could be more efficient if DGS would delegate that authority lower to each department or at agency level.  Same with hiring processes.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve statewide contracts by adding more approved locations. Make contracts like tire purchasing easier to use with more official vendor locations.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Procurement

COMMENT:
I work for the California Department of Corrections and Rehabilitation (CDCR).  One of the largest financial burdens of this department is the amount of movement made for inmates (i.e. transfers from one prison to a different prison).  Specifically, when an inmate's mental health level of care is changed to a "higher" level of care (i.e. to EOP, ICF, Acute, MHCB), the inmate is transferred "short-term" to a different prison.  This transfer takes multiple Correctional Officers driving across the state to take the inmate from Point A to Point B.  As the Mental Health level of care is temporary, they are transferred back (or to a new prison) within a short period of time.  The same inmates are transferred many times throughout the year, whenever their Mental Health changes. 

In my opinion, it would be cheaper, more cost effective, and more efficient, if each prison had the appropriate staff at that prison to treat that inmate's mental health level of care. So... don't transfer the inmate from Point A to Point B multiple times; keep the inmate at Point A, and have on-site staff treat the inmate.  

This would be a huge cost savings.
IDEA_TEXT: Keep inmates at their current prison and add mental health staff on-site instead of transferring inmates to different prisons for temporary mental health treatment.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Qualified staff

COMMENT: I haven't been here long enough to be empowered to make any changes, however, I feel that upper management should allow managers and others to make decisions. It sometimes feels as though most questions are "tabled" and takes quite some time to get an answer back. Some of the processes take extremely long to route through other departments, and when you ask for "timelines" they explain processes to you and avoid the "turn around" time questions. I've seen colleagues get reprimanded for quick responses to Sponsors and told by management Not to respond immediately. If you know the correct answer, that should be applauded and not reprimanded for it.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow staff to respond quickly to sponsors when they know the correct answer instead of punishing fast responses.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Organizational structure and culture; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal communication; Management culture and leadership approach; Trust and openness

COMMENT: I would like to see CA State Parks add a Veterans Day use pass just like National Parks (open to all veterans - peace time, war time, active duty or reserve/national guard).  The current Veterans pass for CA State Parks you must be: A medal of honor recipient, Prisoner of war survivor or a 100% disabled veteran.  This is a very slim portion of the veteran community and not helpful.  As a Veteran i think the being outdoors helps with our mental and physical health and should be encouraged.
IDEA_TEXT: Expand California State Parks Veterans Day pass to include all veterans, not just medal of honor recipients, prisoners of war, and 100% disabled veterans.

COMMENT:
PLAIN LANGUAGE INITIATIVE:

The Plain Language Initiative aims to make California government communications clear, accessible, and equitable. Too often, state forms, letters, and websites are filled with jargon and legalese that create confusion and barriers. By adopting plain-language standards, we can help Californians understand information the first time, reduce duplicate inquiries, and build stronger trust in government.

In the first nine months, the initiative would apply to high-volume documents such as forms, notices, FAQs, and websites, piloted in key agencies like DMV, HR, and EDD. Deliverables include a Plain Language Style Guide, staff training modules, rewritten sample documents, and a feedback dashboard to track resident experience.

Key objectives are to improve readability to an 8th-grade level or below, train 500+ employees, reduce duplicate public inquiries by 10–15%, and boost satisfaction scores by 15% within a year. With a modest budget of about $250K and existing staff expertise, this project is small enough to implement quickly but large enough to show measurable impact.

This initiative directly supports Governor Newsom’s priorities to modernize government, increase equity, and improve service delivery—demonstrating that clearer communication leads to better outcomes for Californians.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a Plain Language Initiative to make government communications clear and accessible. Develop a style guide, train 500+ staff, rewrite high-volume documents like forms and websites to 8th grade reading level, and pilot in key agencies like DMV and EDD to reduce duplicate inquiries and improve customer satisfaction.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Public-facing services and resident experience; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Plain language; Process design and methodologies; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT: I would like to see more avenues for career development and cross training in state government. One specific idea I'd love to see is a more formal opportunity for employees to take temporary assignments or "details" with their own department or another department at the next step up on their career ladder. This is (or was) common in federal government and is a great way to give an individual some experience as a supervisor or team leader while also allowing a department to fill a temporary gap.
IDEA_TEXT: Create formal temporary assignment programs. Allow staff to work short-term jobs in their own department or other departments at higher levels for career growth and leadership experience.

In trainings, I provide layered resources: a presentation, a tips-and-tricks cheat sheet, a process flow chart, and an FAQ. These tools support different learning styles, give staff reliable references after the training, and reduce the need for repeated clarification, leading to greater efficiency and consistency across operations.

COMMENT:
I do not think there is just 1 primary problem.  I been with the state over 15 years, and have held positions as staff, and management. 

Covid put restrictions on in person training. Yes, def. bring back in person training. Not all jobs can be learned virtually, at least not the ones I did. 

As an employee, lack of mgmt guidance on growth, learning and promotions. They make you fill the IDP beginning of the year, and then what? It's not revisited till your next annual. Fancy document serves zero purpose.  Even as a manager, no support or encouragement for growth.

As a manager, you get to see abuse of time by staff clearly. Lack of mgmt support in documenting those staff for performance issues (lack of knowledge, ability, or simply abuse). The employees being written up have more rights than the manager who is documenting them for years, and years. So much time and resource wasted on documenting. Staff files complain against managers, and upper mgmt does nothing to support the manager. Then why would any manager want to waste their time and document ppl? the managers ruin their reputation by incompetent staff filing complains, and sadly upper mgmt. documents that on their annual, making it hard to promote for the manager, who is simply doing their job.  And so, no managers want to document and waste time. 
Can't fire incompetent staff from state is the one benefits highly abused by ALOT of staff.  Huge waste of taxpayer's money, and state resources there.
IDEA_TEXT: Bring back in-person training for jobs that cannot be learned virtually.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training

COMMENT:
Problem: there is often a lack of clear rationale provided for certain statewide policy decisions, such as the return-to-office mandate. This creates confusion and lowers morale, especially when state audit reports indicate that existing hybrid models are effective. 
Solution: Increase transparency by clearly communicating the data, reasoning, and intended outcomes behind major policy changes. Providing this context would help employees better understand the purpose, build trust in leadership, and support more effective implementation across departments.
IDEA_TEXT: Increase transparency in policy decisions. Clearly explain the data, reasoning, and goals behind major policy changes to help staff understand the purpose and build trust in leadership.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Organizational structure and culture; Communication and knowledge sharing; Governance and oversight
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Internal communication; Trust and openness

COMMENT: From my perspective as a Californian, I would like to see housing innovation that supports smaller footprints and community-centered living. Imagine villages designed around a farm or lake setting, where residents benefit from shared green space and sustainability. These communities could also incorporate city-based services such as foster animal programs, elder health care, and farmers markets. By integrating housing with local services, California can create more accessible, affordable, and vibrant communities that reflect both our environmental values and our commitment to quality of life.
IDEA_TEXT: Create community-centered housing villages with shared green spaces and local services. Design neighborhoods around farms or lakes that include foster animal programs, elder health care, and farmers markets to make housing more affordable and accessible.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public policy initiatives; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT: More affordable housing. It is disheartening that, as a state employee, I-- and many of my colleagues--- find owning a home out of reach in California. Despite stable, solid incomes, housing costs are disproportionately high, keeping us from achieving long-term stability. Only 15% of Californian households could afford a median-priced home in 2025 requiring an annual income of $232K.
IDEA_TEXT: Increase affordable housing options for state employees who cannot afford homes despite stable incomes.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: A problem I have noticed is that time is not taken to research and investigate problems and wastes that are barriers to improving processes. One of reasons for this is because managers and staff are overwhelmed with the number of improvements that can/need to be made. A solution to this is to create a Process Improvement Priority Tool where we list the process that needs improving and score it based on the impact it has on customer/patient care and if we already have a policy or procedure in place to mitigate the adverse impact. Based on the scoring we can determine what process/issue to tackle first.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a Process Improvement Priority Tool to score and rank process improvements based on impact on customer care and existing policies.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Policy development process; Process design and methodologies; Public service delivery and responsiveness

State employee pay and benefits should be protected. Overspending should be compensated for by those who overspend. Accountability is a virtue, and California should adopt it as a standard operating procedure.
IDEA_TEXT: Make accountability a standard practice in California government operations.

COMMENT:
I have discerned that CDCR Case Records tasks can be defined and governed by four accuracies: 1. Accuracy of Legal Document Review
2. Accuracy of Data Entry
3. Accuracy of Penal Code Compliance
4. Accuracy of date calculation.

This provides a framework for correctly assessing each task, correctly identifying the pertinent information, and correctly applying the information.
IDEA_TEXT: Use a four-part accuracy framework for CDCR Case Records tasks. Check legal document review, data entry, penal code compliance, and date calculation to improve task assessment and information handling.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Process design and methodologies; Process documentation

COMMENT: As a California state employee and California resident, I cannot overstate how important telework and flexible work schedules are to my family. Our school district mothballed its school bus fleet, so we have no bus service for our children, and must take our children to and pick our children up from school. Flexible work schedules and telework (at least twice a week) enable us to do what we need to do.
IDEA_TEXT: Continue and expand telework and flexible work schedules for state employees. Allow working from home at least twice a week and flexible hours to help staff manage family needs.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Organizational structure and culture; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee retention; Remote work and return to office; Work culture

COMMENT: At Mt. Tamalpais State Park, we frequently have lost or injured hikers/cyclists. Locating them and assessing their status is complicated by the number and length of our trails (approx. 60 trails, up to 7.5 miles in length). Our ranger staff is limited and serves a large geographic area. It would help our responsiveness to have a drone to fly along a trail to locate a hiker/cyclist in distress. Pinpointing the location would help reduce response time for search and rescue.
IDEA_TEXT: Use drones to find lost or injured hikers and cyclists at Mt. Tamalpais State Park. This would help rangers locate people faster and reduce search and rescue response time.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public policy initiatives; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT: Some of the processes have not been modernized.  Everything seems so archaic.  Like even just the process of applying for a job is so complicated and not intuitive.  Our office has no clear onboarding process or training manual.  A lot of things are just word of mouth and vary greatly.  How each regional office does things is totally different.  We don't come together  as a team very often.
IDEA_TEXT: Modernize processes and create standard procedures. Update job application process, create clear onboarding process and training manuals, and make all regional offices follow the same procedures.

IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Employee training; Org structure and hierarchy; Process documentation

COMMENT:
Problem: Independent adoptions are often dealing with children who should have been agency cases, which would come with financial assistance, Medi-Cal, and no cost.  However, because CPS was bypassed, the family has to pay over $4000 and get no assistance.
Solution: Have all guardianship or relative adoptions be at a reduced cost, and/or if there was a CPS history have AAP or Medi-Cal be available for more families.

IDEA_TEXT: Create and share training manuals statewide. Make training materials available to all offices instead of each office making their own.

COMMENT: As a 10-year state employee with 35+ years of building design experience in my field, I see the private sector passing me by.  Training to keep pace with my private sector counterparts is essential to managing their design efforts. Greater support (financial & time) for in-house training of design professional would be a great first step.
IDEA_TEXT: Increase training support for design staff. Provide more money and time for in-house training to help state design professionals keep up with private sector skills.
