COMMENT: Up-to-date Training Manuels or Succession Plans in place. Every time I start a new role in a new State Agency, everyone is too busy doing their actual job to train me. If people would take 2 hours a week to document their process, or explain, my entrance to the department would have been a lot less chaotic or confusing.
IDEA_TEXT: Create up-to-date training manuals and succession plans. Require staff to spend 2 hours per week documenting their work processes to help new employees learn their jobs faster.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Communication and knowledge sharing; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Knowledge management; Process documentation

COMMENT: Rather than having each separate agency be the custodian of record for employee fingerprint clearances, should that responsibility instead be delegated to CalHR? State employees switching departments would not need to submit to a new fingerprint clearance. Hiring agencies would be tasked with verifying fingerprint clearance with CalHR prior to hire. Hiring agencies would also be tasked with notifying CalHR when an employee leaves State service and submit an NLI request for submission to the DOJ.
IDEA_TEXT: Transfer fingerprint clearance records to CalHR. State workers switching departments would not need new fingerprint checks. Hiring agencies would check clearances with CalHR before hiring and notify CalHR when employees leave.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Hiring and recruitment; Shared data

COMMENT: Support upward mobility from seasonal to permanent employee. There are countless hurdles that employees must overcome to get into a permanent position. Most will just go to the private sector and then we lose good talent trying to get truly qualified people "qualified" for a position.
IDEA_TEXT: Remove barriers for seasonal workers to become permanent employees. Create clearer paths for seasonal staff to get permanent jobs.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Career growth; Employee retention; Limited-term positions

COMMENT: Actually seeing the programs work for the people they are intended for. I see great ideas get watered down and "politized" to the point where they no longer help those they were created for. Instead, they go to the wealthy or those groups that don't really need the help because it caught the attention of someone with more politic juice.
IDEA_TEXT: Ensure programs reach their intended recipients. Prevent wealthy or well-connected groups from taking resources meant for those who truly need help.

IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Align policy and implementation; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: Special housing rates for State employees. You either have to be marries, have a roommate, have a second job, or rent to live in California. As a state employee, there is no way to own a home without some help from the government. Even if it's just when the government sells of plots of land they don't need anymore. Throw us a bone.
IDEA_TEXT: Create special housing programs for state employees. Offer reduced-rate housing or sell unused government land to help workers afford homes in California.

IDEA_TEXT: Hold regular team meetings. Schedule monthly department and park meetings to help staff connect, share updates, and support each other.

COMMENT: When we moved recently finding a Doctor that was taking new patients was extremely difficult.  There doesn’t appear to be any requirement for health providers to keep their websites updated as to their Doctors and their availability.  The Dr. offices usually blamed the carrier and the carrier said they were never informed.  Some Dr had moved health groups up to 3 years prior with no updates.  Maybe we could make the update at least an annual requirement and award a “patient service excellence” award to health providers who keep their websites updated!
IDEA_TEXT: Require health providers to update their websites at least once a year with current doctor information and availability. Create a patient service excellence award for providers who keep their websites updated.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digital services; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: Mt. Tamalpais is located in an area with a high cost of living.  Even with state park housing, it is unaffordable for park employees to live here (rangers and maintenance staff) - especially with a family. So, it is hard for us to recruit - and we are in need of staff. It would help to be able to offer a cost of living adjustment/stipend to make it easier for staff on a parks salary to live near the park.
IDEA_TEXT: Provide cost of living adjustments or stipends for park staff at Mt. Tamalpais to help with recruitment and retention in high-cost areas.

COMMENT: Explore revising or eliminating core business hours to reflect new emerging technological capabilities and consumer preferences. A lot of consumers are unable to contact state agencies for services between the hours of 8AM-5PM due to work. Additionally, a lot of state employees would like the flexibility of an earlier or later work shift. Allowing agencies to offer both a day and swing shift would benefit both California consumers and employees.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow state agencies to offer both day and evening shifts to better serve residents who cannot call during regular business hours and give employees more flexible work schedules.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee travel; Public service delivery and responsiveness; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: The employee housing system is flawed. Employee housing is needed in many areas for recruitment and/or because of high costs of living. Issues include: 1) Many State houses are occupied by highly paid staff. 2) Once staff is in a State house, the State should be able to evict or move staff based on a higher need or more appropriate use for the house, but that does not happen. 3) Much funding is spent to maintain State-owned employee residences. A cost-benefit analysis would help to determine appropriate rental rates.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a system to move staff out of state housing based on need and do a cost study to set fair rental rates.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Physical infrastructure

COMMENT: Promote good managers who support their team and does not micromanage them.  Find a way to support the people who are the face to the People.   Good training programs for newer employees, help people to find ways to promote and good managers make for good morale.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve management practices. Promote good managers who support their teams without micromanaging. Provide better training programs for new employees and create clear paths for promotion to boost morale.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Management culture and leadership; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee retention; Employee training; Management culture and leadership approach; Work culture

COMMENT: Within California State Parks, there are ongoing issues with qualifications/hierarchy that creates ripple effects. The state has tried different ways to organize the hierarchy but what we really need is a park manager in charge of each park and the programmatic leads giving guidance for each programmatic area. What we have now are people doing multiple jobs to fill needs. Since that isn't recognized on their duty statement, they are not recognized on paper for it when applying for other positions. They also aren't recognized for it with any pay increase. This creates a very low morale and discourages people to stay within state parks, as they aren't recognized for the work they had to do to keep operations going. So many parks operate off of nonprofit partners, meaning ones without them are left with very few staff working far above their pay. It also creates a huge discrepancy in parks with areas with wealthy donors to nonprofits and parks in low-income areas.
IDEA_TEXT: Restructure California State Parks management. Put one park manager in charge of each park and create program leads for each area. Update job descriptions to match actual work duties and provide pay increases for extra responsibilities.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Governance and oversight; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Job classification; Org structure and hierarchy

COMMENT: We have so much knowledge in our heads but not out for coworkers to see. We are currently working on leaving what we know and have done for coworkers to read so if one person leaves, the whole system does not go along with them. This has been a self-guided thing, would be good to have a better state-wide example of how to do this most clearly and effectively, plus something there for state-wide processes.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a state-wide system for sharing knowledge and procedures. Help staff document what they know so work can continue when people leave.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Communication and knowledge sharing; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Knowledge management; Process documentation; Shared resources

COMMENT: A primary problem to be addressed is the misunderstanding that UI is government aid.  It is not meant to be abused and exhausted ad nauseum.  UI is insurance to be leveraged while in between job opportunities and it needs emphasizing that UI works best for those who work, whether it be temporary, contract and seasonal work until they have obtained permanent work, thereby a new claim may be valid at the BYE so long as there was work lost at no fault of the employee in the most recent 12 months.  The public needs to be aware that they are tapping into the tax reserves of the employers when leveraging these UI benefits and that they should not be abusing the opportunity to benefit from these resources as a matter of sustaining our economic structure. Abuse begets further abuse.  Furthermore, there is a need for workforce localities to reinvest in establishing a new system for hiring that does not involve digital platforms that no longer vet job listing.  Local job listing need to be verified and documented as current, valid and verifiable and then removed upon fulfillment. Human resource systems needs governance and accountability and turning over a system that is respectful of the authority of our local workforce departments could be a viable option for building viable businesses in our communities. This is how it used it used to.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a new local hiring system with verified job listings that are current and valid, removing digital platforms that don't check if job posts are real.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Hiring and recruitment; Software and tools

COMMENT: Other depts are using electronic timesheets but not all. I've been told it's in the works for my dept but it's been years now. COMMENT: One big problem I see is the lack of geographical diversity in our state workforce. That creates enormous blind spots and group think, because the folks that serve certain areas don't know or understand the nuances or barriers/obstacles in the areas they purport to serve because they don't come from those geographical areas - thus there is a real gap there. For example, our state has 58 counties, yet most state employees (depending on the department) are concentrated in one county. We should make a concerted effort to hire statewide to better represent the state in its totality. Afterall, we know that representation matters. This would better mitigate some implicit biases that we all have.
IDEA_TEXT: Hire state workers from all 58 counties to better represent the whole state and reduce blind spots when serving different areas.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Hiring and recruitment; Inclusion and diversity; Qualified staff

COMMENT: I appreciate my manger on a regular basis likes to ask us question such how we can improve our processes or working materials. Asking for input and feedback on a habitual basis is a great thing because it allows us the ability to pivot and/or incorporate recent lessons learned in our internal business processes or materials. We also have templates and workflows that mitigate our opportunity costs. I also appreciate not being micro-managed. Respecting our integrity, expertise, and competence to get things timely is very important.
IDEA_TEXT: Ask staff for input regularly. Create a habit of asking employees how to improve work processes and materials.

COMMENT:
Telework should be seen as a strategic system improvement that enhances organizational efficiency and public value, not just an individual workplace accommodation. Implementing statewide telework reduces overhead costs like office space, utilities, and operational expenses—freeing funds for critical public services.

Studies show telework often increases productivity due to fewer distractions and more flexible schedules. This flexibility supports better work-life balance, reducing burnout and turnover—key challenges for the State workforce.

Telework also expands the talent pool by attracting skilled workers regardless of location, which is vital for hard-to-fill positions. It enhances inclusivity by making State jobs accessible to people with disabilities and those in rural or underserved areas.

Additionally, statewide telework adoption demonstrates environmental responsibility by cutting carbon emissions from commuting. Less commuter traffic means public savings through reduced wear on roads and lower maintenance costs. Telework ensures government continuity during emergencies—like natural disasters or health crises—making services more resilient. Overall, telework strengthens institutional performance and public service delivery.
IDEA_TEXT: Implement statewide telework as a strategic system improvement to reduce overhead costs, increase productivity, expand talent pool, improve inclusivity, demonstrate environmental responsibility, and ensure government continuity during emergencies.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Communication and knowledge sharing; Budget and resources; Workplace operations; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Cross-agency collaboration; Employee retention; Environmental sustainability; Public service delivery and responsiveness; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT:
Consistancy overall, in information provided on the internet on the State Departments.  Should have one format for all departments and simple language.  Especially with Taxes, normal people are not lawyers who are reading the information, if anything we confuse them more. 

Also the image State Workers have, like they don't like their jobs so they make it bad for the public, like government employees don't care.   Government sector customer service and very different from Private sector customer service, we should incorporate some Private sector practices. We should show some empathy, we care.

HR practices, can't be one generic for overall state departments.  Each individual state department is different and unique, let them run their own HR.  Example, Tax Technician and Compliance Reps are in one big bucket and pay range is the same.  But a Tax Tech and Compliance Rep in a Field office learns and knows more then one that is in a central office doing one duty daily.  Field Office staff should be paid more and location should be looked at also.  Ones in Los Angeles cannot be paid the same as one in Sacramento. That's two different areas and cost of living, yet the pay range is the same.

My pet peeve:  "We have always done it that way!"  We need to change with the times and technology.  We improve on the technology but its not embraced, because we have always done it that way.  Not being open to new ideas and/or suggestions.
IDEA_TEXT: Create one standard format for all state department websites with simple language that regular people can understand.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digital services; Plain language; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT:
The primary problem is that the state tax system faces inefficiencies and risks due to data gaps, manual processes, and limited use of modern technology, which lead to:
• Difficulty detecting underreporting or misreporting in a timely manner
• Time-consuming manual work for employees
• Limited taxpayer accessibility and convenience
• Skill gaps among employees in adapting to new technology

The ideas to solve the issue as state employees are:
1. Real-Time Data Matching with Other Agencies
• Build secure connections with agencies like FTB, EDD and IRS.
• Use automated cross-checking to flag discrepancies before audits or deadlines.
• This reduces underreporting, improves fairness, and minimizes manual comparison.

2. Mobile App for Taxpayers
• Provide an easy-to-use app for filing, payments, and notifications.
• Improves compliance by making tax obligations clearer and more convenient.
• Reduces taxpayer confusion and the volume of support inquiries.

3. Training on New Technologies
• Invest in upskilling employees in AI, data analytics, and customer service.
• Prepares the workforce to handle modern tools efficiently.
• Ensures smooth adoption of new systems and better service for taxpayers.
IDEA_TEXT: Build real-time data connections with agencies like FTB, EDD and IRS. Use automated cross-checking to flag tax discrepancies before audits or deadlines.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Shared data; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT:
1. Enhanced Data Sharing and Matching
• Worked on projects where CDTFA data was cross-verified with outside sources such as EDD payroll reports and FTB income filings.
• Helped develop data-matching routines that flagged potential underreporting before audits were launched, saving time and improving enforcement fairness.
• Result: Increased detection of noncompliance while ensuring compliant taxpayers were not unfairly burdened.

2. Training and Knowledge Sharing for Staff
• Conducted internal training sessions on using analytical tools such as SQL and Excel for data reconciliation and fraud detection.
• Shared best practices with newer auditors on reviewing digital records, allowing staff to work more effectively with electronic rather than paper records.
• Result: Reduced onboarding time for new auditors and greater consistency in data-driven decision-making.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve tax compliance detection by sharing data between agencies like CDTFA, EDD, and FTB to cross-check reports and flag potential problems before audits.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Public-facing services and resident experience; Communication and knowledge sharing

• Conducted internal training sessions on using analytical tools such as SQL and Excel for data reconciliation and fraud detection.
• Shared best practices with newer auditors on reviewing digital records, allowing staff to work more effectively with electronic rather than paper records.
• Result: Reduced onboarding time for new auditors and greater consistency in data-driven decision-making.
IDEA_TEXT: Train staff on data analysis tools like SQL and Excel and share best practices for reviewing digital records to improve audit quality and reduce training time.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Technology and digital innovation; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; State-spending audits; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT: the complexity of the state contracting process, particularly when Dept. of General Services is involved. DGS can deny authority and services to Depts. and then we are accountable for failing to meet our mission. There is a consistent lack of understanding about what is required to manage large tracts of land and run fish hatcheries. Rather than serving a purpose (not wasting CA resources), contracting rules create impossible barriers and inefficiencies to acquiring tools and services required to meet our mission. Suggest giving more expansive authorities to Depts while simultaneously doing a better job writing clear policy and statute directives to assist Dept staff with making decisions that are functional and legal.
IDEA_TEXT: Give departments more authority to make contracting decisions while writing clearer policy and statute rules to help staff make legal and functional decisions.

COMMENT: Increase collaboration within and across Departments (as well as reduce building costs and increase business downtown) by creating coworking spaces where multiple state departments utilize the same building. For example, DPH, DHCS, DMHC and DSS could all share one building with large meeting spaces, conference rooms and amenities such as gym, coffee shop to draw people in office to collaborate versus forcing via in-office requirements. Having community spaces would create networking opportunities for partnership and mentorship allowing state staff to grow and promote across Departments with similar visions and areas of expertise. Reinforces concept that we are all stewards of the state working for one employer, Californians.
IDEA_TEXT: Create shared office buildings for multiple state departments. Include meeting spaces, conference rooms, and amenities like gyms and coffee shops to encourage collaboration and reduce building costs.

COMMENT: Sharing information and knowledge across departments. Everything is a part of the state, its one central embodiment, but everything is so segregated in a way that hinders our ability to serve the publics interest on a meaningful level and reasonable timeline. The biggest idea I would have is to allow ECOS and PIMS access across entities in a "view only" capacity for HR departments. Why can I not do employment A01 verifications myself here for someone in another department? Why can I not see applications provided from a candidate in ECOS to another department? Perhaps they had attached transcripts there that could save 10 days to get here.                Another issue I'd like to address is the use of different documents across state entities. Many processes, policies and procedures begin with guidance from CalHR and the State Personnel Board (SPB) but each department then adapts that guidance using its own forms or supporting docs. This results in inconsistencies across departments. Establishing a standardized process that starts with CalHR and SPB guidance and carries forward uniformly would promote consistency, fairness, and equity across CA.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow HR departments to view ECOS and PIMS systems across all state departments for employment checks and application reviews.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Shared data; Software and tools

COMMENT: One example on improving efficiency within our department is the move to scan and digitize paper documentation. Many existing files are old, difficult to search, and cumbersome to manager. Creating searchable PDFs and storing documents on a shared drive, staff can quickly locate information, access each other's work, and reduce time spent hunting for physical files. Virtualizing documents in this way is a significant time-saver and greatly improved our workflow and collaboration within the unit.
IDEA_TEXT: Digitize paper files. Scan documents to create searchable PDFs and store them on shared drives so staff can quickly find information and work together better.

COMMENT: From my perspective as a California resident and a state employee working in human resources over programs that deal with licensure processes, anything that reduces time at each step benefits both staff and the public. Whether waiting for a nursing license, or standing in line at the DMV, smooth and effective processes save time and reduce frustration. This requires processes that are efficient and consistent across all state entities, not just within individual departments. I think that establishing clear communication, and standardizing procedures across the state is essential for ensuring a positive experience for residents and improving overall efficiency.
IDEA_TEXT: Make processes consistent across all state departments. Set up clear communication and standard procedures statewide to reduce wait times and improve service for residents.

COMMENT: I think lack of 1) efficiency, 3) rigorous training (specifically of soft skills) and 2) passion/pride/ownership of the work we do, are the primary problems. The problem "efficiency" can be resolved if we all (those part of the system) put our hearts & minds together to solve this issue. As someone mentioned in this chain - it is as easy as analyzing and identifying areas were we can cut down on bureaucratic surplus - and yet we have failed to resolve this problem.   Among the soft skills, "effective communication" is were we lack the most - I have witnessed both seasoned and inexperienced employees lacking this skill. Although fewer,  we do have some "effective communicators" and mentoring/shadowing with these folks can go a long way in learning effective communication and boosting confidence. Last but not the least, the lack of "passion/pride/ownership of the work" is very common in government employees across the board. This problem can be addressed by hiring inspirational leadership (or having motivated/passionate individuals at leadership level) that can motivate the employees to be the best versions of themselves. Once passionate, the employees will be highly productive/efficient/confident and our state will become a role model  for government efficiency/effectiveness.
IDEA_TEXT: Analyze and cut bureaucratic surplus to improve efficiency.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Bureaucracy

COMMENT: The state is spending billions each year on construction, purchase and maintenance of office space. By allowing all positions that can be performed remotely to be filled by remote workers, the state could save California taxpayers hundreds of millions of dollars a year on office space, equipment, and IT services, while simultaneously expanding the applicant pool for state government positions, and advancing equity by opening up opportunities for career advancement to candidates living in economically disadvantaged areas of the state.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow all jobs that can be done remotely to be filled by remote workers to save money on office space and expand the job applicant pool.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Office management; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: I have done a great job at providing better customer service resulting in getting a lot of accolades (from the industry I serve) for quick turn around, attention to details, being a problem solver, valuable team member etc. I bring over 20 years of private consulting experience to my craft at the State. However, I am at the most junior level in my department and my approaches for improving efficiency etc. is not being heard. I hope to rise to leadership positions (which seems to be a slower path due to positions being offered based on # of years of experience within the organization rather than who is more qualified for the position) one day and not only improve efficiency but inspire a whole generation of public servants to be there best versions.
IDEA_TEXT: Change hiring practices to promote based on skills and qualifications instead of just years of service.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Hiring and recruitment; Qualified staff

COMMENT: The hiring process from the perspective of applicants can be quite complicated. Candidates often face numerous requirements when applying to state positions, which may discourage some qualified individuals. Removing the exam requirement could potentially attract more qualified candidates and streamline the process for all involved. This change could also lead to savings in time, resources, personnel, and finances over time.
IDEA_TEXT: Remove exam requirements for state job applications to attract more qualified candidates and save time and resources.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Hiring and recruitment; Qualified staff

COMMENT: Our goal as State employees should be the most effective use of tax payer dollars - also a perspective as a California resident. The allocated funds for a cause should be primarily used for that cause (e.g. building affordable housing) and not for mountains of bureaucracy oversight.
IDEA_TEXT: Reduce bureaucracy overhead. Use allocated funds mainly for their intended purpose instead of administrative costs.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Bureaucracy

COMMENT: I believe our government could be more efficient by making sure funds are going to the places they are meant to go to and not drawn out of other programs to make ends meet for a current program, thus at some point making the program that had a surplus of funds at some point low on funds because the once surplus was drawn out from. I feel when this happens it just happen it becomes a snowball effect. I believe it can be taken care of by having more auditors to make hold our government accountable as to where funds are going and that the full amount of the fund was used and if there is left over that is put into the surplus so that if another program needs it it will be there for the taking. Hopefully not become unaccounted for.
IDEA_TEXT: Hire more auditors to track government funds. Make sure money goes to the right programs and leftover funds go into a surplus account for other programs to use when needed.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Qualified staff; State-spending audits

COMMENT: CAlPIA and their theft of funds from other agencies, by being forced to purchase from CALPIA with extremally inflated prices. Like $731 for an office chair. Similar chair sells elsewhere for $250 tops.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow agencies to buy office supplies and equipment from other vendors instead of requiring purchases from CALPIA at high prices.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Procurement

COMMENT: I've experienced this in the private sector also, but for some state employees that have worked for the state for decades, there is often little room for change. There is a sense of "we've always done it this way," which can be challenging and create extra work for others. For someone like me, who goes around those who aren't happy about change and makes the change anyway, a solution could be more encouragement around the work we do and the improvements we can make can be a boost of morale. Also, we can normalize change and not make it a daunting or annoying thing in the workplace.
IDEA_TEXT: Encourage staff who make improvements and normalize change in the workplace to boost morale and reduce resistance.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Organizational structure and culture; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Growth mindset; Management culture and leadership approach; Trust and openness; Work culture

COMMENT: I think state departments should encourage more flexibility, in general, when it comes to employee satisfaction.  This includes having exempt employees instead of hourly employees, that have flexible schedules based on their workload.  There is a lot of wasted time that is used on gossip and bullying, because employees are bored and become cliquish.
IDEA_TEXT: Give state departments more flexibility for employee satisfaction. Allow exempt employees with flexible schedules based on workload instead of hourly employees.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Employee retention; Management culture and leadership approach

COMMENT: I report directly to the Deputy Director of my division, and asking lots of questions has proven to create more effectiveness and efficiency than anything else. In turn, I'm able to ask the right questions and effectively communicate the direction of the Division with staff. I've also created systems around ongoing projects to streamline executive review and approval processes (i.e. creating logs, utilizing reminders, prioritizing by determining urgency versus importance, etc.)
IDEA_TEXT: Encourage managers to ask more questions to improve communication and effectiveness. Create tracking systems for projects including logs, reminders, and priority rankings to speed up approval processes.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Process improvement; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal communication; Management culture and leadership approach; Process design and methodologies

COMMENT: Inventory of assets, which should actually be named office equipment: As state employee I receive multiple "Excel Asset Inventory worksheets" from different individuals/departments requesting information about the office equipment I use in my line of work. I suggest streamlining process by centralizing it, creating a dashboard, and give access to interested parties and or departments that need access the data rather than having staff completing multiple worksheets, which creates the risk of inaccurate data and unnecessary processes. Have one dedicated team in charge of managing the disbursement and disposal of office equipment as well as of the maintenance of the database/dashboard for accuracy and accountability.
IDEA_TEXT: Streamline office equipment tracking. Create one central database with a dashboard that all departments can access. Have one team manage equipment distribution, disposal, and database updates instead of using multiple Excel worksheets.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Workplace operations; Budget and resources; Technology and digital innovation; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Digitize processes; Office management; Shared resources; Software and tools

COMMENT: I would like the choice of where my income goes. Working for the state, as a sole income provider, with two children, is tough living in California. Our benefits are awesome and often times better than other agencies, yet I'm still struggling to pay rent and buy groceries/gas/essentials because I can't afford to live closer to work and a huge chunk of my income goes to retirement involuntarily. I also like working for the agency I work for, but I'm supposed to try to work elsewhere just so I can get better pay? That doesn't seem right.
IDEA_TEXT: Give employees choice in retirement contributions. Allow workers to decide how much of their pay goes to retirement instead of requiring it.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits

COMMENT: The cost-of-living is out of control.  There needs to be limits placed by state government on how much electric companies should charge, for example.  Electric companies hold a monopoly and have increased their prices by about 300% in the past ten years.  The use of microeconomics and budgeting may help obtain reasonable guidelines for cost-of-living expenses based on our individual net incomes.
IDEA_TEXT: Set limits on how much electric companies can charge customers.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: I’d push for performance-based funding, where agencies must show measurable process improvements and return on investment before receiving additional resources. This ensures upgrades are strategic, data-driven, and truly benefit the public. A major issue is the excessive number of oversight agencies, which creates duplication, delays, and inefficiencies. From a Lean Six Sigma perspective, this is pure waste. I’d advocate for consolidating oversight functions and holding the legislature accountable for funding only those upgrades that show measurable process improvements. Fewer layers, clearer accountability, and smarter investments lead to faster, more effective public service.
IDEA_TEXT: Consolidate oversight agencies to reduce duplication and delays, with legislature funding only upgrades that show measurable process improvements.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Governance and oversight; Budget and resources; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Budgeting and funding; Consolidate government agencies; Process delays

COMMENT:
One primary problem I would address is the fragmentation and inefficiency caused by too many oversight agencies in California’s government. This creates delays, redundant processes, and unclear accountability. 

My solution would be to streamline oversight by consolidating overlapping agencies and functions. I’d also push for performance-based funding, tying legislative allocations for system upgrades to measurable outcomes like reduced processing times or improved customer satisfaction. This ensures that public resources are used efficiently and that upgrades deliver real value to Californians.
IDEA_TEXT: Combine overlapping government agencies to reduce delays and unclear accountability. Tie funding for system upgrades to measurable results like faster processing times and better customer satisfaction.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Budget and resources; Organizational structure and culture; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Consolidate government agencies; Process delays; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT: Nepotism in hiring and/or promotions given to friends and otherwise unqualified persons. Maybe more review of hiring by outsiders? Like reviewing the application, interview, and selection materials by others outside the department to ensure the reasons for hiring and not hiring were fair and in alignment with the requirements management had chosen to score by?
IDEA_TEXT: Have outside reviewers check hiring decisions. Let people outside the department review job applications, interviews, and selection materials to make sure hiring choices are fair and follow the rules.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Governance and oversight
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Hiring and recruitment

COMMENT:
I’ve worked on several initiatives aimed at reducing waste and improving efficiency in state operations. One example involved streamlining the internal administrative operations. The original workflow had multiple redundant review layers, causing delays of up to 30 days. Using process mapping and stakeholder interviews, we identified non-value-added steps and consolidated approvals, cutting the turnaround time by 40%.

In another case, I led a Kaizen event to improve customer response times in public requests for service. By analyzing call center data and applying root cause analysis, we restructured the triage process and introduced a simple decision tree for frontline staff. This reduced call escalations by 25% and improved first-contact resolution.

These projects not only improved service delivery but also empowered staff by giving them tools to identify and solve problems on their own.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve customer service response times by restructuring triage processes and providing decision-making tools for frontline staff.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Process improvement; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Process design and methodologies; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT:
I’d like to see more accessible, easy-to-understand reporting on how taxpayer dollars are being spent, especially at the local and state levels. Tools like interactive dashboards or annual summaries could help residents feel more informed and engaged.

Given the high cost of living in many parts of California, I think it’s important that tax policies and public programs take into account regional differences. For example, expanding tax credits or subsidies for housing, childcare, and transportation could make a big difference for working families.

I’d love to see more opportunities for residents to provide input on budget priorities, perhaps through participatory budgeting or community forums. It would help ensure that policies reflect the diverse needs of Californians.
IDEA_TEXT: Adjust tax policies and programs for regional cost differences. Expand tax credits or help for housing, childcare, and transportation for working families.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: The state is composed of various departments that are very small in size and often must contract out to provide obtain basic services internally or externally. These contracts can include interpretation/translation, website maintenance, a/v services, cabling, etc. Sometimes staff are stretched thin in smaller offices with various responsibilities because there is not enough personnel to cover issues that a team in a larger organization would do. There is so much time spent in bringing on external providers in addition to the cost of services as well as recruiting for staff turnover due to burnout. The state could better coordinate services across departments under a certain size reducing costs and providing adequate support for staff and the public.
IDEA_TEXT: Combine services across small state departments. Share resources like translation, website help, audio/video services, and cabling to cut costs and reduce staff burnout.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Shared resources; Translations

COMMENT:
One of the biggest problems with working for the state is employee morale. I have come to understand that the "state" as an institution does not care about me. The beaurocracy is created to avoid risk, not create a good working environment.

One small example of this is coffee and kitchen supplies. I was told that it is against state policy to purchase coffee, filtered water, or even dish soap for employees. This is a little thing, but it makes such a big difference in showing employees that you care about them. I cannot imagine the public would think that employees can't have these basic office supplies purchased for them, when it is so commonplace in every other office I have worked in -- even non-profit organizations! 

It is hard to do your best work for an organization that does not care about you. This affects efficiency because it affects employees' productivity.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow state agencies to buy basic office supplies like coffee, filtered water, and dish soap for employees to improve morale and show they care about staff.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee retention; Office management; Work culture

COMMENT: I am excited by the amount of departments who want to start using plain language. It's so important that we write clearly and without jargon.
IDEA_TEXT: Expand plain language use across state departments. Help more departments write clearly without jargon.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Plain language

COMMENT:
One of the most enlightening books I've read recently was 'Poverty, by America' by Matthew Desmond. In it, he points out how, while well-intentioned, so many government programs are not being used by those who need the benefits most because they are too difficult to navigate or confusing for people to use. This creates an impression among low-income folks that state government is AGAINST them, not for them. This creates a self-perpetuating cycle of distrust between the very people who would most benefit from our services. 

We need (not in order):
1. Data: Identify unused gov't services
2. Tech: Provide a unified, easy to remember, accessible access point to connect people with services
3. Reform: Review all public benefits we provide in California from a modern, poverty reduction point of view, at every level, and flag those services that may need legislative reform so we can make recommendations to legislature. 
4. Project management: Task force to address poverty (different than homelessness, and not meant to replace current efforts there) in California.
5. Marketing: Make combating poverty our primary focus, drill it into every state department at every level looking for ways to address, including inter-agency efforts and private sector-style promo to draw people in. Almost like a rebrand.

This would be a huge lift but when we have so many services, so many people struggling, plus such a negative impression of gov't, it seems like it would be a win-win-win for everyone.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a unified system to help low-income Californians access government benefits. Identify unused services, build one easy-to-use access point, review all benefits programs for needed reforms, form a poverty task force, and make fighting poverty the main focus across all state departments with marketing to reach more people.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Public-facing services and resident experience; Governance and oversight
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Cross-agency collaboration; Human-centered design; Public policy initiatives; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT: Data sharing. I understand there are all kinds of legal/privacy hurdles we would have to overcome but if someone, including myself, is approved to view all kinds of FTI as is, there is no reason we shouldn't be able to pull data in other departments may have access to in order to make better analytical decisions about our own projects. Just as an example, if I'm working on an analysis to determine how a certain enforcement action may be disproportionately impacting certain subsets of California population, I can examine my own department's data but I cannot compare that to data about California's entire population, so I end up having to look for outside resources provided by private or non-profit sector groups where I can't confirm reliability. That is crazy. If I am working on a data analysis re: insurance settlement payouts, I should be able to easily access information collected by Dept of Insurance instead of having to do a Google search.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a data sharing system between state departments. Allow approved staff to access data from other departments for analysis while following privacy rules.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Shared data; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT: I just completed a multi-year project to bring in data kept within outside resources that we had to either manually bring in via data entry or log in to an outside vendor's website to look up and then manually re-enter into our system. Our improvements automated bringing that data into our state system. We increased revenue generation and cut time spent doing manual data entry by roughly 60%, freeing up our staff for other tasks.
IDEA_TEXT: Automate data transfer from outside vendors into state systems to reduce manual data entry and free up staff time.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Budget and resources; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Digitize processes; Software and tools

COMMENT: We really need to eliminate arbitrary financial thresholds and create ways for state to look at more data points than just something like 'net income', there are so many other factors in a person's life, including some that can easily be quantified and some that can't, that impact whether a person should qualify for certain benefits or services. These thresholds were understandable back before we had access to so much more intelligent technology but now it seems inexcusable to not be able to take more nuanced looks at peoples' life circumstances and backgrounds. We have all experienced hardships we can't control that put us ahead or behind the curve and those things aren't even that difficult to quantify now that we have 'big data'. If the private sector can use all that data to market products to us, we should be able to use that same data to better support our citizens. Why is it that YouTube knows I'm shopping for a t-shirt but we can't tell that someone is struggling to pay their bills? To be clear I'm not advocating intruding into peoples' lives but surely there is more we can do to ensure that when someone is experiencing a challenge in life, one of the first things they think of is, 'oh wait, what if the state has any options to help me?' We are a high-tax state and I suspect a lot more people would be okay with that if they saw tangible benefits of that money in their day to day lives.
IDEA_TEXT: Replace simple income thresholds with smart data analysis for benefit eligibility. Use technology and multiple data points to better assess who needs help, similar to how private companies use data for marketing.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Process improvement; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Public policy initiatives; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT: The need to push forward on something that clearly isn't working or needs more time in development. Hitting the pause button, while measuring implications, isn't a sign of weakness, it's a sign of self-reflection, accountability, and the want to do things the right way. So many times we have duplicative efforts and efforts that just aren't working, but rather than taking the step back we keep trying to fit the square peg into the round hole.
IDEA_TEXT: Use the pause button on programs that are not working. Take time to measure what will happen before moving forward instead of forcing bad solutions to work.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership approach; Risk aversion

COMMENT: A primary problem in the hiring of state employees is having to retake exams after expiration to rejoin the eligible list. If someone has already passed an exam previously, they should not need to retake the exam to stay on the eligible list - I think instead they can just be asked to confirm yearly if they would like to stay on the eligible list. I also think the exam should not be required for current state employees looking to promote and who qualify based on their years of experience. For example, someone with 1 year of AGPA should not have to take the SSM1 exam to get on the eligible list. They should automatically be eligible for SSM1.
IDEA_TEXT: Remove exam requirements for current state employees who qualify for promotion based on years of experience. Make them automatically eligible for the next level position.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Career growth; Employee pay and benefits; Job classification

COMMENT: It would be good if more forms and documents could be submitted online to our department, including an online payment option, when needed. The amount of paper docs we receive in the mail is huge, when most could be sent by email or online.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow online form submission and payment. Create digital options for documents currently sent by mail or paper.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digital services; Digitize processes

COMMENT: Need a state-wide, electronic portal for reporting timesheets. Some departments are still submitting Excel spreadsheets filled in by hand, which is a very time-consuming process. People also have to learn new timesheet reporting processes when switching positions/departments - if it was standardized, this would be much simpler.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a statewide electronic portal for timesheet reporting to replace manual Excel spreadsheets and standardize the process across all departments.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Process improvement; Budget and resources; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Digitize processes; Shared resources; Software and tools

COMMENT: Given the urgent housing shortage in California, why aren't existing vacant or underutilized properties being prioritized? One idea is to start encouraging the state to revamp tax code incentives and penalties to maintain, occupy, or repurpose spaces that are currently eyesores or not wisely used.
IDEA_TEXT: Change tax rules to encourage using empty or underused properties. Create rewards for maintaining, occupying, or repurposing vacant buildings and penalties for leaving them unused.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Physical infrastructure; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: Hiring exams - after taking an exam, the eligibility shouldn't expire. Nothing has changed with that person's experience (if anything, they'll just gain more and more relevant experience). Current state employees also shouldn't be required to take an exam to get on the eligibility list when they are already in that classification path and qualify - for example, someone with 1 year of AGPA should automatically be on the eligible list for SSM1, without needing to take the exam.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow current state employees to skip exams for promotions within their career path if they meet experience requirements.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Career growth; Employee training; Job classification

IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Hiring and recruitment

COMMENT: During my trainings, I have made sure to speak up to share ideas, and to ask questions. It continues to surprise me how many other people had the same question or think the question was good to ask but would not have done so. Ofcourse , recognition of what are safe spaces to ask questions or share ideas is a must.
IDEA_TEXT: Encourage staff to speak up during trainings by creating safe spaces to ask questions and share ideas.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Communication and knowledge sharing; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Internal feedback; Trust and openness

COMMENT:
As a state employee that has laterally transferred to different Departments, I've noticed a dramatic difference in the responsibilities and level of expectations for the AGPA classification. In one department, as a seasoned AGPA I was required to have every email reviewed that was being sent to an external source. Another department that I worked for places their AGPA staff in lead roles, facilitating and leading discussions with internal and external stakeholders and the management sit back and watch. These types of inconsistencies can cause people to struggle when they believe they are taking a lateral position but their level of responsibility dramatically increases in their new role, it affects efficiencies when AGPAs with multiple years of experience cannot effectively lateral into similarly positioned AGPA roles. 

I believe there needs to be statewide accountability put in place in order to make the level of responsibility across classifications consistent. This cannot simply be done by reviewing duty statements which are broad and do not often reflect the management culture of the office. There should be statewide reviews of internal procedures for varying classifications to ensure that state workers are paid for the same level of responsibility, effort, and commitment.
IDEA_TEXT: Create statewide standards for job responsibilities across the same job classifications. Review internal procedures to ensure state workers with the same job title have similar duties and expectations.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Governance and oversight
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Align policy and implementation; Job classification

COMMENT: We brag about our state being the 4th largest economy in the world. But for the amount of work we do and services we provide to the public, our wages are not keeping up with the inflation rate. So many overworked public-facing employees who bear the brunt of the work are so underpaid (some examples: Motor Vehicle Representatives, Program Technicians, Office Assistants/Technicians, people in call centers, and more!). Also, telework helps the state save money by not spending on monthly leases, overhead costs, electricity, and more. Forcing people to RTO is a huge pay cut for many employees who are barely making ends meet in our HCOL state.
IDEA_TEXT: Increase wages for public-facing employees to keep up with inflation, especially Motor Vehicle Representatives, Program Technicians, Office Assistants, and call center staff.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits

IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: Please consider the process of awarding a construction contract on state-funded projects. The current process of requesting a lump sum bid and then awarding to the lowest bidder has several problems and ultimately costs the state more money and time in the long run. The contracts team needs to create a database and flag contractors who have defaulted, been previously terminated from a project, or have a track record of poor performance. We have been awarding projects to the lowest bidder without being given an opportunity to determine whether that contractor deserves the right to work on a project. If we can establish a resource to the project managers and contracts team to vet through contractors, we should be able to disqualify a bidder due to poor performance, despite being the lowest bidder. The current process in place is actually costing the state more money and time to handle a poor-performing contractor. In addition, bids are requested as a lump sum. This is problematic for several reasons. Once we award a contract to the lowest bidder, we have no way of knowing if that bid actually includes all the requirements of the contract documents. As the process is currently set up, it is the responsibility of the contractor to ensure their bids cover the scope, however in several cases, the contractor ends up misinterpreting the scope and/or missing essential work. Better communication is key and can set up a project for success in the long run.
IDEA_TEXT: Change from lump sum bidding to a more detailed bidding process that ensures contractors understand and include all project requirements.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Procurement

IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Procurement

COMMENT: It takes an extremely long time to hire new staff. The budget change proposal must be submitted by May of the prior year and the actual hiring won't happen till the next year July. This is an extremely long timeframe and slows down the state's ability to respond quickly to staffing shortages. There must be a way to allocate a position once a need for it is determined.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a way to allocate positions quickly once a staffing need is found instead of waiting over a year from budget proposal to hiring.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Process improvement; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Hiring and recruitment; Process delays

COMMENT: We have been exploring opportunities to bring the technical management of a particular website we run in-house in order to improve efficiency, save costs, and build the state's technical capabilities and talent. This is a long-term effort but it has the support of multiple management staff!
IDEA_TEXT: Bring website technical management in-house to improve efficiency, save costs, and build state technical skills.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Employee training

COMMENT: Most people associate the EDD with unemployment insurance or disability insurance. However, it is much more than that. It is a government agency that provides resources, job referrals, referral to services, trainings, etc. to help people in need. We need to do a better job at informing people that we are on their side. The EDD can be a bridge to help people not only get by but literally change their lives for the better.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve public awareness of EDD services. Better inform people that EDD provides job help, training, and other support beyond just unemployment and disability benefits.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public participation; Public policy initiatives; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT: As a Californian and with my expertise as a state employee, the primary problem I would address is the lack of urgency and care in responding to public, as well as internal inquiries (e.g., a penalty abatement request to the IRS taking 60 days to process or internal budget approval requests interfering with immediate needs, such as the need to dispose of hazardous waste within regulatory timeframes, due to long processing times). My ideas for solving the problem I shared includes creating expectations for higher communication standards, such as through departmental policies or other methods, to encourage faster processing times and responses to email and other typical communications methods.
IDEA_TEXT: Create faster response standards. Set department policies for quicker processing times and email responses to public and internal requests.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Communication and knowledge sharing; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal communication; Process design and methodologies; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT: I have developed tools (Excel Workbooks) to help outreach workers track farmworker contacts. Currently working on developing guidance that may increase the provision of services to farmworkers.
IDEA_TEXT: Create Excel tracking tools for outreach workers to track farmworker contacts and develop guidance to increase services to farmworkers.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Software and tools

COMMENT: I have begun working independently, with feedback from my team, on standardized procedures for "evaluating" the local inspection agencies (county and city "Certified Unified Program Agencies") my unit oversees. Prior to this, no such procedures were being used to my knowledge, resulting in inconsistencies between each "evaluator" in the unit, and unnecessary effort being spent every single new evaluation trying to figure out and remember how each analysis was completed. While this project is still incomplete, I believe this suite of procedures in its early stages has already helped to increase consistency between staff and reduce training times, and increase training effectiveness, for new staff.
IDEA_TEXT: Create standard procedures for evaluating local inspection agencies. Develop a complete set of evaluation steps that all staff can follow to ensure consistency and reduce training time.

IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Process design and methodologies; Process documentation

COMMENT:
California's state agencies often have different interpretations of the same policies set by entities like CalHR, the Secretary of State, and DGS. This decentralized approach creates a lack of uniformity in business processes across departments, particularly in areas like Human Resources.

A recent project aimed to identify a "golden image"—a consistent, shared process—for the departments within our agency. However, we discovered that each department follows a different process for the same core functions. This forced us to develop a separate, customized IT system (SaaS) for one department and then hire a consultant to modify the code to fit the next department's unique needs. This constant customization is expensive, with each new system integration costing between $250,000 and $500,000.

By mandating a single, consistent interpretation of state policies, we could achieve significant efficiencies and save millions of dollars. A standardized approach would allow for easier cross-training of staff and eliminate the need for costly, department-specific customizations of IT systems. It would ensure that technology solutions developed for one department are readily usable by others, creating a more cohesive and cost-effective operational model for the state.

Furthermore, it may be beneficial to centralize human resources at the agency level and have one  HR system across the whole agency to save even more money.  This centralized idea can work for other admin areas as well.
IDEA_TEXT: Centralize human resources at the agency level with one HR system across the whole agency to save money.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Communication and knowledge sharing; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Cross-agency collaboration; Shared resources; Software and tools

COMMENT: If we have a huge deficit, why fix something that isn't broken.  The money it would cost to bring state employees back to office is extreme.  We downsized to save on rent and maintenance as well as cost to the state.  RTO will not promote collaboration because staff will still be meeting via Teams.  RTO also means more interruptions with staff walking to restrooms or breakrooms.  Noise levels will return.  There is no flexibility with RTO and managers/supervisors will wind up just micromanaging which takes away from better solutions and better results.  Gas is already expensive, and we took a pay cut for two years.  How is RTO going to improve collaboration, innovation and accountability when we are already doing it remotely?
IDEA_TEXT: Keep remote work to save money and maintain current productivity levels.

COMMENT:
As a California state employee focused on educational improvement, I look to streamline cumbersome regulatory processes into collaborative support systems that better serve our local educational agencies.
Federal Program Monitoring (FPM) 
My most significant contribution has been leading the redesign of our Federal Program Monitoring approach, moving from a compliance-focused audit model to a capacity-building partnership framework. Previously, the FPM process created significant anxiety among LEAs, with extensive document requests and adversarial site visits that consumed enormous staff time on both sides while providing limited educational value.
A key component of this transformation involved conducting a comprehensive review of existing FPM requirements to identify regulatory overreach. After systematically analyzing our monitoring protocols, several questionable requirements were brought to the legal team for review. This process resulted in the elimination of multiple requirements that exceeded our statutory authority and created unnecessary burden on districts without corresponding educational benefit.
IDEA_TEXT: Change Federal Program Monitoring from a compliance audit model to a partnership that builds capacity. Remove requirements that go beyond legal authority and create unnecessary work for districts.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Governance and oversight
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Align policy and implementation; Compliance

COMMENT:
Bias Elimination Through Automation:

RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generator) systems make decisions based on explicit criteria, not writing quality. This is a LLM (Large Language Model) utilizing only documents that have been curated.
Consistent application of standards regardless of reviewer mood or workload
Transparent decision logic that can be audited and explained

Cost Reduction Through Intelligence:

80%+ of applications would never need a review by a human, dramatically reducing staff costs
Human reviewers focus only on genuine edge cases requiring judgment
Real-time feedback eliminates multiple submission cycles

Outcome Focus:

The system learns from actual grant performance, not just compliance
Evidence-based interventions get prioritized automatically
Continuous improvement based on what actually works for students, data would drive decisions.

Practical Implementation Notes:

Start Small: Begin with one grant program (maybe Perkins V) to prove concept and refine algorithms
Data Integration First: The auto-population from existing systems is crucial - RAG works best when it has complete, accurate data
Change Management: Frame this as "freeing staff to focus on helping districts succeed" rather than "replacing human judgment"
Transparency: Make the AI decision logic completely visible to districts - they should understand exactly why they were approved/denied
IDEA_TEXT: Focus the system on actual results instead of just following rules. Use data from past grant performance to automatically prioritize programs that actually help students succeed.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Governance and oversight; Public-facing services and resident experience; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Public policy initiatives; Shared data; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT:
As a state employee, I am writing to propose an innovative idea to standardize specific statewide policies across all departments. Currently, many departments independently develop similar policies in operational, human resources, and administrative areas. Centralizing these policies would create significant efficiencies and result in substantial resource and cost savings.

This proposal includes leveraging technology to automate this process where feasible, developing comprehensive templates, and providing statewide training to ensure consistent implementation across all departments. It is recommended that the state centralize this effort for all general policies within the California Department of Human Resources.

Additionally, there is some statewide centralization for Information Technology (IT) and security policies. However, IT and security policies should be reviewed to identify if there are more opportunities for centralization. While many IT and security policies are proprietary, there may be areas where streamlining is possible. Statewide IT and security policies should continue to be centralized within the California Department of Technology.
IDEA_TEXT: Review IT and security policies for more centralization opportunities. Continue centralizing these policies through the California Department of Technology.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Technology and digital innovation; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Policy development process; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT: A large problem is the manual redundant processes for state mandated reporting. State entities must report quarterly and/or annually to oversight entities. Many of these reports are manual excel spreadsheets or PDF documents that incorporate/reiterate information already available to the oversight entity, putting an undue burden on the workforce to reproduce . Processes could easily be streamlined with a shared platform for reporting and monitoring. Workflows could be automatically initiated at a set frequency to ensure timely updates are provided by the entity in accordance with requirements. Data could be prepopulated for ease of completion.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a shared digital platform for state reporting. Automate quarterly and annual reports with pre-filled data and automatic reminders to reduce manual work.

COMMENT: I have been a buyer, contract analyst and purchasing manager for some years. I think some of the excessive purchasing laws and policies make the State purchasing/contracting procedure unnecessarily complex. Simplifying the process and reducing the number of forms needed not only expedites the process but also saves taxpayer money. One example I suggest removing is the DVBE participation requirement in formal solicitations. Almost, 99% of the times, it's been waived due to highly specialised services or other factors. These types of laws and policies do not help DVBEs; they just add additional paperwork and delay the contracting process. Other examples are forms like Darfur, California Civil  Rights Laws Attachment, and Iran Contracting Act Verification that need to be submitted by bidders/contractors, or 113 pages of Telecom General Provisions included in telecom contracts. The lengthy provisions and forms that are never looked at or used for anything waste taxpayer money and complicate the process for companies. Some vendors, especially Small Business suppliers they do not have a lawyer on board to help them understand the State contract provisions and for that reason do not participate in bidding opportunities. There are a lot of requirements during the purchasing process that could be removed or simplified. I'm referring to the laws and policies included in State Contracting Manuals Volumes I & II and State Administrative Manual (SAM).
IDEA_TEXT: Reduce barriers for small businesses in state contracting. Simplify contract language and requirements so vendors without lawyers can understand and participate in bidding opportunities.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Plain language; Procurement

COMMENT:
1. Less training that doesn't apply to my job. The same online training every year is a waste of time. If certain behaviors are not appropriate, then reprimand the wrong doers. It is mentally draining to have to repeatedly do the online trainings when I wouldn't have been doing it without the training, reprimand the wrong doers.
2. There should be an easy way for employees to submit anonymous commits to their supervisors and managers for change.
3. Make it a priority for the State to pay their bills. There is no reason why businesses should go months and months, maybe even longer, without being paid. We as people can't get away without paying our taxes.
4. How about a large pay cut for those responsible for the budget? If we don't do our job we get reprimanded. But if the budget does work out, we get pay cuts.
5. Make the roads better. Stop making traffic lanes for the rich.
IDEA_TEXT: Create an easy way for staff to give anonymous feedback to supervisors and managers about needed changes.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal feedback
