COMMENT:
One of the primary problems I would address is the outdated and fragmented database systems we rely on as state employees. The current infrastructure is antiquated (over 40 years old and is DOS), and because so many departments and individuals are involved in each process, with systems that don’t communicate with each other, it often creates delays and confusion. Even when tasks are completed promptly on our end, we’re left waiting for other departments to act, which can negatively impact the employees we serve. I'm speaking from within Human Resources. 

I understand that there are efforts underway to create a new system, but past attempts have fallen short due to the complexity of the project. Training has also been limited to overly basic scenarios, leaving staff unprepared for real-world complications. When unique or difficult cases arise, there’s often no clear guidance or consistent answers.

To address this, I believe we need:

A centralized and integrated system that reduces dependency on multiple platforms.

Collaborative development of the system with feedback from front-line users during the build phase, not just after rollout.

Advanced scenario-based training that includes real-life, complex examples.

A dedicated support team that can provide timely and consistent answers when issues arise.

These changes would not only improve internal workflows but also ensure better service and outcomes for the public and our fellow employees.
IDEA_TEXT: Replace old database systems with one integrated system. Include front-line staff in building the new system, provide advanced training with real examples, and create a support team for ongoing help.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Communication and knowledge sharing; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Internal feedback; Software and tools; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT: For our unit we work with foreign vendors to create trade shows or hire consultants in other countries. Our payment of a physical check has presented many issues and in some instances is not a legal payment (i.e., South Africa). Being able to pay via wire transfers is something the Golden State who prides itself in innovation should be able to offer.
IDEA_TEXT: Enable wire transfer payments for foreign vendors and consultants instead of requiring physical checks.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Procurement

COMMENT:
With years of experience in my field, I've had the opportunity to observe what works and what doesn’t. As a result, I’ve helped develop more streamlined processes to improve both efficiency and accuracy. For example, we’ve implemented internal logs, checklists, and verification systems that help double-check our work and reduce errors. These tools have improved consistency and accountability within our team.

I take a hands-on, supportive approach when onboarding new staff. I train them thoroughly and monitor closely while also giving them the grace to learn and grow. I’ve found that setting clear expectations and leading by example, especially when it comes to prompt resolution of issues, helps create a strong culture of reliability and trust. My goal is to avoid making excuses and instead focus on getting things done, which ultimately supports our field staff and leads to better outcomes across the board.

Our team also works very collaboratively, and we’ve built a strong understanding of how to support each other, share knowledge, and problem-solve together. That team-based mindset has made a big difference in how effectively we serve others.
IDEA_TEXT: Build team collaboration and knowledge sharing. Create systems for staff to support each other and solve problems together to serve others more effectively.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Organizational structure and culture; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Internal communication; Knowledge management; Work culture

COMMENT: Any plans to return to office more than two days per week is an economic drain on the state and a  demoralizing prospect for hard-working, family-oriented civil servants. I propose a permanent two days in office policy be put in place (although one or zero days would be even better).

COMMENT: The State's haphazard approach to RTO which includes dismissing telework is certainly a primary concern with my colleagues. The State's attempt this year to needlessly change to 80% RTO ended up with a huge amount of waste. Departments were moved around for ultimately no reason, and without proper research done. When an actual thorough study did come back, it was after things had already been postponed, and the study showed that telework saves taxpayers millions, and falls in line with California's goal of reducing carbon emissions to combat climate change. How many thousands of hours of State Employee time, and consequently, millions in taxpayer dollars, were wasted by this rushed and mis-informed decision? How many thousands of Employees lives (daycare, commute, carpool, home office investments, etc.,) were financially & physically disrupted, changed, and then changed back? All this because the State did not do proper research or go through proper channels. I certainly hope we've learned some valuable lessons and approach this issue differently next year. If not, there will be an exodus of talent, because a primary reason people come to state service is the benefits, and telework is a big one.
IDEA_TEXT: Do proper research before making telework policy changes. Study the costs and benefits thoroughly before implementing return-to-office requirements.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: There is a wonderful opportunity through a COVID bright spot to redesign how state employees engage in their work. The recent legislative audit outlined several ways telework is a benefit to all Californians. I urge all state agencies to build on the efficiency, financial savings, recruitment benefits, and environmental protections telework affords. Don’t we want state agencies to recruit and retain the best employees from throughout the state? Telework is a golden opportunity to move forward to create the most efficient, productive, and inclusive workforce to best meet the needs of Californians.
IDEA_TEXT: Expand telework options for all state agencies. Build on efficiency, cost savings, recruitment benefits, and environmental protections that telework provides.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Communication and knowledge sharing; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Cross-agency collaboration; Environmental sustainability; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT:
Individuals who are hired to carry out a responsibility should have the authority to do so. Excessive oversight and approvals make delegating responsibilities useless and lead to hiring more people who are unable to actually do their work and to delays in processes and product delivery.

Employees at all levels should be empowered to take initiative to carry out the responsibilities of their role and be held accountable for their work rather than placing responsibility and accountability only in those at the top of the chain of command.

COMMENT:
As a tenured state employee with over 10 years of experience, one of the biggest problems I've seen is the retention of employees who don’t contribute to the mission, goals, or vision of the department. It’s frustrating to work alongside individuals who consistently underperform or show little motivation, especially when dedicated employees like myself are paid the same or often less. This impacts morale, slows progress, and weakens the overall effectiveness of the team.

To address this, I’d advocate for a more meaningful and transparent performance evaluation process that includes peer feedback and self-assessments, not just supervisor input. Raises and promotions should reflect both tenure and performance, so that strong employees feel valued and motivated. I’d also propose a peer recognition program to highlight those who go above and beyond, and development plans for employees who need improvement. Finally, training supervisors to actively manage performance issues would help ensure accountability.

These changes would support a healthier workplace culture and better public service. By recognizing effort and addressing complacency, we can build teams that truly reflect the values of public service.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve performance reviews and accountability. Create a transparent evaluation process with peer feedback and self-assessments. Link raises and promotions to both tenure and performance. Add a peer recognition program and development plans for struggling employees. Train supervisors to actively manage performance issues.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Governance and oversight; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Employee pay and benefits; Employee performance reviews; Employee training; Management culture and leadership approach

COMMENT: Over my 10+ years in state service across three departments, I’ve consistently worked to improve processes and enhance customer service. At one department, training compliance was at just 35%. I created a clear tracking system, sent regular reminders, and worked directly with staff to remove barriers to completion. Within months, compliance rose to 82%. In another role, customer service request closeouts were stuck in the 30–40% range. I streamlined the intake process, clarified responsibilities, and helped implement better follow-up procedures, which brought closeout rates to over 60%. These efforts not only improved efficiency but also boosted staff accountability and customer satisfaction. I take pride in identifying gaps and turning them into opportunities for improvement.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve customer service request closeout rates by streamlining intake processes, clarifying responsibilities, and implementing better follow-up procedures.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Process design and methodologies; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT: I initiated discussions that engaged higher-ranking executives. After identifying the key issues, I received approval to procure modern software tools to enhance our digital data collection and sharing from the field and office, benefiting everyone. I also prioritize continuous learning and make efforts to share that knowledge with others. It's amazing what we can accomplish if we take the time to learn how to use the tools we currently have. We need to have the approval to exchange this knowledge, which is not always afforded. I also created a SharePoint website for our specific division, which had no previous internal online presence. The site is used to publish and maintain information uploaded by anyone, creating an encyclopedia on how to accomplish our work. It's "grassroots" actions like this that are most effective, but can be stifled at times if the idea(s) did not originate from management.
IDEA_TEXT: Create internal websites like SharePoint for divisions to share knowledge and maintain work instructions that anyone can update.

COMMENT:
I am particularly concerned about the financing of Public Health services and programs in California. Local Health Jurisdictions (LHJs) rely heavily on grant money allocated by the CDC or the State. Most of these grants appear to be formula-based, while some are competitive. Problems:
1. Funding public health by grants is not sustainable due to factors such as the end of grants and new policies.
2. Allocations are based on factors that don't accurately represent the real needs of each LHJ.
3. LHJs are not usually part of the decision-making process on how to allocate funds to address the diverse and unique needs of LHJs.
4. Strict guidelines on usage, limits the ability of LHJs to address the specific needs of their LHJ creatively.
5. Not all LHJs possess the skillset and resources to compete fairly for competitive grants distributed by the CDC or CDPH.
Proposed solutions include:
1. Updating allocation methodologies with modern economic models that incorporate factors representing real needs and adjusting for population variations.
2. Making the process participatory, allowing LHJs to contribute to the construction of formulas and the utilization of funds.
3. Building local capacity to secure merit-based grants.
4. Shifting funds from clinical services to Public Health, benefiting both Managed Care Plans (MCPs) and LHJs. I would need more space to elaborate on the details how #4 can be operationalized.
IDEA_TEXT: Help Local Health Jurisdictions get better at winning competitive grants by building their skills and resources.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Public-facing services and resident experience; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Employee training; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: In the private sector, there are roles (IT Business Analyst) that sit between IT and the business that help identify opportunities to advance the mission via improved technology tools and/or processes, and serve to translate the business needs to IT and the technology constraints/opportunities to the business. This role has been absent in my government organizations, and as a result I see a reactive IT force and outdated tools serving the public. Agencies/departments should have such a role to proactively identify opportunities to better meet the mission/serve the public via technology tools; coordinate implementation, drive efficiency, overcome silos, and save on costs. One example: CNRA purchased a GovDelivery contract and negotiated so that all departments could use it - great! But when DPR (Parks) communications adopted it, they did not identify other needs within DPR which was using at least 4 different email services, and they didn't set it up well. The public was signing up for GovDelivery mailing lists that were never used, and some that were being used (i.e. State Parks Commission comms) were still being managed in an Excel spreadsheet list copied into Outlook (yikes). It was poorly implemented and misused because there was no role responsible for identifying the department-wide needs, developing an implementation plan, executing the implementation, training users, and staffing a department-wide administrator to manage the product after launch.
IDEA_TEXT: Create IT Business Analyst roles in government agencies. These staff would sit between IT and business units to find ways to improve technology tools and processes, translate business needs to IT teams, coordinate implementation, drive efficiency, overcome silos, and save costs.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Technology and digital innovation; Communication and knowledge sharing; Budget and resources; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Digitize processes; Process design and methodologies; Qualified staff; Shared resources; Software and tools

COMMENT:
PROBLEM

There is no real-time visual summary of statewide sources of funds (revenues) and uses of funds (money spent on programs and services). The absence of real-time data on revenues and expenses may make it challenging for lawmakers, control agencies and constituents to manage/adjust their budgets based on the latest info. 


SOLUTION

Create an online visual tool with real-time data of sources of funds (from CDTFA, FTB, etc.) and uses of funds (to the General Fund, special funds, and locals). 

This would enable lawmakers, control agencies and the public to have a better understanding of the current financial state of revenues and expenses overall as well as to individual programs. A real-time (perhaps updated weekly) tool should also enable the State to better forecast revenues, expenses and surplus/shortfalls.
IDEA_TEXT: Create an online visual tool with real-time data showing state revenue sources and spending. Update the tool weekly so lawmakers, agencies, and the public can see current financial information and better predict future budget needs.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Governance and oversight; Public-facing services and resident experience; Budget and resources; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Public participation; Public service delivery and responsiveness; State-spending audits; Technology and data modernization

IDEA_TEXT: Streamline state contracting and purchasing processes. Create master contracts for public works and services that allow quick work orders to be issued efficiently.

COMMENT:
I would address is the breakdown in communication, specifically how long it takes to report and resolve operational problems. In my experience, communication often functions as a buffer that insulates leadership from the daily realities faced by frontline staff. When issues arise, responses are frequently delayed due to layers of approval, bureaucratic hurdles, or vague assurances that “it’s being looked into.” This delay not only prolongs the problem but can exacerbate it to the point of creating liability or safety risks. Unfortunately, when these issues escalate, leadership may downplay their prior awareness, further eroding trust and morale.

To solve this, I would advocate for a transparent, time-bound communication protocol that includes:

•	A tiered reporting system that tracks issues from initial submission to resolution, with clear accountability at each level.  While some systems are already in place the emphasis should be on ensuring managers cannot minimize their role when issues persist.

•	Mandatory feedback loops where leadership must acknowledge receipt and provide status updates within a set timeframe.

•	Consistent process audits to identify bottlenecks and recurring delays, followed by actionable reforms.

•	And most importantly, a culture shift that values timely action over bureaucratic deflection, recognizing that unresolved issues create stress, inefficiency, and risk for those doing the work.

This should be done while respecting existing labor rights.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a clear communication system with time limits. Track issues from start to finish with accountability at each level. Require managers to respond within set timeframes and provide regular updates. Audit processes to find delays and fix them. Focus on quick action instead of bureaucratic delays.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Process improvement; Governance and oversight; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Internal communication; Management culture and leadership approach; Process delays; Process design and methodologies

COMMENT: I've been with the same department for 11 years, before that, I served in the military for 25 years. The primary problem I see has been the use (or lack of) technology.  There are so many things that would be more efficient if technology was utilized. We manage millions on excel spreadsheets, which I find unbelievable and a risk. When new technology is procured, the IT branch decides what to turn on or not and it's one thing at a time.  For example, we have two IT units, one oversees Outlook and allows us to use CoPilot, but the unit that oversees Teams, won't allow us to use CoPilot.  It makes no sense. I wish I had a solution to recommend.
IDEA_TEXT: Coordinate IT units to allow consistent technology use across all systems. Let staff use the same tools like CoPilot in both Outlook and Teams.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Software and tools; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT:
I've been a civil servant for over a decade, and having come from the private sector, the biggest improvement that we can make is removing unmotivated staff and sub-par performers. Managers need to jump through far too many hoops for progressive discipline with potential legal backlash, and even that doesn't provide sustainable results for improving staff productivity. 

We need the ability to terminate staff that continuously fail to meet the expectations of their duties. For example, if there are at least three documented conversations about the same issue, managers should be able to move forward with termination instead of issuing a Letter of Instruction (LOI). The truth is that a lot of managers are hesitant to issue LOIs because these can hinder that staff's ability to leave their current role for another position. This means that the problematic staff won't be moving anywhere and will continue to be a problem in the current unit. Staff like this bring down morale and create more work for others on their teams. This inefficiency is costly and fiscally irresponsible to State funding, and hence, to California taxpayers.
IDEA_TEXT: Make it easier to fire poor performers. Allow managers to terminate staff after three documented conversations about the same issue instead of requiring Letters of Instruction.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee performance reviews; Management culture and leadership approach

COMMENT:
A primary problem I would address is understaffing. At the district where I work, we have been chronically understaffed for as long as I've worked here and long before I started. Presently, we are short two supervisory managers that are needed to help direct key program areas and many full time positions in our district remain unfilled. At the same time, we are told that we need to provide the same, if not higher level of service to the public. This leads to existing staff being worn beyond thin working multiple jobs and duty statements that do not accurately reflect their workload. The situation just feels like a crash happening in slow motion with me as a rank-and-file watching it happen with little that I can do about it.

If I had a magic wand that could easily change things, I would focus on ramping up hiring and retention. I understand there are budgetary considerations, but it is not possible to recruit and retain long term employees if our district does not have any growth opportunities for seasonal staffing. An overreliance on seasonal employees seems to be the crutch that my department relies on, forcing interested seasonals to endure years of attrition and competition before landing a permanent position.

By retaining more long term employees, they will have a better chance of learning how state systems work and managing them efficiently and effectively. Longer term, this will be more sustainable than relying/spending on technology centered "efficiency" campaigns.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve hiring and retention by creating more permanent positions and growth opportunities. Reduce reliance on seasonal staff by offering clear paths to permanent jobs.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Career growth; Employee retention; Limited-term positions

COMMENT:
Invest in people, not flashy products backed by multibillion dollar marketing campaigns.

As Silicon Valley pushes their ideas of innovation and visions of the future, I can't help but feel that the basics are being ignored. Sure, there are a lot of things that can be automated with better software and the pursuit of efficiency, but we have fundamental issues that have hardly been addressed and papered over in the pursuit of an overly idealistic future driven by marketing and the unending quest for financial profit.

Particularly in this moment where students of all ages are leaning on AI to complete basic coursework, we are at a critical juncture for ensuring future generations can think for themselves. How can people govern themselves if they cannot arrive at a conclusion through their own thought processes?

Let's focus on improving the fundamentals before jumping onto bandwagons.
IDEA_TEXT: Focus on basic needs and skills before adopting new technology. Invest in people and fundamental services instead of expensive tech products.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Public-facing services and resident experience; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Public policy initiatives; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT:
Full time telework solves so many problems.  Many private leases can be cancelled to save millions for the State.  For staff that still must come into the office for a legitimate in person requirement (processing mail, etc), multiple departments can consolidate into existing State owned buildings.  The Franchise Tax Board campus would be a great place to consolidate these staff.  There are multiple buildings to house different departments with free parking.

Staff that get to telework 100% would be much happier and more productive at work and at home.  It was proven to work during the pandemic.  There was never a problem with full time telework and it didn't need to go away.  Staff in positions that can't telework would have something to strive for in future positions.  The State government would be able to obtain the best talent away from the private sector and offer a new incentive to work for the government.  Taxpayers would be happy to see the cost savings.

I suppose the only people unhappy with full time telework would be urban business owners.  It is said that urban areas hurt when people telework.  But there are winners and losers in everything in life.  For every downtown business owner that is unhappy, there is a business owner in the suburbs that is getting more business.

Let people live and work where they want to.  Less commuting is less polution and traffic, which also benefits everyone.
IDEA_TEXT: Implement full-time telework for eligible positions. Cancel private office leases and consolidate remaining in-person staff into state-owned buildings like the Franchise Tax Board campus to save millions in costs while improving staff productivity and retention.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Employee retention; Physical infrastructure; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT:
California’s government must recommit to a centralized IT strategy—one that prioritizes shared services, specialized expertise, and fiscal responsibility. The current decentralized approach, where individual departments manage their own IT infrastructure, is inefficient, costly, and misaligned with the state’s legislative intent.

Many state agencies were created to fulfill specific missions—public health, transportation, education, and more. Yet today, these departments are investing significant time, energy, and capital into building and maintaining their own IT systems. This diverts focus from their core responsibilities and leads to duplication of effort, fragmented infrastructure, and inconsistent standards.

The California Department of Technology (CDT) was established to solve this problem. Assembly Bill 2408 and the resulting Government Code provisions laid the foundation for a centralized IT model—one that consolidates services, reduces redundancy, and enables departments to focus on their primary missions while leveraging CDT’s expertise.

Despite this mandate, many departments continue to operate isolated IT silos. This not only undermines the intent of AB 2408, but also wastes taxpayer dollars and slows statewide progress. The result: overlapping systems, inconsistent security protocols, and missed opportunities for innovation.
IDEA_TEXT: Centralize IT services through the California Department of Technology (CDT). Stop departments from running their own IT systems and make them use CDT's shared services instead.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Organizational structure and culture; Budget and resources; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Consolidate government agencies; Cross-agency collaboration; Shared resources; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT:
Something I've noticed from working for several years in local and state government is that living in a constantly changing digital world, we need updates to some of the software we use every day. For example, when using our system's database, we should be able to customize and refine searches with filters, run reports that show only the data we need, save/edit groups of favorite sections when we cover other desk areas, etc. 

I believe there is a need for a tech or IT committee composed of one person(s) in charge of collecting ideas from his/her department(s) and discussing strategies with the IT department in meetings. This could lead to discussion about how to optimize or implement more efficient and effective use of software, websites, and useful settings. Even further, this could result in more communication and transparency from IT in the form of occasional trainings for those interested in solving their tech needs.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a tech committee with department representatives to collect ideas and work with IT on software improvements and training.

COMMENT: As a former Federal Employee, and current state employee, I think there is a gap when it comes to holding employees and leadership accountable to their responsibilities. While there are a ton of dedicated and competent employees, there are also those that utilize the protections afforded to public employees and those in unions to not meet even minimum potential. To solve this problem, I think we should ensure that employees and supervisors are being periodically checked in on. I have had three roles within the state government since 2021 and have never had a performance review or worked with a supervisor on a plan for trainings or moving forward in my career. I also think supervisors should undergo a 720 degree review - where they are appraised by their supervisors, employees, and themselves.
IDEA_TEXT: Create regular check-ins for all staff and supervisors. Include performance reviews and career development plans.

COMMENT: As a former Federal Employee, and current state employee, I think there is a gap when it comes to holding employees and leadership accountable to their responsibilities. While there are a ton of dedicated and competent employees, there are also those that utilize the protections afforded to public employees and those in unions to not meet even minimum potential. To solve this problem, I think we should ensure that employees and supervisors are being periodically checked in on. I have had three roles within the state government since 2021 and have never had a performance review or worked with a supervisor on a plan for trainings or moving forward in my career. I also think supervisors should undergo a 720 degree review - where they are appraised by their supervisors, employees, and themselves.
IDEA_TEXT: Require 360-degree reviews for supervisors. Have them rated by their bosses, staff, and themselves.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee performance reviews; Management culture and leadership approach

COMMENT: I am newer to the state, as are some of the commenters below, and I have to say I wholeheartedly agree with there needing to be more direction and guidance. We know that some people learn and work differently (it is standard practice to identify our "types" when taking entry community college courses) and that should be applied in the workplace. The bureaucratic comments may be to blame for a lot of the unwillingness to step outside of our role to assist a coworker who asks for help or something like that. I have always been like "we are on the same team," and will help any way i can but quickly learned that others are not like minded. Only in my state jobs though. I think rank-and-file employees have first hand experience and are kept far from the decisions about their own roles and what processes should be changed to save money.
IDEA_TEXT: Include rank-and-file employees in decisions about their roles and processes to save money.

COMMENT: I am not in a leadership position but have had to take on responsibilities of my supervisor in his absence, and I was responsible for a specific run that required driving to pick up our caller service at the post office. I did it for 3 months and it got done between 11 and 12 every day. Upon my supervisors return from leave, he wanted control back (aligning with other comments about superiority complex) and instead of asking me about how its been working and issues i have run into, he did his own trial and error and his process wound up taking half of the day and multiple different people being involved to complete it. We still do it this way and it makes no sense to me. It is little things like that which add up, but not being a supervisor its hard to mention these things
IDEA_TEXT: Ask staff who do the work how to improve processes before making changes. Use their knowledge instead of starting over with trial and error.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal feedback; Process design and methodologies

COMMENT: Waste. Regarding departments I've worked in and in talking with at least a half dozen other departments and heads of departments it seems a common theme. I was picking up something inter-office today and heard finger pointing on a department meeting that could be avoided with simple process improvement protocols. There's a lot of wasted time on meetings that have no clear goals/agenda/template/time constraints, redundancy in process due to lack of process awareness and documentation, no standardized document management protocols, no clear mission of waste reduction, hierarchical structures are preached rather than going direct to a solution which wastes enormous time and hinders solution iteration/revision/implementation, not offloading to contractors the busywork related to their contract housekeeping, etc. Lean culture needs to be implemented asap with everyone trained in Lean methodologies so the mission is clear and the Gemba is understood to be open and the org chart flat for these purposes. Many don't want to rock the boat but it seems like the dirty little secret of water cooler talk. Lean initiatives would save the taxpayer millions each year if done well.
IDEA_TEXT: Implement Lean culture and methods across all departments. Train all staff in Lean practices to reduce waste, improve meetings with clear goals and time limits, create standard document management, flatten hierarchy for faster solutions, and have contractors handle their own paperwork.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Management culture and leadership; Governance and oversight; Process improvement; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Digitize processes; Employee training; Management culture and leadership approach; Org structure and hierarchy; Process design and methodologies

IDEA_TEXT: Use Lean methods to improve government work processes.

COMMENT: PR. There is such a deep misunderstanding of why working in the public sector must be different than working in the private sector. All people hear about are the slow wheels of bureaucracy and government waste, not realizing that the design is purposeful. Obviously there ways to make things more efficient, like digitizing forms, modernizing ways to make payments, etc. However, my experience is that most agencies are working on these issues. Just look at the DMV, or being able to pay a parking ticket on your phone, amazing! However, due to the nature of our work, sometimes it's important to work slowly and methodically, with standard procedures. When we "move fast and break things" or suffer from burnout trying to "optimize" our workday into hyper-efficiency, those things effect people's lives very deeply and if we can be thoughtful, we can make them better than the original concept. When we do that, it always pays us back, fiscally as well as socially. Because the public is so focused on waste, instead of hiring internally, we hire contractors, and instead of hiring the best contractor, we have to hire the one with the lowest bid. This creates real waste that hurts the tax payers and frustration of employees. There's a reason why most private tech companies crash, and that's no way to run a government.
IDEA_TEXT: Change hiring practices to hire the best contractors instead of the lowest bidders to reduce waste and improve service quality.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Procurement; Qualified staff

COMMENT:
CEQA reform. Too many good-for-the-environment projects are stopped due to CEQA.  This needs to start with an information campaign, because it sounds like a bad idea initially.

Too much "bean-counting".  Yes, it is good to people and the state accountable, but when the process of holding-people-accountable uses up more time and money then that actual project, it is inefficient and wasteful.  There should be accountability on the bean-counters themselves!  

The hiring process is biased when questions are limited and have to be pre-approved.  The current process stifles follow-up questions, and this is inherently unfair to people who speak english as a 2nd language, or who come from a culture where that is more demure and respectful.  The loudest people who overshare and not your best candidates, but speaking for too long after each question is an effective interview strategy (and it shouldn't be).
IDEA_TEXT: Improve the hiring process. Allow follow-up questions and make interviews fairer for people who speak English as a second language or come from different cultures.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Organizational structure and culture; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Hiring and recruitment; Inclusion and diversity

COMMENT:
Focus on policies that can retain the best employees. Internal promotions should be an option, instead of people having to leave agencies to get a promotion - it creates a brain drain. NOTE - I also think hiring outsiders is good and brings fresh insight.  But there needs to be a balance, and it needs to be based on merit, not seniority.  

California agencies are supposed to serve all of California, not just Sacramento.  People from ALL of California should be eligible to work for the state, and there should be local representation. There needs to be a mechanism for geographic flexibility, whether that's full-time telework or a network of offices in all corners in the state.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow geographic flexibility for state workers. Offer full-time remote work or create local offices across California so people from all areas can work for the state.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: Modernizing procedures and turning legal forms into plain language so that access to justice isn't hidden behind jargon. It makes it better for Californians, but also for those processing the paperwork. When people understand, the whole process goes smoother, even if the reason you have to fill out the form is frustrating. This doesn't mean dumbing things down, but thinking about them deeply to make sure we convey exactly what we need and removing any excess fluff. This takes a lot of thought, because it requires us to question why we are doing something, why we are asking other people to do things, and whether the current process really gets us there, in practice and not just in theory. It takes more work upfront, and can take some convincing, but it's really just applying a "measure twice, cut once" mentality to work. I've seen staff workload get lighter as a result, freeing them to do more in other areas (especially ones they're interested in). I work with a historically difficult demographic and I've seen these methods systematically repair our relationship with this population, making our work easier, but also allowing us to build on those improvements. Improving processes and providing better customer service, when done mindfully is a circle.
IDEA_TEXT: Modernize procedures and convert legal forms into plain language. Remove jargon and excess content to make forms easier to understand for both residents and staff who process them.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Plain language; Process design and methodologies

COMMENT: I would really like to see more transparency about why things are done the way they are and making the human element visible. This means explaining that certain things may be done slowly because of understaffing and underfunding, where fully staffed/funded agencies are successful, and when things are done slowly in order to be deliberate and thoughtful in order to be successful long term rather than provide a quick fix. Inversely, when a quick fix is just that, and that there are long term solutions being worked on. To update the mental image of a state worker. The public image is that of a lazy person procrastinating filling out their paperwork, when in reality there may one be a single person who is responsible for processing that paperwork...for the entire state. When I explain this to friends, they're shocked and often way more understanding of public servants after that. I'd like to see more windows into the humanity of these agencies.  I'd like to also see more promotion of these public sector jobs, their importance and what they provide to local communities, etc. We need to move away from thinking about budgets like pie chats and graphs and more like people in buildings, who work all day to improve the public welfare, pushing those numbers (and dollars) towards the most good possible with the current information/resources (hindsight is always 20/20 and we're always looking to improve), and who are regular people, neighbors, and support local communities.
IDEA_TEXT: Increase transparency about government operations. Explain to the public why processes take time, show staffing levels, and highlight the human side of state work to improve public understanding of government employees.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Governance and oversight; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Public participation; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT: Increased efficiency in State procurement for information technology needs. The two control agencies (CDT and DGS) need to simplify and streamline IT goods and services approaches. Many of these are antiquated. CDT could provide cost savings by being the front of many basic IT goods and services procurements, then leverage this out to state entities. Also, although the focus on small business is important,  for complex IT goods and services, most small businesses need additional support from DGS to develop relationships with vendors, skill sets, and expertise to support technical procurements. DGS could rely on technical staff to develop enterprise and mandatory agreements (with CDT) to reduce overall risk. Another area is GenAI and AI procurements. We need to get faster on understanding risks and benefits. Reliance on private data centers, as well as the global impact of GenAI power consumption, need to be examined.
IDEA_TEXT: Help small businesses with complex IT contracts. Provide DGS support to build vendor relationships and technical skills.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Cross-agency collaboration; Employee training

COMMENT:
Document/form management is a statewide challenge that could more effectively be coordinated centrally. In the previous century, everything went through the Office of State Publishing, and the State Library had catalogued copies of everything that was published, but now each agency has its own content management system and publishes to the web with no centralized coordination. Retrieval largely depends on private 3rd party services.
Index each web address and tag with metatext; leverage librarians' expertise to structure a retrieval system facilitating public access. Track revision dates, ownership, and language translations within the same platform. Use AI document translation with a common lexicon, have a disclaimer and feedback mechanism to prioritize review (e.g. vital docs), ID bad translations, recommend improvements. Also flag items past a certain revise date.
At present we have active standard forms owned by agencies that no longer exist and historic documents posted that pop up when searching for current policy. It is not always clear what is current and what is outdated.
Systems like WorldCat and GoogleBooks provide models, and there could also be a concierge function for the public to punch in what they need and receive instructions along with any necessary forms and guidelines (perhaps compiled by AI and verified by human staff, moving to spot checks as effectiveness of the tech is demonstrated).
IDEA_TEXT: Create a central system to manage all state documents and forms. Index web addresses with tags, track revision dates and ownership, use AI translation with human review, and flag outdated items. Include a public help feature where people can request what they need and get the right current forms and instructions.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Communication and knowledge sharing; Technology and digital innovation; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: AI and large language models; Digitize processes; Public service delivery and responsiveness; Shared data; Software and tools; Translations

COMMENT: The State of California has many Agencies that do the same work, but in a different way. The Governor has talked about consolidating some of those departments, but that's not really being done according to the mandate. The main issue is that those that are in charge of making changes have a big ego that gets on the way of serving California effectively.  For example, in the homeless issue, sometimes it takes 3 to 4 months for a homeless to be referred to an apartment and receive all the medical, mental and housing needed at that time sometimes it's too late.
IDEA_TEXT: Consolidate state agencies that do similar work to reduce delays and improve service delivery for programs like homeless services.

COMMENT: Executive Leaders/Managers should be subject to 360-like, anonymous reviews and required to implement changes proposed in those reviews that could result in better processes and procedures. A simple thing like having less meetings (because so many are unnecessary and add to the bureaucracy), or delegating responsibilities more effectively, or identifying and establishing SOPs to resolve gaps. Again, this review should be required. Even well-intended leaders/managers can be blind to their short-comings or lack self-awareness or act defensively when given critical feedback.
IDEA_TEXT: Require managers to get anonymous feedback from staff through 360-degree reviews and implement the suggested changes to improve processes, reduce unnecessary meetings, delegate better, and create standard operating procedures.

COMMENT:
I agree that hands-on training needs to be much better.  I have moved around in the same Dept. at the same level now for 20 years and I find it difficult to understand why there isn't consistency from one are to another.  I usually have to spend a lot of time trying to put things in order in order to find what I need, find what others need, find the processes to do the job, find the resources to do the job, all of which is time consuming and shows a lack of consistency.  I agree with the person below, the training, not the annual training we all get, but the hands-on training we get is poor.  I get the impression that the supervisor doesn't really know what the person in the position does.  Instead of help, it's criticism and sometimes yelling at a person.  I come from years of private sector work and the environment between state and private is so different.
I'd like to see more cooperation and less dictatorship and egotism.  We all have a job to do, and we do it better when we cooperate instead of arguing or putting each other down.  I'd like to see consistency from one area to another within the Dept.  There was an attempt to make all institutions the same and that is a great idea, but it didn't really happen like it was intended to.  I still see a lot of confusion and inconsistency which makes it difficult to get things done.

I would like to see a real retention schedule that gets reviewed by a committee and updated; it surely helps when getting audited.
IDEA_TEXT: Change management style from criticism to cooperation. Train supervisors to support staff instead of using criticism or yelling.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Management culture and leadership approach; Trust and openness; Work culture

COMMENT:
I would like to see better housing and someone to realistically look at housing.  It's way too expensive; incomes don't rise with the exorbitant rate rents do, what one gets for what has to pay for is woefully substandard.  I'd like to see statewide, consistent rent caps.  When folks are stressed out just by one of the three necessities of life, food, shelter and clothing, let alone the other two, it is extremely stressful, affects job performance and mental health.

Seems to me California should be able to do a better job in these areas.  After all, they are life necessities.

COMMENT:
Coming from the military into the state, I have noticed a lack of proper training and materials to aid in job support, roles and responsibilities, as well as understanding the state rules/policies. When I met with HR, I explained being new to state and was handed a document with links and was told to go research with no explanation. 

For my job, I was told it takes about 1-2 years to grasp even the lingo in our unit, let alone how we operate. There were no procedure logs or training as there isn't any turnover for the last employee to show a new one what it was they did. The structure seems a bit unorganized and the chain of command doesn't seem to be followed.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a proper training program for new state employees. Include job procedures, state rules and policies, and clear roles and responsibilities with materials and guidance from HR.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Governance and oversight; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Align policy and implementation; Employee training; Onboarding new employees; Process documentation

COMMENT: I made things simple. Instead of solely relying on teams or emails, I made phone calls to discuss issues or processes with customers and had very positive feedback. I eliminated internal lingo and simplified what it is we do and how to help them. COMMENT: Living in CA is expensive! I truly believe if you can work fully remote, let your staff do so to save money and manage their work/life balance. I have great staff who get more done than when they go into the office. If going into the office is truly for morale purposes, have it be one day a month where the entire building comes in to have everyone meet and do a team building event and not necessarily work. Then have an outing to do events in the community or eat out to support businesses.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow staff to work fully remote to save money and improve work-life balance. Hold monthly in-person team building events with community outings to support local businesses instead of regular office work.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Organizational structure and culture; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee retention; Remote work and return to office; Work culture

COMMENT:
I think California is a great state to work for and I believe that my department truly wants to improve. I work for CDCR and I have observed some areas where improvements can be made.

From my anecdotal observations, we have a poor hiring process for managers and tend to promote people into management roles without the skills, knowledge or abilities to be a manager. I noticed we promote those who may have good supervisory skills into management roles, but not necessarily management skills.

I would prefer CDCR to look outside the department to staff management roles. We currently tend to have an unofficial requirement to come "up the ranks" to staff management positions when getting someone "off the streets" may be of better value as a manager. Supervisors and staff must be technical experts; managers need to manage. 

Additionally, we tend to undervalue education in this department. I believe having a relevant education should be strongly considered for promotion. Many studies have proven the value of education, and I never understood why CDCR's hiring practices put no value in having an education. If I were able to make the decision, I would require a BS degree and experience to be interviewed for a management position.

I am not implying all of CDCR's managers are horrible people, but I have observed a lack of critical thinking and poor management skills with many of them.
IDEA_TEXT: Hire managers from outside CDCR instead of only promoting from within. Require a bachelor's degree and relevant experience for management positions to improve management skills and critical thinking.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Hiring and recruitment; Manager training; Qualified staff

COMMENT:
I found many issues start with poor communication. Through several years in my department, I have observed management routinely fails to communicate down to the supervisors or rank and file. Additionally, I have observed management almost never looks down for answers to problems. I believe this is due to a consistent belief that only managers are stakeholders on departmental issues.

In my role as a second-line supervisor I have made it a point to know what is changing and keep updated on regulatory changes. When something does change, I make it a point to communicate the change to my staff and allow my staff to provide input on how that change is implemented. 

I found through my career, CDCR management consistently fails to identify the stakeholders when programs change or get implemented. I try to overcome this failure by allowing my staff to feel as if their expertise and opinion have value. Though one-on-one and group conversations with my staff I ask how they would make it better, or how they could have handled issues differently in the future. Unfortunately, I cannot share this information with management as either they would demand staff to be "written up" for a minor mistake or explain management makes all the decisions.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve communication between management and staff. Keep supervisors informed of changes and let staff give input on how changes are put in place.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal communication; Internal feedback; Management culture and leadership approach

COMMENT:
Problems: 
- Lack of vision and commitment to achievement, growth, and excellence. This saps motivation and can lead to a public service workforce that's just "going through the motions" to get the paycheck / no longer spurred by desire to serve the public.
- Hierarchical org structure that minimizes the ideas and opinions of rank-and-file employees.
- There are few "senior staff," non-management positions. This results in many people being promoted to manager roles that they're either unprepared or not suited for.

Solutions: 
- Opportunities for people at all levels to participate in developing organizational vision and goals. 
- Foster a culture where staff are encouraged to share ideas even if/when they're contradictory to "direction" from above.
- Creation of more senior staff positions where people can move up in agencies without becoming managers.
IDEA_TEXT: Add more senior staff positions so people can advance without becoming managers.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Career growth

COMMENT: Many state employees in lower-wage classifications struggle with housing costs, and some are even homeless while serving Californians every day. A state-facilitated housing match program could safely connect Californians who have rooms or rentals with civil servants who need affordable housing, with the state verifying employment (with consent) to protect both sides. This creates a win-win: Californians gain reliable tenants, and state workers gain stable housing so they can continue serving our communities.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a state housing match program. Connect state workers who need affordable housing with Californians who have rooms or rentals. Have the state verify employment to protect both sides.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public policy initiatives; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT:
One issue I have consistently encountered is the challenge of working with state procured contractors, especially in programs that involve direct public service like case management.

In my experience, onboarding and training contractors can be time consuming, and often their staff do not fully understand program policies or the scope of work. This can lead to inconsistent messaging and confusion for the Californians we serve. Many of these vendors are also based out of state and may not fully grasp the unique needs of our communities.

I believe we should explore bringing more of this work in house. For example, having state employees handle case management could improve consistency, accountability, and transparency. It would also allow us to better control outcomes, reduce excessive billing, and build internal capacity by developing staff skill sets.

While contractors can play a role, I think a more balanced approach with a stronger in-house presence would ultimately lead to better service delivery and public trust.
IDEA_TEXT: Bring more case management work in-house instead of using outside contractors. Have state employees handle case management to improve consistency, accountability, and transparency while reducing costs and building internal staff skills.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Communication and knowledge sharing; Governance and oversight; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Contracts and vendors; Employee training; Internal feedback; Shared resources

COMMENT:
One issue that deserves more attention is the disconnect between state employee compensation and the rising cost of living across many regions in California.

While I understand that salaries can improve over time through merit-based increases in line with the classifications pay scale, the reality is that many state positions, some of which require degrees and several years of relevant experience, start out with compensation that is classified as low income under both state and federal income limits in numerous counties. These are not entry level roles, yet the starting pay often does not reflect the level of responsibility, expertise, or the cost of living in the areas we serve.

Public service is deeply meaningful and essential work. Most of us do not enter this field with the expectation of becoming rich. However, when experienced professionals are struggling to meet basic living expenses, it creates an external burden that can impact their ability to perform at their highest potential. Financial stress can affect focus, morale, and long-term retention, ultimately impacting the quality of service we provide to the public.

Addressing this issue would not only support the well-being of dedicated employees but also strengthen the effectiveness and sustainability of our workforce. Investing in fair and competitive compensation is an investment in the quality and continuity of public service.
IDEA_TEXT: Increase starting pay for state jobs to match the cost of living in different areas. Make sure experienced workers can afford basic living costs to improve job performance and keep good employees.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Employee retention

COMMENT: I haven’t been a state employee long, but I work with a lot of people that have so I’m basing this on coworkers’ explanations/comments along with what I’ve seen so far. I would say address the repetitiveness/excess/disconnection. How many departments provide the same service to the public/have the same or similar program? Does that need to be provided in multiple departments? Do those departments work together or at least know of the other programs and departments? Are the processes as efficient and streamlined as possible? As mentioned in other comments, the purchasing process for example. Do we really need 3 bids for all purchases? Is that an outdated requirement? There seems to be a lot of oversight/"red tape" in some areas, yet huge contracts can be awarded with little to no oversight of the program/department/funding disbursements after.
IDEA_TEXT: Balance oversight better. Reduce red tape in some areas while adding more oversight to large contracts and funding.

COMMENT: Less Paper: I would love to be on a team where any office who is using paper to officially transmit records is given an efficiency audit where the team determines if there is a better way to do the data transfer. For example, I have seen entire jobs where one staff completes a piece of paper to give to another staff who then retypes the paper into a system. The entire job consists of hand entering data already completed by staff from the same department. This is true for the public giving the State data too. Today in my public citizen life I had to complete a paper to a state agency I know will be entered into an online portal. I assume that data is being hand read and entered based on my experience of 15 years and 4 agencies.
IDEA_TEXT: Create efficiency audit teams to review paper-based record transfers and replace them with digital data transfer systems.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Process design and methodologies; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT: Waste Watchers: I loved Gov. Schwarzenegger had a waste watchers type thing. Although the tiny savings of each one person turning off a budling's lights or illegal use of State vehicles (two stories I remember reported there), those individual savings probably didn't pay for the cost to run the waste department, but I think we need something like that. The State Auditor/Whistly blower, I called once and they said, "we are not Big Brother, we have 5,000 calls a year, you have to provide all the evidence to the State Auditor for us to do anything". But as a regular employee I can't access my bosses' records, I can't photocopy the subject state records, nor bring a camera into the place I had another waste/audit complaint. I think in the long run, the state investigating waste, even if not quote fraud, would pay off in long term efficiencies and cost savings. Maybe like a Mello-Roos Tax, if a waste if found the Waste Watcher department gets a cut of the savings to help fund the cost to investigate. Then the Department won't be cut in the next hard budget.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a Waste Watchers program to investigate government waste and inefficiency. Fund the program by giving it a percentage of the money saved from waste it finds, similar to a Mello-Roos tax structure.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Governance and oversight; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Budgeting and funding; State-spending audits

COMMENT:
As a Californian state employee with expertise in enterprise architecture, one primary problem I would address is the slow execution of projects due to repetitive and lengthy approval processes. A key improvement would be to implement a "Smart Approval Framework" that leverages a centralized repository of previously approved use cases. This system would use metadata tagging and AI-driven similarity analysis to identify when a new request closely matches a previously approved one. If a match is found, the system could auto-recommend fast-track approval, reducing redundant reviews. 

This would not only accelerate execution but also promote consistency, reduce workload on review boards, and encourage reuse of proven solutions—ultimately driving more agile and responsive public service delivery.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a Smart Approval Framework that uses a central database of past approvals and AI to fast-track similar new requests, reducing review time and promoting consistency.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: AI and large language models; Digitize processes; Process design and methodologies; Technology and data modernization

It makes completing our jobs in a timely manner impossible.

COMMENT: "bulk" training procurement by state department. If CNRA could contract and offer trainings that a large percentage of staff may be required to take, staff would simply have to sign up through some interface, rather than every staff person or department having to individually procure the training. COMMENT: The primary issue I have experienced is ineffective management. COMMENT:
Deep and miring bureaucracy. I know this goes with the territory, but it's really hard to take sometimes. I often feel like I'm wading through molasses trying to do BASIC parts of my job. This manifests in too many ways to count, but a couple issues are top of mind: 
1) The regulatory environment limiting restoration-- hell, even basic ongoing maintenance-- is stifling work when the need for urgent action is so high. The "cutting the green tape" initiative was good, but didn't really fix structural issues. For example, replacing a minor culvert under a remote road in the middle of summer when it's bone dry still costs $10,000 in labor before ground is even broken. Surely there could be a streamlined (no pun intended) process for this-- perhaps a straightforward checklist or rubric to determine if there are major concerns or perhaps statutory exemptions for which you qualify. 
2) Basic tasks like purchasing and timesheets are so complicated that we literally need a TEAM of administrative staff to handle navigating the esoteric, bureaucratic backwaters of Sacramento. Even with their help, it's frustrating, time-consuming, and hilariously dysfunctional. This is don't have solutions for. Ours is a large and complicated state. I've seen too many failed attempts at systems integration to believe there's a single fix for this. It may not be possible to put humpty-dumpty back together again.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a simple checklist or rubric to decide if restoration and maintenance projects need full review. Give statutory exemptions for basic work like replacing small culverts.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Governance and oversight; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Align policy and implementation; Policy development process; Process design and methodologies

IDEA_TEXT: Create an anonymous communication tool for employees to share feedback with upper management without fear of punishment.

COMMENT:
I worked at California Department of Water Resources (DWR). The department depends on accurate, timely data to manage California’s water, yet many workflows remain manual, siloed, and slow. For example, the current processes for generating critical data—like ETaw used in water budget calculations—span multiple teams, take up to 12 weeks, and involve redundant steps such as manual file transfers, duplicated analysis, and outdated tools. These inefficiencies limit transparency, slow responses to urgent requests, and create inconsistencies across programs.

I proposse Land and Water Use Cloud Pilot aims to address these bottlenecks by demonstrating how a modern, cloud-based data stack can streamline workflows. By automating repetitive tasks, integrating land use and water use analyses, and leveraging scalable platforms such as Google Earth Engine and Snowflake, the pilot will reduce turnaround times, improve collaboration, and ensure results are both accurate and transparent.

This proof-of-concept will show how moving from fragmented, manual systems to an integrated cloud environment can cut processing times dramatically (e.g., reducing ETaw delivery from 12 weeks to 4), while laying the foundation for broader adoption of modern solutions across DWR. Ultimately, this approach strengthens our ability to conserve water, respond quickly to partners, and deliver high-value information to millions of Californians
IDEA_TEXT: Create a cloud-based data system for the California Department of Water Resources. Use modern platforms like Google Earth Engine and Snowflake to automate manual tasks, connect different teams, and cut processing times from 12 weeks to 4 weeks for critical water data like ETaw.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Technology and digital innovation; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Digitize processes; State cloud storage; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT:
People often blame CEQA for slowing fuel reduction projects, but the real barriers are limited capacity, planning inefficiencies, and overlapping regulations. Even when CEQA has been waived—such as the 45-day priority projects or the 2025 Secretary’s suspension—we still must comply with all other environmental laws. That often results in more paperwork and delays than if we had simply gone through CEQA. For example, CDFW frequently requires a take permit, which adds 90+ days to a project, making timely fuel reduction even harder.

A more effective solution would be to create a 4(d) rule under the California Endangered Species Act (CESA), modeled after the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service’s approach under the federal ESA. These rules allow targeted exemptions or modifications to take prohibitions for threatened species, so beneficial projects like fuel reduction can move forward without lengthy permitting when impacts are minimal or positive. This is especially critical for California’s fire-adapted species, which often benefit from vegetation treatments.

Currently, CDFW offers Memoranda of Understanding for take, but these are case-by-case, drafted after listing, and far less efficient. A 4(d) rule would instead be established at the time of listing by the Fish and Game Commission, ensuring consistency and streamlining the process. Where federal 4(d) rules already exist, the Commission could adopt them directly, avoiding duplication and aligning state and federal protections.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a 4(d) rule under the California Endangered Species Act (CESA) to allow fuel reduction projects to move forward without lengthy permits when impacts are minimal or positive. The Fish and Game Commission would establish these rules at the time of listing to ensure consistency and streamline the process.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Public-facing services and resident experience; Governance and oversight
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Align policy and implementation; Permits, licensing, and fees; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT:
Yes, I have seen opportunities where services could be more effective and efficient, and I’ve also tried to model that in my own work. For example, I’ve dedicated personal time to developing a proposal to modernize our departmentwide data infrastructure. I approached this both from the top down—by contributing to department strategic action to re-writing data preservation policy—and from the bottom up—by building user cases that demonstrate practical benefits. On my own, I’ve developed scripts to streamline workflows and integrate processes that were previously manual and siloed.

At the same time, I’ve found it challenging. My proposal for a modern data stack hasn't really 'caught fire' there. In fact, you could say it backfired on me. It didn't ignite innovation; instead, it felt a bit more like – drawing trouble to myself.  Things have pretty much stalled out. It's been a bit frustrating, to be honest – sometimes my ideas get totally sidelined, or I end up doing really repetitive, manual tasks that feel like a step backward.  It’s clear that breaking down silos and advancing modernization is larger than what can be achieved in my current role alone. Still, these experiences reinforce my belief that a more integrated, cloud-based approach would significantly improve efficiency and service delivery across the State.
IDEA_TEXT: Modernize data systems statewide. Use cloud-based technology to connect departments and reduce manual work.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Communication and knowledge sharing; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Digitize processes; Shared data; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT: Overlapping and sometimes duplicative permitting processes across different departments create frustration with the permitted community. Specifically, the California Department of Fish and Wildlife regulates substantial alterations to or diversions from streams. Similarly, the Regional Water Quality Control Boards regulate fill discharge to Waters of the State. The State Water Resources Control Board regulates water diversions. Sometimes all three departments are involved in permitting a single project, sometimes it is only two of three, or only one. But in the case where all three departments are involved, there are three completely separate and independent processes for submitting the project description and receiving state authorization (not even mentioning the local permitting requirements that are also typically involved). Combined permits may not be possible, but having a single hub for applying for state permits could be a start. Alternately, creating better mechanisms to collaborate across departments (maybe shared office spaces and shared virtual teams) would be extremely useful. Sharing resources across departments in general feels like a win for the state and the various departments doing similar work.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a single hub for applying for state permits across the California Department of Fish and Wildlife, Regional Water Quality Control Boards, and State Water Resources Control Board.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Communication and knowledge sharing; Budget and resources; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Digitize processes; Permits, licensing, and fees; Software and tools

COMMENT:
To improve efficiency and accountability, state government should pair top-down evaluations with structured bottom-up feedback. Currently, employees are reviewed by supervisors, but managers rarely receive input from the staff and programs they oversee. This creates a blind spot: how can managers become better leaders, improve efficiency, and address challenges if they never hear from the people most affected by their decisions?

Once someone becomes a manager, the department’s instinct is often to protect them from criticism. While meant to support leadership, this practice can unintentionally foster waste, fraud, abuse, and inefficiencies. Without meaningful feedback, even well-intentioned managers may not know what needs to change.

A balanced system would introduce safe, constructive ways for staff to share input—such as anonymous surveys or 360-degree reviews—while preserving fairness and professionalism. The goal is not blame, but growth: identifying strengths, addressing weaknesses, and building stronger teams. This feedback loop would improve trust, strengthen programs, and ensure managers are as accountable as the staff they supervise.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a two-way feedback system where staff can safely give input to managers through anonymous surveys or 360-degree reviews while keeping current top-down evaluations.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Communication and knowledge sharing; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee performance reviews; Internal feedback; Management culture and leadership approach

COMMENT:
Less paperwork for every day functions.  For example, we have fuel pumps and paper sheets to track the fuel usage.  It is subject to human error, and extra time balancing the sheets every time we make a fuel purchase.  If the fuel pumps had electronic sign in for unit number, and PCA code option, we would be saving lots of headaches and potentially recover lots of money that otherwise gets lost from the emergency fund.  if you had to punch in your unit number every time you filled up, through some kind of key pad, it would streamline that aspect of our department.  Look in to a company called Link2Pump.  

Another note

I don't understand why the process takes years for me to get a contractor to fix up my facility.  Our building is from the late 60's, and If I ever want to get something done or fixed, the easiest option for me is to do it myself.  I dont see why having the lowest bid contractor shouldn't be able to start work in a few months. Instead im told it takes 3 years.  It is causing me more work in the long haul instead of letting a licensed contractor do all of the work.
IDEA_TEXT: Replace paper fuel tracking with electronic systems. Install keypads at fuel pumps for unit numbers and PCA codes. Consider Link2Pump company for implementation.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Software and tools
