COMMENT:
The biggest problem in CA government is authority.  Authority is going unchecked.  I know several occasions where decision makers are overstepping their boundary.  Even worse is when low level (say AGPA) is trying to do the best job they can, but don't realize the negative impact they are creating or the laws that they are breaking.  For example, a AGPA needs data from another agency.  They are blanketly ask for the information.  The other agency, doing the best job they can do, says Ok and collects the data. However, collecting that information could have taken 80 hours, or require a "system" to be built to accommodate the request.  When looking deeper into "the information", it was never required by law to collect the data. This is unnecessary waste.  Times this by 1000's of request, and we're all working for each other, and not the people of CA.

State employees are very hard workers and try their best.  Sometimes, we need to step back and make sure we are doing what we should be doing, and not what we want to do.  This wasteful work leads to wasted time spent, delays in the work that should be done, and selfish pride.

Laws - we all have to abide by them.  However, many laws have "sunset dates".  it would be nice if the State of CA did a cross analysis of what policy was created for what law.  if that law was changed, modified, or unsetted, then the policy needs to do the same.  Laws aren't typically created from scratch; they take an existing law and totally rewrite it.
IDEA_TEXT: Create clear authority limits and approval processes. Require legal review before agencies can request data from other agencies to prevent unnecessary work and law violations.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Governance and oversight; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Compliance; Process design and methodologies

COMMENT:
I was directly involved in a request from DGS to Caltrans.  DGS wanted to collect data on "every asset" that Caltrans controls.  That's a huge, tall order since we have millions of assets.  After looking into the "Why?".  It turned out that DGS wanted to find out all the vacant property that Caltrans controls.  Just by saying the word "all assets", creates a whole new meaning to this request.  It's a list of about 1000 properties that Caltrans has stored in a database, and not the millions of trucks, guardrail, nuts and bolts, maintenance equipment, etc....  big difference.

In addition, this was coming from an AGPA who interpreted the law to collect all assists.  Luckly, we elevated to much higher authority, and we all concluded it was a misinterpretation of the law.  This saved Caltrans millions.
IDEA_TEXT: Clarify project requirements before starting work. Ask why the request is needed to avoid misunderstanding the scope and save resources.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal feedback; Process delays; Process design and methodologies

COMMENT: We need to find a way to make State government more efficient. I’ve seen more and more rules, regulations, oversight, and impediments added over the course of my 20+ year career. We need to be allowed to do our job, yet it seems like at every turn there is a roadblock— from hiring, to purchasing, contracts, etc.  All these regulations are not making us better, more honest, or more effective. We have to find a way to reduce the oversight and allow us to do the jobs we were hired to do— to benefit the public and gain their trust
IDEA_TEXT: Reduce government rules and oversight that block daily work. Remove roadblocks in hiring, purchasing, and contracts to let staff do their jobs better.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Bureaucracy; Contracts and vendors; Procurement

COMMENT:
The use of Lean 6-Sigma under the Brown administration was working well.  There were many proven strategies that streamlined the processes for many agencies.  We lost that program once COVID hit.  If you want to make significant improvements, use tools and techniques that actually work.  A measurable approach to efficiencies.  

Efficiencies should come from the ground up, not the top down.  As Managers, challenge your staff to improve their workload, workflow, and decision-making improvements.  If there is a high demand for rework, your process is broken.  It needs some simple fixes to drop the error rate to zero.  It's called mistake proofing.  Don't allow mistakes to carry through a process until the end.  Stop it in its tracks.
IDEA_TEXT: Bring back Lean Six Sigma program with measurable approach to improve government processes.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Process design and methodologies; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT:
Each agency has a Mission, Vision, and Goals.  It makes us strive for a purpose.  However, some of these goals need to be cross referenced.  A goal for one agency maybe in direct conflict with another agencies goal.  There are countless examples of this.  Especially when it comes to Environmental.  We (the taxpayers) want a healthy environment, but at what cost?  We (the State Employee) want to do the best we can.  However, typically, low level employees are creating processes that increase regulations, aren't customer friendly, and drive-up cost for everyone.  They are not looking at the consequences of their decisions.

In State government, "everyone has a boss".  Just because we have Directors and other leadership with a goal, doesn't mean they have the authority to dictate regulations.  Many of them make decisions on experience and "the way it's always been done", and that's wrong.  They need to check the big picture.  Sometimes decision makers require unnecessary actions and these cause issues for others.

A great example are Governing boards, such as ARB.  They have good intentions of cleaner air for CA, but at what cost?  Should CA pay $10/gal when the rest of the world is creating global warming.  We are not the problem.  It seems like CA always has to lead.  It would be fine if the taxpayers voted on those regulations, but not when decisions are made by unelected officials.  They have too much power and are just doing what their boss wants them to do.
IDEA_TEXT: Cross-check agency goals to prevent conflicts. Make sure one agency's goals do not work against another agency's goals, especially for environmental rules.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Governance and oversight; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Align policy and implementation; Cross-agency collaboration; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT:
All boards and bureaus under the Department of Consumer Affairs (DCA) share the same goal: to protect Californian consumers. I often see representatives from various DCA entities at community resource fairs, senior fairs, etc. Outreach teams from different agencies are constantly searching for these events and trying to join them. We pay registration fees, go back and forth with organizers to arrange everything, and travel to different locations.

Why don’t we organize a DCA Consumer Protection Fair ourselves? As many DCA boards and bureaus as possible can join and set up a table/booth. Members of the public can attend and learn about all the resources available to help keep them safe, from how to find a trustworthy accountant to staying safe online, what to do if they were sold a bad car by a dealership, etc. A big issue facing us is that consumers simply don't know that our resources exist until we actively seek them out and inform them.

An Annual Consumer Protection Fair would be a great event for the public and help bring together the work that the Department of Consumer Affairs does in an efficient and targeted way. We could host it as part of National Consumer Protection Week (March 1-7, 2026).
IDEA_TEXT: Create an Annual Consumer Protection Fair. Have all Department of Consumer Affairs boards and bureaus set up booths at one event during National Consumer Protection Week to help consumers learn about available resources and services.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Public participation; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT:
This comes from the top. When you have engaged leadership (not to be confused with micromanagement), it truly helps.

For example, reframing that the people we serve are customers has been incredibly inspiring. The State does not need to market, per se, to get people to use their products. On the flip side, the State does not have to measure or iterate on an existing product. So that means we fall into the "we've always done it this way" problem.

Leaders who want their departments to meet the expectations the public has in their personal lives leads to innovation. If you can order food on your phone, turn on your camera with an app, certainly, you don't need to use Microsoft Edge on a desktop to fill out a form or print a .pdf to get your needs met.

Also it's important to seek ideas from the boots on the ground staff. They are working in lock step with customers and have already identified the needs that won't be met with a traditional RFP approach.
IDEA_TEXT: Get ideas from front-line staff who work directly with customers and know what traditional processes cannot fix.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal feedback

COMMENT:
This comes from the top. When you have engaged leadership (not to be confused with micromanagement), it truly helps.

For example, reframing that the people we serve are customers has been incredibly inspiring. The State does not need to market, per se, to get people to use their products. On the flip side, the State does not have to measure or iterate on an existing product. So that means we fall into the "we've always done it this way" problem.

Leaders who want their departments to meet the expectations the public has in their personal lives leads to innovation. If you can order food on your phone, turn on your camera with an app, certainly, you don't need to use Microsoft Edge on a desktop to fill out a form or print a .pdf to get your needs met.

Also it's important to seek ideas from the boots on the ground staff. They are working in lock step with customers and have already identified the needs that won't be met with a traditional RFP approach.
IDEA_TEXT: Have engaged leadership that treats residents as customers and expects modern service standards like mobile apps instead of outdated desktop forms.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Technology and digital innovation; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digital services; Management culture and leadership approach; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT:
I would like CDCR to look for management skills and abilities when hiring or promoting people to management positions. It seems the state tends to promote people into management roles who may be good workers or supervisors but lack management skills or training. A lot of the errors and roadblocks encountered by my peers and myself could be attributed to poor project planning or management principles. One glaring problem I routinely see is the lack, or even refusal, to have stakeholders involved with project planning. (Just a hint, stakeholders are almost never management employees.)

I think looking down for answers, properly identifying stakeholders in projects, and management staff learning how to manage would greatly help California.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve hiring and promotion practices for management roles. Look for management skills and abilities when selecting managers, not just good workers or supervisors.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee retention; Hiring and recruitment; Manager training

COMMENT: I would love to see teleworking as a permanent option. I do not like that we are going back and forth on when the return to office is going to be implemented. Teleworking has provided a better work / life balance and I love it. I am more productive at home over being in the office. I am able to focus on my health and have not been sick in well over a year. I do still go into the office eight days a month as mandated but when I am there I don't get nearly as much done and at the end of the day I am exhausted because I have to wake up earlier and between waking up earlier, getting ready for work, the work hours and commuting I have no time to focus on myself or my families well being.
IDEA_TEXT: Make teleworking a permanent option for state employees.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: Environmental compliance laws and policies exist because there is a history of resource degradation without regulations, and corresponding protective measures, like best management practices, have a proven track record of effectiveness.  However, when environmental restoration projects are constrained by environmental regulations such that the project must reduce the scope (size, duration, etc.), or they significantly drive up the costs (e.g. engineering, compliance, planning) of said projects, the public funds (State and Federal) go less to the intended use, and more to the churn of bureaucracy.  There should be more streamlining of statewide permits or more exemptions for restoration projects, and these should inherently recognize long-term benefits of restoration projects outweigh short-term impacts that follow standard BMPs.
IDEA_TEXT: Streamline environmental permits for restoration projects. Create more exemptions that recognize long-term benefits outweigh short-term impacts when projects follow best management practices.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Permits, licensing, and fees; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT:
My experience as a state employee is that many managers—and the culture they shape—need a mindset upgrade. Gallup shows that office culture and morale flow from the top down, and morale is the foundation of productivity, engagement, efficiency, and retention.

Too often, I see managers:
-Blaming staff for failures while taking credit for successes.
-Struggling to delegate effectively, creating confusion and unclear expectations.
-Avoiding leadership when it’s most needed, leaving staff to fill the gap.
-Focusing on process over results, stifling creativity and growth.
-Failing to empathize with staff as individuals, imposing unrealistic standards.
-Resisting feedback, dismissing new ideas, or fearing innovation.
-Managing by fear, micromanagement, or “command and control” rather than trust and accountability.
-Prioritizing compliance and appearances over genuine outcomes.

The result is lower morale, weaker teams, and higher turnover. Spending the time to learn how to delegate wisely, listen actively, and lead with vision pays off with stronger, more productive teams. Far too many state resources are wasted replacing staff who leave managers—not jobs.

For inspiration, I recommend Simon Sinek’s leadership talks on YouTube.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve manager training and leadership skills. Teach managers to delegate well, listen actively, lead with vision, and manage through trust instead of fear or micromanagement.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Organizational structure and culture; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership approach; Manager training; Trust and openness

Reducing the number of steps to get the job done should take top priority for every department. Investing in training every employee in LEAN would be a starting point to transform how the State does business more efficiently and serve the public more effectively.
IDEA_TEXT: Train all state employees in LEAN methods to reduce steps and make government work more efficiently.

This makes sense to spend 20 million dollars. But a few hundred bucks for pizza? There should be an easier process for small purchases.

COMMENT:
There is a huge range of income-qualified programs (IQP) and subsidies offered by the state. However, each one often has its own individual requirements. Centralizing coordination on streamlined/simplified IQP requirements would save a huge amount of work in terms of verification and avoiding having to replicate similar work across departments. 

Here's the vision someone who is qualified for a subsidized electricity rate may also be qualified for CalFresh. However, the requirements are not aligned, which means that these two programs cannot automatically "talk" to each other. Ideally, it would be good to have a universal crosswalk of IQPs so that if you sign up for one, you are offered access to all of the others at the same time. This might look like a central hub for accessing all social/welfare benefits run by the state where you fill out an initial intake survey (limited to 15 questions *max*) and then have all programs you qualify for presented to you for sign up, in such a way that you can just check a box and batch-enroll in multiple at once.

Undoubtedly this would require some legislation to fully align, but doing the groundwork to understand all IQP requirements and set up the crosswalk is the first step. But that requires someone who can work across all departments that offer some kind of IQP.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a central hub for all state income-qualified programs where residents fill out one short form (15 questions max) and can sign up for all programs they qualify for at once. Start by mapping all program requirements across departments and creating a universal crosswalk system.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Public-facing services and resident experience; Process improvement; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Digitize processes; Public policy initiatives; Public service delivery and responsiveness; Shared data

COMMENT: This is a big question!  A problem I would like to address is that bans on harmful practices, products or substances often are passed with enforcement beginning years later.  These delays often seem too long to me, and when it comes to practices / products / substances that harm the environment and / or public health, to prioritize the need of industry to transition slowly is to de-prioritize public health and the environment.  Things that are harmful, like dragnet fishing, or PFAS in dental floss, to give actual examples, should be banned as soon as possible, rather than as soon as is profitable.  If it were up to me, I would have banned dragnet fishing immediately and PFAS in dental floss within 6 months.  Both were passed with several years for implementation.
IDEA_TEXT: Speed up enforcement of bans on harmful practices and products. Start enforcement immediately or within months instead of waiting years for industry to adjust.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Governance and oversight
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: At my agency, a group of employees with an interest in how to improve the way we process permit records and data has grown as more new hires with training in data analysis and, frankly, a more contemporary perspective on how things can and should be done. What started as a few meetings on how to tackle a specific kind of data need has grown to a larger effort with IT. A culture that encourages people to take on projects that improve the agency's mission and freedom to pursue those projects so long as you're willing to do the work (a "do-ocracy") has fostered innovations. We're just limited by resources and sometimes know-how - it would be even better if there were ways to connect and learn from other departments, share tools, etc.
IDEA_TEXT: Create cross-department connections for sharing tools and learning. Build ways for agencies to work together on data projects and innovations.

COMMENT: I'll admit I'm no expert in organizational governance, but it has always struck me how fragmented CA's agencies/departments are. I think it's important to have decision-making independence for some kinds of agencies e.g., it might not be a good idea to have a Commission regulate itself, but does the staff need to be as separated as they are today? Maybe it should be easier to work on projects or tasks across agencies so good ideas can spread more quickly and agencies can have a better understanding of what their partners do and how they do it. This might be more true for the departments and agencies working on building things or managing natural resources.
IDEA_TEXT: Make it easier for staff to work on projects across different agencies so good ideas can spread faster and agencies can better understand what their partners do.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Internal feedback; Shared resources

COMMENT: I think it makes sense to have clear regulations that are not so subjective. Any choice to build something or not, to pursue a project or not, has tradeoffs. It feels like we over emphasize the potential for doing something to have an adverse impact but we deemphasize the potential for not doing something to have an adverse impact. I'd rather we set a clear rulebook for how to build things, pursue and provide the data necessary as a State to make that rulebook clear and easy to understand, and then frequently assess how the rules have been effective or not at achieving the outcomes set by decisionmakers/policymakers.  Subjectivity also leaves room for unjust application of the law but it admittedly does help tackle complex issues that sometimes just need a good judgement call.
IDEA_TEXT: Create clear, objective rules for building projects and decisions. Set up a clear rulebook with required data, then regularly check if the rules work well to meet policy goals.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Governance and oversight
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Align policy and implementation; Policy development process; Process documentation

COMMENT: This is specific to the California Model of incarceration.  The California Model is not about money and it is not about modifications.  The California Model is about mindset! Change the mindset of the leaders and, through the principle of Extreme Ownership, we can collectively change the mindset of the staff and the incarcerated.  At this time, insufficient effort has been made to change the mindset of the policy makers and stakeholders at the top, so, as a result, we will never success on the front lines.
IDEA_TEXT: Change the mindset of policy makers and stakeholders at the top of California's prison system. Use Extreme Ownership principles to shift leadership thinking, which will then change staff and prisoner attitudes.

IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership approach; Trust and openness; Work culture

COMMENT: The underutilization of the pharmacists. Pharmacists can play a transformative role in California Correctional Health Care Services by expanding their clinical, operational, and educational impact. They can contribute more effectively through a Collaborative Drug Therapy Management: 1- Work closely with physicians and other healthcare providers to optimize medication regimens for chronic conditions like diabetes, hypertension, mental health disorders, and substance use disorders; 2- Patient Education: Provide direct counseling to incarcerated individuals on proper medication use, side effects, and adherence strategies, especially for complex therapies; 3- Protocol-Based Care: Use approved clinical protocols or collaborative practice agreements (CPAs) to manage drug therapy independently in certain cases, improving access and outcomes.
IDEA_TEXT: Expand pharmacist roles in California Correctional Health Care Services. Allow pharmacists to work with doctors to manage medications, teach patients about proper drug use, and use approved protocols to manage drug therapy independently for better access and outcomes.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Public policy initiatives; Qualified staff

COMMENT: Not a primary problem, but my most recent thought: CDSS has janitors coming to our floor every day and emptying wastepaper baskets that have maybe one item in them (and, as well, waste time passing by empty cubicles several days of the week). Office janitorial work is still allocated as if we don't recycle or compost our waste (I noticed this excessive janitorial wastepaper basket effort pre-Covid), much less take into consideration hybrid schedules. If we merely asked employees not to put anything smelly in their trash (place it in the kitchen trash to be emptied daily), the janitors could empty the office wastewater baskets weekly. Or, as CalEPA does, each person is responsible for emptying their trash, as needed, in a central hallway receptacle and the janitors merely empty that one, as well as the separate receptacles in the kitchen (compost, recycling and landfill) on a daily basis.
IDEA_TEXT: Reduce janitorial waste basket emptying from daily to weekly by asking staff not to put smelly items in desk trash, or have staff empty their own trash into central hallway bins that janitors empty daily.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee retention; Environmental sustainability; Office management

COMMENT:
I think efficiency is a huge part of things for me. Being able to appropriately convey expectations in an efficient and effective manner is a challenge that I think we can all relate to in some aspect. Whether this means streamlining systems or revisiting process steps, I've long felt the impact of circuitous actions which, especially in repetitive tasks, limit the potential for more meaningful task completion, learning, and even have negative impact on employee engagement and morale. 

Proper education in evolving with technology and developing less complicated means to accomplish required tasks enables big changes to be handled in a more incremental manner, which, in turn, allows more adequate training and mitigates the inherent aversion to change by offering what appears more like help, rather than a potentially overwhelming or burdensome addition/change to one's workload or processes.

This ultimately improves job satisfaction, thus improving morale and even reducing costs as work completion times not only reduce, but the ease and desire for employees to complete more work increases. Supplementing this with relative and engaging training invites staff to become more invested in not only the evolution of the process, but the overall objective(s) of the larger department/industry.
IDEA_TEXT: Streamline work processes and systems to reduce repetitive tasks. Provide technology training to help staff learn simpler ways to complete required work. Make changes in small steps with proper training to improve job satisfaction and reduce costs.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Employee training; Process design and methodologies; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT:
I would like to see more incentivization for companies to provide for the communities they serve. In exchange for these investments, companies could simultaneously enjoy tax breaks. This could be in the form of parks, sponsoring improved education programs, donation to public transportation improvements, etc. 

This would act as a mutual benefit to the company and the community, thus allowing reduction of civilian taxation, which would mean greater money able to be injected into the community by civilian spending. 

Tax breaks could even be tiered based on the area and type of community investment(s) made. For example, a company who provided funding for a state-of-the-art housing development for lower-income individuals would receive a different tax break than another company who donates canned goods. Incentives could also be stacked, this way a company could, theoretically SIGNIFICANTLY reduce their taxation by providing extensive contributions to the community. 

I feel like this would make more companies want to move to and stay in California; plus, it could provide a mutually beneficial way to not heavily tax residents while still enabling California communities to flourish.
IDEA_TEXT: Create tax incentives for companies that invest in communities. Offer tiered tax breaks based on the type and scale of community investments like parks, education programs, public transportation, and housing for low-income residents. Allow companies to stack multiple incentives to significantly reduce taxes through extensive community contributions.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Public participation; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT:
As a state employee, my experience has been that the merit system has very few checks and balances and has largely failed. In my agency, cronyism, nepotism and favoritism run rampant. The rules speak out against these practices, but it seems that the system has slowly evolved to where it is not designed to encourage them.

One of the reasons this happens if SPB has a rule that examination results are kept private, so there is no way to know the exam results of others who have taken the same exam, and it becomes extremely difficult to prove when others rig and/or cheat the system.

Another reason is the heavy reliance on job references, which seem to these days be more important than actual work experience and meeting job qualifications.

Get a hold of all of the dishonesty and restore actual merit in the merit system and you will have better, more qualified, reliable, knowledgeable and more efficient state employees. I am tired of having to work for "the kids" of former executives who do their absolute best to ensure their next of kin get out of doing actual work.
IDEA_TEXT: Make exam results public so staff can see scores and catch cheating in the merit system.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Governance and oversight
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Internal feedback

COMMENT:
Big Idea: Streamlining Student Internships at State Agencies

Right now, if a college student in California—say, someone attending CSU Fullerton—wants to do an academic internship at a state agency like California State Parks, they face a long and complicated process. Their university has to negotiate a legal agreement (a MOU) with the agency, often involving lawyers on both sides. This process can take months and discourages students and agencies from even trying.

My proposal: The Department of General Services (DGS) should create a blanket MOU that covers academic internships between all California public college systems (Community Colleges, CSU, and UC) and all state agencies. This would eliminate the need for each university and agency to negotiate separate legal agreements every time a student wants to intern.

Agencies would still define the details of each internship—like duties, schedules, and supervision—but the legal framework would already be in place.

This would:
- Make it easier for students to gain hands-on experience in public service.
- Reduce administrative burden for both colleges and agencies.
- Create a clear recruitment pathway for state agencies to identify and develop future talent.
- Expose students to careers in public service, helping build a stronger, more diverse pipeline into government work.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a statewide agreement for student internships. Have the Department of General Services make one legal agreement that covers all California public colleges and all state agencies so students can intern without each school and agency making separate deals.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Cross-agency collaboration; Shared resources

COMMENT:
I've been a state employee at the prison for more than 10 years. These are the most common problems we see there.  
-Overriding Inmates to a lower level when they don't deserve it. By overriding youth offenders, medical Inmates or non-violent offenders to a lower-level causes disruptions in positive programing. Inmates should be placed into an appropriate security level based on their security points only.
-Promoting and Pushing the Suboxone program to receive time off makes Inmates that would normally not take it, sign up for the program just to get time off or sell the Suboxone in the dorms/housing area. Don't give time off for the program and you will have only the Inmates that really need it on the program or don't give out Suboxone because by the time they get to State prison they should have already gone through withdraws and be clean.
-The overuse of medications to Inmates is out of control. We are no longer a prison but a medical facility. Most Inmates take medication in hopes to get time off of their sentence or get on Social Security when they get out. Thousands of pills are spit out or sold in the dorm. Have doctors not medicate Inmate so easily.
IDEA_TEXT: Place inmates at appropriate security levels based only on their security points, not overrides for youth, medical, or non-violent offenders.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public policy initiatives

COMMENT:
Primary Problem
Sales and use tax regulations are complex and often contain gray areas. True expertise usually comes from auditors who have dealt with specific, real-world applications of the law. However, there is no easy or safe way for auditors to share their knowledge across the department. Getting clarity on how to apply a regulation often requires layers of approvals, and managers may hesitate to elevate questions up the chain. This slows audits, wastes staff hours, and delays clear guidance to taxpayers.

Proposed Idea
Create a safe, department-wide collaboration channel where auditors can directly share and discuss regulation questions with a large group of experienced peers. For example, an internal email group or moderated forum could allow auditors to submit questions and receive input quickly, without having to navigate multiple levels of approval. Faster feedback would reduce audit hours, improve consistency, and give taxpayers clearer answers sooner, all while fostering a culture of shared expertise and professional growth.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a department-wide collaboration channel where auditors can share and discuss tax regulation questions with experienced peers. Use an internal email group or moderated forum to allow quick feedback without multiple approval layers.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Internal communication; Internal feedback; Software and tools

IDEA_TEXT: Set firm deadlines for taxpayers to provide books and records during audits. Combine this with faster internal collaboration to move cases forward quickly while maintaining fairness.

COMMENT: Allowing staff to do more things on their own, without need for technical support. If there is a simple, but common task that someone could be taught and allowed to do, it saves everybody time by allowing that person to take care of it themselves. There is a compromise between security and convenience with the tradeoff being that individuals can now solve their own problems quickly without needing to wait for somebody else. Simple example is password resets. Many public products offer self-service password resets where is emails you. There are a lot of systems that don't allow that and require reaching out to a helpdesk instead, possible waiting hours for someone to manually reset it.
IDEA_TEXT: Give staff more self-service options for common tasks. Allow employees to handle simple tasks like password resets on their own without waiting for technical support.

Positions are filled much faster and you reduce the number of resources required. This saves a lot of time by not having to post multiple job postings, do screening for multiple positions, and holding multiple rounds of interviews.

COMMENT: A fair and unbiased review of telework for State employees should be completed. Departments should be allowed to present production data from before and during telework. Have them paint the picture of how the department's work is or isn't impacted by telework, and the cost savings associated with telework. A true cost benefit analysis needs to be done. Additionally, survey State employees to see how many in person meetings they have during their in-office days. Are they engaging in person more or less? Do they still log in to Teams or Zoom for meetings?
IDEA_TEXT: Conduct a fair review of telework for state employees. Have departments show work output data from before and during telework, including cost savings. Survey employees about their in-person meeting habits during office days.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Process improvement; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee performance reviews; Internal feedback; Process design and methodologies; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: The primary problem is low salary scales. This isn't just my opinion: The California Legislature's Bipartisan Fiscal and Policy Advisor released a report with data that shows average monthly salary figures have continued to fall behind market averages even calculating for inflation, and top step for salaries has also been in decline. An employee will abide great discomforts if they're compensated handsomely. But, that same employee will pursue other employment if neither salary nor working conditions reach respectable levels. Getting "paid in sunsets" is insufficient to recruit and retain top talent. Field staff are repeatedly told we're the most valuable members of the organization. But, HR, finance, management, and safety policies loudly declare the true narrative which contradicts all niceties.
IDEA_TEXT: Increase salary scales to match market rates and improve working conditions to recruit and retain top talent.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Employee retention

COMMENT: As a skilled trades worker, the problem I would like to see addressed is obtaining and retaining other skilled trades workers like me. The State of California has and does promote individuals from non-skilled trade roles to skilled trade positions. While the opportunity for one to promote and improve their wages is a great thing, the flip side is that the non-skilled staff create a dilemma that highlights the destitution of knowledge which hinders the efficiency of skilled trade workers. Skilled trade staff are forced to pick up the slack, figuratively speaking, by correcting bad work or having to train individuals on how to correctly administer trade work, things that are usually taught through apprenticeships or trade unions.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve hiring and training for skilled trades workers. Require proper apprenticeships or trade union training before promoting non-skilled staff to skilled trade positions.

COMMENT: Let's get rid of the low income housing tax credit. It costs twice as much to build using IRC sec. 42 than it costs using market rate building. The exact same goal could be accomplished by 1. Allowing mom and pop landlords to rent to low income folks using either Sec 8 credits or just reduced rent but getting reduced or no property tax, and 2. Having developers build using market based terms not LIHTC, which adds bells and whistles to the units, like granite countertops.
IDEA_TEXT: Remove the low income housing tax credit program. Replace it with property tax breaks for landlords who rent to low-income tenants and let developers build affordable housing using regular market terms instead of adding expensive features.

COMMENT:
I would like to continue addressing the need for Californians with lower incomes (e.g., main source of income is Social Security) to have easier access to quality assisted living care when needed. My ideas for helping with this would be: 
- An Assisted Living Navigator who can personally work with individuals and their families to help them take the steps needed to access funding for care. 
- A one-stop portal (one-doorway) to allow individuals and families to make the linkages necessary to access funding for care. For example, an individual who qualifies for Medi-Cal and Medicare who wants to qualify for the MasterCare assisted living program needs to work with county Medi-Cal, their insurance, and a healthcare organization, and MasterCare to enjoin their insurance to access benefits.
IDEA_TEXT: Build a one-stop portal where individuals and families can connect with all needed programs like Medi-Cal, Medicare, insurance, and MasterCare in one place.

COMMENT: As a state employee I've realized that there really is no precedent in place that dictates how we shall do our job. There are only OP's that show and ends to a mean but doesn't show how to get there. It is up to the managers and past employees (if you're fortunate enough to be enlightened by their strategies) to aide us on how to complete our job in the most efficient manner. To that I'd like to state that when an employee promotes, transfers, or leaves CDCR completely, there is no overlapping of employment of the predecessor and successor for training. When an employee assumes a position for the first time, that employee must learn to govern themselves. They must teach themselves to be efficient and make the most out of their time. With that said, I think all employees have had to invent ways or utilize methods to ensure efficiency, whether self-taught or passed down from their predecessor- nothing is put in place to give employees direction on best practices to ensure work is completed in a tried-and-true efficient manner. There is nothing for employees to utilize and build on to tailor it for their institution specific duties. Employees are overall responsible for how their day is managed and it can highlight ones' drive to do well or not and can increase turnover rates.
IDEA_TEXT: Create overlap periods when employees promote, transfer, or leave. Have the old employee train the new employee before they leave.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee retention; Employee training; Knowledge management

COMMENT: Instead of writing a mail station number on an inter-department delivery envelope, and then continuing to scratch out the previous one and write a new one below it, we should all simply just place a sticker with our intended mail station number ON TOP OF the previous sticker, and that way it would make the inter-department delivery envelopes LAST FOREVER, thus saving the state money by NEVER needing to purchase more inter-department delivery envelopes ever again. Think about it. If everyone keeps hand-writing mail station numbers below the previous one, and scratching out the prior one, eventually the envelope becomes full and has to be thrown away and more envelopes get purchased which wastes money. BUT if everyone were to simply place a sticker with the mail station ON TOP of the previous mail station rather than below it, then those envelopes would last forever without ever needing to get replaced.
IDEA_TEXT: Use stickers on top of previous mail station numbers instead of writing below them. This would make inter-department delivery envelopes last forever and save money on purchasing new envelopes.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Office management; Physical infrastructure

COMMENT:
As a Californian and state employee, one primary problem I would address is how we support and retain the state workforce. Too often, employees face challenges such as outdated classifications, pay that does not reflect the cost of living, and limited flexibility in how we work.

To solve this, I would advocate for three key improvements: first, expanding telework options where appropriate, since telework has proven to increase productivity, save costs, and improve work–life balance; second, raising pay scales to be more competitive so that we can attract and keep skilled workers; and third, ensuring proper classifications so employees are recognized and compensated for the full scope of their responsibilities.

By addressing these issues, we would not only strengthen morale and retention among state workers but also improve the quality of service delivered to Californians.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve state worker support and retention through three changes: expand telework options where appropriate, raise pay scales to be more competitive with cost of living, and update job classifications so employees are properly recognized and paid for their full responsibilities.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Employee retention; Job classification; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT:
A primary problem I would address is the gap in support for Californians who work hard but earn just above the eligibility limits for food or housing assistance. Many working families face food insecurity or risk of homelessness because their income technically disqualifies them from aid, yet their wages are not enough to meet California’s high cost of living.
To solve this, I would propose expanding tiered or “bridge” programs that provide partial assistance on a sliding scale rather than an all-or-nothing cutoff. For example, a working family over the income limit for SNAP could still receive reduced benefits to help offset grocery costs, while rental support could offer temporary subsidies based on local housing markets. Additionally, strengthening public-private partnerships with nonprofits, food banks, and community organizations would stretch resources further.
By reimagining assistance as a continuum of support, rather than a strict threshold, we can help stabilize California’s working community and reduce the cycle of crisis many families face despite being employed.
IDEA_TEXT: Expand tiered assistance programs that provide partial benefits on a sliding scale instead of all-or-nothing cutoffs. Include reduced SNAP benefits for families slightly over income limits and rental subsidies based on local housing costs.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public policy initiatives; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT: California should stop virtue signaling and spending taxpayer dollars on things that may not be helpful to California residents. The state's resources have gone to many controversial items during this current governorship that would have been better spent earmarked for things that improve the lives of California citizens. I recently heard the governor wanting to spend millions on redistricting when I was under the impression that the state surplus is in the red. I work at an institution that is receiving less money than the years prior but with inflation and tariffs, it now costs more to operate-it does not seem reasonable to me that the state is claiming to be in deficit therefore withholding positions from being filled and money getting spent to repair much needed infrastructure all the while finding hundreds of millions of dollars for retribution acts in the name of increasing ones relevancy on the national stage for perceived bonus points for political aspirations.
IDEA_TEXT: Stop spending taxpayer money on controversial items that do not help California residents. Focus state resources on things that improve citizens' lives like filling positions and fixing infrastructure.

IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Public policy initiatives; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT: The primary issue I see is ineffective individuals in the same job for decades without being held accountable for the work they do or don't do. I believe that mandatory and semi-annual performance evaluations should be implemented for all roles. Continuing employment and pay should be based on those evaluations. Two under-performing scores in a row would initiate a Performance Improvement Plan with specific and measureable goals to meet by the end of the PIP period. If these goals are not met, depending on the severity of the underperformance, either a salary cut or termination.
IDEA_TEXT: Create mandatory performance reviews twice a year for all staff. Base pay and job status on review scores. Start a Performance Improvement Plan for two bad scores in a row. Cut pay or fire staff who do not meet improvement goals.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Employee performance reviews; Employee retention

COMMENT: In a nutshell, understand what is already in place creating new solutions. There is so much overlap between agencies that creates a great deal of duplication and confusion. This is actually pretty simple. Do your homework first. Know all stakeholders, internal and external. Create meaningful engagement upfront. I have been a state employee for 25 years and worked for 4 agencies. I have been through a great deal of change, often mandated by legislation. The implementation is always an afterthought. Implementation is essential to success. Employees in the trenches too often are not identified as stakeholders, their expertise overlooked. This is not good stewardship, and all too often results in program duplication and implementation failure.
IDEA_TEXT: Research existing programs before creating new ones. Map all stakeholders and engage them early in planning. Include front-line staff as key stakeholders and use their expertise in program design and implementation.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Internal feedback; Process design and methodologies

COMMENT: I would address the EDD Unemployment Benefits program. At a time when individuals need their benefits the most, accessibility should not be a factor. The system used to manage unemployment benefits is antiquated and the whole infrastructure needs to be modernized. Individuals having issues applying or accessing information about their claim are unable to speak to a live representative because their call center hours are extremely limited making it more difficult to reach a live person. Why can't call center hours be extended? With disability and paid family leave benefits, an individual is able to go into a local office and speak with an employee. Why was that option removed for unemployment benefits?
IDEA_TEXT: Modernize the EDD unemployment benefits system and improve customer service. Update the old computer system, extend call center hours, and bring back local offices where people can speak with staff in person.

COMMENT: Government should design public programs to help communities respond to the needs of their own community members, rather than government programs replacing the way individuals rely on community.  Therefore, every public program should rigorously evaluate the extent to which programs, and the public resources they administer, strengthen real connections between communities, families and individuals who are in need.  One area of policy this idea could be applied to is homelessness services.  Every homeless person has people who love them and want to help them, but there is often no safe way for family and friends to stay connected and engaged.  Government spending should always start from a place of prioritizing ways that it can strengthen/rebuild a vulnerable person's connectivity to their family and community. That could include creating pathways for a caring family member to make financial contributions to a family member struggling with mental illness or addition that are dedicated to housing, to food, to pro-social activities.  Or to create safe environments that are dedicated to helping people in a mental health crisis reconnect to people who love them.  People only heal in the context of community and belonging.  Our programs should prioritize paying for things that rebuild those actual connections.
IDEA_TEXT: Build safe spaces dedicated to helping people in mental health crisis reconnect with people who love them.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: Process documentation to ensure continuity of services and processes at every level. It requires governance and monitoring within the organization to develop the programs and the successful execution from the top down. It is also important to communicate successes to build confidence and employ a change management strategy.
IDEA_TEXT: Create process documentation to ensure services continue smoothly. Set up governance and monitoring from leadership down. Use change management and share successes to build confidence.

COMMENT: Purchasing and procurement! I regularly have to manage the purchase of items that are distributed to youth. These items and their related youth program are a widely recognized public good with broad public support. To purchase these items I have to spend months submitting and waiting (and waiting, and waiting) for first quotes and then waivers from CalPIA and from OSP; if I am required to work with either, I win the privilege of having 10 times the turn-around time at almost twice the price available with commercial producers. I spend countless taxpayer-funded hours navigating statewide contracts and multi-tiered purchasing requirements, all with minimal administrative support. I have to ask proprietors of small businesses to submit mountains of forms, including one that confirms they never supported a 20 year old international atrocity, and then I have ask them to float their own overhead costs before I can pay them for their services. I loose weeks, if not months, of staff time and project delivery timeliness when vendors are unable to comply with our cumbersome requirements, especially when I am required to have multiple quotes. As it stands, I see almost no benefit to the people of California or the budgeting of their tax dollars.
IDEA_TEXT: Streamline purchasing and procurement processes. Reduce wait times for quotes and waivers from CalPIA and OSP, simplify paperwork requirements for small businesses, and provide more administrative support for staff managing purchases.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Bureaucracy; Process delays; Process design and methodologies; Procurement

COMMENT: I develop and lead trainings to support the understanding of new processes. I have successfully developed a community within my various projects that support change and a willingness to execute at the program level.  I also develop both technical resources (such as databases, websites, articles, etc...) and procedural documents to support ongoing education and personal development to foster a willingness to take on new tasks.
IDEA_TEXT: Develop training programs and build supportive communities to help staff understand and adopt new processes. Create technical resources like databases and websites plus step-by-step guides to support ongoing learning and help staff take on new tasks.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Technology and digital innovation; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Employee training; Process documentation; Software and tools

COMMENT: Our exams/hiring process. First, there are extremely limited resources available to applicants to help them navigate a hiring process that is fundamentally different from most others. This means the most successful applicants are ones who either receive direct support from State Workers or who have the luxury of time and money to learn the slow way. This is a major impediment to hiring equity. Second, the almost cartoonishly strict interpretation of MQs regularly disqualifies intensely qualified applicants. The staff responsible for interpreting MQs are not experts in the fields they are judging and frequently discredit intensely relevant work experience simply due to the use of a synonym; I have seen applicants with decades of relevant experience have it all thrown out for using a slightly different word on their applicant than what the exam/application reviewer was looking for. Same too for calculating time of relevant work experience. Again, this only serves to exclude marginalized applicants.
IDEA_TEXT: Provide more resources and support to help job applicants navigate the state hiring process. Train staff who review applications to better recognize relevant work experience and use more flexible interpretation of minimum qualifications.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Hiring and recruitment; Qualified staff

COMMENT: Mentoring new employees on the important aspects of government work. Some of us joined the state as seasoned professionals, bringing a wealth of specific expertise, but are not contributing to the fullest extent of our knowledge, because the bureaucracy gets in the way. Understand that some redundancies are intentionally baked in our processes, which is why I feel that a mentor with a longer state employment could help us navigate the barriers for better efficiency and reduced frustrations.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a mentoring program. Pair new employees with experienced state workers to help them navigate government processes and reduce barriers to using their expertise.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Mentorship programs

COMMENT: The strict hierarchy in the state does not allow for innovation. Supervisors preserve the status quo instead of encouraging staff to find streamlined solutions to problems that affect the lower staff levels.
IDEA_TEXT: Encourage innovation by changing the strict hierarchy. Train supervisors to support staff ideas for streamlined solutions instead of preserving the status quo.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Organizational structure and culture; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Growth mindset; Management culture and leadership approach; Trust and openness; Work culture

COMMENT:
I feel like one major point of contention as a state worker is the RTO policy that the governor tried to implement this last year. It has been proven that we are capable of doing our jobs while maintaining a hybrid schedule.  And as someone who does purchasing,  watching my agency waste money preparing for workers to return to office seemed like such a waste of money. 
There is such a huge expense to bringing people back tomoffice that includes leading office buildings, the additional cost to workers and the additional traffic. Please keep workers well being in mind
IDEA_TEXT: Keep hybrid work schedules instead of requiring all staff to return to office full-time.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: There are many positions that can stay hybrid or remote.  If yoy are going to force workers back to office, please also compensate us more so thst we can afford the additional gas and parking costs
IDEA_TEXT: Keep hybrid and remote work options for positions that can work this way. If requiring office return, increase pay to cover extra gas and parking costs.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: California Government Code requirements around maintaining a former employee's old position as limited-term while they electively go explore another limited-term position elsewhere in State Government. The idea that we have to "keep their seat warm" while they explore other options at their own discretion (for up to 2 years or more!) is nonsensical and does nothing to support the dedication and program cohesion of the employee who fills their old job. If the job is originally funded as a permanenet position, it's status should not change nor should any preference or "reinstatement rights" be granted an ex-employee who left of their own choosing.
IDEA_TEXT: Change California Government Code rules about keeping former employees' jobs open. Remove the requirement to hold positions for up to 2 years when employees leave to try other state jobs. Keep permanent positions as permanent and do not give special rights to employees who chose to leave.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Governance and oversight
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee retention; Job classification; Org structure and hierarchy

COMMENT: Teleworking that cut down on overhead costs, improved morale, and increased productivity. But then....new edicts came down from the Governor in 2025 amidst courting the President for federal relief monies. The memo that was sent to State employees had no real validity or rationale for changing back to a system that was identified as inefficient a mere 5 years prior by the same administration.
IDEA_TEXT: Continue teleworking programs that cut overhead costs, improve morale, and increase productivity.

COMMENT: The budget always seems to be the topic that is brought up when it comes to asking for funding for different projects. We work in a huge state that covers many different areas with a wide variety of assets. It should be easier to reassign assets from one area to another within the state. These assets have already been purchased with state money. If there is a department / agency that could use a piece of equipment that is no longer needed by another, it should be easier to reassign it. If one agency no longer requires it or is due for an upgrade, another agency should be able to utilize it instead of trying to go through an entire procurement process that there is no funding for.
IDEA_TEXT: Make it easier to move equipment between state agencies. Create a simple process to transfer unused equipment to departments that need it instead of requiring new purchases.

COMMENT: It is challenging to give too much autonomy to any department when it comes to statewide issues, such as recruitment practices, policies and administrative programs, pay systems, etc., when we are all ONE EMPLOYER. It leaves a lot of room for error when we are all subject to the same laws and regulations but are allowed to interpret and apply that interpretation differently. It would be most helpful to actually have our CONTROL AGENCIES truly control, provide THEIR interpretation to laws/rules/regs that should be applied across the board, and have them develop the statewide policies (not just as a model) and practices that are most defensible to create standardization and consistency across agencies.
IDEA_TEXT: Have control agencies create and enforce statewide policies for all departments. Make sure all state agencies follow the same rules for hiring, pay, and other programs instead of letting each department make their own rules.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Governance and oversight
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Align policy and implementation; Cross-agency collaboration

COMMENT: As a Californian, one of the biggest problems I see is the inefficiency in how government work is structured. Too much emphasis is placed on rigid rules about where employees sit, how many minutes their breaks are, and other political battles, instead of focusing on improving programs and actually saving money. For example, cutting back on telework only forces more cars onto the road, increases congestion, adds to air pollution, and wastes time and money on commuting and parking. It lowers employee satisfaction and productivity. A better solution is to invest in flexible, results-oriented work structures. Telework should be preserved and expanded where possible, with clear performance metrics and accountability built around outcomes rather than physical presence. This reduces costs tied to office space and commuting while allowing employees to devote more energy to meaningful work that directly benefits Californians.
IDEA_TEXT: Create flexible, results-based work structures. Preserve and expand telework with clear performance metrics focused on outcomes rather than physical presence to reduce office costs and improve productivity.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Organizational structure and culture; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee performance reviews; Management culture and leadership approach; Remote work and return to office; Work culture

COMMENT: I have taken extensive trainings to learn more about SharePoint and the features we have access to. I have implemented automations and written down multiple policies and procedures so that others know how to create/update these automations. I also manage a team and meet with them regularly and make myself available to them at all times.
IDEA_TEXT: Invest in staff training and automation tools. Provide SharePoint training to help staff create automated processes and document procedures for others to follow.

IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Employee training; Process documentation; Software and tools

COMMENT:
Overall, I think the state training program is woefully inadequate. The two-week new supervisor/manager training is a waste of time and doesn't cover most of what managers actually face. There are so many leadership development and coaching programs in the private sector that could benefit state employees, like Harvard Business Review or Stanford. Lareg companies often have leadership development tracks. VSP, for example, has two types of tracks you can enroll in -- one for those wanting to become executives and a second track for those who want to be subject matter expert leaders (but not necessarily an exec). They provide mentors, hold classes, and you feel encouraged that you're proactively growing professionally.

Employee management training, specifically, is reactive instead of proactive. I've worked closely with performance management units to discuss personnel issues and sent emails or disciplinary plans for their review. Rather than being trained ahead of time, before issues start, I'm stuck with escalating issues waiting for PMU to review emails and provide guidance. And then their guidance changes frequently depending on what they've recently experienced in court. It's immensely frustrating.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve state training programs. Replace the two-week supervisor training with better leadership development programs like those used by Harvard Business Review, Stanford, or large companies like VSP that offer executive tracks and subject matter expert tracks with mentors and classes.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Career growth; Employee training; Manager training

IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee performance reviews; Manager training

COMMENT: I really like how the DMV is trying to make the services they provide more accessible through different methods with the kiosks, on-line, etc. I hope that other agency/departments have done or working on doing the same.
IDEA_TEXT: Expand accessible service options like kiosks and online services to all state agencies and departments.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accessibility; Cross-agency collaboration; Digital services

COMMENT:
I believe training is a critical issue that affects both new hires and experienced employees. To ensure consistency and clarity, we need centralized training guides that are regularly updated with real-time changes. Too often, I’ve approached different SMVTs or managers with the same question and received conflicting answers.

Ongoing training, regular reminders, and open communication between managers and staff should be a core part of our Wednesday meetings. Currently, these meetings often feel unproductive. However, they present a valuable opportunity to conduct reviews, discuss updates to tasks or procedures, and align on best practices.
IDEA_TEXT: Create centralized training guides that are updated regularly and use Wednesday meetings for training, reviews, and sharing best practices.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Communication and knowledge sharing; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Internal communication; Process documentation

COMMENT: I previously was CA Police Department Records Specialist and took time off for adoption and when it was time to come back to the work for I wanted to use some of the skills I have learned at the PD and felt that the DMV was a good fit as i used the DMV records on a daily basis.   I have promoted to SMVT and now am able to share alot of my customer service and tech skill with my co-workers to help them either understand how to communicate in a better way or how to search and understand records that they are reading in a more effective way, thus improving customer service from our department as a whole.
IDEA_TEXT: Share skills and knowledge between departments. Use experienced staff to train coworkers on customer service and technical skills to improve overall department performance.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Employee training; Knowledge management

COMMENT: Communication and training are the biggest issues.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve communication and training programs.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Internal communication

COMMENT: Staffing shortages need to be addressed. Every public facing department does not have enough staff to meet customer demands.
IDEA_TEXT: Increase staffing levels in all public-facing departments to meet customer demand.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public service delivery and responsiveness; Understaffing

COMMENT: As a California resident, one problem I see is that many Californians see State agencies in a negative light. To address this issue, I believe agencies need to be a little more transparent and also staffing issues seem to be a concern with the public as well. I also, utilize agencies for personal needs as required, and I notice that alot of the agencies have a negative attitude toward the public if they are not educated on what options or needs they need addressed, that being said, the state workers are not very transparent in explaining or assisting the public in that regard.
IDEA_TEXT: Increase agency transparency and improve staff attitudes toward the public. Train workers to better explain options and help residents who are not familiar with agency services.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Governance and oversight; Public-facing services and resident experience; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Employee training; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT: There is an urgent need to make all aspects of my agency electronic and remote. The effort does not take into consideration human knowledge and the multiple scenarios a customer’s record could have. There is not always an automated way to handle all transactions. And instead of realizing this, we continue to push out bad products causing confusion and frustration for both the customers and employees.
IDEA_TEXT: Consider human knowledge and different customer situations when making systems electronic. Test and improve digital products before releasing them to reduce confusion for customers and staff.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Process improvement; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digital services; Digitize processes; Human-centered design

COMMENT: Requiring employees to return to the office for an antiquated manner of managing staff is absurd. We have been actively completing our duties and flourishing with remote opportunities. Our agency has been able to complete more projects during remote work than ever before yet there is still a desire for us to return in-person. We should be allowed to continue remote work.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow staff to continue working from home instead of requiring them to return to the office.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: As a newer state employee, I was shocked that I did not have a sit-down meeting with someone in HR to help explain the benefits and at what level I was eligible for.  I didn't even know what type of employee I was, and I could not talk to anyone, I had to ask co-workers what the differences were and how it would affect me and my future.  It seemed like this was so secretive.  I would think that would be an essential part of onboarding. In addition to overall better communication, access to information needs to be improved.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve new employee onboarding. Create required HR meetings to explain benefits, job types, and eligibility levels. Make employee information easier to access and understand.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Internal communication; Onboarding new employees

COMMENT: Once a week meetings with my managers are a godsend. It doesn't feel like I'm being constantly micromanaged and it gives me enough time to make reasonable progress and show my manager in the coming week. If there is no reasonable progress, then the manager has every right to make some corrective measurements.
IDEA_TEXT: Hold weekly one-on-one meetings between managers and staff. This gives employees time to make progress and reduces micromanagement while keeping managers informed.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee performance reviews; Internal communication; Management culture and leadership approach

COMMENT: We need to significantly improve efficiency. As someone who worked in the private sector for decades before coming to state service a few years ago, I continue to be shocked at the amount of inefficiencies. Processes take too long, resources and time are wasted continuously. If we were a private business, we would have declared bankruptcy long ago. We need more innovation and get over the idea that we've always done things this way. We need to do better at keeping up with the times, learn to be more flexible and adaptable. The stereotype that government works slow is completely accurate, we often joke about it, but it should be unacceptable.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve government efficiency by embracing innovation and flexibility. Stop using 'we've always done it this way' as an excuse and adapt processes to keep up with modern times.

COMMENT:
Problem 1 – Customer Experience
From a customer’s perspective, the State’s reliance on mailed notices and mailed responses is outdated and inefficient. Californians expect the same level of convenience they experience with banks, healthcare providers, and private businesses. Mailing forms back and forth creates delays, risks lost documents, and forces residents to rely on slower communication when technology could streamline the process.

Proposed Solution:
Require high-volume departments to offer an online option for submitting responses or uploading documents. Provide funding to ensure these systems are secure and user-friendly.

Gives Californians faster, more reliable ways to communicate with the State.

Reduces dependence on paper, manual scanning, and case management bottlenecks.

Aligns government services with modern technology standards and customer expectations.

Problem 2 – Employee Retention and Knowledge Gaps
State programs are highly complex, requiring years of training to master. While it is positive that employees advance through promotions, frequent turnover combined with limited pay results in ongoing knowledge loss and operational disruptions.

Proposed Solution:
Leverage technology and AI to improve knowledge retention and training:
  
Real-time guidance systems for staff when processing cases.

AI-assisted training that adapts to an employee’s pace and learning style.
IDEA_TEXT: Require high-volume departments to offer online options for submitting responses and uploading documents. Provide funding to ensure these systems are secure and user-friendly.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Digital services; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT: Executive decision making. Control agencies create guidelines, guidance, guardrails, etc., but then allow each Department to interpret it differently. Why is hiring easier at one agency than another? Why can a contract with CARB get executed in 2 months and a contract with Caltrans takes a year?
