COMMENT:
Flatten the organization. Multi-tier mgmt hierarchies tend to result in excess procedural steps thereby slowing the overall process. Eliminate "red tape" where none are needed.

Review steps in a process to assess value in the overall procedure. If step(s) don't serve a legitimate purpose then the procedure should be tightened up to improve & fast track the execution of the procedure. In short, streamline by investigating the overall process for value & timeliness. Pare down where there are redundant or no-value steps/processes.

If there is an opportunity to automate a process/procedure, empower knowledgeable staff to explore/implement solutions even if there is an intended enterprise-wide solution targeted for deployment downstream. There is value in short-term solutions which, until a potential future large-scale solution is implemented (which can sometimes/often be unavailable for years), can greatly reduce considerable man-hours that would otherwise be necessary.

Create bridges between units that share knowledge, require similar experience/tools, feed information across units, present opportunities to consolidate & standardize output either for internal or public use. There are current silos of units that have very similar processes but are handled/implemented differently. Normalize processes across these silos.

Establish inter-division training plans to retain staff expertise. Grow/support internal advancement plans based on org job forecast. Hire from within.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow staff to create short-term automated solutions while waiting for larger system changes that may take years.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Process design and methodologies; Software and tools

Establish inter-division training plans to retain staff expertise. Grow/support internal advancement plans based on org job forecast. Hire from within.
IDEA_TEXT: Flatten management structure. Remove extra management layers and cut red tape that slows down processes.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Governance and oversight
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Bureaucracy; Org structure and hierarchy; Process delays

COMMENT:
Flatten the organization. Multi-tier mgmt hierarchies tend to result in excess procedural steps thereby slowing the overall process. Eliminate "red tape" where none are needed.

Review steps in a process to assess value in the overall procedure. If step(s) don't serve a legitimate purpose then the procedure should be tightened up to improve & fast track the execution of the procedure. In short, streamline by investigating the overall process for value & timeliness. Pare down where there are redundant or no-value steps/processes.

If there is an opportunity to automate a process/procedure, empower knowledgeable staff to explore/implement solutions even if there is an intended enterprise-wide solution targeted for deployment downstream. There is value in short-term solutions which, until a potential future large-scale solution is implemented (which can sometimes/often be unavailable for years), can greatly reduce considerable man-hours that would otherwise be necessary.

Create bridges between units that share knowledge, require similar experience/tools, feed information across units, present opportunities to consolidate & standardize output either for internal or public use. There are current silos of units that have very similar processes but are handled/implemented differently. Normalize processes across these silos.

Establish inter-division training plans to retain staff expertise. Grow/support internal advancement plans based on org job forecast. Hire from within.
IDEA_TEXT: Review and streamline procedures. Remove steps that do not add value and speed up important processes.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Bureaucracy; Digitize processes; Process design and methodologies

COMMENT: Development of very thorough desk guides and document templates that are designed to ensure all the rules, regulations, laws, etc. are correctly vetted and organized in a final product to be leveraged multiple times. In addition, if I see redundancy in tasks, I look for ways to automate steps which improves on the time spent and ensures accuracy in completing tasks (with steps automatically triggered for you).
IDEA_TEXT: Create detailed desk guides and document templates to ensure rules and regulations are properly organized for repeated use. Automate repetitive tasks to save time and improve accuracy.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Process documentation

COMMENT:
While strongly adopting a vision is admirable, it has to be done in the context of what is feasible and logical. Lowering gas emissions and establishing programs to promote climate control is a great vision. But you can't simply say everybody needs to purchase a hybrid or electric car by year YYYY without considering the financial ability of each resident to do so. You can't forge ahead on the vision outcome without looking at what is needed to support the outcome (i.e. infrastructure).

And make it affordable for residents to "be" residents of California.
IDEA_TEXT: Consider what residents can afford and what support is needed when making climate policies. Build needed infrastructure before requiring people to buy electric or hybrid cars.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Environmental sustainability; Public participation; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: The need for remote work nationwide.  My restricting state employment to California and an in-office requirement, we are immediately limiting the ability to recruit the best and the brightest.  Although California is a great place to live, we exclude individuals who would be great assets to the state, both in mind and talent, if they don't want to relocate.  This creates an unnecessary barrier to recruiting the best, which only helps our state succeed.  My suggestion would be to identify classifications and areas which require unique and specalized skillsets and expanding the ability of those classifications to have more flexible remote work option.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow remote work for jobs that need special skills. Let state workers work from anywhere in the country to hire the best people.

COMMENT: Around 12 years ago I convinced my managers that we need to cross train all the pharmacy technicians to enter physician's orders, and not just the select few that were always being used.  We went from having four technicians that enter to now having over 14 that enter orders, and our productivity has improved greatly from this.   We now utilize each employee to a higher efficiency, and sick calls are more easily managed.
IDEA_TEXT: Cross-train all staff on key tasks instead of relying on a few people. This improves productivity and makes it easier to cover when people are sick.

COMMENT: One of the guidelines stated that we should not discuss telework or return to office policies; however, I noticed that others have posted about it already so I would like to add my request as well. As a state worker for more than 25 years I have enjoyed working in person alongside my peers and building relationships with people that have shared the workload struggles, so I am not arguing against the benefits of having in person collaboration. However, through the years my family situation. Currently, my greatest need would be to have an option for those of us that are caring of very ill and elderly family members or that have other family situations that make it difficult to commute into the office to be able to be exempted from the in-office requirement. We have programs like Paid Family Leave that allow for paid time off to take care of family members, but the needs of aging or ill family members do not always impede our ability to meet our workload commitments and do not meet the requirements for PFL or an RA request. Personally, leaving my mom in the care of a stranger so that I can travel 50 miles to go sit in front of a computer is a big mental, emotional, and financial burden that doesn't make sense when the workload doesn't demand it. Please consider it. I know there are many state workers that would greatly benefit from having an option.
IDEA_TEXT: Create telework exemptions for staff caring for ill or elderly family members when work duties can be done remotely.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Employee retention; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: I proposed an ideal to my franchise tax board department to address the issue of Spam. The situation is when a taxpayer calls in and asks for a call back 90% of the customers do not answer their phone. Because franchise tax board callback shows up as Spam on the everyday taxpayer's phone. They are not going to answer it. So when they call back again and hold for 2 to 3 hours, they are angry, upset. And you do not get things done. Except for addressing their anger. I spent four hours on this proposal because I thought it would help all of the state of california and it has gone nowhere.But the anger of the taxpayers wanting to know why leave a message when you do not get any calls back?I think this is nonproductive.And this is causing a serious backlog and more people holding.Because we cannot address this issue of spam.Basically.I believe you would have to go to the fcc and have them.Do something with our telephone to where it does not show as spam.When we place outbound calls, it seems simple.But no one is listening
IDEA_TEXT: Work with the FCC to prevent Franchise Tax Board callback numbers from showing as spam on taxpayer phones.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digital services; Public service delivery and responsiveness

COMMENT: Lack of direction, clarity of job duties. I asked for training in my new post, as the training I had for this new position was over 10 years ago. I was told " you will have a partner to help you" My partner has now been off 5 of the 10 weeks we have worked together. I am often left to figure it out on my own and cover her area as well. Then am reprimanded for not doing something that I didn't even know I had to do. It's so frustrating. I suggest allowing time for this training. Properly train all staff and if staff are asking for help then help them.
IDEA_TEXT: Provide proper training for all staff. Allow time for training and help staff when they ask for it.

COMMENT: For State contracting, it is challenging when the Department of General Services has not provided any templates for contracts and solicitations. There should be standardized templates that state employees in a buying role can use for their contracts and solicitations. Similar to the CMA Model Template that the CSU and UC universities use. It's a required template used for all contracts and grants with CSUs and UCs. DGS should develop standard templates for contracts and solicitations that can be used by all departments. This would streamline the contracting process tremendously. It creates uniformity, and will increase the review and approval process by departments internal processes, as well as DGS' Procurement Division and Office of Legal Services. It would also impact the prospective contractors that are interested in bidding on our solicitations. If I build an RFP today, it likely looks drastically different from another department's RFP. The content may capture the same info - however, where the information might be found in the solicitation, and the formatting of the solicitation, would be drastically different from others. RFPs can be hundreds of pages, and can be overwhelming for contractors. A standardized contracting template, and a standardized solicitation template for things like RFPs and IFBs - would save the State time and money across the board. DGS, as the business manager for the state, should implement these templates in partnership with other teams.
IDEA_TEXT: Create standard contract and solicitation templates for all state departments. The Department of General Services should develop required templates similar to the CMA Model Template used by CSU and UC universities. This would make the contracting process faster, create consistency across departments, and make it easier for contractors to respond to bids.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Cross-agency collaboration; Procurement

COMMENT:
The problem I continue to encounter is that when purchasing items for my factory, each time I request a quote, I’m required to obtain three documents: the recycled content certification, a signed RFQ, and the No Russia business form.

This process becomes unnecessarily repetitive because these vendors are already approved suppliers that we’ve worked with before. It doesn’t make sense to require the same paperwork for every purchase when the information and compliance status haven’t changed. The redundancy creates excess paperwork, slows down procurement, and adds unnecessary administrative burden for both us and the vendors.

Additionally, requiring a signed RFQ seems redundant when the vendor is already providing an official quote on their company letterhead, which inherently serves as their formal offer.

A more efficient approach would be to have vendors complete these forms once per year, or only when there’s a material change to their status, rather than for every individual quote.
IDEA_TEXT: Reduce paperwork for approved vendors. Have vendors complete recycled content certification and No Russia business forms once per year or only when their status changes, instead of for every purchase.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Digitize processes; Process design and methodologies

IDEA_TEXT: Improve staff training programs. Make sure all staff get proper training and know who to ask for help when needed.

COMMENT: Better training is needed on all levels. An online course with a quiz is not working for real life situations. Telework is great, but making sure employees are connected and feel safe to ask questions is important. New employees and counselors feel overwhelmed when trying to learn the job while also doing the job. Staff development should assess each person' learning style and have a safe and/or test environment before they are expected to do the actual job. And our structure has manager that have no idea what the role of support is. How do you manage and train staff if you do not understand their job? Our previous structure had management for clerical staff and management for counselors. It was better then than now.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve training programs. Replace online courses with hands-on training that matches each person's learning style and includes practice in a safe test environment before doing real work.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training

COMMENT:
Over the years I have identified two major inefficiencies in stat service: siloing and mismanagement. Siloing leads to duplication of effort across state government. When we conduct duplicative engagements with external audiences, it wastes their time and makes clear that we aren't coordinating internally the way we should. Collaboration and breaking down silos would lead us to be more efficient and make better use of expertise within and across agencies and departments. 

Mismanagement is another very common problem I observe in state service. There are very few effective managers who support and empower staff to do their work effectively. Others are either totally checked out and non-responsive, or are some of the worst micromanagers I have ever encountered. So many times I see the state put forth goals for improving state service that ignore these issues. Hiring the right people won't matter if they don't get the support they need to do the work. Managers and supervisors need training to help them develop self-awareness, recognize signs of mismanagement in themselves, and grow to become supports rather than barriers for their staff.
IDEA_TEXT: Break down silos between agencies. Improve coordination to avoid duplicate work with outside groups and better use expertise across departments.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Communication and knowledge sharing; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Shared data; Shared resources

COMMENT: As the State Auditor's Office showed recently, telework is effective. It is a great tool available to the state to foster collaboration and retain highly skilled staff. In my role, I engage with community based organizations, researchers, and local government all across the state. I would not be able to do that without teleconferencing tools and support for telework—even if we had the funds to support travel, I can't be in multiple places at once, and it is more effective to have everyone in conversation together. The State government of California does not only serve Sacramento, yet without telework I am less effectively able to serve communities outside of the Sacramento area. Telework saves money, reduces pollution and traffic, and makes state workers more efficient. I am able to do more work in the same amount of time if I don't have to run around a poorly planned building to find a bathroom or navigate a lengthy commute. When working at headquarters, my colleagues frequently have to interrupt meetings in order to move their cars because we don't have the infrastructure to actually support employees working in the office, even on a 2 day/week in office schedule. Additionally, I am less likely while working remotely to experience microaggressions and other behavior from coworkers that could lead to a lawsuit against the state.
IDEA_TEXT: Expand telework options for state employees. Support remote work with teleconferencing tools to improve efficiency, reduce costs, and better serve communities across California.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Public-facing services and resident experience; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public service delivery and responsiveness; Remote work and return to office; Software and tools

COMMENT: Telework works! It saves money, reduces pollution and traffic, prevents unnecessary wear on our roads and driving infrastructure, and it is an attractive perk of working for the state when wages are otherwise not particularly attractive. I have not met a single person who supports the return to office push in the state. We all understand that telework has empowered us to be more efficient, productive, and effective. We have lost extremely skilled staff who can't be replaced from the policy shift away from telework. It is also extremely silly to think that we would spend scarce resources on additional space to accommodate everyone on our staff in the office in the current funding landscape. Please support telework.
IDEA_TEXT: Support and expand telework options for state employees.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: Really listen to the feedback you are receiving from this engagement. On the topic of telework especially, state worker feedback has been abjectly ignored and shut down. It makes me skeptical that this request for feedback is coming from a place of actually wanting to hear out our concerns and learn from our experience, and more from a public relations perspective. It looks good to those on the outside that you ask us for input, but if it isn't put to practice, it will only further contribute to our disillusionment with state service. It is very sad because I chose this line of work because I believe in state service, and I want to help, but the system is breaking me.
IDEA_TEXT: Actually use staff feedback to make changes. Listen to employee concerns about telework and other issues, then put their input into practice instead of ignoring it.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Organizational structure and culture; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal feedback; Management culture and leadership approach; Trust and openness

IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Public policy initiatives

COMMENT: Inequitable hiring processes, such as differences in examinations and exam administration. Exam questions that no longer work as intended; they do not evaluate experience and knowledge, but only self-assess experience. I recommend removing examinations from the civil service process. They are no longer necessary. Focus instead on better screening (and I don't mean MQ determinations). Another problem is outdated classifications. Solution is to continue to consolidate classifications. There's also lot of management, some new, that are not trained how to manage within state regulations/practices. There's a lack of understanding/balance of being engaging, caring and yet firm, setting expectations and keeping employees accountable. (Or for some, to not be so caring, that they fail to hold employees accountable.) It's not modeled by managers that have been in state service for years. Sometimes, we push things like performance evaluations for compliance, but the quality and engagement are severely lacking. One solution is to update the 80-hour training to be "real talk" and not philosophical / general principles. New managers need more performance management and employee engagement training. It shouldn't be self-paced where we know some just click through e-learning without really learning.
IDEA_TEXT: Continue to consolidate job classifications to reduce outdated categories.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Job classification

COMMENT:
A primary way to become more effective and efficient is to meaningfully invest in recruitment and retention. Like many here, I've worked at different departments over several years and it’s become incredibly challenging to recruit and retain talented professionals. Telework and statewide hiring in the early 2020s helped us recruit skilled professionals from across the state. But now, with pay falling behind the private sector, few opportunities for advancement, and new in-office requirements, we lost dedicated employees who have years of experience and institutional knowledge, and try to replace them (if we can), which is expensive and slow. 

One of the biggest reasons people choose state service is PSLF. Recent proposed federal changes that raise monthly repayments upwards of 500% and will likely disqualify employees from certain states have taken that recruitment/retention tool away. 

I believe CA should create its own Loan Repayment Assistance Program (LRAP) for state employees. We already have successful models with the CA Legislature authorizing an LRAP through CalATJ for legal aid attorneys, and CalHealthCares using Prop. 56 funds to offer up to $300,000 in loan repayment for physicians who commit to five years of service. Building on those for a broader program that is for all state employees would be a powerful tool to recruit and retain talented professionals, keep expertise in-house, and strengthen the quality and stability of public service across the state.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a statewide loan repayment assistance program for all state employees. Model it after existing programs like CalATJ for legal aid attorneys and CalHealthCares for physicians to help recruit and keep talented staff.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Employee retention; Public policy initiatives

COMMENT:
One of the best ways to strengthen our communities is by investing in the people who serve them. Recruitment and retention have become real challenges across departments. Telework and statewide hiring in the early 2020s helped bring in skilled professionals from every region, but with pay falling behind the private sector, limited advancement opportunities, and new in-office requirements, we’ve lost experienced employees who carried years of institutional knowledge. Replacing them, if we can, is costly and slow, and the impact is felt directly by Californians through slower services and reduced program quality.

A sustainable solution would be a statewide Loan Repayment Assistance Program (LRAP) for state employees. CA already uses LRAP models successfully for legal aid attorneys and physicians serving Medi-Cal patients. Expanding them to all state employees would help recruit and retain talented professionals, keep expertise in-house, and strengthen the delivery of public services, like environmental protection, health care, and consumer rights, that Californians rely on every day.

Student loan debt affects more than 3.8 million Californians. I’ve seen colleagues delay buying homes, starting families, and saving for the future because their loan payments consume such a large portion of their income. Creating a statewide LRAP would not only help employees build stability and remain in public service, but it would strengthen the economic and social fabric of our communities.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a statewide Loan Repayment Assistance Program (LRAP) for all state employees to help recruit and keep talented workers by reducing their student loan debt.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Employee retention; Hiring and recruitment

COMMENT: As a field inspector, I ask the State to consider satellite office provision and telework arrangement. Commute consumes energy and time in Southern California. One hour forty five minutes one way is not sustainable. In order not to lose experienced field inspector, please keep some flexibility on reporting to office in the coming 4-day a week schedule. Management can design a mechanism to evaluate employees' work and performance by quality and quantity of the field work, case load, etc.
IDEA_TEXT: Create satellite offices and allow telework for field inspectors. Keep flexible office reporting in the new 4-day work week. Measure performance by work quality and quantity instead of office time.

COMMENT: We need to continue to address racial and gender disparities in the workplace by recognizing the harms of diversity indifference in the forms of colorblindness, gender-blindness, and diversity blindness. Required reading can be assigned to state workers with a short paragraph essay on each chapter written by the state worker to acknowledge what they have learned. Acknowledging the history of vulnerable demographics such as Women, Black, Asian, Hispanic, LGBTQIA, and the disabled can help mitigate colorblindness, gender-blindness, and any feelings of resistance against understanding race and gender vulnerabilities. Required reading and writing can also maintain reading comprehension and critical thinking qualities which are rapidly declining even at managerial levels. Required reading and writing about diversity can also help to heal the injustice of native American genocide, racial indifference (and racism) against people of color, and gender discrimination that were unfortunately inherited from the beginnings of the USA nation and ensuing statehoods, California included. The book New Jim Crow by Michelle Alexander is a good start. Bury My Heart at Wounded Knee is another recommendation. By remembering the history of how the USA and CA statehood came to be, we can repair and heal these centuries-old traumas and the recurring injustices that continue due to ignorance of and indifference towards marginalized communities and state workers.
IDEA_TEXT: Create required diversity training with reading assignments and written essays. Assign books like The New Jim Crow and Bury My Heart at Wounded Knee to help staff understand the history and impact of discrimination against women, people of color, LGBTQIA individuals, and disabled people.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Organizational structure and culture; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Inclusion and diversity

COMMENT: The practice of posting a job position at the DLSE, which is overseen by DIR HR, is inefficient, and damaging to the agency. Often, a position is posted, when the current employee wants to transfer to another office. When this happens, members of the public apply, and none of them are selected because the reason for posting was just a transfer. This is a huge waste of government resources. The employee should be allowed to transfer without applying for a new job. Also, the public feels angry when they are not selected despite their qualifications, and they decide not to apply again because they know that internal candidates will be selected. The same thing happens when promotion in place is difficult to gain as has happened at DLSE. HR spends dozens of hours reviewing resumes, interviewing candidates, only to turn everyone external down. Qualified candidates refuse to continue engaging in the hiring process because they get turned down so frequently. I have seen this happen, and because I am an internal candidate, I know that the internal employee gets hired. We would save hundreds of hours of work if we eliminated these practices. We would also increase the pool of qualified applicants if the hiring process was genuine.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow employees to transfer between offices without posting job openings and going through the full hiring process.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Employee retention; Internal feedback

COMMENT: In my daily work, I strive to ensure excellence in communicating effectively. This means utilizing reading comprehension and critical thinking in thoroughly understanding questions being asked. I also strive to maintain the quality of my work by calling out inaccuracies and/or deceptive/false/misleading statements, because if they are not called out, they will cause corruption in my own work integrity and morale at the workplace. As a result of the 'See something, say something' DHS directive, this protection emboldens people to speak up and say something when they encounter any kind of corrupt or false statement or action, whether small or large, whether discovered immediately or in the future. The importance of protecting whistleblowers will help to improve and maintain work culture, safety, and integrity. When state workers feel safe, protected, and heard, those they govern in the State of CA will also feel the same. This work of maintaining civility, integrity, and morale, starting at the State government level, will positively ripple outwards in maintaining a culture of civility, integrity, and morale for the residents of California as well. As the State of CA is a leader amongst USA states, this example, if executed successfully will help the other states who are influenced by the State of CA. This ripple effect could influence a positive outcome for the USA, as it currently suffers from a rapid decline in civility, integrity, and morale.
IDEA_TEXT: Protect whistleblowers and encourage staff to report problems. Create a safe environment where employees feel heard and protected when they speak up about issues or false statements.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Governance and oversight; Communication and knowledge sharing; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Internal feedback; Trust and openness

COMMENT: I would address telework as the priority issue for CA state employees. The CA State Auditor stated in August 2025 that a one-size-fits-all telework policy does not work for employees or the state as whole. As a state employee whose work is entirely conducted online, I'd love to see CalHR and department management prioritizing a telework schedule. It allows me to get my work done in a timelier manner and prevents carbon emissions from commuting and powering office spaces. I believe it would also allow the state to be a more competitive employer and retain employees long-term.
IDEA_TEXT: Create flexible telework policies that fit different job types instead of using one policy for all state workers.

COMMENT:
Besides the challenges related to RTO, one of the biggest issues I’ve experienced as a state employee is that some salaried managers expect hourly staff to work on-call, weekends, or beyond regular hours without additional compensation. When I’ve tried to set boundaries or decline unpaid work, it has at times resulted in retaliation or a sense of “quid pro quo,” where cooperation is exchanged for fair treatment.

A potential solution could be to ensure that duty statements clearly define expectations around on-call work and overtime. Additionally, management should receive better training on employee classifications, what constitutes on-call/duty officer status, and how compensation for these duties should be handled.
IDEA_TEXT: Clearly define on-call work and overtime expectations in job descriptions. Train managers on employee classifications and proper compensation for extra duties.

COMMENT:
Allow state employees to work out of a state office of their choice. 

The return-to-office mandate has created challenges for many employees—particularly those hired into remote roles. While some employees value in-office collaboration (myself included), requiring relocation to a specific office location is impractical and disruptive for many. Many people were hired for remote positions and are now facing the option to move to the specific location of their state office or find a new job. However, in many cases, employees live near a state office, just not the one affiliated with their agency. Forcing them to move or resign creates unnecessary turnover and hardship. Further, many offices that are designated for a specific agency/department are sitting mostly vacant because they have hired staff all over the state for remote work that do not live near their specific office. Therefore, state employees should be allowed to choose an office to work from, rather than office space being designated only for one agency/department. Providing more flexibility in where state employees can work solves problems for the employees who would like to work near where they live and solves problems for the state by filling currently vacant office space. 

Side benefits would occur from this change by introducing state employees that would otherwise not meet, breaking down silos, and creating community based on location.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow state employees to work from any state office instead of requiring them to work at their assigned agency office. This would fill empty office spaces and let employees work closer to home without having to move or quit.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Office management; Physical infrastructure; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT:
Fragmentation across state/local systems that serve the same people. Programs in health/housing/social services work separately with different rules but serve the same people. They have fragmented data and separate funding/rules. This creates confusion for the consumers, duplication of efforts, and gaps in care especially for the people that most need it. 

1. State depts should work together for shared goals and coordinate service instead of running parallel programs that don't connect. And leadership should facilitate that collaboration. 
2. Agencies should secure shared data to see full picture of a person's needs and measure outcomes of wellbeing. 
3. Give counties/regions flexibility to test local solutions using funding and simplified access to services. 
4. Train/rotate staff across departments/agencies so employees can understand how other systems work and work together better to solve problems.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow agencies to share data safely so they can see the full picture of a person's needs and measure how well services work.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Technology and digital innovation; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Public service delivery and responsiveness; Shared data

COMMENT: I've worked in the private sector and I'm shocked by how antiquated and labor intensive our travel approvals, travel booking, travel expense reporting processes are.  It takes hours to wade through all the layers and forms and rules.  And extensive pre-approvals have us losing out on lower cost flights, early bird discounts, and hotel blocks at higher cost to the State of CA.  We used concur in the private sector and things could be done in close to real time.  If the time my small department spends on limited travel is magnified across all departments and agencies it's a huge loss of time and productivity.  The CalAters site is so slow and even says on the front screen plan for several minutes to even open the page.  I believe CA, with our technology and innovation can do better in a digital world.
IDEA_TEXT: Replace outdated travel systems with modern technology like Concur. Allow real-time booking and expense reporting to save time and get better prices on flights and hotels.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Workplace operations; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Employee travel; Software and tools

COMMENT:
One major issue is the lack of managers who actively manage their staff. This isn’t about micromanagement, it's about setting clear expectations, assigning tasks with due dates, tracking progress and holding staff accountable. Instead, agencies often hire expensive project managers, funded by taxpayer dollars, to direct the work. So what, then, is the role of the actual manager? Too often, it’s limited to signing off on timesheets, completing IDPs, attending conferences, and to often encourage staff to “get the work done when they can”, so 2-weeks later or never. 

In my experience, when managers don’t establish timelines or hold staff accountable, tasks take significantly longer to complete. Project managers, on the other hand, drive progress and lead teams to results more efficiently than many state managers. If managers owned their empowerment and held accountable for directing work, we could reduce reliance on costly PM contracts and save public funds.
IDEA_TEXT: Train managers to actively manage staff by setting clear expectations, assigning tasks with due dates, tracking progress, and holding staff accountable to reduce reliance on expensive project manager contracts.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Budget and resources; Governance and oversight
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Contracts and vendors; Manager training

COMMENT:
The workplace culture needs improvement. Staff often compete with one another instead of collaborating. The mindset of “that’s not my job,” “someone else will do it,” or “I’m not volunteering” contributes to the negative stereotype of state workers being lazy. Unfortunately, that perception is often reinforced by reality. We need more people willing to step up, work together, and focus on shared goals.

I appreciate the effort to provide jobs and recognize the importance of accountability and stewardship to taxpayers. But we must also streamline processes, empower managers, and foster a culture of teamwork if we want to improve outcomes and public trust.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve workplace culture by streamlining processes, empowering managers, and fostering teamwork to encourage collaboration over competition.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Management culture and leadership; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership approach; Process design and methodologies; Work culture

COMMENT: I would like to suggest opening up the possibility for state employees of one department being allowed to use another departments empty/vacated locations for alternative onsite days.  As an example, a telecommute employee working under a hybrid plan and living a distance away from their respective home office, could use a hotel cubicle in another state office closer to their home for an onsite day.  Just a thought for using those unoccupied office locations.  Consideration would have to be made for Internet access, but most all state offices have that capacity already.

COMMENT: DoR has solicited Master's level professionals with a statement on the JD that says service with DoR may possibly be able to get clinical hours yet they have been evasive about this opportunity.  QRP's should have a ladder.  QRP I: Master's degree non-associate.  QRP II - Associate (ASW, AMFT, APCC). QRP III - IV Licensed professional (under 2 years (III), or more than 2 years (V). There is no reason to group differently qualified QRPs together and we will lose talent without the opportunity for clinical advancement.  Additionally, we need licensed people at each site.  The acuity is too high for just MSWs or CRCs.  We need clinical expertise and support of that expertise in VR settings. Unfortunately, our executive leadership does not have the clinical lens or skill set to validate the importance of this need.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a career ladder for Qualified Rehabilitation Professionals (QRPs) with four levels based on education and licensing. QRP I for Master's degree holders, QRP II for associates, QRP III for licensed professionals under 2 years, and QRP IV for licensed professionals over 2 years.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Career growth; Job classification; Qualified staff

COMMENT:
The Department of Industrial Relations has a Unit called CFOI (Census of Fatal Occupational Injuries) for California. It is a deep dive into occupational deaths going back many years.  

Our title says Engineer, but we don't do any of that with this data.

An Engineer would evaluate the data of many years of deaths and attempt to do the greatest good for the greatest number of people who are dying.

We don't do much of that.  Take a look at our annual plans - they are mostly activity based (we will do x inspections, turn them in in y time, etc.), and other political activity such as that. 

Engineering is not that.
IDEA_TEXT: Change the Census of Fatal Occupational Injuries (CFOI) unit to focus on data analysis and prevention. Have engineers study death data to find ways to save the most lives instead of just doing inspections and paperwork.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Governance and oversight; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Align policy and implementation; Employee performance reviews; Process design and methodologies; Qualified staff

COMMENT: One of the most difficult things about working for the State as a healthcare employee is having decisions made at headquarters in Sacramento that directly affect the lives of the people that I serve, often to their detriment, made by individuals who do not ever meet our clients or understand what they need. Decisions made by a top-down hierarchy often are made with administrators or managers in mind, and I think it should be the other way around. The primary role of a state worker is to serve the public, but often I feel that I am following regulations that are contradictory, don't make sense, or are needlessly burdensome at the expense of my clients for the sake of placating headquarters. I would want fewer top-down decisions; for leaders at headquarters to spend more time at the worksites they direct and oversee, to interact with the people who live there, and to pay closer attention to their needs and input; and more open communication from our leaders about what they are doing to address the concerns that we and our clients bring to them. Another piece that I think deserves a review is the State's policy of going with the lowest bidder for purchasing. Oftentimes, we get what we pay for, and products and services from the lowest bidders are also the lowest quality. There at least should be another metric we go by in addition to price, such as taking into consideration product reviews or how responsive a vendor is to customer needs.
IDEA_TEXT: Change purchasing policy to consider quality, not just lowest price. Add metrics like product reviews and vendor responsiveness when choosing contractors.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Procurement

COMMENT: State workers need to acknowledge the foundation we stand on. Some agencies like the Department of Rehabilitation have had leaders which fundamentally shaped our state for the better. COMMENT:
California Governor’s Office of Emergency Services
Executive Memorandum
To: Fire Chief Brian Marshal-CALOES-Fire Rescue
From: Dave Norman, Assistant Chief-CALOES-Special Operations-US&R
Date: October 12, 2025
Subject: Request for CAL-Card Issuance to Special Operations Assistant Chiefs
Purpose:
This memorandum requests the issuance of CAL-Cards to CAL-OES Special Operations Assistant Chiefs to support operational effectiveness during emergency deployments and pre-positioning of specialized response teams.
Background:
Special Operations Assistant Chiefs serve as Agency Representatives (A-REPs) during high-impact emergency operations and pre-positioning missions involving:
•	Hazardous Materials (HAZMAT) Teams
•	Swiftwater Rescue Teams
•	Regional Urban Search & Rescue (US&R) Task Forces
•	FEMA US&R State Teams deployed in-state or out-of-state via EMAC
These deployments require rapid, on-the-ground decision-making and logistical coordination. Currently, the absence of direct purchasing authority via CAL-Cards limits the ability of Assistant Chiefs to procure essential resources, services, and supplies in real time—impacting mission agility and service delivery. The CAL-Card can be used for unexpected costs that arise, and that the assigned IMT might not be familiar with, such as the N95 mask needs for US&R Personnel during the Wide Area Search Operations at the January Brush Fires.
Currently several agencies provide CAL-Cards to their supervisors, in some cases to Company Office
IDEA_TEXT: Issue CAL-Cards to Special Operations Assistant Chiefs to allow direct purchasing of essential resources during emergency deployments and pre-positioning missions.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Procurement

COMMENT:
Expedited Purchases: CAL-Cards enable the quickest possible acquisition of goods and services. During a disaster, such as the Mammoth Snow Incident, The January Fires (2025), and the Texas Flooding (EMAC-HR K9 MRP), Traditional purchasing methods like purchase orders or contracts are to slow and can endanger public health, safety, and property.

Acquisition outside of normal business hours: Disaster often occurs at unexpected times, such as nights and weekends. CAL-Cards provide a payment mechanism that functions at all hours when supplies are available.
Elimination of red tape: The Emergency Response CAL-Card ERC (Emergency Response CAL-Card) program is specifically designed to cut through bureaucracy for disaster response. It is intended for use after the Governor’s Office has issued a disaster proclamation and when other payment mechanisms are unavailable.
Flexibility with vendors: Unlike standard purchasing, the ERC (Emergency Response CAL-Card) has a broad allowance for merchant categories, giving card holders the flexibility to purchase from vendors that are accessible and have the needed supplies and services during emergencies.
Recommendation:
Authorize and issue CAL-Cards to all Special Operations Assistant Chiefs to:
•	Facilitate immediate procurement of mission-critical items during deployments and pre-positions
•	Support fiscal accountability and streamline reimbursement processes
•	Enhance operational autonomy for A-REPs managing complex field operations
IDEA_TEXT: Give CAL-Cards to all Special Operations Assistant Chiefs. This will help them buy needed items quickly during emergencies, track spending better, and work more independently in the field.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee travel; Expense reimbursement; Procurement

COMMENT: Communication from management and upper management to rank and file employees.  Example:  There are two managers in one department; one manager will share information with rank and file employees, and the other manager will not share information.  Half of the rank and file employees report to one manager and other half report to the other manager.  When discussions amongst rank and file employees occur, there is confusion and/or concern for everyone not having the same knowledge.   Idea to solve problem of communication from management:  Departmental staff meetings once a week/month with all employees followed by an email to all employees with information, discussion, and outcome from the meeting.  Idea to solve problem of communication from upper management:  Upper management meetings with departmental managers at least once a month.  Departmental managers should have knowledge of pertinent information at the State agency (especially 24-hour facilities).  All-Hands staff meetings should be encouraged at every State agency.  Emails are only one way to communicate, and the most effective way is face-to-face.
IDEA_TEXT: Encourage all-hands staff meetings at every state agency and prioritize face-to-face communication over email-only approaches.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Organizational structure and culture; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal communication; Management culture and leadership approach; Work culture

COMMENT: Communication from management and upper management to rank and file employees.  Example:  There are two managers in one department; one manager will share information with rank and file employees, and the other manager will not share information.  Half of the rank and file employees report to one manager and other half report to the other manager.  When discussions amongst rank and file employees occur, there is confusion and/or concern for everyone not having the same knowledge.   Idea to solve problem of communication from management:  Departmental staff meetings once a week/month with all employees followed by an email to all employees with information, discussion, and outcome from the meeting.  Idea to solve problem of communication from upper management:  Upper management meetings with departmental managers at least once a month.  Departmental managers should have knowledge of pertinent information at the State agency (especially 24-hour facilities).  All-Hands staff meetings should be encouraged at every State agency.  Emails are only one way to communicate, and the most effective way is face-to-face.
IDEA_TEXT: Hold regular staff meetings with all employees followed by email summaries to improve communication between managers and staff.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal communication; Management culture and leadership approach

COMMENT: I would also like to see updated administrative practices. In my department, we are using an Excel Sheet that we export to a PDF and give to our manager. Also, although there is too much bureaucracy in some situations, in other situations, I find I don't have the answers and there are inconsistent rules...
IDEA_TEXT: Update administrative practices. Replace Excel sheets and PDFs with better systems. Create consistent rules and clear answers for staff.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Process documentation

COMMENT: As a funding opportunity, I believe state agencies should be eligible to qualify for grant funding secured by other agencies. Many state departments serve sensitive populations and often rely heavily on the state general fund to support expansive services. While some grant funding or federal revenues are specifically designated for these sensitive populations, strict requirements sometimes prevent state agencies from qualifying directly. To address this, it should be encouraged that state agencies partner through interagency agreements to collaboratively fund relevant work where applicable.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow state agencies to qualify for grant funding secured by other agencies through partnerships and interagency agreements.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Contracts and vendors; Cross-agency collaboration

COMMENT: Post-COVID, it’s essential to ensure that processes and practices align with today’s industry best practices. This involves reviewing existing policies and procedures, comparing them against governing authority, and updating guidance to remain relevant. Additionally, recognizing the significant evolution of the workforce is crucial—making strategic hires that will propel the organization forward in alignment with its strategic plans and roadmap. I believe this approach cuts costs, improves efficiencies, and boosts morale.
IDEA_TEXT: Make strategic hires that align with the organization's plans and goals to move the workforce forward.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Hiring and recruitment; Qualified staff

COMMENT: I’m truly excited and pleased to see the ongoing classification consolidation efforts, which effectively leverage the skillsets and experience needed to perform essential work. However, I believe there is still room for improvement. It appears that rank-and-file employees are becoming more protected, while exempt and at-will positions are becoming more volatile and undermined. We should create competitive job offerings that modernize outdated processes and mindsets, while enhancing our government’s reputation—but in a way that restores productivity, trust, and integrity.
IDEA_TEXT: Create competitive job offerings that modernize outdated processes and mindsets while restoring productivity, trust, and integrity in government.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Organizational structure and culture; Management culture and leadership; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Management culture and leadership approach; Trust and openness; Work culture

COMMENT:
The cost of doing state business is too high. We pay contractors ridiculous amounts to do work state employees could do and  the amount of money we pay for leases and office space that we proved was not necessary for 4 years from 2020-2024 is outrageous. Every department could hire more staff to do work for the people if they weren’t paying so much for unnecessary business leases.
Additionally, forcing employees to return to office has reduced morale because employees have less money and time to spend with their families, leading to less positive customer service experiences for the public. 

If the people delivering the service are miserable, they aren’t going out of their way to make that experience positive for the public. 

Lastly, exempting employees more than 50 miles away but limiting their ability to promote is a guaranteed way to lose staff that hold critical institutional knowledge. When I leave my department as a result of this limitation, I will be taking with me the knowledge that only I possess. I support systems that save my department millions of dollars each year and there is one else in my department can support these critical systems.  It would take at least a year and a LOT of money to hire and train someone such that they can fill the gap I’ll be leaving behind. 

I don’t want to leave state service but the governor’s return to office mandate leaves me no other choice because uprooting my family is not an option.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow remote work to improve staff morale and customer service.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Public-facing services and resident experience; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee retention; Public service delivery and responsiveness; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT:
Let employees continue to telework like they did for four years when the state needed it. 

This saves money, improves morale, and gives state employees work/life balance that leads to better services for all of California.

COMMENT: Sometimes, state agencies holds money that belongs to the public, in the form of refunds, or lost wages, as examples. The state agencies sometimes cannot locate those people who are owed money. We should create databases that the public can use to look up if they are owed money. This would increase the percentage of people who get their money back.

COMMENT:
In CALFIRE with have 3 command and control platforms North Ops, South Ops and Sac ECC. 

With technology we should at look going to a super gacc where all command and control comes from one spot the effectiveness would be one stop shop for ordering equipment etc during an emergency
IDEA_TEXT: Create a single command center for CALFIRE. Combine North Ops, South Ops, and Sac ECC into one super GACC for all command and control and equipment ordering during emergencies.

COMMENT: I created step-by-step procedures, including snip pics and highlights, for a few of my duties.  I trained a student assistant and a modified light duty worker to assist me when the work volume was high.  They used the procedures as a guide and found them to be very helpful.  Management commended my efficiency as this improved the process.
IDEA_TEXT: Create step-by-step procedures with pictures and highlights for job duties. Train assistants to use these guides when work volume is high.

IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Process documentation

COMMENT: Grants - If I was running a business that $100,000,000,000.00 in debt, the last action I would take is to just give out free monies. Our Grant programs are horribly wasteful and inefficient where 50% of the funding often goes towards administration.
IDEA_TEXT: Stop giving out grants or make grant programs more efficient by reducing the 50% of funding that goes to administration.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Process delays; Procurement

COMMENT: I would like to address the issue of homelessness as regards housing resources. Right now, we have various service providers doing various things, and each organization has their own way of going about things. We need a standardized system of working that makes sense across the board and is uniform. The Access Centers seem to me on the whole to not be as efficient and helpful to our clients as I would hope. As a result, I have had to approach 1 or 2 organizations that I know to be reliable. This should not be the case. Each organization should be able to provide us with the resources needed to assist our clients in a way that is clear, functional, and pragmatic. We need to fix this system. It is broken, and people in need are not being helped in the ways they need. We need transparency about housing availability at each organization and at the various housing sites that they have. So much money has poured down into a problem that is not getting fixed. It is excruciating to be working with those who desperately need help but can't get it.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a standardized system for homeless service providers. Make all organizations use the same clear processes and share housing availability information openly.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Technology and digital innovation; Communication and knowledge sharing; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Process design and methodologies; Public policy initiatives; Shared data

COMMENT: I have created employee instructions manuals for roles I have been in, to pass down to those who would come into the role. I have tried to create legacy pieces such as these to ensure, where there is a lack of training or the training is not pragmatic and effective, that instructions are clear, readable, and useful across the board.
IDEA_TEXT: Create employee instruction manuals for all roles. Pass down clear, readable guides to help new staff when training is lacking or not practical.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Knowledge management; Process documentation

COMMENT: As an overqualified woman who has gotten passed over for lesser competent, male colleagues, especially those with connections from within, I find it difficult to be able to respect management decisions fully. I also have seen supervisors favor male colleagues more and treat them with favoritism and coddling. They get special treatment; they are given respect; their ideas and thoughts are valued over women's perspectives. It's a trend in the office such that women are not even surprised anymore. This is a distasteful yet realistic aspect of working for state today. Perhaps it is emblematic of a larger trend of continued lack of respect for the perspectives of women and of their works, which is seen as of lesser value. We need fairness, impartiality, and respect.
IDEA_TEXT: Create fair and equal treatment for all staff. Stop favoritism based on gender or connections and respect all employees' ideas equally.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Organizational structure and culture; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Inclusion and diversity; Management culture and leadership approach; Trust and openness

COMMENT:
We need documented current processes and *updates* for our unit work so that people can aim for the same goal.  This seems like it should be obvious and not difficult.

My unit has changes to procedure that occasionally come from within the unit, but they also come from sources outside the unit (other units, law change, higher ups) - these changes have never, in the 7 years I have been here, every change has been word of mouth, sometimes in a meeting but without a written record.  Nothing is used to adjust written procedures used to train new hires.  This creates stratified production.  Managers each train to a different standard from when they last learned the work.
IDEA_TEXT: Create written procedures and update them regularly. Document all process changes and use them to train new staff consistently.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Employee training; Process documentation

COMMENT: The State of California should only sign contracts with businesses who are physically located and/or physically operate in California.  Example: Most of my (and other staff) job duties have been transferred to a third-party administrator who is physically located in the states of Minnesota and Colorado.  CAL HR and Department of Legal should work together to keep all duties of civil service classification positions within the State of California.
IDEA_TEXT: Only sign contracts with businesses located and operating in California. Work with CAL HR and Department of Legal to keep civil service jobs within the state instead of transferring them to out-of-state companies.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Cross-agency collaboration; Internal feedback

COMMENT:
From a hiring and recruitment standpoint, I believe the state should explore implementing relocation stipends and location-based incentives in areas where compensation does not align with local housing costs.

For example, the Communications Operator classification in Siskiyou County currently offers the same pay as in San Diego County, despite the significant difference in cost of living — with housing costs in San Diego nearly triple those in Siskiyou. This imbalance contributes to low tenure and retention within the classification, especially in higher-cost regions.

If the state were to establish automatic stipends or geographic pay adjustments, similar to those used by the federal government and based on GSA locality rates, it could make these positions more competitive and appealing to prospective candidates. This approach would support workforce stability, improve retention, and help ensure statewide operational readiness across all regions.
IDEA_TEXT: Create location-based pay adjustments and relocation stipends for state jobs. Use federal GSA locality rates to set automatic pay differences based on local housing costs.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits

COMMENT: There is too much bureaucracy when doing basic things, like buying a printer that works with our computers. It takes too long to approve contracts with vendors and service providers.  How can we lessen or automate some of the hurdles put in place?  We need to audit the process to determine whether all steps are really necessary, and to the extent they are not or can be streamlined, work to do that. We need to invest in software and other technological solutions to improve our systems.
IDEA_TEXT: Invest in technology solutions. Buy software and other tech tools to improve government systems.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Software and tools; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT: inefficiencies due to outdated technology and resources. In my department so many processes are still paper and hardcopies that could be streamlined to computer-based technology.
IDEA_TEXT: Switch from paper processes to computer-based technology to make work more efficient.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT:
As a Californian and a state employee, one primary problem I would address is the gap in consistent internal training and the redundancy that often slows down our processes. Over time, new employees receive strong onboarding, but tenured staff may not always get the same opportunities for refreshers or updates on evolving policies, technology, and our mission. This can create uneven knowledge levels and inefficiencies in how we carry out our responsibilities.
To address this, I would advocate for a structured internal training program that includes regular refresher sessions focused on our core duties, our mission, and the ‘why’ behind our work. This would help reinforce a shared sense of purpose and accountability.
Another problem I would address is the overlap and redundancy that can occur between agencies or divisions within our department. While each unit has an important role, sometimes our responsibilities intersect in ways that create duplicated efforts, confusion over jurisdiction, or inefficiencies in communication and data sharing.
Additionally, I would look at streamlining redundant paperwork and leveraging technology to make our processes more efficient — for example, consolidating overlapping forms into a single digital system or using data-sharing tools between departments. This would reduce administrative burden and allow us to focus more time on the impactful parts of our job, like serving the public and ensuring workplace safety.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a structured training program with regular refresher sessions on core duties, mission, and policies for all staff including tenured employees.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Governance and oversight; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Align policy and implementation; Employee training

COMMENT: Over my state career I have been employed by more than one agency, and across the board this problem exists.  The lack of procedures, resources and training references on the varied applications utilized by differing state agencies to achieve assigned task.  Specifically in the Human Resources capacity, there should be agency specific recognized and adhered to manuals and procedures for reoccurring task.  The processes contain too much grey area, and it cause errors and penalties.
IDEA_TEXT: Create standard manuals and procedures for Human Resources tasks. Make agency-specific guides that all staff must follow to reduce errors and penalties.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Process documentation; Shared resources

COMMENT: I would address the wasteful spending during procurement of goods services and contracts. It's so difficult to procure goods and services that the department doesn't factor in the cost of staff time to adhere to antiquated policies to make purchases utilizing SB/DVBE and low bid companies and contractors. Many times the low bid is the low bid for a reason and we get a subpar service and often times has to be redone or fixed in a short period. If a more qualified, higher cost contractor was awarded we would save money in the long run. This makes it difficult to be good stewards of the state dollar.
IDEA_TEXT: Reform procurement policies to allow awarding contracts to higher-quality vendors instead of only the lowest bidder. Consider total cost including staff time and long-term value when selecting contractors and suppliers.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Contracts and vendors; Procurement

COMMENT:
As a Californian and state employee, I’ve focused on improving efficiency and expanding our department’s reach through inclusive and community-based engagement. One example is how the supportive leadership of our Region Chief has allowed our team to strengthen recruitment efforts in historically marginalized communities. By having the resources and approval to participate in outreach events across the state—not just within our immediate jurisdiction—we’ve been able to share our message more broadly and connect with a wider, more diverse pool of prospective employees.

This approach not only promotes equity and representation in state service, but also makes our recruitment process more effective by reaching individuals who may not have otherwise considered a career with our department. Through these efforts, we’ve built stronger community relationships, enhanced public awareness of our mission, and created new pathways for Californians to serve their state.
IDEA_TEXT: Expand recruitment to underserved communities. Give teams resources and approval to attend outreach events statewide, not just in their local area, to reach more diverse job candidates.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Organizational structure and culture; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Hiring and recruitment; Inclusion and diversity

COMMENT: Our pay which has a direct effect on our retention . We spend hundreds of thousands of dollars training employees, they work for us for years promote through the ranks with valuable experience and we lose them to local government fire departments. They get paid more money as a probation firefighter then as a seasoned fire captain with us. We can’t keep good employees.
IDEA_TEXT: Increase pay for state fire employees to match local government fire department wages to improve retention.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Employee retention

COMMENT: The primary problem I would address is ensuring that personnel have the proper tools, resources, and support to effectively perform their roles. As a station captain, I’ve completed all the necessary classes for purchasing, but I currently do not have a purchase card, which means I must rely on other captains for essential station needs like paint, upkeep, and crew subsidies, slowing down operations. Additionally, technology issues with systems like the MDT on fire engines frequently require manual resets, which creates more radio traffic and interrupts workflow. To address these challenges, I would focus on providing the proper tools and resources for each position, clarifying roles and responsibilities so staff understand their scope and accountability, and implementing a mentorship program where experienced personnel guide and train newer individuals. These steps would improve efficiency, support staff in their roles, and ensure the station operates effectively to serve the community.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a mentorship program where experienced staff train and guide newer employees. Make roles and responsibilities clear so staff know what they are accountable for.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Management culture and leadership approach; Mentorship programs
