COMMENT:
Classifications within bargaining units need to be thoroughly reviewed. I currently serve in a classification designated by the State as an Administrative Worker, yet I am exposed to toxins and unsafe environments approximately 90% of the time. Despite performing duties comparable to others who receive State Safety retirement benefits, my position is excluded from that designation.

I believe the State should evaluate positions assigned to agencies with inherently high-risk environments and implement reclassifications where appropriate, rather than requiring these changes to go through the bargaining process where they often become negotiation points. Employees in specialized or marginalized classifications—such as mine, which includes only 13 positions statewide—lack the collective representation needed to advocate effectively for these changes. A more equitable review process is necessary to ensure fair recognition and protection for all employees performing hazardous work.
IDEA_TEXT: Review job classifications in high-risk work environments. Move employees doing dangerous work into safety retirement benefit categories without going through bargaining negotiations.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Job classification; Qualified staff

COMMENT: One way I’ve improved efficiency and service is by fostering strong communication and teamwork through shared meals at the station, such as breakfast and dinner. These gatherings allow the crew to check in with each other, delegate tasks, address needs, and build a supportive environment, which helps ensure daily operations run smoothly. On the public-facing side, I prioritize engaging with the community by visiting local businesses, being available to answer questions, and maintaining a visible presence. This approach builds trust, strengthens relationships, and helps us better understand and serve the needs of our area.
IDEA_TEXT: Build team unity and community trust. Hold shared meals at work stations to improve communication and teamwork. Engage with local businesses and maintain visible community presence to build relationships and better serve residents.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Public-facing services and resident experience; Organizational structure and culture
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal communication; Public participation; Trust and openness; Work culture

COMMENT:
Primary Problem:
When state departments restructure teams or redistribute workloads without hiring additional staff, employees are often left managing the same amount, or more work, with fewer people. This creates burnout, inefficiency, and errors. Team members begin to feel like they’re constantly behind, unable to meet expectations, and working reactively instead of proactively. Over time, this erodes morale and productivity, leading to higher turnover and lost institutional knowledge. 

Before any team restructuring, require a Workload Impact Assessment from management to evaluate how the redistribution of tasks will affect timelines, quality of work, productivity, and employee well-being. This should include clear metrics such as workload ratios, task priorities, and realistic turnaround times and involve direct input from the staff who understand the day-to-day demands. Departments should also implement temporary workload support plans, and training managers how to support their team when they are taking on a doubled workload. 

By assessing impact before implementing a big change like that, California can prevent inefficiencies caused by chronic overload, promote employee well-being and reduce the likelihood of good employees leaving, and ensure high-quality service delivery even during tough transitions.
IDEA_TEXT: Require Workload Impact Assessments before team restructuring. Management must evaluate how task redistribution affects timelines, work quality, productivity, and employee well-being. Include input from staff who know daily demands, set clear workload ratios and realistic deadlines, create temporary support plans, and train managers to help teams handle increased workloads.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Management culture and leadership; Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Organizational structure and culture; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee retention; Internal feedback; Management culture and leadership approach; Process design and methodologies; Work culture

COMMENT: One problem I'd like to address is the amount of unnecessary traffic created by work that could be done digitally. Every day, thousands of unnecessary extra cars are on the road for tasks that don't actually need to happen in person, which adds to air pollution and street/freeway congestion. This not only harms the environment, but also makes commuting harder for workers whose jobs truly require them to be on-site, like public safety or field staff. By improving and leaning into digital systems and tools to promote remote work, we'd cut emissions, ease traffic, and make it easier for essential employees to get there where they need to be.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve digital systems and tools to promote remote work. This will reduce traffic, cut emissions, and make it easier for essential workers to get to job sites.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Technology and digital innovation; Public-facing services and resident experience
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digital services; Environmental sustainability; Public transportation; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: Several problems really. But, the first is the integration of newer technology into the workplace. Each State agency has individual IT departments without a centralized, standardized model for operations. Yes, CDT exists, but they have limited control over which systems each agency uses. This minimizes their ability to provide statewide solutions to tech-based problems. It also means that each agency is essentially trying to solve their tech problems independently, sometimes without the necessary expertise to do so. This same idea applies to HR departments. Each HR department is under the guidance of CalHR, but often do things their own way. That lack of standardization causes issues in the short and long term, as policies and procedures are often more at the whim of senior leadership, without a larger strategy, and can even, at times, be in conflict with CalHR expectations. In both the CDT and CalHR cases, having these agencies be the owner of all IT and HR positions that are effectively deployed to other State agencies could resolve the lack of standardization across both critical functions. It ensures that every position receives the same base training for their respective fields, and even centralizes the hiring process for thousands of positions, freeing up other State agency partners to focus on their individual mission areas.
IDEA_TEXT: Centralize IT and HR functions statewide. Have CDT own all IT positions and CalHR own all HR positions that work at other state agencies. This would create standard training, hiring, and procedures across all departments.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Budget and resources; Communication and knowledge sharing; Governance and oversight
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Employee training; Org structure and hierarchy; Shared resources

COMMENT: There's a need for geospatial intelligence to support State operations. The Federal government has the NGA, but California has no State counterpart. CDT could adopt the role if their staff was increased. Or, a separate agency could be established to acquire, analyze, and provide geospatial intelligence products to State agencies that need them for their operations. Without such a centralized hub, each State agency is on its own to develop a GIS cadre, contract out to receive overhead (satellite) imagery and/or drones. The decentralization ultimately costs the state more money and provides less support, as those with limited budgets might not even request something they need but cannot afford. Establish a California Geospatial Intelligence entity.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a California Geospatial Intelligence agency or expand CDT to provide mapping and satellite data services to all state agencies from one central location.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Technology and digital innovation; Communication and knowledge sharing; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Shared resources; Software and tools; Technology and data modernization

COMMENT: Onboarding new employees is a painful process; especially gearing them up with the right tools, which are usually technology based. Since very few prospects start the day they receive the offer letter; imaging laptops/tablets/pads /cellular telephones/ computers so that they have the same imprint, days before an employee starts should be helpful; checklists to see if they have access to all of the right tools (HR/timekeeping, browsers, e-mails) should be part of the duty statement; there should be an automatic ticket created with IT, days before new employees start, for a check-in at the end of their 2nd full day; to put some teeth behind it. managers/supervisors should be evaluated on how fast their new hires get equipped (specific to the department & role) & as a further incentive, laggards (those in the bottom quartile) should have to attend mandatory training on proper onboarding processes.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve new employee setup process. Image laptops and devices with standard software before start date, create automatic IT tickets for equipment checks, use checklists to verify access to HR systems and email, and evaluate managers on how quickly new hires get equipped with mandatory training for poor performers.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Technology and digital innovation; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee performance reviews; Employee training; Manager training; Onboarding new employees; Software and tools

COMMENT: An issue we have been dealing with is lack of support from DGS regarding auto inspector approvals for repairs to state vehicles. They are not providing training or communication for their policies, besides expecting employees to memorize the Fleet manual. When an employee makes a mistake with obtaining pre-approval for a repair, or if the shop completes additional repairs and authorization is not obtained, the auto inspectors are denying approvals and are not able to provide constructive next steps.  Auto Inspectors should be advocating for the State's vehicles and equipment being maintained for safe operation and should be HELPING employees with the process. There is currently no process in place for when DGS will not approve a repair since SCO requires the DGS stamp of approval (for auto repairs over the established dollar amounts) in order to process voucher payments. DGS needs to work with state agencies instead of against us in this matter. Proactive communication and training should be available and provided to enforce and educate on changing expectations.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a clear process for handling denied vehicle repair approvals between DGS and SCO to ensure payments can still be processed.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Digitize processes; Process design and methodologies; Process documentation

COMMENT:
I think as a society in general we should be working towards less cars on the road, and therefore there should be a push for EVERYONE that can work from home to do so, not just state employees. It is a total waste of time, money, space, resources to make millions of people clog up the freeways to commute to an office to do the same work they can do in their own home office. That is the way of the future, and I really wish California would step up and lead the way. 

Sadly, I feel like this might never happen because of our capitalist society.
IDEA_TEXT: Push for all workers who can work from home to do so, not just state employees. Lead California toward less car traffic and remote work as the future.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Remote work and return to office

COMMENT: Simplify and streamline the hiring process by removing requirements on both the applicant and the hiring department. Polling applicants and departments could reveal the most arduous requirements, which could then be considered for revision or removal.
IDEA_TEXT: Make hiring easier by removing hard requirements for job seekers and hiring departments. Survey both groups to find the most difficult requirements and consider changing or removing them.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Process improvement; Communication and knowledge sharing
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Hiring and recruitment; Internal feedback; Process design and methodologies

COMMENT:
Telework Favoritism

Problem:

Managers allow their favorite employees to telework without supervision and provide all these reasons why their favorite people are allowed to telework. The employees they do not like, they do not allow them to telework, and make up all these excuses as to why they are not allowed to telework. 

Solution:

Telework should not be on a case-by-case basis, because that allows managers to create their own criteria for which employees can telework and which cannot. Telework should be based on qualifications. Can that position be done remotely, or can it only be done in the office.
IDEA_TEXT: Make telework decisions based on job requirements, not manager preferences. Create clear rules about which positions can work remotely based on whether the job can be done from home.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership approach; Remote work and return to office

COMMENT:
Job Positions on Hold  

Problem:

Some positions have been on hold for months and years, and managers give them to their favorite people; they do not advertise the position —they give it to a transfer employee. 

People who can apply for that position are having their opportunity taken away because the position is not advertised and given to transfer employees. These transfer positions are not the exception; they are the rule and are being abused. 

Solution:

Transfer employees and current employees should be able to apply for the available position. No favoritism should be allowed in job positions, and they should be advertised.
IDEA_TEXT: Make job hiring fair and open. Require all positions to be advertised publicly so current staff and transfer employees can both apply. Stop managers from giving jobs to their favorite people without posting the position.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Governance and oversight
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Accountability and transparency; Hiring and recruitment

COMMENT: I have noticed that innovation and technology are being implemented inconsistently and mostly for the benefit of the state employee, not for the public or registered users accessing the system or requiring assistance. Electronic payment options, integration with access systems similar to IDme, and more direct engagement with the public (which has declined over the last few years) would all be beneficial.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve technology systems for the public. Add electronic payment options, integrate with access systems like IDme, and increase direct engagement with residents.

COMMENT: Clarity, specificity, and guidance. I am fairly new to the state. My role isn't the easiest to define and probably can't be trained as such. COMMENT: It might sound counterintuitive, but shortening the work week has been shown in many companies and countries to actually improve productivity. I've noticed at my organization that in groups where there's less strict time management, people are a lot happier and more motivated. California could be a leader in the movement to have a four-day work week!
IDEA_TEXT: Implement a four-day work week for California state employees to improve productivity and job satisfaction.

COMMENT: There are many ways in which we can boost morale that need to be taken into consideration. Employees should not be paying to park to work. At my office, if you are lucky, you can find a spot at the lot that charges $8 per day. Some offices have a parking structure that charge $300 per month. We already pay with our time to commute, we pay for car insurance, we pay for gas, and to pay to park when we have to return to the office is a financial stressor for many employees. There are additional hidden fees of employment besides what is already taken from our checks. We had to pool our resources to replace the microwave, we pay for our water, the coffee, replacement of kitchen appliances, and mandatory holiday gatherings. We are told we have to attend team building events, but we are expected to pay or make the food and decorations. We are asked to give for food drives, for fundraising, for birthdays, and potlucks, and we give and give and give... I don't want to break even when I work after paying for food, gas, parking, and stocking my employer with appliances because we won't be able to heat or store our food otherwise. Morale isn't low because we lack a talent show. I don't want a party; I want free parking and free cold water.
IDEA_TEXT: Provide free basic workplace amenities like water, kitchen appliances, and coffee instead of requiring employees to pay for them.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Workplace operations; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Office management

COMMENT: As a new employee in a large department, I'm already seeing some issues with siloing between branches and divisions that is resulting in duplication of effort, which leads to turf issues and confusion among staff and external partners. In some of these cases, we have consultants doing work in one branch that staff are doing in another branch, with no awareness between the two branches that the same work is being done. I think a few things would help with this: more communication both up and down the chain within branches, better communication between branches, and stronger leadership to communicate the expectation of collaboration and alignment between branches to break down silos, reduce duplication of effort, and clarify roles and responsibilities.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve communication and break down silos between branches. Create stronger leadership that expects collaboration, reduces duplicate work, and clarifies roles between branches and divisions.

COMMENT: The primary issue among state employees is the wasteful spending of having to use a small business. Small businesses are marking items up so much that it adds up so quickly and eats away at our lab consumable/supply budget. The small businesses also add a $49 shipping fee (why?) when items do not come from the small business but rather come from various vendors to us. They are the middleman placing orders with the vendors. $49 x # orders equal a lot of money. Small businesses are taking advantage of the state it seems to line their pockets. Also, the time it takes to process orders through purchasing takes a month to two months. We are not set up to conduct research in the lab as a result.  When I joined the state many years ago, we had a limited $ blanket and we called the vendor directly and placed our order. We received our orders fairly quick. We need to have the ordering better streamlined in terms of processing time and how to save taxpayer $ and get rid of utilization of small business and be able to go back to ordering from vendors directly.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow state agencies to order supplies directly from vendors instead of using small businesses as middlemen. Speed up the purchasing process to reduce order times from 1-2 months to weeks.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Bureaucracy; Procurement

COMMENT:
First of all, I want to thank all the staff responsible for bringing this Forum into existence and the staff that are running and monitoring this forum. 
Here's my contribution after working for the State of California on and off for 15+ years.

State salaries
Certain State positions are pigeonhole into arbitrary (or based on outdated data) salary ranges without taking account location and parity with the private sector.

For example, the HFEN (Health Facility Evaluator Nurse) position. A comparable position in the private sector, in the San Francisco area, (according to Google) is around $155K (for a nurse consultant).  My compensation for 2023 according to Transparent California =$98,271.44 (not counting benefits as the average salary quoted by Google does not include benefits)

I've been with the State long enough to know that trying to ask for fair compensation is like trying to get blood from stone. 
I get it. We are painted with the same brush. We are stigmatized as being lazy, inefficient, bureaucratic etc. What tax payers would reward State workers with a pay raise? (slim to none)

So here is my proposal. Don't reward my efficiency with more work and flowery praise. Compensate me financially. There's an Over Time budget within each department. Give me access to the OT budget, I don't mind writing a proposal on how I can increase your/my Department's efficiency or backlogs to solve your problems. I'm ready to kick start this in my Department ...Are any Managers ready?
IDEA_TEXT: Update state salary ranges to match private sector pay and location costs. Give high-performing staff access to overtime budgets to work on efficiency projects and reduce backlogs instead of just giving praise.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits; Employee performance reviews; Employee retention

COMMENT:
Salay compensation for positions comparable to the private sector. 
Ok, that's a pipe dream.

How about within $20K of the private sector. 

I'm looking at my printed office phone number list from 2023 to now. 28 employees left (90% because of salary, 5% retirement, 5% others). 
I've been here on and off for 12 years. It looks like management would rather spend money training new employees rather than pay more for quality.
IDEA_TEXT: Increase salary compensation to be within $20,000 of private sector pay for comparable positions.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee pay and benefits

COMMENT: Excessive programming, projects, and the associated encumbered funds. Over the years I've seen much waste. Some in inescapable since ideal systems don't exist.  But I do see: -funds encumbered for staff or programs that go unstaffed for several years. -Projects and programs and their associated spending that are "good ideas" but do not support greater objectives or strategies. -Overfunded projects and programs that don't seem to bring value to Californians when compared to other underfunded programs that do bring value. -Frivolous last minute spending to justify retention of "Use it or Lose it" funding resulting in acquisition and storage of unnecessary and useless materials that ultimately get thrown out years later. If a program has no need of the money, it should be let go for other programs that do have need.
IDEA_TEXT: End wasteful last-minute spending. Stop buying unnecessary items just to use up budgets before losing funding.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Budget and resources

COMMENT:
There are too many review steps in almost every government process. This usually happens because of errors in the process which lead to management wanting additional review to ensure there are less errors. When errors continue to happen, the review level continues to escalate. This results in leadership thinking their staff are unable to complete the task correctly and frustrating staff on the "moving target" or unclear expectations caused by various style preferences from different reviewers. This leads to requirements for staff to create additional documentation such as summaries, cover memos, and routing packets to navigate the higher management level reviewing the work. It is extra, non-value added work to explain what the work is why they are approving it. I recommend lowering the approval level to the lowest supervisory level who knows the program. This will reduce turn-around time for requests, improve customer satisfaction (since they get whatever service we provide sooner), increased confidence in employees, and better morale for teams.
Additional levels of review do not eliminate errors, but instead increase them due to different standards between different levels of reviews. Instead, find out why the errors are happening and fix them at the source. This can be done easily between supervisors and front-line staff.
IDEA_TEXT: Find and fix the root causes of errors instead of adding more review steps. Have supervisors work directly with front-line staff to solve problems at the source.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Communication and knowledge sharing; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Internal feedback; Management culture and leadership approach; Process design and methodologies

COMMENT:
Hiring practices often appear inconsistent and inequitable. On one hand, there is a strong emphasis on rewarding current civil service experience, while on the other, external candidates are expected to meet disproportionately high educational and experiential thresholds. For instance, some classifications equate one year of state service to a master’s degree plus three years of professional experience. This raises a critical question: how can a single year of internal service be considered equivalent to such extensive academic and professional preparation? This practice not only undermines the value of formal education and diverse professional backgrounds but also creates systemic barriers for qualified individuals outside the state system—effectively making the process exclusionary.

To improve both efficiency and equity in hiring, California should implement a transparent, competency-based hiring framework. This would involve clearly defining the skills, knowledge, and outcomes expected for each classification and allowing candidates—whether internal or external—to demonstrate their qualifications through a combination of experience, education, and performance-based assessments. Such a model would reduce arbitrary equivalencies, promote fairness, and ensure that the most capable individuals are selected to serve the public, regardless of their entry point into government.
IDEA_TEXT: Create a fair, skill-based hiring system. Define clear job skills and let all candidates show their abilities through experience, education, and tests instead of using unfair comparisons between state service and outside experience.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Hiring and recruitment; Job classification; Qualified staff

COMMENT: One thing that’s really helped improve our effectiveness is using a external SharePoint site to manage aspects of our program work and how we connect with our counties. Our external site has streamlined how we collect deliverables, track performance measures, and coordinate with local partners. Instead of juggling emails and scattered files, everything is organized in one place—report templates, meeting schedules, contact lists, policies and procedures, and scopes of work. It’s made collaboration smoother, reduced delays, and helped ensure everyone is aligned on expectations, deliverables, and timelines.
IDEA_TEXT: Use external SharePoint sites to manage program work and connect with partners. Create one central place for deliverables, performance tracking, report templates, meeting schedules, contact lists, policies, procedures, and scopes of work to improve collaboration and reduce delays.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Process improvement; Communication and knowledge sharing; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Digitize processes; Process design and methodologies; Shared data; Software and tools; State cloud storage

COMMENT: I have worked as a State employee for approximately 7 years now and I feel the lack of accountability in departments and individual employees contributes to the burn out of highly productive employees. I also manage employees, and the current performance management system makes supervisors and managers feel unsupported to the point where it is easier to let an employee do what they want rather than hold them accountable. Prior to working in the public sector, I worked 12 years in the private sector and find it difficult to understand why it is so much more difficult to hold employees in the public sector accountable than in the private sector.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve the performance management system to better support supervisors and managers in holding employees accountable.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee performance reviews; Management culture and leadership approach

COMMENT: The state would save SO much money if it would revisit the mandatory new supervisor 80 hour training, 40 hour mandatory CEA training, and 40 hour mandatory manager training.  These used to be in-person classes where departments would pay CalHR approximately $1500 and staff would have an opportunity to collaborate with colleagues from other departments, get training, have an opportunity to ask questions and share ideas, etc. During COVID these classes went virtual and of the approximately 30 staff who have taken these courses since who I have personally discussed this with, they receive no value from the virtual trainings.  They are go-at-your-own pace, there is no engagement with others, and staff just have them running in the background to "knock them out" while they get their work done; yet departments still pay CalHR $1500 per employee.  Is there really a $1500 cost to CalHR when it's a virtual course with no instructor, no meeting room rental, no CalHR staff time, etc?  I understand this funds CalHR, but for industry-funded departments and programs who have to pay this cost, are they really getting any value for their dollar?  If CalHR would like to keep these courses automated and with no CalHR involvement the cost should be drastically reduced.  Or, to get more bang for their buck, it would be a much better investment if these courses went back to in-person.  Or at least allow departments that option.
IDEA_TEXT: Allow departments to choose between virtual or in-person training for mandatory supervisor, CEA, and manager courses to get better value for the $1500 cost.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Management culture and leadership
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Manager training

I understand this funds CalHR, but for industry-funded departments and programs who have to pay this cost, are they really getting any value for their dollar?  If CalHR would like to keep these courses automated and with no CalHR involvement the cost should be drastically reduced.  Or, to get more bang for their buck, it would be a much better investment if these courses went back to in-person.  Or at least allow departments that option.
IDEA_TEXT: Reduce costs for mandatory supervisor and manager training. Either cut prices for virtual courses or bring back in-person classes with the option for departments to choose.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Management culture and leadership; Budget and resources
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Budgeting and funding; Manager training

COMMENT:
I work in a department that trains new staff to do their role within our agency and we have been working to improve onboarding processes within the agency for all staff. When I was first hired the agency in work for only focused on onboarding for one group of staff and not the staff that support our agency mission, today it is better with more training focusing on onboarding and training for all classifications within the agency. 

Looking at current practices for onboarding and training is the first step to improving how an agency moves forward to training and retain employees. Training departments also need to understand the importance of surveys/evaluations in their training to get real feedback on how they are doing with their training. However, surveys are no good if a department/agency does not have someone to look at the data and bring forth the results of the survey. 

The second step is to have good documentation of current and past processes used within the agency or department.  I have found the lack of documentation waste time and does not allow improvement to easily happen. Understand that people leave and how they do there job is important for the next person who will continue that role within the department. If the role is new to the department, have a clear plan of what is expected for the new person and the resources available to them. 

The third step is to have someone assigned to new staff as a mentor. Make sure the mentor understands their role.
IDEA_TEXT: Improve staff onboarding and training. Look at current practices, use surveys to get feedback and review the data, document all processes clearly, and assign mentors to new staff with clear roles.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Recruitment, hiring, and retention; Process improvement
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Employee training; Mentorship programs; Onboarding new employees; Process documentation

COMMENT: Two of the biggest problems I've come across in State Employment in three different departments are closely related. These problems are the the lack of standardized training, and that the allocated number of job positions do not scale based on each office's workload. The number of staff available to give effective training is impacted by the face that the number of staff in each office within the same department, branch, and division is not scaled for the workload within their respective jurisdictions. For example, every office has the same number of AGPAs and PT IIs while 35% of the offices had at least double the workload within their respective jurisdictions than the rest of the offices. Unbalanced workloads between two locations will lead to the loss in retention of staff. This eventually will require training for new staff that replace those employees that leave a busier office. When there isn't standardized training each employee, and office, will come up with processes that may be incorrect. Eventually an audit may be completed and the staff in the office find out after the audit that they were incorrectly trained and things have been processed incorrectly. Some of the shortcuts in processes occur because there is a lack of staff, which ties back to the issue of job positions not scaling based on workload.
IDEA_TEXT: Create standardized training programs across all departments and offices.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Recruitment, hiring, and retention
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Employee training

COMMENT: One of the best outcomes that has occurred within the department I'm employed in was due to COVID-19. We now have monthly all staff meetings across all branches in the department. Additionally, all of our field offices had monthly, now bi-monthly, meetings for all the field offices' PT IIs and AGPAs to attend. In those meetings we are able to ask questions and share any issues we have with processes, or ask for more guidance. There may be a SME in another office who is able to provide the information, or the facilitator of the meeting is able to find out or elevate the issue to leadership. Since these meetings occur via Microsoft Teams, the chat is open and anyone may ask questions at any time.
IDEA_TEXT: Hold regular all-staff meetings across departments using Microsoft Teams. Include field offices and allow open chat for questions and guidance sharing.
IDEA_MAIN_THEMES_LIST: Communication and knowledge sharing; Technology and digital innovation
IDEA_SUBTHEMES_LIST: Cross-agency collaboration; Internal communication; Software and tools
